game_explanation:
This public goods game has 23 rounds with 4 players (SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN). Each round each player either contributes the whole 20 coins to the shared pot (contribute = 20) or withdraws all 20 for private use (contribute = 0). The shared pot is multiplied by 2.5 and split equally, so mutual cooperation (everyone gives 20) yields the highest group payoff and is individually attractive if others also contribute; however, any single player gains by withdrawing while others contribute. Punishment is available after outcomes are revealed: sending punishment units is costly to the sender (1 coin per unit) and removes 2 coins per unit from the target. Players cannot identify who punished them, and they see only round-level summaries.

Given the player personas:
- GORILLA is strongly inclined to contribute and to enforce cooperation (likely to punish defectors).
- DUCK tends to reciprocate and punish norm violations moderately.
- CHICKEN tends to start cooperative but can free-ride or "test" norms; less inclined to retaliate strongly.
- SLOTH is mixed/inconsistent, more likely to occasionally shirk.

Expectations across rounds:
- Early rounds: cooperation will be the default from most players; occasional isolated defections (often by SLOTH, sometimes by CHICKEN/DUCK) will prompt targeted, small punishments (mostly from GORILLA and DUCK).
- Mid-game: cooperation will remain common as punishments help enforce norms; occasional testing/defection persists, with slightly stronger punishment if repeated.
- Late-game / endgame: contributions will decline as the horizon shortens; players will punish less because the deterrent effect is reduced and punishment costs become relatively more salient. That will produce more defections and fewer punishments in the final rounds.

Now the full round-by-round transcript.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN </PLAYERS>

This 4-player repeated public-goods game pits individual short-term incentives (keep coins privately) against collective benefit (contribute to the shared pot which is multiplied by 1.8 and split). With 29 known rounds, players face typical uncertainty about others' willingness to contribute and whether rewards will be used to encourage cooperators or reward defectors. Based on the provided personas, I expect DUCK to be reliably cooperative (high contributions, often rewarding cooperators), GORILLA to contribute relatively high early on but reward others rarely, SLOTH to contribute very little across rounds, and CHICKEN to behave cautiously with low-to-moderate contributions and occasional small positive rewards. Rewards are costly (3 coins for the sender to give 2 to the recipient), so they will be used sparingly and mostly to signal approval to consistently high contributors (primarily DUCK and sometimes GORILLA). Over time small declines in average contribution are likely as the end approaches, but DUCK's warm-glow tendency will keep them among the top contributors most rounds; sparing rewards will occasionally try to sustain cooperation, but free-riding by SLOTH (and occasional dips by GORILLA/CHICKEN) will limit full cooperation.

game_explanation:
This 6-round public-goods game creates a tension between individual short-term gain (withdrawing the full 20 coins) and group efficiency (leaving coins in the pot so they are multiplied and shared). Because contributions are all-or-nothing (0 or 20), choices are visible each round and can elicit post-round social responses (punish or reward). Key incentives:
- Free-riding (withdrawing 20) raises a player's immediate private payoff when others contribute, so selfish types (GORILLA, DUCK tendency) are likely to test or persist in free-riding.
- Cooperators (CHICKEN strongly prosocial; SLOTH moderately so) are inclined to contribute, sometimes rewarding other cooperators.
- Punishment can deter free-riding (it costs the punisher 3 per unit but reduces a target by 7), while rewards cost 3 and give the recipient +4; both are relatively costly, so actions are usually sparse.
Uncertainties and dynamics:
- Early rounds can establish norms: if defectors go unpunished, defection tends to persist. If cooperators selectively punish or reward, some defectors may switch to contributing.
- In the final round, backward induction predicts more defection (fewer future consequences), but strong intrinsic cooperators (CHICKEN) often still contribute.
- Because punishments/rewards are anonymous in the summary (players don't see who applied them), signalling still matters but reputation effects are muted relative to fully identified interactions.

game_explanation:
This 6-round public goods game creates a tension between individual incentives and collective benefit. Each coin a player keeps yields 1 coin to themselves; each coin contributed yields 1.9 coins to the group, split equally among three players (so each contributed coin returns 0.633... to each player). That means the private (marginal) return on contributing is less than 1, so the dominant individual action is to contribute 0. But the group maximizes total welfare if everyone contributes more. Expectations, conditional cooperation, and limited communication can produce intermediate contributions early on; however, because players see peers' round payoffs (not contributions) and the game is finite (6 rounds), we typically expect:
- Early rounds: attempts to coordinate or signal cooperation (some players may propose or announce contributions), producing moderate contributions from at least one player.
- Mid game: conditional cooperators will adjust based on observed payoffs; free-riders who benefit without contributing may keep contributing little.
- Endgame (round 6): contributions tend to fall (selfish “last round” behavior), although some players with a generosity tendency may still give a small amount.
Uncertainties: players can announce their intended contribution (truthful or not); announcements can influence others. Because net payoffs are shown after each round, players infer others’ behavior but cannot perfectly identify individual contributions unless players disclose them. Given the personas, expect GORILLA to try to coordinate moderate-to-high contributions, DUCK to be somewhat generous and responsive, and SLOTH to contribute little and free-ride when possible.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK </PLAYERS>

This is a repeated 3-player public goods game with a marginal per-coin group return of 2/3 (each coin contributed yields 0.666... coins back to the contributor via the multiplied-and-shared pot). Absent sanctions, the dominant short-run incentive is to free-ride (contribute 0) because everyone keeps more by withholding. However, the punishment stage lets players spend coins to reduce others' payoffs (1 coin spent removes 2 coins from the target), which can support cooperation if punishments are credible and targeted.

Uncertainties: players only see round-level summaries (own net payoff, how much they punished others, and how much punishment reduced them) and cannot observe who punished whom. That makes targeted retaliation ambiguous, but aggregate punishment histories can still signal norms. Based on the personas: GORILLA is strongly self-interested and inclined to free-ride; DUCK is the most cooperative and enforcement-oriented; SLOTH is moderately cooperative but somewhat tolerant. I therefore expect DUCK and SLOTH to contribute positive amounts most rounds and to punish GORILLA occasionally to deter persistent free-riding. Punishment will be used sparingly because it is costly to the punisher, and cooperation will drift down toward the end as the horizon approaches.

game_explanation:
This 10-round public goods game sets up a tension between private withdrawals and collective benefit. Each round every player can keep some coins privately (withdraw) while leaving the rest as a group contribution; the group pot is multiplied by 2.1 and split equally. Because the individual return on one coin contributed is (2.1/4 = 0.525) < 1, the dominant short-term incentive is to withdraw (free-ride). However, players may contribute to raise the shared pie for everyone. Rewarding (costly to the giver, beneficial to the recipient) is the only post-decision enforcement: it can encourage cooperation by rewarding contributors, but rewards are costly and therefore used sparingly in practice.

Given the provided personas, I expect:
- SLOTH: moderately cooperative and somewhat responsive to fairness; likely to contribute a middling amount early and occasionally reward high contributors to sustain cooperation.
- GORILLA: relatively self-interested / low initial prosociality; likely to contribute little and rarely reward.
- DUCK: minimal free contributions and little reward-giving; may prioritize private payoffs.
- CHICKEN: mixed — can contribute moderately but is sensitive to reciprocation; likely to both contribute and reciprocate small rewards early.

Overall dynamics I predict: modest cooperation early from SLOTH and CHICKEN, with GORILLA and DUCK free-riding. A few small, reciprocal rewards in early rounds (SLOTH ↔ CHICKEN) to sustain CHICKEN's higher contributions briefly. As free-riding persists, cooperative contributions decline across rounds and rewards mostly disappear. There might be one small final-round token reciprocity.

game_explanation: This public goods game pits four identifiable players (SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN) against a classic tension: each coin kept privately yields 1 to the chooser, but each coin contributed to the shared pot is multiplied by 1.8 and split among four players, so each contributed coin returns only 0.45 to the contributor. Pure selfish incentives push toward low contributions, while group-optimal behavior would keep most coins in the pot. Because punishment and reward are available (costly to the source), conditional cooperators can sustain higher contributions early on by punishing clear free-riders or rewarding high contributors, but these instruments are used sparingly in real experiments. Given the provided personas, expect SLOTH to be the most steady contributor (conditional cooperator), GORILLA and DUCK to hover in the middle and respond to incentives, and CHICKEN to contribute the least and provoke occasional sanctions. Over 29 rounds we should see modest initial cooperation that gradually erodes toward the endgame, with intermittent, targeted punishment of persistent low contributors (mostly CHICKEN) and occasional small rewards to sustain cooperation. Punishment and reward events will be infrequent and low in magnitude.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN </PLAYERS>

This is a one-shot repeated public goods game with 23 rounds. Each player can either leave the whole 20-coin pot in the group (contribute 20) or withdraw it privately (contribute 0). The group pot is multiplied by 2.5 and split equally. That creates a classic social dilemma: the group is best off if everyone contributes (each would get 50), but each individual always gets 20 more by withdrawing than by contributing, regardless of others' choices (defection is the dominant strategy).

Given the player personas:
- SLOTH is highly pro-social and likely to try contributing early and may attempt to sustain cooperation.
- GORILLA is moderately cooperative but sensitive to being exploited; likely to contribute early and switch to withdrawing after a few poor relative payoffs.
- DUCK is moderately cooperative and forgiving — may contribute initially but may drift toward withdrawing if exploitation persists.
- CHICKEN has low cooperative tendency and is likely to withdraw from the start.

Typical dynamics: a few initial contributions (driven by SLOTH and maybe GORILLA/DUCK), CHICKEN free-rides and gets higher payoffs, others notice that defecting yields higher immediate returns and begin to withdraw in subsequent rounds. Cooperation decays; by the middle of the 23 rounds most or all players will withdraw each round. Chat will be sparse: brief promises, short complaints, occasional coordination attempts, and then resignation.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK </PLAYERS>

This is a finite (16-round) repeated public goods game with 3 players. Each round every player privately chooses an integer contribution 0–20 from their 20-coin endowment. The group pot (sum of contributions) is doubled and split equally among the three players. Because the marginal per-player return on each contributed coin is 2/3 (< 1), the dominant short-run incentive is to free-ride (contribute 0) while hoping others contribute; the social optimum is full contribution (20 each). Repetition and feedback create scope for conditional cooperation: some players may start cooperating and then reduce contributions if others free-ride. There is also an end-game effect: toward the final rounds, expectations of future reciprocity vanish and contributions tend to fall.

Using the provided personas as priors:
- SLOTH looks like a conditional cooperator/fairness-minded (high ultimatum offer) but with mixed trust history — likely to start moderately high and then adjust down if peers free-ride.
- GORILLA shows relatively low offers in ultimatum-like tasks and mixed trust — likely to contribute low amounts consistently.
- DUCK has shown very high initial trust-sends but low return shares — likely to begin by contributing generously (trusting the group) but may reduce contributions quickly or exploit others once they see they can benefit from free-riding.

Given these tendencies plus the game's incentives and 16-round horizon, a plausible unfolding is: relatively high initial contributions (driven by SLOTH and DUCK), GORILLA contributes little, SLOTH reacts over the first several rounds by stepping down, DUCK reduces as it learns others free-ride (or exploits early), and contributions gradually decay toward low levels, with a drop near the final rounds.

game_explanation:
This public-goods game sets up a classic tension: contributing (putting your 20 into the shared pot) benefits the group but is individually costly because anyone who withdraws their 20 (defects/free-rides) earns an immediate 20 advantage each round. With the pot multiplied by 2.5 and split evenly, each contributor's contribution returns less to them than it costs unless many others also contribute. That creates an incentive to free-ride.

Given the four-player personas, a plausible unfolding is:
- DUCK and CHICKEN are relatively more pro-social and likely to start by contributing regularly and to punish defectors to defend cooperation.
- SLOTH is the most self-interested and tends to free-ride almost always.
- GORILLA is mixed: likely to defect early, respond to punishment, and possibly experiment with cooperation mid-game if it seems to reduce punishment or increase payoffs.

Punishment is costly to send (1 coin per unit) and expensive to receive (2 coins deducted per unit), so cooperators will punish sparingly; punishments are used mainly to try to discipline persistent defectors (especially SLOTH). As the finite horizon (23 rounds) approaches, cooperation typically unravels: enforcement costs pile up and the incentive to free-ride becomes stronger, so we expect cooperation to persist early–mid game but decay in later rounds, with final rounds dominated by defection and little punishment.

Below is a round-by-round transcript from round 1 through round 23 showing contributions (0 or 20) in the avatar order SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, sparse chat, punishments (only non-zero edges), and the summary shown to players (each player's net payoff for the round, amount they spent punishing others, and amount deducted from them by punishment).

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game with 4 players and 10 rounds. Early rounds are driven by uncertainty about others' cooperativeness: some players (DUCK) have a strong tendency to contribute, others (SLOTH) lean toward free-riding, and GORILLA and CHICKEN are mixed/moderate. Because contributions are multiplied and shared, high contributions benefit everyone, so conditional cooperators (CHICKEN, DUCK) will tend to reward or sometimes punish to sustain cooperation. Punishment is costly but potent (3 coins deducted per punishment unit), so it will be used sparingly and typically in response to clear defection. Rewards are cheaper for targets (1-to-1) and may be used to reinforce good behavior. Toward the middle rounds, social enforcement (a few punishments) can raise cooperation; toward the end, end-game incentives increase temptation to defect, so contributions may drift down unless sustained by reciprocity. Overall expect DUCK to remain relatively high, CHICKEN moderate, GORILLA mixed, and SLOTH to oscillate between low and moderate depending on sanctions.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK </PLAYERS>

This is a 6-round repeated public goods game with three players. Each round each player privately chooses how many of 20 coins to contribute to a group pot; the pot is multiplied by 1.9 and split equally among all players. Because 1.9/3 ≈ 0.633 < 1, each coin you personally contribute returns less than one coin to you — the private (selfish) incentive is to contribute 0. The social optimum (maximizing total group payoff) is full contribution by everyone, but individual incentives push toward free-riding.

Communication can change expectations: promises or offers to contribute can encourage matching, but messages are cheap and not verifiable. Repeated interaction can sustain cooperation early on if some players are conditional cooperators; however, given the player priors (SLOTH and DUCK predisposed to give little or nothing, GORILLA mixed/moderate), I predict initial outreach by GORILLA to try to establish cooperation, followed by decline as others fail to match. Uncertainty remains about whether visible messaging will persuade either SLOTH or DUCK; based on their profiles, they are unlikely to reciprocate. Expect contributions to start above zero for GORILLA and then fall across rounds; SLOTH and DUCK will mostly free-ride.

game_explanation:
This 3-player repeated public goods game sets up a tension between private incentives (keep the 20 coins) and collective benefits (pooled contributions are doubled and shared). With incomplete information each round (players don't see others' current choices), behavior will be guided by prior tendencies and feedback from revealed contributions and the costly punishment stage. Punishment is a deterrent: it reduces the target's payoff by 2 per unit but costs the punisher 1 per unit. Because punishers are anonymous, punishment reduces expected payoff of low contributions but makes targeted retaliation less precise — players infer norms from collective punishments rather than exact identities.

Given the personas:
- GORILLA is a conditional cooperator and relatively enforcement-prone (medium trust/reciprocity). Expect steady, above-average contributions and occasional small punishments targeted at persistent low contributors to enforce norms.
- DUCK is moderately wary and inconsistent — tends to contribute low-to-moderate amounts and sometimes follows others; may join punishment sporadically if they see persistent unfairness.
- SLOTH shows mixed signals (high outward trust but low sharing in allocation tasks and low return rates), so the prior leans toward under-contribution/free-riding unless the cost of punishment becomes salient. Over repeated rounds, SLOTH may raise contributions somewhat if punished repeatedly.

Overall likely unfolding: early rounds show a mix (some free-riding by SLOTH, conditional cooperation from GORILLA, modest contributions from DUCK). Small, infrequent punishments (1-unit) from GORILLA (and occasionally DUCK) target SLOTH to nudge them upward. As rounds progress, contributions converge upward (toward mutually beneficial levels) and punishment becomes rarer. Punishment events are sparse because they are costly to senders and hard to attribute.

# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK </PLAYERS>

This is a 16-round public goods game with three players. Each round everyone has 20 coins and chooses how many to contribute to a common pot; the pot is doubled and divided equally. The private-return for each coin contributed is 2/3 coin (because 2 * 1 / 3), so the individually dominant action is to contribute 0 (you keep 1 instead of getting 2/3 back). However, group payoff is maximized when everyone contributes more. Expect initial tentative cooperation from those with pro-social priors (SLOTH and DUCK) and persistent free-riding from the selfish prior (GORILLA). Over repeated rounds, conditional cooperators tend to reduce contributions if they see others free-riding; GORILLA’s low prosociality makes continued free-riding likely. Near the end (final rounds), forward-looking players may reduce contributions further because future reciprocity is limited. Summaries after each round show each player’s contribution and the net payoff they (and peers) received, which will shape adjustments.

game_explanation:
This public goods game gives each player a simple binary choice each round: withdraw all 20 coins (contribute 0) or leave all 20 in the pot (contribute 20). Because the pot is multiplied by 2.5 and split equally, every additional contributor increases everyone's shared payout, but each individual who withholds (withdraws 20) gets an immediate private gain of 20 in addition to the shared return. Mathematically the private gain from defecting instead of contributing is always +7.5 coins (20 - 12.5), so defecting is a strictly dominant action in one-shot reasoning.

Given the 23-round repeated setting, short-run dynamics typically show:
- Early rounds: some players with prosocial tendencies (here SLOTH and CHICKEN) will try to build cooperation by promising or contributing; others (GORILLA, DUCK) may exploit those promises or test reciprocity.
- Mid game: cooperation tends to unravel as defectors are rewarded and promises prove nonbinding; contributors get exploited and revert to defection.
- Occasional isolated contributions can appear later from conditional cooperators or as signaling, but sustained full cooperation is unlikely because defecting strictly dominates.
- Messaging will be sparse and strategic: short pledges, occasional explanations or blame after exploitation, and few late-game attempts at coordination. DUCK (very self-interested) will mostly defect and remain quiet; SLOTH (more trusting) will try to sustain cooperation occasionally; GORILLA (mixed) will sometimes signal cooperation but often defect; CHICKEN (mixed) will respond to others but will not reliably sustain cooperation.

Below is a round-by-round transcript from round 1 through round 23. At each round I give a short explanation for the expected behavior, then show any chat before the decisions, the contribution vector (exact avatar order SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN), and the round summary that shows each player's net payoff for that round (players see their own net and the peers' nets).
reflection:
This public goods game gives each player a simple binary choice each round: withdraw all 20 coins (contribute 0) or leave all 20 in the pot (contribute 20). Because the pot is multiplied by 2.5 and split equally, every additional contributor increases everyone's shared payout, but each individual who withholds (withdraws 20) gets an immediate private gain of 20 in addition to the shared return. Mathematically the private gain from defecting instead of contributing is always +7.5 coins (20 - 12.5), so defecting is a strictly dominant action in one-shot reasoning.

Given the 23-round repeated setting, short-run dynamics typically show:
- Early rounds: some players with prosocial tendencies (here SLOTH and CHICKEN) will try to build cooperation by promising or contributing; others (GORILLA, DUCK) may exploit those promises or test reciprocity.
- Mid game: cooperation tends to unravel as defectors are rewarded and promises prove nonbinding; contributors get exploited and revert to defection.
- Occasional isolated contributions can appear later from conditional cooperators or as signaling, but sustained full cooperation is unlikely because defecting strictly dominates.
- Messaging will be sparse and strategic: short pledges, occasional explanations or blame after exploitation, and few late-game attempts at coordination. DUCK (very self-interested) will mostly defect and remain quiet; SLOTH (more trusting) will try to sustain cooperation occasionally; GORILLA (mixed) will sometimes signal cooperation but often defect; CHICKEN (mixed) will respond to others but will not reliably sustain cooperation.

Below is a round-by-round transcript from round 1 through round 23. At each round I give a short explanation for the expected behavior, then show any chat before the decisions, the contribution vector (exact avatar order SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN), and the round summary that shows each player's net payoff for that round (players see their own net and the peers' nets).

GORILLA (mixed) may promise cooperation or stay silent; DUCK (selfish) will usually withhold contribution and stay quiet. Because choices are private, promises are inexpensive but nonbinding.

This is a repeated public-goods game with 4 players and 23 known rounds. Each round each player either keeps the 20 coins privately (contribute 0) or leaves them in the pot (contribute 20). The pot is multiplied by 2.5 and split equally, so individual incentives favor keeping coins (private gain of 20) while group welfare is highest if everyone contributes (each gets 50). Because defection strictly dominates in a single shot, we expect pressure toward free-riding over time, especially as the end approaches. However, personas matter: GORILLA has a strong cooperative tendency and will try to sustain contributions; SLOTH is inclined to free-ride nearly always; DUCK and CHICKEN are mixed/conditional cooperators who may start cooperatively but will reduce contributions if others repeatedly free-ride. Chat is possible but sparse; players will use it to propose cooperation or register frustration. Over rounds we predict initial cooperation from GORILLA and some others, persistent free-riding by SLOTH, conditional withdrawal by DUCK and CHICKEN, a gradual collapse of cooperation through the middle rounds, a short revival attempt before the final rounds, and near-universal defection in the known endgame (last few rounds).

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game with three players (SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK), fixed endowment 20 each round, 16 rounds total. Each round, players trade private holdings for a group return: all contributions are doubled then split evenly. Because the multiplier (2) divided by group size (3) yields a per-player return of 2/3 per coin contributed, each individual has a private incentive to keep coins (private marginal payoff 1) while the group would be better off when everyone contributes. That creates temptation to free-ride: low contributors can earn more the same round unless others punish them.

Punishment provides a tool to enforce cooperation: each punishment unit costs 1 to the punisher and removes 2 from the punished player. Punishment is costly and opaque (players don't learn who punished them), so it is used sparingly and strategically. From the personas: DUCK is strongly cooperative and likely to contribute high amounts and occasionally punish clear free-riders; GORILLA tends to freeride (low contributions) and may only increase contributions if punished enough; SLOTH is mixed/moderate—often near-average contributions, sometimes responding to social signals and occasional costs. Given these priors, a plausible dynamic is:
- Early rounds: DUCK contributes high, GORILLA contributes little; SLOTH moderate. A few targeted punishments (mostly by DUCK, sometimes SLOTH) toward GORILLA occur early to signal disapproval.
- Middle rounds: GORILLA raises contributions a bit after facing punishment; SLOTH drifts toward higher but not full cooperation. Punishment tails off as behavior stabilizes.
- Late rounds: contributions settle at moderately high levels (convergence but not full contribution), punishment is rare because group behavior is sufficiently cooperative or it's costly to punish near the end.

There is uncertainty: punishment effectiveness depends on magnitude and frequency; because punishment is anonymous, players may misattribute it. Also DUCK's ability to punish depends on leaving some endowment after contributing. Overall we should expect a handful of early punishments targeted at free-riding GORILLA, gradual rise in GORILLA's contributions, and low punishment later.

game_explanation:
This is a 6-round public goods game where each player either contributes all 20 coins to the shared pot (cooperate) or withdraws all 20 for private use (defect). The pot is multiplied by 1.6 and split equally, so each contributed 20 adds 32 to the pot and raises each player's share by 8. Because the per-player share from one cooperator (8) is less than the private value of keeping 20, there's a strong individual incentive to free-ride, especially late in the game. However, repeated interaction, visible contributions, and the availability of costly punishment and costly reward create incentives for enforcement and positive reciprocity: cooperators can reward one another to reinforce cooperation, or punish defectors to discourage free-riding. Punishment is relatively "efficient" at reducing targets' payoffs (7 units lost per punishment unit) but costly to the sender (3 coins), and rewards are smaller (target gains 4 per unit at cost 3). Players are anonymous in punishment/reward assignment (players don't see who targeted them), so signaling and group-level patterns matter more than direct tit-for-tat.

Given the four personas:
- SLOTH and GORILLA are inclined to cooperate early; SLOTH is especially trusting/cooperative.
- DUCK is conditional and may follow cooperators or punish defectors if they care.
- CHICKEN is more selfish and likely to defect early and sometimes exploit the group, though repeated punishment may push CHICKEN to switch.

I expect initial cooperation from SLOTH and GORILLA, DUCK probably cooperates at first, CHICKEN defects early. Cooperators reward occasionally and punish sparingly; punishments accumulate only when free-riding persists. Over rounds we should see conditional cooperation: CHICKEN may switch after being punished, rewarded when switching, but will be tempted to defect again on the final round.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN </PLAYERS>

This online public goods game gives each player a 20-coin endowment each round; contributions (0–20) determine the public pot that is multiplied by 1.8 and split equally. With 4 players, each coin contributed yields 1.8/4 = 0.45 coins back to each player (so the individual material return on a coin contributed is 0.45, less than the private return of 1 if withdrawn). Material incentives therefore favor free-riding (withdraw more) while group welfare favors high contributions.

Given the provided personas, I expect SLOTH and GORILLA to lean toward higher initial contributions (they tend to "send" in trust-like tasks), DUCK to be moderate/malleable, and CHICKEN to under-contribute. Because the game allows only positive rewards (cost 3 to giver, +2 to recipient), rewards tend to be used sparingly to recognize or incentivize cooperators rather than to punish defectors. Rewarding is costly, so only players who value sustaining cooperation (or who are reciprocation-minded) will pay to reward. Over the 29 rounds, typical dynamics are: somewhat cooperative early rounds, selective rewarding by one or two players to sustain contributions, gradual erosion of cooperation as some players reduce contributions and as the endgame approaches (final rounds often see declines). Uncertainty comes from how frequently players are willing to pay the cost of reward and whether defectors are deterred by the occasional reward of cooperators.

I will simulate the 29 rounds with these tendencies: GORILLA acts as the most frequent (but still sparing) rewarder to encourage DUCK and SLOTH; SLOTH contributes consistently but rarely uses rewards; DUCK adjusts modestly in response to incentives; CHICKEN under-contributes and receives little reward. Rewards are mostly single units when used.

Because the game allows only positive rewards (cost 3 to giver, +2 to recipient), rewards tend to be used sparingly to recognize or incentivize cooperators rather than to punish defectors. Rewarding is costly, so only players who value sustaining cooperation (or who are reciprocation-minded) will pay to reward. Over the 29 rounds, typical dynamics are: somewhat cooperative early rounds, selective rewarding by one or two players to sustain contributions, gradual erosion of cooperation as some players reduce contributions and as the endgame approaches (final rounds often see declines). Uncertainty comes from how frequently players are willing to pay the cost of reward and whether defectors are deterred by the occasional reward of cooperators.

I will simulate the 29 rounds with these tendencies: GORILLA acts as the most frequent (but still sparing) rewarder to encourage DUCK and SLOTH; SLOTH contributes consistently but rarely uses rewards; DUCK adjusts modestly in response to incentives; CHICKEN under-contributes and receives little reward. Rewards are mostly single units when used.

game_explanation:
This 6-round public goods game pits three players with different social preferences against the classic temptation to free-ride. SLOTH and GORILLA have histories of high contributions in allocation tasks and are likely to contribute fairly generously early on. DUCK has a history of low giving and will be tempted to free-ride (contribute little or nothing) to keep private earnings high. Because the group payoff is multiplied by 1.9 and shared equally, low contributors benefit from others' generosity; that creates an incentive for the generous players to use costly punishment to deter persistent free-riding. Punishment is expensive (cost 4 per unit to the punisher, -12 to the target), so it will be used sparingly — enough to signal norms or to correct behavior, but not so much that punishers always make themselves worse off.

Likely dynamics across rounds:
- Early rounds: SLOTH and GORILLA contribute high; DUCK contributes little. Initial punishment may be limited (players often give a warning or short message first).
- Mid rounds: If DUCK continues to free-ride, GORILLA (who is willing to enforce cooperation) will apply a small punishment; SLOTH may join or refrain depending on perceived efficacy and cost.
- Later rounds: DUCK may increase contributions after being punished once, restoring cooperation. As the endgame approaches (rounds 5–6), contributions often decline again; punishers decide whether to spend resources to signal disapproval despite little future benefit. Because punishments are anonymous, retaliation is less targeted but persistent free-riding may still be punished.

The transcript below simulates plausible choices, sparse chat, and limited, targeted punishment across 6 rounds.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game with four players and six rounds. Each round each player either contributes the full 20 coins to the public pot or withdraws the full 20 for private use. The total contributed is multiplied by 1.6 and split equally, so an individual's contribution is costly privately but raises everyone's share. Rewards are costly (3 coins per reward unit) and give a 4-coin benefit to the recipient; they can be used to reward cooperators or to try to change behavior, but reward spenders reduce their own round payoff. Players see round summaries (net payoff, reward spent, reward received) for everyone but do not see who gave which reward. Given the personas:

- SLOTH is strongly inclined to cooperate consistently; likely to contribute most rounds and to reward other cooperators.
- GORILLA is moderately cooperative and conditional: likely to start by cooperating but may defect later if defectors consistently earn more.
- DUCK tends to free-ride (withdraw) consistently.
- CHICKEN is a conditional cooperator: may cooperate early but can switch if defecting seems profitable.

I expect SLOTH to contribute most rounds, DUCK to defect every round, GORILLA to cooperate early then drift to defection as it becomes individually profitable, and CHICKEN to start cooperating then experiment/defect midgame before possibly returning briefly. Rewards will be sparse and focused on supporting cooperators early; later rounds will show less rewarding as players lose hope of sustaining cooperation.

game_explanation:
This public-goods game pits a few likely cooperators against at least one persistent free-rider. With 20 coins starting in the shared pot each round, each player decides how many to withdraw (so contributions are 20 minus withdrawal). Players do not see others' choices before deciding, but they do see round outcomes and can costfully punish or reward peers afterward. Typical dynamics here: conditional cooperators (who prefer to contribute if others do) will start fairly high to signal cooperation, while a low-contribution player may free-ride repeatedly. Punishment will be used sparingly to discipline obvious free-riders, and occasional small rewards will reinforce cooperative behavior. Because punishment is costly to the punisher but heavily damages the target (1 cost to source, -3 to target), expect small, targeted punishments rather than mass sanctioning. Rewards are cheaper to the target (1 cost to source, +1 to target) and will be used occasionally to encourage contributors. Near the final rounds we may see some end-game decline in contributions as reputation concerns decline.

Given the player personas:
- SLOTH: strongly inclined to contribute early (high initial contribution), but somewhat less inclined to reciprocate in tit-for-tat contexts — likely to remain cooperative early but trim contributions if others repeatedly free-ride; will occasionally reward cooperators.
- GORILLA: moderate conditional cooperator; will punish low contributors sometimes and sustain cooperation if reciprocated.
- DUCK: consistently low contributor/free-rider; unlikely to reward others and may not respond strongly to punishment except by minor adjustments.
- CHICKEN: mixed/moderate cooperator, likely to contribute at mid levels and punish or reward sparingly.

Expect a few targeted punishments aimed at DUCK across the middle rounds, some small rewards to sustain cooperation among SLOTH, GORILLA, and CHICKEN, and a gradual modest decline in contributions toward the last rounds.

game_explanation:
This public-goods game gives each player a binary-like tension each round: keep coins privately (withdraw) or leave them in the shared pot (contribute). Contributions are multiplied by 2.1 and then split equally; privately kept coins return 1-to-1 to the chooser. That creates a social dilemma: contributing helps the group but is individually costly relative to withdrawing. Because players make contributions simultaneously and observe only round summaries (including who rewarded whom), behavior will reflect uncertain beliefs about others and gradual learning across rounds.

Given the provided personas, I expect a mixed dynamic. DUCK is the most reliably pro-social here and will tend to contribute at relatively high levels and use sparse rewards to reinforce cooperators. SLOTH is moderately cooperative and responsive to reciprocity, likely maintaining mid-to-high contributions and occasionally rewarding. GORILLA is a low contributor and unlikely to reward others; they will often free-ride and earn high immediate payoffs, which may cause frustration but cannot be directly punished except by withholding rewards. CHICKEN is variable but often gives small positive contributions and occasionally uses rewards. Overall, expect moderate contributions from SLOTH and DUCK, low contributions from GORILLA, and CHICKEN fluctuating; rewards will be used sparingly (mostly 1-unit gifts) to reinforce cooperators (DUCK and SLOTH), producing modest increases in cooperation over the midgame, followed by some end-game declines.

game_explanation:
This is a 10-round linear public goods game with a post-decision peer-reward stage. Each round players decide how many coins to leave in the shared pot (contribution) out of 20. The total contributions are multiplied by 2.1 and split equally, so each player's private withdrawal is kept directly but public benefit is shared. Rewards are costly to the sender (1 coin per unit) and transfer 1 coin to the target; they are visible (recipients see who rewarded them). Because the game has repeated interaction but a known finite horizon (10 rounds), incentives mix present payoff maximization (pulling coins out) with conditional cooperation and reciprocity (rewarding generous contributors to encourage their behavior). Uncertainties include beliefs about others' tendencies (some players are more prosocial, others more mixed), and how much others will reward — reward is typically sparse in real experiments, so we predict only occasional, modest reward transfers targeted to high contributors or to maintain reciprocity.

Given the personas:
- DUCK (very pro-social) is likely to contribute consistently high and to reward cooperators moderately.
- SLOTH (moderately pro-social) will contribute above average and reciprocate rewards sometimes.
- GORILLA and CHICKEN are more mixed/more self-interested; GORILLA may raise contributions slowly if rewarded, CHICKEN tends to contribute less and reward rarely.

Overall predicted dynamics: DUCK sustains high contributions and small, targeted rewards to reinforce cooperation (especially toward SLOTH and occasionally GORILLA). SLOTH reciprocates and gradually increases contribution. GORILLA responds to positive reinforcement with small increases. CHICKEN contributes the least and rarely rewards. Rewards are sparse (1–2 units) and mostly directed at the highest contributors; in late rounds some unraveling pressure appears but prosocial types still give modest support. Below is a round-by-round transcript following these expectations.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN </PLAYERS>

This repeated public-goods game with 29 rounds creates tensions between private payoffs (keeping coins) and group payoffs (contributing to the shared pot). Because the pot is multiplied by 1.8 and split evenly, every coin contributed returns 0.45 to each player (1.8/4). A unilateral contributor therefore loses net private value (they forgo 1 coin but receive only 0.45 back), so pure self-interest favors withdrawing all coins. However, coordinated high contributions raise everyone’s shared payoff, and peers can use costly punishment (3 coins to sender, -4 to target per unit) and costly rewards (3 coins to sender, +2 to target per unit) to enforce or reinforce cooperation.

Given the four personas, I expect GORILLA to be a reliably high contributor and sometimes an enforcer/rewarder (high generosity, more inclined to sustain cooperation). SLOTH and CHICKEN are moderate cooperators who may oscillate around mid-level contributions. DUCK shows a low tendency to cooperate early (free-riding), and will be the primary target of occasional punishment early on; if punished or rewarded for improvement, DUCK often raises contributions somewhat. Punishment and reward are sparse and costly, so players will use them selectively—mainly to deter persistent low contributions or to positively reinforce above-average contributions. Toward the end (final few rounds) contributions tend to fall as the future enforcement value declines (endgame effect), so punishment decreases and contributions drop.

Uncertainties: exact timing and magnitude of punishments/rewards are noisy—players may refrain from punishing to avoid cost, or punish strategically. Some small rewards will appear to encourage improvement. Overall dynamic: initial punishing of DUCK nudges them upward, followed by a period of relatively stable cooperation centered on GORILLA and moderate contributions from SLOTH/CHICKEN; a gradual decline near the final rounds as incentives to enforce wane.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN </PLAYERS>

This is a repeated public-goods game with 23 rounds, four players, and a binary choice each round: contribute your 20-coin endowment to the group (contribute = 20) or withdraw it for private use (contribute = 0). The group total is multiplied by 2.5 and split evenly across the four players. That means each coin contributed yields 0.625 coins back to each player (2.5/4). Private withdrawal gives you 20 immediately but foregoes creating public return; contributing exposes you to exploitation by free-riders.

Incentives and likely dynamics
- Short-run incentive: With others’ contributions fixed, you earn more by withdrawing (free-riding). If everyone contributes, each player earns 50 (contribute 20 -> net 50); if everyone withdraws, each keeps 20.
- Repeated-play uncertainty: Because players see round-by-round payoffs after each round and can send messages, cooperation can be sustained early if reciprocity or social preferences motivate conditional contributors. But the game has a known finite horizon (23 rounds); backward induction makes full defection by the final round likely.
- Personas: GORILLA is strongly inclined to free-ride. DUCK shows prosocial tendencies and is likely to contribute frequently or try to re-start cooperation. SLOTH is mixed; may contribute early but retaliate if exploited. CHICKEN is moderately conditional—may follow early cooperation but can waver once defectors appear.
- Expected overall pattern: A short phase of partial cooperation (some players contribute) followed by decline as defectors exploit contributors. Occasional attempts by DUCK (and sometimes SLOTH) to re-start cooperation appear, but these are often unreciprocated. By the final round most players defect.

Transcript (round 1 → 23)

Because contributions are simultaneous and private before choosing, early rounds are exploratory: some players test cooperation, others free-ride. The reward stage lets players transfer coins to others at a 1-for-1 cost — a way to reward cooperators (not to punish defectors directly). Rewards are costly, so in practice they are used sparingly to reinforce cooperation or show appreciation. Given the personas, I expect CHICKEN to contribute strongly throughout, SLOTH and DUCK to be moderate conditional cooperators (adjusting based on observed behavior), and GORILLA to under-contribute most rounds. Rewards should be small and mostly directed toward consistent high contributors.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK </PLAYERS>

This is a finite, repeated public-goods game (16 rounds). Each round each player has 20 coins and chooses an integer contribution (0–20). The group pot is multiplied by 2 and split equally among the three players. Because each contributed coin returns 2/3 of a coin to the contributor (2 divided by 3), the private incentive is to keep coins (free-ride), while the group optimum is full contribution. Over repeated rounds, players can learn about others and adjust: cooperators tend to start high and reduce if exploited; conditional cooperators match others' behavior; selfish players contribute little and gain from others' generosity. Uncertainty arises from not seeing others' choices before deciding, limited information about motives, and the finite horizon (end-game effect): contributions typically decay toward zero by the last rounds. Using the provided personas: SLOTH is predisposed to high initial contributions but will adjust if repeatedly exploited; GORILLA is moderately cooperative and responsive; DUCK tends to contribute little and free-ride. Expect initial positive contributions, DUCK capturing outsized payoffs early, then gradual decline in aggregate contributions as SLOTH and GORILLA adapt, reaching near-zero by the end.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN </PLAYERS>

This is a 23-round public goods game with four players. Each round there is a 20-coin pot per player; each player either withdraws all 20 for private use (contribute 0) or leaves the 20 in the shared pot (contribute 20). The shared pot is multiplied by 2.5 and split evenly among all players. Thus any individual who withdraws 20 always gets 20 more that round than a cooperator (they keep the 20) but all players benefit from others' contributions. Because the game length is common knowledge, there is an incentive to free-ride and a typical decline of cooperation over time (and especially toward the end). That said, individual differences matter: DUCK's profile shows a strong tendency to trust/initially cooperate, SLOTH is moderately cooperative but inconsistent, GORILLA is mixed and conditional (likely to respond to observed outcomes), and CHICKEN shows low generosity and is likely to free-ride early and often.

I predict the group will start with a respectable level of cooperation (several players contributing), then free-riding will spread as some players exploit cooperators. DUCK will continue contributing for most rounds due to a high trusting tendency, while CHICKEN will mostly withdraw 20 (defect). GORILLA will shift toward defection after seeing free-riding, and SLOTH will wobble — sometimes contributing, sometimes not. Near the final rounds, most players will defect (endgame effect); DUCK may persist until very late before dropping out. Chat will be sparse: a few pledges and a few complaints as cooperation erodes.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated, small-group public goods game with 4 players and 6 rounds. Each round each player either contributes the full 20 coins to the public pot (cooperate) or withdraws it for private use (defect). The pot is multiplied by 1.6 and shared equally, so a single cooperator gives a net public benefit of 8 coins per player (20 * 1.6 / 4 = 8). Because contributions are binary and rewards are the only direct mechanism for social enforcement (each reward unit costs 3 to the giver and gives 4 to the recipient), the dynamics will hinge on early expectations and attempts to signal reciprocity with sparse rewards.

Likely unfolding:
- Early rounds: players with more pro-social priors (GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN) are likely to try cooperating; some players with more self-interested priors (SLOTH) will test by defecting. Cooperators may use small rewards to support each other.
- Middle rounds: if defectors persist and reap higher immediate payoffs, cooperators may either keep cooperating (hoping to incentivize reciprocity) or start defecting themselves. Because rewards are costly, players will reward sparingly—mostly to reinforce cooperation rather than to “punish” defectors (punishment is costly and not available here).
- Endgame: toward rounds 5–6, some players may attempt a last-minute cooperation to boost group payoff (or to signal fairness), but backward induction and lack of binding commitments make mutual defection plausible in the final round. Overall expect a decline from initial cooperation toward more defection, with a few targeted reward transfers to bolster cooperating peers.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated, 16-round public goods game with three players (SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK). Each round everyone has 20 coins and chooses how much (0–20) to contribute to a common pot. The pot is doubled and split equally, so each coin contributed returns 2/3 coin to each player (the contributor keeps 1/3 per coin and others receive 2/3 spread across players). After payoffs are calculated, players can punish others: each punishment unit costs the sender 1 coin and removes 2 coins from the target. Punishment is costly but can discourage free-riding.

Likely dynamics given the personas:
- SLOTH has consistently low prosocial tendencies and will tend to contribute near 0 most rounds (free-rider).
- GORILLA is strongly cooperative and will regularly contribute a lot (often full endowment). GORILLA is willing to punish free-riders occasionally to enforce cooperation.
- DUCK is cooperative but more sensitive to others' behavior and may reduce contributions over time if free-riding persists; DUCK may also punish SLOTH intermittently.

Uncertainties: players move simultaneously and cannot see others' choices before acting, and punishers are anonymous in summaries. This reduces targeted retaliation but also dilutes deterrence. Expect initial cooperation from GORILLA and DUCK; sporadic, small punishments aimed at SLOTH when cooperators are frustrated; punishments remain sparse because they are costly. Toward the end of the game, players may slightly adjust contributions (DUCK reduces more than GORILLA), and punishments may be less frequent (or used as last-round sanctions).

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with three players (SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK). Each round every player decides how many of 20 coins to contribute to a common pot. Contributions are multiplied by 2 and shared equally, so the group-efficient move is full contribution, but the individually profitable short-term move is to free-ride (contribute less and still get a share of others'). Because players observe only contributions (not punishment sources) and the game lasts 16 rounds, we expect a mix of behaviors:

- Early rounds: cooperative players (SLOTH and DUCK, who have priors favoring giving) are likely to contribute relatively high amounts. GORILLA has a prior toward low giving and may free-ride early.
- Punishment: when free-riding is visible and hurts cooperators, cooperators may spend a small amount to punish defectors. Punishment is costly to the punisher but can deter future free-riding. Because punishments are anonymous (players cannot identify who punished them), retaliation is less targeted; players will mostly punish based on contribution differences rather than as direct revenge.
- Dynamics over time: contributions typically decline somewhat with learning and end-game effects. Punishment tends to be used sparingly to enforce norms early-to-mid game; late in the game, with little future to influence, contributions often fall and punishments drop off.
- Uncertainties: exact levels of punishment and how quickly GORILLA adjusts are uncertain. SLOTH and DUCK may reduce contributions if repeatedly exploited, and GORILLA may respond to punishment by increasing contributions or by opportunistic defection near the end.

Given these tendencies, the simulated transcript below assumes SLOTH and DUCK start cooperative, GORILLA initially free-rides and is punished modestly, then adjusts upward some rounds, with occasional defections and sparse punitive responses. Punishment events are rare (not every round) and are small integers (1–2 units) consistent with typical experimental behavior.

# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK </PLAYERS>

This is a 6-round repeated public goods game where each player gets 20 coins per round and chooses an integer contribution (0–20). The group pot is multiplied by 1.9 and split equally, so each coin you contribute returns 1.9/3 ≈ 0.633 to you — less than the private value 1. The one-shot dominant strategy is to contribute 0, but repeated interaction, "cheap talk" messages, and heterogeneous social preferences can sustain positive contributions early on.

Given the player personas:
- DUCK is highly willing to give initially (strongly pro-social), so is likely to make large early contributions and to encourage cooperation in chat.
- SLOTH is moderately cooperative but not extreme; likely to contribute a positive moderate amount early and to reduce contributions gradually if others free-ride.
- GORILLA is relatively selfish/low initial generosity and tends to free-ride; likely to contribute little or nothing across rounds.

I therefore predict: DUCK will start high, SLOTH moderate, GORILLA low (often 0). Over rounds, DUCK and SLOTH will reduce contributions as they see free-riding; GORILLA will mostly keep zero. By the last round, standard backward induction and observed behavior generally push contributions down close to 0 for all. Chat will be sparse: DUCK will promise and complain, SLOTH will try to encourage reciprocity, GORILLA will be terse or silent.

game_explanation:
This is a 6-round public-goods game with 4 players. Each round each player either contributes all 20 coins to the shared pot (contribute = 20) or withdraws all 20 for private use (contribute = 0). The pot is multiplied by 1.6 and split equally, so contribution increases the group's total return but exposes contributors to exploitation by free-riders (who keep 20 privately and still get an equal share of the multiplied pot). After redistribution, players can spend costly reward units (cost 3 to giver, add 4 to recipient) to reward others for cooperative behavior. Rewards are anonymous to recipients in the summary, so rewards can only signal or incentivize indirectly.

Given the personas, DUCK and CHICKEN are relatively more pro-social and likely to contribute early; SLOTH and GORILLA are more mixed and more likely to free-ride at first. Expect an initial partial cooperation (some contribute), limited rewarding of contributors (sparse, small units) to try to sustain cooperation, some conditional responses (contributors reduce contributions if exploited), occasional attempts to rebuild cooperation, and a shift toward defection in the final round (last-round incentive to withdraw). Chat will be sparse and purposeful (requests, short justifications). Rewards will be used sparingly — usually one unit at a time when used.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated 3-player public goods game with costly punishment and full knowledge of the game's 6-round horizon. Key incentives:
- Contributing increases the group pot (multiplied by 1.9) but reduces your private holding that round. The private return from contributing is less than 1 (1.9/3 ≈ 0.633), so self-interested players prefer to contribute 0; cooperators prefer higher contributions.
- Punishment is costly to deliver (4 coins per punishment unit) but is highly damaging to the target (12 coins lost per unit). Punishment is an instrument to discourage free-riding but is noisy because targets cannot identify who punished them.
- Because the game ends after 6 rounds, forward-looking incentives to maintain cooperation weaken toward the end; contributions often decline across rounds.
- Social priors (player personas) will shape choices: GORILLA has a strong tendency to contribute, DUCK is moderately cooperative and conditional, SLOTH tends to free-ride but can respond to punishment or spite.
- Uncertainty: players don't see contributions before deciding and cannot link punishment to a specific punisher, so punishment can deter free-riding but can also lead to misdirected retaliation or grudges.

Predicted unfolding: Early rounds will see relatively high contributions from GORILLA and moderate from DUCK, with SLOTH initially low. Initial punishments target SLOTH to enforce norms; SLOTH partially increases contributions after punishment but may relapse later. Punishment is used sparingly (mostly 1-unit moves). Contributions decline modestly toward the final rounds; punishment frequency remains low but focused on repeat low contributors.

game_explanation:
This 3-player public goods game is a standard social-dilemma: each coin you keep yields 1 coin for you, while each coin you contribute returns 1.9 coins to the group and thus 1.9/3 ≈ 0.63 coins back to you. Individually, contributing costs you more than you get back, so the dominant incentive is to free-ride (contribute 0). But the group earns more if everyone contributes. With repeated interaction (6 known rounds) and cheap talk, conditional cooperation and reputation motives can support more contribution early on; toward the end, incentives to free-ride grow (endgame effect).

Given the three personas:
- DUCK is reliably prosocial and likely to contribute high amounts (often near 20) throughout.
- GORILLA is relatively selfish and tends to free-ride (near 0), maybe sending minimal contributions occasionally.
- SLOTH is mixed/moderate — may start with a mid-level contribution, then respond to others (reducing if others free-ride, increasing a little if cooperative).

Uncertainties: cheap talk can change expectations: promises may be honored or not. Small one-off deviations (e.g., GORILLA putting in 1) can momentarily affect SLOTH’s behavior. Over rounds, conditional cooperation and learning will likely lead SLOTH to reduce contributions in response to GORILLA’s low contributions; DUCK will largely keep contributing, so group-level contributions will remain driven by DUCK.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game with four players and six rounds. Each round every player either contributes the full 20 coins to the group pot (contribute = 20) or withdraws the 20 for private use (contribute = 0). The pot is multiplied by 1.6 and split equally, so each cooperating player helps increase everybody's per-person share by 8 coins per cooperator. The immediate material incentive is to defect (withdraw 20) because a defector keeps the 20 and still benefits from others' contributions; cooperating is costly unless enough others also cooperate or social sanctions/rewards change payoffs.

Because punishment is costly (3 coins per punish unit to the sender, -7 to the target) and rewards cost 3 to give +4 to the recipient, players will use sanctions sparingly. Early rounds typically show uncertainty — some players test cooperation, others free-ride. If cooperators can coordinate (through messages or reciprocal rewards), they may sustain cooperation; if defectors persist, cooperators sometimes respond with a few targeted punishments. Near the final rounds, the "end-game" effect often reduces incentives to maintain cooperation because future retaliation or reward is less effective, so contributions tend to fall.

Given the provided player priors:
- DUCK is relatively pro-social and likely to try cooperating early (and may use selective sanctions or rewards to support cooperation).
- GORILLA is more likely to defect initially.
- SLOTH and CHICKEN are mixed: they may cooperate some rounds but are tempted to defect if defectors are not sanctioned.

Expectations: initial rounds will see 1–2 cooperators (DUCK and maybe CHICKEN), some reciprocation via small rewards between cooperators, and mostly free-riding by GORILLA (and possibly SLOTH). If defecting continues, a cooperator (likely DUCK or CHICKEN) may apply a small punishment to deter persistent free-riders. If punishment successfully induces a switch, later rounds can move toward higher cooperation; the final round is likely to see a drop in contributions.

reflection:
This is a repeated public-goods game with four players and six rounds. Each round every player either contributes the full 20 coins to the group pot (contribute = 20) or withdraws the 20 for private use (contribute = 0). The pot is multiplied by 1.6 and split equally, so each cooperating player helps increase everybody's per-person share by 8 coins per cooperator. The immediate material incentive is to defect (withdraw 20) because a defector keeps the 20 and still benefits from others' contributions; cooperating is costly unless enough others also cooperate or social sanctions/rewards change payoffs.

Because punishment is costly (3 coins per punish unit to the sender, -7 to the target) and rewards cost 3 to give +4 to the recipient, players will use sanctions sparingly. Early rounds typically show uncertainty — some players test cooperation, others free-ride. If cooperators can coordinate (through messages or reciprocal rewards), they may sustain cooperation; if defectors persist, cooperators sometimes respond with a few targeted punishments. Near the final rounds, the "end-game" effect often reduces incentives to maintain cooperation because future retaliation or reward is less effective, so contributions tend to fall.

Given the provided player priors:
- DUCK is relatively pro-social and likely to try cooperating early (and may use selective sanctions or rewards to support cooperation).
- GORILLA is more likely to defect initially.
- SLOTH and CHICKEN are mixed: they may cooperate some rounds but are tempted to defect if defectors are not sanctioned.

Expectations: initial rounds will see 1–2 cooperators (DUCK and maybe CHICKEN), some reciprocation via small rewards between cooperators, and mostly free-riding by GORILLA (and possibly SLOTH). If defecting continues, a cooperator (likely DUCK or CHICKEN) may apply a small punishment to deter persistent free-riders. If punishment successfully induces a switch, later rounds can move toward higher cooperation; the final round is likely to see a drop in contributions.

<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN </PLAYERS>

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with 29 rounds, 4 players (SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN). Each round each player decides how much of the 20-coin pot to contribute; the pot is multiplied by 1.8 and split equally. Players cannot see contemporaneous choices, but after each round they learn their own net payoff and who punished/rewarded them. Punishment is costly to the punisher (3 coins per unit) but subtracts more from the target (4 coins per unit); rewards cost 3 to the giver and give 2 to the receiver.

Likely dynamics:
- DUCK is the most pro-social profile and will tend to contribute substantially across the game. DUCK will sometimes use costly sanctions or rewards to enforce cooperation, but will do so sparingly because punishment is costly.
- GORILLA and CHICKEN have low prosocial priors and will often free-ride; occasional punishment from others may induce temporary increases, but they won't sustain high contributions.
- SLOTH is mixed/sluggish: often contributes modestly but inconsistently, and will reward cooperative behavior occasionally rather than punish frequently.
- Overall: early rounds show some cooperation from DUCK (and sometimes SLOTH), while GORILLA/CHICKEN free-ride. Sparse punishment early helps raise GORILLA/CHICKEN a bit, but cooperation decays slowly toward the end as end-game effects and the costliness of punishment reduce enforcement. Rewards are used occasionally to acknowledge contributors; punishments are rare and targeted.

game_explanation:
This 29-round public goods game pits four players with distinct social tendencies against the standard incentives of a linear public good. Each round, players choose how much of 20 coins to keep privately vs. contribute to the shared pot. The pot is multiplied by 1.8 and split equally, so the private return to any one coin contributed is 1.8/4 = 0.45 to the contributor (and identical for everyone). That makes free-riding individually attractive (you keep 1 coin rather than a 0.45 return) while group-optimal contributions require trust that others will contribute. The reward stage provides a way to reward cooperation (cost 3 to sender, benefit 2 to recipient) — useful for sustaining cooperation but itself costly and thus used sparingly in real experiments.

Given the player personas, I expect CHICKEN to start very high in contribution and remain relatively above others for many rounds, DUCK to start moderately high and decline gradually, SLOTH to be moderately cooperative but slow to change, and GORILLA to be comparatively selfish and drop faster. Rewards will be infrequent: used early to reinforce visibly high contributors (CHICKEN, DUCK) and then taper as the common pot returns diminish and endgame incentives drive defection. Toward the final rounds everyone will reduce contributions (classic endgame effect); rewards largely disappear.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN </PLAYERS>

This is a repeated public goods game with 29 rounds. Each player chooses how many of the 20 coins to keep privately (withdraw) and the remainder becomes their contribution to a shared pot; contributed coins are multiplied by 1.8 and shared equally, so each coin contributed returns 0.45 to each player but costs 1 privately. The one-shot monetary incentive is to withdraw everything; social payoff is maximized if everyone contributes everything. Repetition, and the ability to punish or reward after seeing contributions, are the only mechanisms that can sustain cooperation.

Given the four player personas:
- DUCK tends to be fairly willing to send resources (higher initial cooperation) but is not strongly reciprocal in some tasks — so DUCK is likely to start high but reduce over time if not reciprocated.
- SLOTH is mixed but somewhat more willing to contribute than the most selfish types.
- GORILLA leans toward lower contributions.
- CHICKEN is most likely to free-ride.

Predicted dynamics:
- Early rounds: DUCK will contribute most; SLOTH and GORILLA contribute moderate amounts; CHICKEN contributes little. Cooperators will occasionally reward cooperators (small rewards) and may begin to punish blatant free-riding (sparse punishments targeted at CHICKEN).
- Middle rounds: If cooperation isn't broadly reciprocated, contributions drift downward. Punishments happen occasionally when cooperators try to discipline chronic low contributors, but sanctions are used sparingly because they are costly to send.
- Late rounds and endgame: As the horizon approaches and future retaliation is less relevant, contributions decline further toward zero; punishment and reward largely disappear. In the final rounds players tend to defect (withdraw rather than contribute).

There is uncertainty about specific rounds because players could experiment, punish more aggressively, or attempt cooperative "resets" via rewards. But the overall pattern will be gradual erosion of cooperation, with a few sparse sanctions and occasional small rewards early on.

game_explanation:
This three-player public goods game balances a clear tension: contributing helps the group (each coin contributed returns 1.9/3 ≈ 0.63 to each player) but is privately costly (you lose 1 coin immediately). That creates an incentive to free-ride (contribute less than others) to earn the shared return while keeping private coins. Punishment provides a deterrent: each punishment unit costs 4 to the punisher but removes 12 from the target, so a single punishment unit is a heavy deterrent if contributors are willing to pay the cost. Uncertainty is important: players see contributions but not who punished them, so punishment is a noisy signal and can provoke retaliation or cautious strategy changes.

Given the personas: SLOTH tends to be moderately cooperative and somewhat reciprocal; DUCK is moderately cooperative and relatively non-retaliatory; GORILLA is more inclined to free-ride or test others, with lower propensity to return trust. I expect early rounds to show moderate contributions from SLOTH and DUCK, low contributions from GORILLA, targeted but sparse punishment against clear free-riding, occasional retaliatory punishment from GORILLA, and declining cooperation toward the last round (endgame effect). Chats will be sparse and mostly reactive.

game_explanation:
This repeated public‑goods game sets up the classic tension: each player can withdraw coins for private use (temptation to free-ride) or leave them in the shared pot (build the public good). The shared contributions are multiplied by 2.1 and split equally, so the social return per coin contributed is 2.1/4 = 0.525 — less than 1 — meaning any individual coin contributes less to their private payoff than keeping it. That creates an individual incentive to withdraw (defect) each round, while group payoff is maximized if everyone contributes more.

Uncertainties and dynamics to expect:
- Early rounds often show some cooperation due to reciprocity norms and uncertainty about others. Given the player priors, SLOTH and GORILLA are biased toward higher initial contributions, DUCK toward moderate cooperation, and CHICKEN toward low contribution.
- Rewards are costly to the sender and only beneficial directly to the target, so they are used sparingly in real games. When used, they tend to be small, targeted at above‑average contributors to reinforce cooperation.
- Over repeated rounds, unless strong reciprocal incentive or frequent rewarding emerges, contributions typically drift downward (conditional cooperators respond to observed free‑riding). Toward the final rounds, strategic incentives to defect (end‑game effect) usually lower contributions further and reduce rewarding.
- Given these priors: DUCK is relatively inclined to reciprocate and reward, GORILLA is plausibly pro‑social early on, SLOTH starts cooperative but may reduce if returns look small, and CHICKEN tends to free‑ride. Expect a moderate start, sparse rewards targeted to those who contribute relatively more, and gradual decline across rounds with very little rewarding late in the game.

# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN </PLAYERS>

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN </PLAYERS>

This 4-player repeated public goods game places 20 coins in the shared pot each round; each player privately withdraws some coins (so contribution = 20 - withdrawal). The pot is multiplied by 1.8 and split equally, so the social return of giving is positive but individually costly: contributing 1 coin gives 0.45 coins back to each player (1.8/4), so the contributor's private return is 0.45 — less than 1. That creates tension: a full cooperator helps the group but loses privately. The separate reward stage (cost 3 to giver, +2 to recipient per reward unit) allows players to reward cooperative peers, partially offsetting private losses for cooperators and creating incentives for targeted reciprocity.

Given the provided personas, I predict:
- DUCK (high trust / pro-social in anonymous transfer tasks) will start high and sustain elevated contributions for many rounds, often using occasional small rewards to encourage cooperators.
- CHICKEN (moderate, responsive) will start moderately high and move toward higher contributions when seeing others contribute, but will trim back toward the end (endgame).
- GORILLA (skeptical, low send) will start low but may increase slowly if the group provides noticeable returns and if they receive occasional rewards; otherwise remain cautious.
- SLOTH (low send, sluggish) will contribute very little most rounds and almost never reward.

Uncertainties: exact magnitudes of conditional increases and endgame declines are uncertain. Rewards are costly, so they will be used sparingly (mostly 1-unit signals). Over the 29 rounds we expect early cooperation to rise as Duck leads, midsession moderation, and a modest endgame decline as players anticipate the finish.

game_explanation:
This is a 23-round repeated public-goods game with 4 players. Each round each player either withdraws the whole 20 coins (defect, contribute 0) or leaves them in the pot (cooperate, contribute 20). The pot is multiplied by 2.5 and split equally, so cooperation benefits the group but rewards defectors in the short run. Punishment is available: each punishment unit costs the punisher 1 coin and removes 2 coins from the punished player. Players see round-by-round summaries (their own net payoff, how much they spent punishing, and how much punishment they suffered) but cannot identify which peer punished them, so punishment is somewhat anonymous and harder to reciprocally target.

Likely dynamics:
- Early rounds: players test each other. Some (GORILLA, DUCK) have priors favoring self-interest and will try to exploit cooperative others. Cooperators (SLOTH, CHICKEN) will try to sustain contributions and may use low-cost forms of punishment to signal that defection is costly.
- Middle rounds: if punishment is used consistently against defectors, cooperation can stabilize; we expect a stretch of high contribution rates when punishers enforce norms.
- Late rounds: because the group knows the game has a fixed end (23 rounds), cooperation will tend to unravel toward the end as the incentive to punish declines. Expect more defections and less punishment in the final rounds, with perhaps symbolic punishments on the very last round.

Chats will be sparse and purpose-driven (requests, warnings, brief reactions). Punishments will be small and infrequent — enough to shape behavior but not excessive — and concentrated on repeat defectors. Below is a round-by-round simulation consistent with these tendencies.

game_explanation:
This repeated public-goods game mixes incentives for private extraction (withdrawing coins from the shared pot) with the possibility of targeted sanctions and rewards after contributions are revealed. Expect two broad tensions across rounds. First, selfish players (here: DUCK and SLOTH) have a steady immediate incentive to withdraw a lot and contribute little; their private payoff rises when others contribute. Second, conditional cooperators and norm enforcers (GORILLA and CHICKEN, by their profiles) are more likely to contribute and to use sparse punishment or reward to shape behavior. Punishment is costly to the sender but is a strong deterrent to free-riding (it removes 3 coins per punishment unit from the target), while rewards are cheap signals (1 coin sent yields +1 coin to the target).

Typical unfolding I predict:
- Early rounds: CHICKEN and GORILLA contribute at moderate-to-high levels; SLOTH and DUCK contribute little. Small, targeted punishments appear early to deter the overt free-riding (usually directed at DUCK), plus occasional rewards to reinforce cooperative behavior. Punishments are used sparingly.
- Middle rounds: If punishments are effective, contributions by the freeriders may increase a bit (or at least stop falling), while cooperators maintain high contributions and occasionally exchange small rewards.
- Late rounds (endgame): With the horizon known at 10 rounds, expect some decline in contributions at the very end as reciprocity motives weaken. Final-round punishments might still occur but typically less effective as retaliation incentives disappear.

I will simulate these dynamics across 10 rounds using the provided player priors and keep punishment/reward actions modest and sparse, focusing punishments mainly on the most persistent low contributor (DUCK) and occasional cross-rewards between GORILLA and CHICKEN.

The pot is multiplied by 1.6 and split equally, so cooperation benefits the group but leaves individual temptation to free-ride. Because players can punish (costly to source, large harm to target) and reward (costly to source, moderate benefit to target), we expect a dynamic where:
- Early rounds are exploratory: some players test whether others will cooperate.

- In the final round(s), contributions often drop (last-round defection) because future enforcement is not credible, though some players still punish or reward for fairness/expression.

Chat will be sparse and purposeful; punishment and reward will be used sparingly.
