game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game with four players, 10 rounds, and both costly punishment (1 cost to sender, -3 to target) and costly reward (1 cost to sender, +1 to target). Each round players decide how many of the 20 coins to withdraw privately (so contribution = 20 - withdrawal). The group pot is multiplied by 2.1 and split equally. Main incentives: contributing increases the group return but lowers your private immediate withdrawal; withdrawing (free-riding) boosts your private take but reduces the shared payoff to others. Punishment and reward allow costly enforcement and positive reinforcement. Because interactions repeat with identified peers, we expect:

- SLOTH (high cooperative priors) will tend to contribute a lot most rounds and sometimes use small rewards (or occasional mild punishment of clear free-riders).
- GORILLA (mixed/coarser strategic priors) will play moderately, occasionally punish obvious free-riders or give a small reward to reliable cooperators.
- DUCK (low cooperative priors) will contribute little early on and may only modestly increase after being punished/rewarded; retaliation is unlikely because punishment is costly.
- CHICKEN (moderate/reciprocal) will contribute moderately-high and will reward cooperators and punish very sparingly if someone persistently free-rides.

Because punishment and reward are visible and rounds are finite, we expect modest use of these tools: enough to signal norms and nudge behavior but not heavy, sustained punishment. Toward the final round punishment tends to drop (no future consequences), though some small tokens of reward may appear. Below is a plausible transcript with round-by-round explanations, contributions (ordered SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN), sparse punishment/reward tuples, and the summary each player would see.

game_explanation:
Players choose how much of the 20 coins to withdraw for themselves; the remainder is contributed to the public pot. Contributions are multiplied by 1.8 and split equally, so each coin contributed returns 1.8/4 = 0.45 to each player (0.45 to the contributor plus 1.35 distributed to others). Withdrawing is privately profitable, so individual incentive favors free-riding. Rewards allow players to transfer benefits to specific others (each reward unit costs 3 coins to sender and gives 2 coins to recipient), which is costly for the sender and slightly pro-social overall only when used to sustain cooperation through reciprocity or signaling. Players do not see others' current contributions before choosing, but they learn who rewarded them (and their own round payoffs) after the round; this supports personalized reciprocity but does not fully reveal peers' contributions.

Given the personas: GORILLA is a reliable high contributor early; SLOTH is moderately cooperative but somewhat mixed; CHICKEN is a conditional/mid-level contributor who may trail cooperation; DUCK tends to free-ride. I therefore predict an initial phase with GORILLA propping up the public good and occasional mutual small rewards between cooperators. Over repeated rounds, contribution levels will drift downward as DUCK persistently withdraws and the private incentive dominates; rewards will be used sparingly (mostly 1-unit signals) to thank or reciprocate cooperators. Near the finite end (round 29) cooperation declines to low or zero levels and reward activity largely disappears.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN </PLAYERS>

This is a 23-round repeated public goods game with four players. Each round there is a single shared pot of 20 coins; each player either withdraws the 20 (so contributes 0) or leaves it (contributes 20). The pot is multiplied by 2.5 and split evenly among all four players, so each contributor increases total payout but gets less private return than a defector in the same round. That structure creates a tension: contributing helps the group but temporarily rewards defectors more when others contribute.

Given the personas:
- GORILLA is strongly inclined to cooperate and likely to contribute in early rounds, and to use chat to encourage cooperation.
- SLOTH has pro-social tendencies but also a tendency to be inconsistent; SLOTH may contribute early but will quickly respond to exploitation.
- DUCK and CHICKEN show mixed/low cooperation in anonymous tasks and are most likely to free-ride repeatedly.

Predicted dynamics: modest cooperation early (GORILLA plus sometimes SLOTH), then rapid decline as free-riders reap higher immediate payoffs. Chat will be sparse: early commitments from cooperators, short appeals when exploitation appears, and a few expressions of disappointment. Cooperation will largely collapse and remain low for the majority of rounds; by the final rounds players anticipate the end and tend to defect.

game_explanation:
This 4-player public goods game pits a short-run incentive to withdraw coins for private use (free-riding) against the group benefit of leaving coins in the pot (contributing). With a 1.8 multiplier split equally among four players, every coin you leave in the pot returns 1.8/4 = 0.45 to each player, so the marginal per-player return is well below 1. The dominant private incentive is therefore to withdraw as much as possible; sustaining high contributions requires either reciprocity (matching others' contributions) or costly social incentives (punishments and rewards).

Expectations given the personas:
- SLOTH tends to contribute little unless pressured — a persistent freerider candidate.
- GORILLA is a moderate cooperator who also enforces norms; likely to punish occasional low contributors.
- DUCK is mixed: sometimes contributes moderately but may drop off if others defect.
- CHICKEN is relatively pro-social and may use sparse rewards to encourage contributors; may also punish rarely.

Punishment is costly (3 coins per unit to sender, 4 coins deducted from target) so it will be used sparingly and mainly as enforcement against repeated low contributors (especially SLOTH). Rewards are also costly (3 to sender, +2 to recipient) and will be used occasionally as positive reinforcement. Over rounds we should see early attempts at cooperation by CHICKEN and GORILLA, SLOTH free-riding, a few enforcement actions that nudge SLOTH up sometimes, DUCK shifting between moderate and low contributions, and some end-game unraveling (people reducing contributions as the game approaches the final rounds).

game_explanation:
This PGG creates a tension between private withdrawal (immediate private payoff) and contributing to a common pot that benefits everyone after a 2.1 multiplier. Because the redistributed share equals 0.525 × total contributions for each player, each coin one contributes raises everyone's payoff by 0.525 but costs the contributor 1 — so contribution is individually costly but socially beneficial. With anonymous simultaneous choices, players face uncertainty about others' cooperativeness; typical behavior is "conditional cooperation" (people contribute moderately but reduce contributions if they believe others defect). Punishment (cost 1 to sender, -3 to target) and reward (cost 1 to sender, +1 to target) allow players to deter free-riding or reinforce cooperation, but both are used sparingly in real experiments because they are costly to the sender.

Given the four personas:
- DUCK tends to be the most pro-social and will contribute high amounts and sometimes reward cooperators.
- SLOTH shows moderate reciprocity and will often contribute moderately and occasionally punish or reward when they perceive persistent free-riding.
- GORILLA and CHICKEN are relatively more self-interested and likely to contribute less on average, which may trigger occasional punishments from DUCK or SLOTH. GORILLA is a bit more strategic and may vary contributions.
Over 10 rounds we expect some cooperation early on, occasional targeted punishments of repeated low contributors, small rewards to reinforce high contributors, and some decline toward the end as "endgame" incentives to free-ride increase. Punishments and rewards remain rare and targeted rather than widespread.

game_explanation:
This public-goods game pits private incentives (keep 20) against group incentives (contribute 20 so the shared pot is multiplied and split). With 4 players and a multiplier of 2.5, full contribution (everyone 20) yields a large group benefit (each gets 50, net +30 compared to keeping 20), but unilateral defection is personally attractive when others contribute (a defector gets 57.5 vs a cooperator's 37.5). Because choices are binary (0 or 20 contributed) and rounds are repeated with punishment available (cheap to impose but costly to the punisher), we expect these dynamics:

- Some players (CHICKEN, SLOTH) are predisposed to cooperate early and encourage cooperation; GORILLA, who is consistently selfish in priors, is likely to defect often.
- Punishment will be used sparingly but strategically to deter persistent free-riders. Punishment is costly to send (1 coin per unit) but can substantially lower the target’s payoff (2 coins deducted per unit received). Because players cannot see who punished them, retaliation is noisy and diffuse.
- Cooperation will appear in stretches, often sustained by occasional punishment of defectors. Toward the end of the 23-round horizon, contributions are likely to decline as backward induction and fatigue set in; punishment also tends to taper off late because its future deterrent value declines.

Below is a round-by-round transcript from Round 1 through Round 23. At the start of each round I give a short explanation of the immediate incentives, then the observed chats, the contributions, the punishment actions (only non-zero edges shown), and the round summary as visible to players (each player’s round net payoff, punishment spent, and punishment received — sources are not revealed).

game_explanation:
This is a 6-round public-goods game with four players (SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN). Each round each player either withdraws the full 20 coins (defect / keep privately) or leaves it in the shared pot (cooperate / contribute 20). The shared pot is multiplied by 1.6 and split equally among all four, so a single defection gives an immediate private advantage of 20 coins over a cooperator before any punishment/reward. Punishments and rewards allow players to alter payoffs after contributions, but punishments cost the punisher 3 coins per unit and remove 7 coins per unit from the target; rewards cost the rewarder 3 coins per unit and add 4 coins per unit to the target. Punishment and reward are anonymous in terms of who exacted them, and players see round summaries showing their net payoff and amounts used/received.

Likely dynamics:
- Defection is individually tempting (dominant in a one-shot), so some players will defect early. Players with higher inclination to enforce norms (CHICKEN) are likely to punish defectors occasionally; some players (GORILLA) may reward cooperators sparingly.
- Because punishments are costly to deliver and remove only 7 per unit, a single 1-unit punishment usually doesn't fully eliminate the defectors' advantage; punishers must pay repeatedly or in larger units to deter persistent defection.
- Over rounds, we should see modest cooperation early on (supported by CHICKEN's enforcement and GORILLA's occasional rewards), but cooperation will tend to decline toward the final rounds as the endgame approaches and enforcement becomes less effective or too costly.
- Chat is usually sparse and focused (requests to cooperate, short justifications), and punishment/reward use is sparse.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN </PLAYERS>

This 6-round public goods game pits short-run private incentives (withdraw 20 coins privately) against the group benefit from contributing (put 20 into the pot). With 4 players and a 1.6 multiplier, each 20 contributed yields 32 total return or 8 per player. So a single cooperator (contributing 20) loses 12 relative to defectors that round (they get 8 vs defectors' 28) unless rewarded by others. Rewards are costly (3 per unit) but give a modest boost (4 per unit) to the target; because reward senders are not identifiable in the summary, positive reinforcement must be deliberate and sparse.

Given the player priors:
- GORILLA is the most reciprocity-inclined and is likely to try cooperating early.
- SLOTH, DUCK, and especially CHICKEN have low return-by-reciprocity tendencies and are likely to defect (withdraw privately) most rounds.
- A small, persistent benefactor (here DUCK shows occasional positive acts in the profile) might reward a cooperating GORILLA occasionally, but rewards will be sparse because they cost senders and benefit recipients anonymously (senders cannot be later identified by recipients).

I therefore expect early-round unilateral cooperation by GORILLA that is exploited by others; a single player (DUCK) may give small, costly "thank you" rewards to GORILLA a few times. After a couple of rounds of exploitation, GORILLA is likely to switch to defection, and by the final rounds the game will largely unravel to universal defection. Chats will be few and mostly declarative (intentions or short comments).

game_explanation:
This is a 23-round repeated public goods game. Each round the group pot contains 20 coins; each player either withdraws the whole 20 (contribute 0) or withdraws 0 (contribute 20). The pot is multiplied by 2.5 and split equally among the four players. Individual incentive: withdrawing 20 (defect) always gives a higher private return in that round than contributing 20, because the private return from contributing is only (2.5*20)/4 = 12.5 < 20. Group optimum: if everyone contributes every round each gets 50 per round. The main dynamics come from this tension: contributing raises everyone’s payoff but is individually costly; defection is tempting because defectors keep 20 and still share the multiplied pot.

Uncertainties: players do not observe others’ choices before acting, so early rounds are formative—cooperators may try to signal trust and encourage reciprocity via chat, defectors may exploit cooperators. Over repeated rounds with known horizon (23 rounds), standard predictions are a decline in cooperation: some altruistic players may sustain contributions, others will exploit or switch to defection once they see free-riding. Chat can modestly sustain cooperation if a convincing appeal or reciprocity emerges, but the temptation to free-ride and the finite horizon make steady cooperation unlikely for all players.

Using the provided personas as priors:
- CHICKEN (very trusting) is most likely to contribute repeatedly and to send encouraging messages.
- GORILLA is the most likely to defect consistently and may respond with short, self-interested messages.
- SLOTH is moderately pro-social but somewhat passive; may contribute early then reduce contributions if exploited.
- DUCK is somewhat indifferent/low on giving and will likely defect often, but may occasionally match others.

I predict CHICKEN will contribute in most/all rounds; GORILLA will defect every round; SLOTH will contribute early but shift to defection after seeing exploitation; DUCK will defect mostly, with a couple of sporadic contributions. Chats will be sparse and purposeful: short appeals by CHICKEN, occasional reactions by others.

# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN </PLAYERS>

game_explanation:
This is a repeated 6-round public goods game with three players (SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK). Each round everyone privately chooses an integer contribution from 0–20. Contributions are pooled, multiplied by 1.9, and split equally. Players see only each player's net payoff at the end of each round (their own and the peers' payoffs), so they can infer others' contributions indirectly. Players can send cheap-talk messages at any time.

Likely dynamics given the personas:
- GORILLA is strongly prosocial and very likely to contribute the full endowment each round (20). That sets a cooperative baseline.
- SLOTH is moderately cooperative and begins with a positive contribution but may reduce contributions if others persistently free-ride.
- DUCK tends to free-ride (low contributions). DUCK may occasionally make small concessions after being called out but is unlikely to fully match GORILLA.

Over rounds we expect GORILLA to stay high (20 each round). SLOTH will likely start moderate, adjust up or down in response to visible payoffs (punishing or rewarding DUCK). DUCK will often contribute very little or zero; occasional small upticks are possible if criticized. Chat will be sparse: GORILLA may pledge consistency, SLOTH may express disappointment when exploited, DUCK may send short defensive/apologetic messages occasionally.

game_explanation:
This 10-round public-goods game pits a strongly self-interested player (SLOTH) against more conditional or mixed cooperators (GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN). Incentives:
- Each player keeps (20 - contribution) privately but also shares equally in 2.1×(sum of all contributions). So high contributors subsidize everyone; low contributors can free-ride.
- Punishments are costly to send (1 coin) but costly to the target (−3 per unit). Rewards cost the sender 1 and give the recipient +1.
- Because punishment and reward are visible individually, conditional cooperators may punish persistent free-riders or occasionally reward high contributors. Real-world behavior: punishment/reward are used sparingly.

Predicted dynamics:
- SLOTH, with a profile that strongly favors private extraction, will systematically withdraw most/all of their 20 coins (contribute 0) each round.
- CHICKEN, as a conditional cooperator, will contribute substantially early on and occasionally punish SLOTH to signal disapproval. CHICKEN may also taper contributions later if free-riding persists.
- GORILLA is mixed: modest contributions and occasional strategic rewards to support cooperators (sparse).
- DUCK is low on prosocial transfers and will mostly contribute little or nothing.
- Punishments are infrequent and targeted at the clear free-rider (SLOTH). Rewards are even rarer and used to acknowledge high contributors.

game_explanation:
This is a multi-round public goods game with 4 players who each decide whether to contribute the full 20 coins to the shared pot (contribution = 20) or withdraw them for private use (contribution = 0). The pot of contributed coins is multiplied by 2.5 and divided equally among the four players. With this payoff structure, withdrawing (keeping 20 privately) strictly increases a player's round payoff by 20 compared to contributing, regardless of others' choices. That makes defection (withdrawal) the dominant strategy, so we should expect cooperation to erode over time.

However, players differ in cooperative tendencies. DUCK has relatively stronger prosocial priors and may try to sustain cooperation longer. GORILLA is somewhat conditional/cooperative but has little willingness to punish. SLOTH is mixed and may experiment early but is sensitive to exploitation. CHICKEN is strongly selfish and will defect consistently. Given these tendencies and the clear individual incentive to defect, a likely unfolding is: some initial attempts at cooperation (possibly by DUCK, maybe supported by GORILLA or SLOTH), early exploitation by CHICKEN (and possibly others), then gradual withdrawal of contributors until the group converges on universal defection for most of the remaining rounds. Chat will be sparse — a few short promises, complaints, or resignation messages as players respond to observed payoffs.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated 29-round public goods game with four players. Each round players choose how much of the 20 coins to leave in the shared pot (contribution) and then may punish or reward others. Multiplication (1.8) and equal splitting create a tension: contributing helps the group but is individually costly, so free-riding is tempting. Players' personas give priors: SLOTH tends to under-contribute, GORILLA is a moderate conditional cooperator and enforcement-minded, DUCK and CHICKEN are mixed conditional cooperators who sometimes reward cooperation.

Likely dynamics:
- Early rounds: conditional cooperators (GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN) start at moderate contributions; SLOTH starts low. Peers may respond with sparse punishment to signal that low contributions are discouraged.
- Middle rounds: contributions will fluctuate. If punishments are effective, SLOTH will sometimes raise contributions briefly; otherwise SLOTH will exploit the pot, causing gradual declines in others' contributions. Rewards will be used occasionally to reinforce high contributors, but reward use is sparse because it is costly to the source.
- Late rounds: some end-game decline is possible as the incentive to influence future behavior weakens, so contributions may drop overall. Punishments remain relatively rare because they cost the punisher and have smaller net benefit late in the game, but a few targeted punishments from GORILLA are plausible across the experiment to enforce norms.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK </PLAYERS>

This is a standard repeated public goods game with three players, 16 rounds, and no communication. Each round every player privately chooses an integer contribution from 0–20. The group pot is doubled and split equally: each player's round payoff = (20 - their contribution) + (2 * total contributions) / 3.

Key incentives and likely dynamics:
- Socially optimal behavior is to contribute everything (20), because the multiplied pot benefits everyone. But individually, each coin you contribute returns only 2/3 to you (since 2/3 < 1), so the one-shot individual incentive is to contribute 0.
- Without communication and with imperfect information about others' motives, cooperation often starts higher if some players are predisposed to be cooperative, then declines as free-riding is observed.
- Repeated play creates scope for conditional cooperation: players who care about fairness or reciprocity may reduce contributions in response to free-riders. Selfish players (strong free-riders) tend to keep contributing 0.
- Given the personas: DUCK shows strong initial trust/cooperation tendencies and is likely to start high. GORILLA shows almost no willingness to give and is likely to free-ride (0 each round). SLOTH leans toward lower trust and lower returns but may make moderate, somewhat inconsistent contributions (starting moderate then drifting downward). Together, this makes a plausible path where DUCK starts high, SLOTH moderate, GORILLA 0, and contributions erode over rounds as DUCK and SLOTH reduce contributions in response to sustained free-riding.
- There is uncertainty in exact magnitudes and timing of decline, but the predicted overall pattern is initial cooperation from DUCK, moderate SLOTH, persistent zero from GORILLA, and a gradual collapse toward widespread defection by the final rounds.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with 29 rounds. Each round players decide how many coins to leave in the shared pot (contribution) while the remainder is withdrawn for private use. The pot is multiplied by 1.8 and split equally; after payoff sharing, players may spend costly actions to punish or reward others (punish: source pays 3 per unit to reduce target by 4 per unit; reward: source pays 3 per unit to give target +2 per unit). Key forces:

- Social dilemma: contributing raises group payoff but exposing yourself to exploitation. With 1.8 multiplier and 4 players, the marginal per-player return per contributed coin is 1.8/4 = 0.45 — less than the private return of 1. So unilateral defection is tempting.
- Repeated interaction and the ability to identify punishers/rewarders enable informal enforcement: cooperators may punish free-riders and reward cooperators, which can sustain higher contributions.
- Costly sanctions and rewards are used sparingly in real PGGs — players punish occasionally to deter persistent free-riding and reward selectively to reinforce cooperation.
- End-game effect: contributions often decline toward the end rounds as incentives to build future reputation fall.
- Personas shape priors: SLOTH is strongly pro-social and likely to contribute consistently and occasionally to reward/punish; GORILLA is more self-interested and will often free-ride; DUCK is moderate/cooperative but sensitive to others' behavior; CHICKEN is typically uncooperative and likely to contribute very little.

Uncertainties: how much the more cooperative players will maintain contributions when repeatedly exploited, and how persistently they will spend on sanctions (costly). With 29 rounds we expect an initial phase where cooperators contribute relatively high and try light sanctions/rewards, some mid-game adjustment as defectors are targeted, and a falling trend toward the end (end-game free-riding).
reflection:
This is a repeated public goods game with 29 rounds. Each round players decide how many coins to leave in the shared pot (contribution) while the remainder is withdrawn for private use. The pot is multiplied by 1.8 and split equally; after payoff sharing, players may spend costly actions to punish or reward others (punish: source pays 3 per unit to reduce target by 4 per unit; reward: source pays 3 per unit to give target +2 per unit). Key forces:

- Social dilemma: contributing raises group payoff but exposing yourself to exploitation. With 1.8 multiplier and 4 players, the marginal per-player return per contributed coin is 1.8/4 = 0.45 — less than the private return of 1. So unilateral defection is tempting.
- Repeated interaction and the ability to identify punishers/rewarders enable informal enforcement: cooperators may punish free-riders and reward cooperators, which can sustain higher contributions.
- Costly sanctions and rewards are used sparingly in real PGGs — players punish occasionally to deter persistent free-riding and reward selectively to reinforce cooperation.
- End-game effect: contributions often decline toward the end rounds as incentives to build future reputation fall.
- Personas shape priors: SLOTH is strongly pro-social and likely to contribute consistently and occasionally to reward/punish; GORILLA is more self-interested and will often free-ride; DUCK is moderate/cooperative but sensitive to others' behavior; CHICKEN is typically uncooperative and likely to contribute very little.

Uncertainties: how much the more cooperative players will maintain contributions when repeatedly exploited, and how persistently they will spend on sanctions (costly). With 29 rounds we expect an initial phase where cooperators contribute relatively high and try light sanctions/rewards, some mid-game adjustment as defectors are targeted, and a falling trend toward the end (end-game free-riding).

game_explanation:
This 10-round public goods game pits four players with different social tendencies against the temptation to free-ride. Each round, players decide privately how many of the 20 coins to withdraw (so contribution = 20 - withdrawal). Contributions are multiplied by 2.1 and split equally, so individual payoff from the pot depends on others' contributions. After redistribution, players may spend coins to punish (cost 1 to sender, deducts 3 from target) or reward (cost 1 to sender, adds 1 to target) others. Punishment and reward are visible and identifiable.

Likely dynamics:
- Early rounds: some cooperative contributions from more pro-social players (GORILLA, CHICKEN) and lower contributions from less cooperative ones (SLOTH, DUCK). Players see immediate returns and begin forming norms.
- Enforcement: Because punishment is costly, it will be used sparingly — typically targeted at salient free-riders. Rewards may be used even more sparingly to sustain cooperation or reciprocate.
- Adjustment: Punished players are likely to increase contributions temporarily; rewarded players may be reinforced to stay cooperative. Some drift toward selfishness can reappear mid- to late-game as the end approaches, especially from players with lower pro-social priors (SLOTH, DUCK).
- Uncertainty: We don't know exact thresholds each player uses. Punishments can deter defecting, but overuse is costly to punishers and may reduce their contributions later. Final-round behavior often shows more defection because punishment has no future effect.

I simulate a plausible trajectory consistent with these tendencies: GORILLA and CHICKEN act as moderately cooperative enforcers/rewarders; SLOTH and DUCK fluctuate, sometimes free-riding and sometimes responding to sanctions.

game_explanation:
This 6-round public goods game gives each player a binary choice each round: either keep 20 privately (defect) or leave it all in the shared pot (cooperate). The pot is multiplied by 1.6 and split equally, so each coin contributed returns 0.4 coins to every player. That creates a social dilemma: contributing helps the group but is individually dominated in the one-shot sense (you earn 20 more by defecting if others contribute). The added elements — costly punishment (3 to sender, −7 to target) and costly reward (3 to sender, +4 to target) — allow players to impose sanctions or incentives after seeing aggregate outcomes. Punishment and reward are anonymous in the summary, which reduces direct retaliation but still shapes behavior across rounds.

Given the personas:
- SLOTH (high initial trust/cooperation tendency) is likely to contribute most rounds and sometimes punish free-riders to defend cooperation.
- CHICKEN (very cooperative and reciprocating) is likely to contribute and selectively punish defectors and reward returnees.
- DUCK (very low initial trust) tends to defect early but may switch to cooperating if punished enough or rewarded when cooperating.
- GORILLA (mixed) tends to be variable — may cooperate early but is susceptible to experimenting/defecting mid-game.

Uncertainties: exactly who will punish or reward (and how much) is ambiguous because punishers are anonymous. Players typically use punishment and reward sparingly. Expect early rounds to show cooperation by most except DUCK's defection; targeted punishments on DUCK should encourage a shift toward cooperation by mid-game; end-game incentives to defect mean one or two last-round defections are plausible.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game with 4 players (SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN). Each round each player chooses how many of 20 coins to withdraw (private payoff) and the remainder becomes their contribution to the group pot. The pot is multiplied by 1.8 and split equally, so the marginal per-capita return on a contributed coin is 1.8/4 = 0.45. Individually, players gain more by free-riding (keeping coins) but the group is better off if many contribute. Over many rounds, common empirical patterns are: relatively high initial contributions by cooperative types, gradual decline as free-riding is rewarded, and occasional interventions (punishments or rewards) that can slow decline. Because punishments cost the sender more than they hurt the target and rewards are relatively cheap to give positive reinforcement, players typically punish sparingly and reward even more sparingly. Identification of who punished or rewarded someone allows targeted retaliation or reciprocation: that increases the strategic complexity and can both sustain cooperation (if costly sanctions are credible) or escalate retaliatory cycles.

Given the provided personas: SLOTH tends to be strongly cooperative/trusting and gives positive rewards occasionally rather than punishing; DUCK tends to give a lot early but can be strict about norms (more likely to punish free-riders); GORILLA is more self-interested and will drift toward lower contributions; CHICKEN is inconsistent but often generous in one-shot dictator contexts so may both reward cooperators and punish blatant free-riding occasionally. I predict an initial phase with relatively high contributions from SLOTH and DUCK, moderate from CHICKEN, and more moderate-to-low from GORILLA. As GORILLA reduces contributions across rounds, DUCK and CHICKEN will occasionally punish to signal norm violations. SLOTH will reward cooperators occasionally. Overall contributions will trend downward across the 29 rounds, with sparse punishment targeted at clear free-riding and sparse rewards to sustain cooperative behavior at several points. Endgame incentives will push most contributions toward 0 in the last rounds.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game with 4 players and 23 rounds. Each player either contributes all 20 (cooperate) or withdraws all 20 (defect). Contributions are multiplied by 2.5 and split evenly, so cooperators help raise everyone's payoff but get a lower private draw that round; defectors keep 20 privately and may benefit if others cooperate. The punishment stage lets players spend 1 coin per punishment unit to reduce a target's payoff by 2 coins per unit; punishment is costly to the punisher and anonymous to recipients. Messages are allowed but identities of punishers are hidden in the summaries.

Likely dynamics:
- Early rounds: conditional cooperators (DUCK, CHICKEN) will contribute to try to build cooperation; opportunists (SLOTH, GORILLA) may free-ride initially. Because defectors benefit immediately, cooperators may use sparse punishment to deter freeloading.
- Middle rounds: if punishment is credible (even occasional), cooperation can become common. Punishment will be used sparingly to correct repeat defectors.
- Endgame: as players approach the last rounds (known horizon), cooperation typically unravels. Fewer or no punishments will be used late because punishers have less future leverage; that drives more defection in the final rounds.
- Uncertainties: whether punishers are willing to pay repeatedly (it is costly), and whether free-riders respond by switching to cooperation. Because punishers are anonymous, strategic retaliation is limited to general deterrence rather than targeted tit-for-tat.

I will simulate a plausible path: DUCK and CHICKEN act as conditional cooperators and punish occasionally; SLOTH starts as a freerider and gets punished repeatedly, eventually cooperating mid-game; GORILLA fluctuates between free-riding and cooperating. Punishment is used sparingly to enforce norms. Late rounds show erosion of cooperation.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated anonymous public goods game with 3 players, 16 rounds, and an explicit punishment stage. Key incentives:
- Contributing increases the group pot (sum doubled and shared), so cooperating raises everyone’s payoff but costs you privately. The unilateral incentive is to contribute less to keep private holdings while still benefiting from others’ contributions (free-riding).
- Punishment lets players impose costs on low contributors (each punishment unit costs 1 to the sender and removes 2 from the target). Punishment is costly to the punisher, so it is typically used sparingly when players want to deter persistent free-riding or signal disapproval.
Important uncertainties and dynamics:
- Punishment is anonymous: players see how much each peer spent on punishment and how much was deducted from each peer, but cannot see who targeted whom. This weakens precise retaliation or targeted enforcement, and creates some ambiguity about motives.
- Conditional cooperation is likely: cooperators (higher contributors) may reduce contributions if others free-ride. Punishment can sustain cooperation, but only if punishers persist despite costs.
- End-game logic: because the game length is known (16 rounds), contributions typically fall toward the end as the deterrent value of punishment declines.
How the personas bias behavior:
- SLOTH (high trust-sender tendency) leans toward higher contributions and is somewhat willing to punish free-riders.
- GORILLA (moderate/mixed) tends to make mid-level contributions and sometimes punishes.
- DUCK (high initial sending tendency but low return rate) may contribute variably and is more likely to defect or lower contributions relative to early trust signals.
Predicted unfolding: early rounds show moderate-to-high contributions from SLOTH and DUCK, with GORILLA moderate; occasional, targeted punishments against persistent low contributors (mostly DUCK) occur to deter defection. Contributions drift downward over the middle and late rounds, with more pronounced drops in the final rounds and sparse punishment used as the end approaches.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK </PLAYERS>

This is a standard repeated public-goods game with costly punishment. Players get 20 coins each round and decide (simultaneously) how much to contribute to a common pot; the pot is multiplied by 1.9 and split equally among the three players. After contributions are revealed, players can spend coins to punish others: each punishment unit costs the punisher 4 coins and reduces the target's payoff by 12 coins. The game lasts six rounds and players cannot identify who punished them, only how much they were deducted and how much others spent punishing.

Likely dynamics:
- Early rounds: players test each other's cooperativeness. Moderately cooperative types (SLOTH, DUCK) tend to contribute above minimal amounts; the low-cooperation type (GORILLA) tends to contribute less. When free-riding is visible, moderate cooperators may use a small amount of punishment to enforce norms.
- Middle rounds: those punished often increase contributions to avoid future punishment; cooperators maintain moderate-high contributions. Punishments are usually small (1 unit) and used sparingly because of the high cost to the punisher.
- Endgame (round 6): contributions typically fall (backloading), since there is no future punishment incentive; however, a few players may still punish in the final round for fairness or spite, but punishment frequency is reduced.

Uncertainties:
- GORILLA's responsiveness to punishment: may increase contributions after being punished, or continue to free-ride.
- Extent of punishment: players punish sparingly because each unit is costly; punishments will usually be single-unit responses to clear free-riding.
- Chat can influence norms (short messages), but identification limitations make targeted retaliation impossible; punishment is aimed at correcting behavior rather than identifying punisher.

game_explanation:
This public-goods game starts each round with 20 coins in the shared pot; each player chooses how many coins to withdraw for private use and the remainder (0–20) becomes their contribution. The group contribution is multiplied by 2.1 and split evenly among the four players. After payoffs are realized, players can spend coins to reward other players (each reward unit costs the sender 1 coin and gives the recipient 1 coin). Players see who rewarded them and the round summary shows each player's contribution, reward given, reward received, and net payoff for that round. The game lasts 10 rounds.

Key dynamics and incentives
- Free-riding temptation: keeping more coins privately increases private payoff that round, but reduces the group benefit that is multiplied and shared.
- Conditional cooperation: many players start with moderate-to-high contributions; others may begin low. When high contributors are noticed and rewarded, cooperation tends to be sustained or to increase.
- Rewards act as social incentives: because senders are identifiable and recipients see who rewarded them, small targeted positive transfers can encourage higher contributions in later rounds. Rewards are costly to give, so they are usually sparse and directed toward those perceived as cooperative.
- Uncertainties: players do not observe choices before making theirs each round, so early rounds often show cautious or heterogeneous contributions. Anticipation of endgame (final round) sometimes reduces incentives to reward or to contribute late in the game.
- Personas: SLOTH tends to be cooperative and generous; GORILLA is more cautious/self-interested but will reciprocate fairness; DUCK tends toward low initial contributions and low enforcement; CHICKEN is conditionally cooperative and responsive to rewards.

Expected path: initial mixed contributions with a few modest rewards that target the most cooperative players. Over rounds, players who receive rewards (and those who habitually contribute) are more likely to keep or increase contributions; those who are ignored or rarely rewarded (likely DUCK) will often remain at low contributions. Rewards remain sparse (1–2 units typically) and targeted; toward the final round rewards may persist between established cooperators but overall rewarding may taper.

game_explanation:
This is a 16-round repeated public-goods game with 3 players (SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK). Each round each player privately chooses an integer contribution 0–20 from their 20-coin endowment. The group pot (sum of contributions) is multiplied by 2 and split equally among the 3 players. That means each coin contributed returns 2/3 ≈ 0.667 to each player, so an individual who contributes 1 coin loses 1 but receives 0.667 back (a private net loss of ≈0.333), while the group as a whole gains. Private incentive: contribute 0. Social (group) incentive: contribute more.

Typical dynamics to expect here:
- Free-riding is individually profitable, so someone disposed to low contributions is likely to stay low.
- Conditional cooperators will respond to observed peer payoffs/contributions: they tend to contribute more early and then reduce if others free-ride.
- With no communication, uncertainty about peers' intentions keeps early rounds exploratory; players update from the observed round summaries.
- End-game effect: in the last rounds (especially round 16), forward-looking players often reduce contributions toward zero because there is no future to influence.
- Using the provided personas as priors: SLOTH tends to give very little and is likely to free-ride consistently; GORILLA is mixed/conditional and will reciprocate moderately but may moderate downward if others defect; DUCK leans toward higher cooperation early but will reduce contributions if confronted with persistent free-riding. The plausible pattern is: high initial cooperation from DUCK, moderate from GORILLA, low from SLOTH; then a gradual erosion of contributions across rounds, with a final collapse to low contributions by the end.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated 6-round public goods game with four players. Each round each player either contributes all 20 coins to the shared pot or withdraws all 20 for private use. The shared pot is multiplied by 1.6 and split equally, so free-riding (withdrawing) gives a clear immediate advantage unless others punish or reward to change incentives. Punishment is effective at lowering a target's payoff (-7 per unit) but costly to the sender (-3 per unit); rewards add payoff (+4 to recipient) and cost the sender (-3). Because players cannot see who punished or rewarded them and the game is finite (6 rounds), behavior will be shaped by: (1) initial uncertainty about others, (2) the tradeoff between the cost of enforcing cooperation and the private benefit of free-riding, and (3) a likely end-game decline in enforcement as the last round approaches.

Using the provided personas as priors: CHICKEN is highly inclined to contribute and to try to sustain cooperation (and will sometimes pay to reward or punish); GORILLA has a stronger tendency to free-ride; SLOTH and DUCK are mixed/conditional cooperators and will often contribute early but respond to others' behavior. I therefore predict an early pattern of mostly cooperative contributions with GORILLA exploiting them, occasional punishment from cooperators aimed at GORILLA, some targeted small rewards to encourage cooperators, a response by GORILLA (briefly switching to contribute after heavy punishment), and then a final-round unwind where many choose private payoff (withdraw) because no future rounds remain.

game_explanation:
This 6-round public goods game pits three players with distinct social priors against repeated, anonymous contribution choices. Each round players choose an integer 0–20 from their private 20-coin endowment; the group pot is multiplied by 1.9 and split equally. Players only observe round net payoffs (their own and peers'), not raw contributions, so inferences about others' generosity are noisy. Expect typical patterns: an initially cooperative type (DUCK) who gives relatively more, a mixed reciprocator (GORILLA) who gives moderately, and a more self-interested/exploitative type (SLOTH) who gives little. Because net payoffs reward low contributions when others give, SLOTH will often earn the highest round payoff early, which can encourage free-riding. Over rounds, contributions usually drift downward as conditional cooperators reduce giving after feeling exploited and uncertainty about others’ motives persists. Chat is allowed but tends to be sparse; messages will be short appeals or mild complaints, which can slightly influence behavior but not fully reverse the decline without enforceable commitments.

game_explanation:
This public goods game gives each player 20 coins, they choose how many to withdraw for private use and the remainder is contributed to a shared pot. The pot is multiplied by 2.1 and split equally, so each coin contributed returns 0.525 to every player (including the contributor). That creates a private incentive to withdraw (keep) coins but a group incentive to contribute: every coin you give reduces your private return by 1 but increases each player’s payoff by 0.525 (so total group benefit 2.1). Because 0.525 < 1, the selfish short-run incentive is to withdraw (free-ride), while the socially efficient profile is higher contributions.

After contributions are revealed each round, players can spend coins to reward others: each reward unit costs 1 coin to the sender and grants 1 coin to the recipient. Rewards are unidirectional and only positive, so they can be used to reinforce cooperators but not to punish defectors. Rewarding is typically sparse because it’s costly.

Uncertainty and dynamics:
- In early rounds players may test others’ cooperativeness; those who see consistent contributors often respond by rewarding them and sometimes increase their own contributions.
- Players with more prosocial priors (CHICKEN, SLOTH) tend to contribute more initially; GORILLA is moderate and may respond to reciprocity; DUCK is more mixed/low and may free-ride unless directly incentivized.
- Because the game ends after 10 rounds, contributions often decline in the final round as future reciprocity becomes impossible (final-round defecting).
- Rewards will be used sparingly to reinforce cooperation, typically targeted to noticeably higher contributors. Summaries let players see who rewarded them, so rewarders can build reciprocal ties across rounds.

game_explanation:
This 4-player repeated public goods game balances a simple cooperation decision (contribute all 20 coins to the public pot, or withdraw all 20 for private use) with optional costly punishment and reward after seeing revealed contributions. Cooperation increases everyone's group return (contributed sum ×1.6 split evenly), but defectors get the private benefit plus the group share, so free-riding is tempting. Punishment and reward can change incentives, but both are costly to the sender: each unit of punishment costs the sender 3 coins and subtracts 7 coins from the target; each unit of reward costs the sender 3 coins and grants 4 coins to the target.

Given the player personas:
- SLOTH is a mixed/conditional cooperator; likely to try cooperation early and sometimes reward cooperators, but may defect if exploited.
- GORILLA tends to keep private money (low cooperation).
- DUCK is also likely to free-ride or defect.
- CHICKEN tends to enforce cooperation (punishing low-return), and may cooperate to signal norms.

Likely unfolding across 6 rounds:
- Early rounds: SLOTH and CHICKEN try cooperation; GORILLA and DUCK often free-ride. CHICKEN uses occasional punishment to signal intolerance of free-riding; SLOTH sometimes gives small rewards to cooperators.
- Middle rounds: SLOTH may briefly defect after repeated exploitation; CHICKEN will punish defectors but pays a cost, so punishment is sparing.
- Final rounds: With no future rounds beyond round 6, cooperation tends to decay (last-round defection). Punishment becomes less frequent because it's costly with no future enforcement payoff.

I predict sparse, targeted punishment by CHICKEN and occasional small rewards from SLOTH to CHICKEN; GORILLA and DUCK mostly free-ride. The transcript below shows these dynamics round-by-round.

Contributions are multiplied by 1.6 and split equally, so cooperators help the group but individually earn less in the short run than free-riders unless rewarded. Because players cannot see others' choices before deciding, decisions are driven by priors about others and the anticipated use of the reward stage.

Expect initial cooperation from CHICKEN (and likely SLOTH), higher payoffs for defectors early on, occasional reciprocity between cooperators via single reward units, and some fluctuation: SLOTH may defect when the personal payoff gap becomes salient (especially near the end). Chat will be sparse and used to praise, prompt, or complain; rewards will be rare and targeted.

game_explanation: This is a repeated public-goods game with 4 players, 10 rounds, and incomplete information each round (players do not see others' choices before acting). The social optimum is for everyone to contribute all 20 coins (because the pot is multiplied by 2.1 and split equally), but each individual has an immediate incentive to withdraw coins for private use. Because contributions are not observed before choosing, initial uncertainty favors cautious or aspirational cooperation. Over repeated rounds, typical patterns emerge: an initial tendency toward cooperation (especially from players with pro-social priors), followed by gradual erosion as some players free-ride; occasional targeted punishments or small rewards can stabilize cooperation but are costly and therefore used sparingly. Given the personas: SLOTH is highly likely to contribute generously throughout; GORILLA and DUCK are mixed/conditional cooperators and will sometimes punish free-riders or reduce contributions if they expect others to shirk; CHICKEN is more prone to lower contributions and opportunistic behavior. Punishments (costly, remove 3 units per unit punished) will be rare and mostly targeted at clear low contributors; rewards will be used occasionally to reinforce cooperative behavior. Expect modest cooperation overall, some corrective punishments early-to-mid game, and an end-game decline as the horizon approaches.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated (6-round) public goods game with four players. Each player chooses, each round, whether to keep the 20 coins for private use (contribute 0) or leave them in the shared pot (contribute 20). The total contributed is multiplied by 1.6 and split equally. That makes free-riding attractive in the short term (keep 20 plus your share) but cooperation increases group payoffs. After redistribution, players can spend costly reward units (cost 3 to sender, gives 4 to receiver) to reward others; rewarding cannot directly punish but can support cooperators and signal approval.

Key dynamics and uncertainties:
- Immediate temptation: defectors keep 20 and still receive their share, so defecting yields higher immediate earnings unless enough others cooperate repeatedly.
- Reputation via rewards: rewards are anonymous to recipients in their summary, but senders and targets are visible to the game transcript. Players can use rewards to sustain cooperation among a subset (a cooperative clique).
- Strategic responses: cooperators may try to sustain mutual cooperation by rewarding each other; free-riders (here GORILLA) will often keep exploiting the group unless punished or excluded, but there is no formal exclusion.
- End-game unraveling: because the game has a known finite horizon (6 rounds), we expect cooperation to decline toward the final round; some players may still act prosocially out of preference even in the last round.
- Individual priors: SLOTH is relatively cooperative and tends to reward, GORILLA is selfish and unlikely to reward, DUCK and CHICKEN are mixed/reciprocal — likely to cooperate early and reciprocate rewards, but opportunistic in later rounds.

Given these elements, I predict a pattern where three players (SLOTH, DUCK, CHICKEN) mostly cooperate in early- and mid-game, GORILLA free-rides throughout, rewards are used sparingly to sustain cooperation among the three, a small breakdown occurs in the middle (one lapse by CHICKEN), cooperation is restored, and final-round defection (unraveling) occurs with only a couple of small, costly gestures remaining.

game_explanation:
This is a 29-round public-goods game with 4 players (SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN). Each round every player effectively decides how much of 20 coins to keep privately (withdraw) vs leave in the shared pot as a contribution; contributions are revealed after the contribution stage and the pot is multiplied by 1.8 and split equally. After seeing contributions and redistribution, players can spend costly reward units (cost 3 each) to send small positive transfers (each adds 2 to the recipient). Rewards are the only peer-directed mechanism available.

Key incentives and likely dynamics:
- The social optimum is to contribute a lot (since each contributed coin returns 1.8/4 = 0.45 to each player, so group benefit > private benefit only when aggregated), but individually the dominant short-term incentive is to withdraw more (free-ride).
- Rewarding can encourage contributors but is itself costly; in practice rewards are used sparingly (small integers), typically to signal appreciation for higher contributions or to reciprocate.
- Given the provided personas: SLOTH and GORILLA have strong tendencies toward high contributions and toward trusting/cooperative moves, so they are likely to contribute near-high levels fairly consistently and to use small rewards to support other cooperators. CHICKEN is moderately cooperative and responsive; CHICKEN will typically contribute in the midrange and occasionally reward reciprocally. DUCK has a low tendency to contribute or to return in social allocation tasks, so DUCK is the likely persistent low contributor (free-rider) and rarely sends rewards.
- Because contributions are revealed before rewarding, rewards will be targeted (mostly towards higher contributors). Players only see their own net payoffs and who rewarded them after each round; they do not see peers’ summaries, which limits precise inference about others but still provides direct social feedback when someone rewards you.
- Over repeated rounds, expect stable cooperation from SLOTH and GORILLA, gradual adjustment by CHICKEN toward higher contributions if rewarded, and persistent low contributions from DUCK with modest improvements only late in the game. Rewards will be sparse and mostly flow from SLOTH and GORILLA to each other and to CHICKEN on rounds when CHICKEN contributes relatively high. DUCK receives few or no positive rewards.

game_explanation:
This is a 23-round public goods game with 4 players (SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN). Each round each player either keeps all 20 coins for themselves (contribute 0) or leaves them in the shared pot (contribute 20). The pot is multiplied by 2.5 and split equally. That makes cooperation (contribute 20) socially efficient but individually costly relative to free-riding: a contributor gets 12.5 times the number of contributors, while a defector keeps 20 plus the same public return. Punishment is available: each unit a player spends to punish costs them 1 coin and removes 2 coins from the target. Players see totals (how much each player spent punishing and how much was deducted from each player) but do not see who punished whom.

Given the provided personas, CHICKEN is very likely to contribute consistently; GORILLA is very likely to free-ride every round; SLOTH and DUCK are mixed but biased toward cooperation. To sustain cooperation, SLOTH and DUCK are likely to use occasional, small punishments targeted at persistent free-riders (mainly GORILLA). Punishment is used sparingly because it is costly. Occasional defections by SLOTH or DUCK may be punished by the others, but cooperation among the three predictable cooperators should remain high. Toward the end (final rounds), punishment typically tapers off (the endgame effect), so enforcement becomes lighter in the last rounds.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game with 4 players and 23 rounds. Each round starts with a shared pot of 20 coins per player; each player either withdraws all 20 (contribute 0) or withdraws nothing (contribute 20). The remaining total in the pot is multiplied by 2.5 and split equally among the four players. Withdrawing (defecting) gives an immediate private gain and still yields the player an equal share of the multiplied pot — so there is a strong short-run incentive to withdraw while others contribute (free-riding). However, if too many withdraw, the public return shrinks and everyone earns less.

Typical dynamics here are:
- Early rounds often show partial cooperation: some players contribute hoping to obtain higher group returns; others free-ride.
- Observing round summaries (everyone sees each player’s round payoff) allows inference about who withdrew. High payoffs typically indicate withdrawal; low payoffs indicate the player contributed.
- Conditional cooperators (those who tend to match others’ behavior) may start cooperating but reduce contributions once they detect free-riders; persistent free-riders will continue to exploit cooperators.
- Low-volume chat can be used to coordinate, signal intentions, or shame free-riders. Occasional attempts at group coordination can produce brief returns to cooperation, but without credible enforcement cooperation tends to unravel before the final rounds.
Given the player personas (SLOTH: mixed/cooperative tendency; GORILLA: relatively selfish; DUCK: conditional/cooperative; CHICKEN: highly willing to contribute), I expect initial cooperation by CHICKEN (and likely DUCK and SLOTH), repeated exploitation by GORILLA, gradual erosion of cooperation, a few coordination attempts via chat, a brief successful full-cooperation round, and otherwise frequent defection toward the end.

game_explanation:
This repeated public-goods game pits private incentives (keep your 1 coin for yourself) against group benefit (each coin contributed yields 1.9 total, split among 3 players so each gets 0.6333). The Nash incentive is to free-ride (contribute 0) because the private return on a contributed coin < 1, but group welfare is maximized by full contributions. With cheap talk available and 6 rounds, initial rounds often show some cooperation sustained by promises and conditional responses; cooperation typically drifts downward across rounds as players learn others' behavior and respond to short-run incentives. Uncertainties include whether cheap talk is credible, how fast conditional cooperators (Sloth, Gorilla) will reduce contributions after perceived free-riding, and whether the strongly prosocial DUCK keeps contributing even when it lowers their own payoff.

Using the provided personas as priors: DUCK is very likely to start and often keep high contributions (pro-social, high initial trust). SLOTH tends to be cooperative enough early but is somewhat passive and may taper contributions slowly. GORILLA is mixed/conditional and will reduce contributions more when others appear to free-ride. So I predict an initially cooperative profile with DUCK contributing the maximum in most rounds, and SLOTH and GORILLA reducing contributions gradually (Gorilla more aggressively). This will make DUCK's net payoff fall over time while SLOTH/GORILLA benefit from freeriding.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game with four players and 29 rounds. Each round each player chooses how many of the 20 coins to withdraw privately (so contribution = 20 − withdrawn). The shared pot is multiplied by 1.8 and split equally across the four players; each coin a player contributes returns 1.8/4 = 0.45 coins to her own payoff while generating 0.45*4 = 1.8 total group value. That means the dominant individual incentive is to withdraw everything (contribute 0) even though group welfare is higher if everyone contributes more. Information is limited: players do not see others' choices before deciding, but they learn everyone's contributions after the round and can reward individuals afterwards. Rewards are costly (cost 3 per reward unit to the sender, benefit 2 per unit to the recipient) and thus will be used sparingly. Because the game is finite (29 rounds), we also expect some end‑game decline in contributions.

Given the player priors (SLOTH: moderately inclined to cooperate; GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN: low-to-very-low returning/cooperation tendencies), a plausible unfolding is:
- Early rounds: SLOTH experiments with above-average contributions to signal cooperation; others start lower due to distrust.
- Occasional small rewards will appear when a player perceives unusually high contributions from a peer (rewarding is rare because it’s costly).
- Observed reciprocity: a small reward can trigger a one-off reciprocation, but sustained reward-driven cooperation is unlikely because most players have low cooperative priors and the reward-to-cost ratio is low (2 benefit vs. 3 cost).
- Over time, absent strong mutual reinforcement, contributions drift downward; by middle-to-late rounds most players contribute little or nothing. In final rounds, contributions typically collapse to 0 as strategic endgame behavior dominates.

Below is a simulated round-by-round transcript (round explanations and predicted actions) consistent with these incentives and priors.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with 4 players (SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN). Each round every player decides how many of the 20 coins to withdraw privately; the remainder (0–20) is their contribution to the public pot. The group pot is multiplied by 1.8 and split equally, so each coin contributed returns 1.8/4 = 0.45 coins to the contributor — less than the private value of 1 — creating a strong individual incentive to withdraw (free-ride). The group optimum is to contribute everything, but selfish incentives push toward low contributions.

The reward stage lets players transfer costly rewards: each reward unit costs the giver 3 coins and gives the recipient 2 coins. Rewards are therefore costly to the sender and slightly beneficial to the receiver; they can be used sparingly to encourage cooperators or reciprocate kindness, but they are inefficient in aggregate. Because rewards are visible and identify the source, they can sustain cooperation through reciprocity or selective rewarding despite the personal cost.

Given the player personas:
- SLOTH is moderately pro-social and likely to start contributing relatively high and to reward cooperators occasionally.
- CHICKEN is mixed/pro-social in some settings and likely to try to sustain cooperation by contributing and sometimes reciprocating rewards.
- GORILLA tends to contribute little but may respond to occasional rewards with small upticks.
- DUCK is a persistent free-rider (near-zero contributions) and rarely rewards others.

I therefore predict initial moderate contributions from SLOTH and CHICKEN, low contributions from GORILLA, and near-zero from DUCK. Rewards will be sparse and mostly directed between SLOTH and CHICKEN (mutual reinforcement), with occasional small rewards from CHICKEN to GORILLA after slight increases. Over time, exploitation by DUCK and low contributions by GORILLA will gradually erode cooperation; contributions will drift downward across the 29 rounds. Rewards will appear intermittently, mainly 1-unit gifts, used to sustain reciprocity rather than to fully counteract free-riding.

game_explanation:
This is a standard 3-player public goods game with a multiplier of 1.9 on the group pot and three players. Each coin you contribute returns 1.9 coins total to the group, so each coin gives 1.9/3 ≈ 0.633 to each player. Individually, keeping a coin (private value = 1) dominates contributing it (private return ≈ 0.633), so the one-shot dominant strategy is to free-ride. However, if everyone contributes, the group gets higher total payoffs. Because the game repeats for six rounds and players can punish others (costly to the punisher, costly to the punished), we expect some mix of cooperative contributions early and social enforcement (punishment) of low contributions when deviations are noticeable.

Uncertainties: players differ in baseline cooperativeness and willingness to punish. SLOTH has medium pro-social tendencies and may start relatively cooperative and punish sometimes. GORILLA is more mixed and may opportunistically free ride; less likely to initiate costly punishment but will sometimes retaliate. DUCK is mixed and can be swayed by peer behavior; may test cooperation early. Punishment is relatively costly to the sender (4 coins per unit) and very costly to the receiver (12 coins per unit), so punishments tend to be rare and aimed at clear low contributors. Messaging is sparse but can coordinate expectations or call out free-riding.

Expected unfolding: modest-to-moderate initial contributions, low use of punishment in round 1, a test or two where someone deviates and is punished modestly, then recovery to higher contributions as punishment signals are effective. In the final round, some players may attempt to free-ride again and risk punishment within that round.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated, finite public-goods game with 4 players and 6 rounds. Each round players choose either to leave the 20 coins in the shared pot (contribute 20) or withdraw them for private use (contribute 0). The pot is multiplied by 1.6 and split equally, so each contributed 20 yields a group return of 8 coins per player (20 * 1.6 / 4 = 8). Individually, withdrawing 20 yields an immediate private gain of 20, while contributing 20 yields no private reserve that round. So the dominant short-term incentive is to withdraw (defect), but contributing benefits the group if others also contribute.

Costly rewards are available after redistribution: each reward unit costs the sender 3 coins and gives the target 4 coins. Rewards can thus partially offset the private incentive to defect, and they create opportunities for cooperators to support each other at personal cost. Rewards are anonymous in the summary players see (they know how much they received and how much others received, but cannot identify who gave any particular reward), which weakens direct reciprocal signaling but still allows directed rewarding during play.

Given the player personas:
- GORILLA is highly cooperative and likely to contribute most rounds and use rewards to support cooperators.
- CHICKEN is moderately cooperative and may start by contributing, possibly becoming less willing if exploited.
- SLOTH and DUCK are more selfish and likely to withdraw (defect) in most rounds.

Typical unfolding I predict:
- Early rounds: GORILLA and CHICKEN contribute; SLOTH and DUCK withdraw. Cooperators (GORILLA and CHICKEN) use sparse mutual rewards to reinforce cooperation. Free-riders (SLOTH, DUCK) earn higher short-term payoffs, making cooperation fragile.
- Middle rounds: GORILLA persistently contributes and may increase modest rewarding of cooperators; CHICKEN may continue cooperating for a few rounds but will become frustrated and switch to withdrawing as the end approaches.
- Final rounds: cooperation collapses toward universal withdrawal (round 6 especially expected to be all-defect due to the known finite horizon). Rewarding becomes rare at the end.

Now the round-by-round transcript.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated 6-round public goods game with three players (SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK). Each round each player privately chooses an integer contribution from 0–20; the group pot (sum of contributions) is multiplied by 1.9 and split equally. Individually, each coin contributed returns 1.9/3 ≈ 0.633 coins to its contributor — less than 1 — so the one-shot selfish incentive is to contribute 0. However, total group payoff is higher when people contribute more, so there is a tension between private incentives (free-ride) and collective benefit (cooperation). Over repeated rounds, players can send messages and observe each round’s payoffs for all players (but not their raw contributions directly before choosing), so they can condition behavior on others' revealed payoffs and on chat.

Given the three personas:
- GORILLA is highly cooperative and likely to start with high contributions and try to encourage cooperation.
- DUCK is moderately cooperative and likely to contribute a positive moderate amount, responsive to social signals.
- SLOTH leans toward low contributions (less prosocial), so will likely free-ride early and possibly throughout.

Likely unfolding: GORILLA will propose cooperation early; DUCK will tentatively join; SLOTH will contribute little. Seeing SLOTH’s low payoff advantage from free-riding, GORILLA and DUCK may reduce contributions in later rounds (reciprocity / punishment). Near the end (round 6), standard “end-game” incentives make everyone more likely to defect; we should therefore expect contributions to fall by the final round. Chat will be sparse — a few calls for cooperation and occasional responses, mainly from GORILLA and DUCK, with SLOTH terse or defensive.

game_explanation:
This is a 6-round public goods game with 4 players and binary contribution choices: in each round a player either leaves their 20 coins in the shared pot (contribute 20) or withdraws them for private use (contribute 0). The shared pot is multiplied by 1.6 and split equally, so any single contributor increases the total social return but individually receives only 8 coins from a 20-coin contribution (20 * 1.6 / 4 = 8). That makes free-riding appealing: defectors keep 20 privately and still receive the shared return. Punishment and reward let players sanction or incentivize others, but punishments are costly to apply (3 coins per unit for sender, -7 to the target per unit) and rewards are also costly (3 per unit for sender, +4 to target per unit). Messages are allowed and actions are anonymous in the summary.

Likely dynamics given these incentives and the provided personas:
- Duck has the most pro-social tendency and is the most likely to contribute early and try to sustain cooperation.
- Sloth and Gorilla are more self-interested and are likely to free-ride early; Sloth may be somewhat selfish and retaliatory, Gorilla opportunistic.
- Chicken is also likely to free-ride initially and punish only if strongly provoked.

Expect cooperation to start low, with Duck contributing while the others predominantly withdraw. Duck may use costly punishment sparingly to discourage free-riding; free-riders who are repeatedly punished may retaliate. Rewards may appear rarely (to encourage a lone cooperator), and cooperation will tend to collapse toward the final rounds (especially the last round) because future retaliation or reciprocity loses force.

game_explanation:
This repeated public-goods game pits four identifiable players with different cooperation tendencies against the usual social dilemma: contributing to the public pot increases group payoff (the pot is multiplied by 1.8 and split), but withdrawing coins for private use yields a guaranteed private return. Players also can reward individual peers after seeing contributions; rewarding is costly (3 coins per reward unit) but benefits the recipient (adds 2 coins per unit). Because rewards are sparse and costly, they tend to be used selectively to reinforce cooperative behavior or, less commonly, to punish free-riding indirectly by withholding rewards.

Given the provided personas, I expect a stable core of cooperation from CHICKEN (consistently high contributions), moderate and somewhat responsive cooperation from DUCK (raises contributions when rewarded and when CHICKEN is reliably cooperative), mixed behavior from GORILLA (occasionally cooperative, sometimes opportunistic), and persistent low contributions from SLOTH (frequent free-riding). game_explanation: This is a 16-round repeated public goods game with three players (SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK). Each round every player gets 20 coins and simultaneously chooses an integer contribution (0–20) to a common pot. The pot is doubled and split equally among the three players; so each round a player's payoff = (20 − contribution) + (2 × total contributions / 3). The dominant short-run incentive is to contribute 0 (free-ride) because a player keeps 1 coin by withholding a coin while the increased group return from that coin is only 2/3 < 1. Over repeated rounds players may condition contributions on observed behavior: cooperators can be exploited by persistent free-riders, and without communication or enforcement we expect downward pressure on contributions as cooperators reduce their giving when exploited. The 16-round finite horizon makes end-game decline likely, but typical human play often starts with some cooperation before declining. Using the provided personas: GORILLA has very selfish priors and is likely to contribute 0 repeatedly; DUCK is moderately cooperative and will start relatively high but taper off if exploited; SLOTH is mixed/moderate and will tend to make modest contributions and adjust slowly.

Each round, everyone starts with 20 coins, chooses an integer contribution (0–20) to the shared pot, the pot is multiplied by 1.8 and split equally, and then players can optionally pay 3 coins per reward unit to give another player +2 coins per unit. Contributions are observed before rewarding each round, so rewards can be targeted to signal approval and shape behavior; but rewards are costly, so they tend to be rare and selective.

Contributions are multiplied by 2.5 and split equally, so cooperators raise the group pot but earn less privately that round; defectors get the higher private share but risk being punished. Punishment is costly to the punisher (1 coin per punishment unit) and harsher to the target (2 coins deducted per unit). The game lasts 23 rounds, so players weigh short-term gain from defecting against possible punishments and the prospect of future enforcement.

Given the player priors:
- GORILLA is the most reliably cooperative and will often try to enforce cooperation with selective punishment.
- DUCK is the most likely persistent free-rider (defecting most rounds) and occasionally retaliatory/spiteful.
- CHICKEN is mixed—leans to cooperating but will punish defectors sometimes.
- SLOTH is inconsistent/mixed and will switch between cooperation and defection.

DUCK continues to defect, occasionally retaliating. Final round sees more direct punishment (no future repercussions).

game_explanation:
This online public goods game pits four players (SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN) against a standard collective-action problem: each round, 20 coins are available per player; each chooses an integer contribution (0–20) to a shared pot, keeping the remainder privately. The pot is multiplied by 2.1 and split equally. After payoffs from the public good are revealed, players may spend coins to punish (cost 1 to the punisher, -3 to the target) or reward (cost 1 to the sender, +1 to the recipient) others. The game lasts 10 rounds and players see who punished/rewarded them in the summary.

Likely dynamics
- Immediate incentives favor withdrawing many coins (private gain), but mutual cooperation (high contributions) raises everyone's payoff.
- With anonymity of simultaneous decisions, early uncertainty about others' cooperativeness leads to mixed behavior: some players (based on personas) tend to contribute more (CHICKEN, SLOTH) while others tend to contribute little (GORILLA). DUCK is moderate.
- Punishment and reward act as enforcement/teaching signals. Because punishments are costly (to enforcers) and particularly harsh on targets (-3 per unit), they will be used sparingly—mainly to sanction persistent low contributors (GORILLA) or to encourage high contributors via small rewards (CHICKEN rewarding SLOTH).
- Over rounds, we expect mild downward pressure on cooperation because persistent free-riding by someone like GORILLA makes others either punish periodically or slightly reduce contributions. Rewards are rarer and small; punishments are also rare but concentrated on the low contributor.
- Because final-round incentives reduce the benefit of long-run reciprocity, players may cut contributions slightly toward the end; punishments drop a bit in the final round.

Uncertainties
- Exact magnitudes of conditional responses (how much SLOTH or CHICKEN change their contributions when others free-ride) are uncertain.
- Whether GORILLA reacts to sustained punishment by raising contributions or simply continues to free-ride depends on their tolerance for punishment losses.

We now present a plausible round-by-round transcript (round explanations, actions, and the summaries players would see). Avatar order used for contributions and summaries is: SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game with 6 rounds, four players, and a tight binary choice each round: either withdraw the whole 20 (contribute 0) or leave it in the pot (contribute 20). The private incentive is strong: a defector keeps 20 and still receives the equal share of the multiplied public pot, so short-run gain from defecting is high. However, cooperators can use costly positive rewards (3 coins spent to give 4 coins to someone) to sustain cooperation by rewarding contributors. Players know the horizon (6 rounds), so toward the end we expect more defections (backward induction / endgame effects). Based on the provided personas:

- CHICKEN (very low prosocial scores) is almost certain to withdraw every round (defect).
- GORILLA (moderately pro-social) tends to contribute repeatedly and reward cooperators.
- DUCK (medium-high reciprocity) tends to contribute and reward cooperators, but may experiment if payoffs for defecting look much better.
- SLOTH (mixed) may start by free-riding but can be nudged toward cooperation if rewarded.

Overall predicted dynamics: early rounds — a mix of cooperation and defection with sparse mutual rewarding among cooperators (Gorilla and Duck), possibly SLOTH initially free-rides then shifts to cooperation after seeing targeted rewards. Middle rounds — cooperators keep supporting one another; some experimentation by Duck or Sloth may occur. Late rounds — defections rise, and reward use falls; final round most will defect.

predicted_rounds.explanation: With only one round after this, backward induction pressures rise: defecting becomes more attractive. Sloth may revert to defecting again; Duck has already defected last round; Gorilla may still try to hold the line but is vulnerable. Rewarding is less useful and often falls off late in the game.

predicted_rounds.explanation: Final round: standard prediction is universal defection because there is no future to sustain cooperation. Rewards are usually not used in the last round (no incentive to incur costs).

messages.speaker: GORILLA

messages.speaker: CHICKEN
