game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK </PLAYERS>

This is a standard repeated public goods game with 10 players, 6 rounds, and a large multiplier (6.2). Each round each player must either contribute their whole endowment (20) or nothing (0). Contributing increases the total pot by 20 but costs 20 privately; the marginal per-person return from one person's 20 is 0.62 * 20 = 12.4 to each player (including the contributor). So contributing is individually costly (−7.6 compared with free-riding) but benefits the group. Because players see round-by-round payoffs (their own and peers'), we expect early-round cooperation from some pro-social types, then a decline as free-riders earn more and contributors feel exploited. Messages can signal intent and sometimes sustain cooperation, but chat is sparse and usually not fully credible. By the middle rounds contributions typically fall; by the final rounds most players are likely to free-ride. A few highly cooperative or trusting players (e.g., PARROT, GORILLA, OWL, CHICK) may contribute initially; selfish types (DOG, RABBIT, CHICKEN) will likely free-ride throughout. Uncertainty about others' moves and a few short messages may produce temporary pockets of cooperation that erode as the game proceeds.

game_explanation:
This repeated public-goods game creates a classic tension: contributing (20) increases the group's pot (it is multiplied by 6.2 and shared equally), which benefits everyone, but withholding (0) gives a higher private payoff within any given round because you keep the 20 while still receiving the shared return. With 10 players, each coin contributed yields 6.2/10 = 0.62 coins back to any one player; a contributor loses 20 but gains 0.62*20 = 12.4 in return per round from their own contribution — so contributing is individually costly (net loss relative to keeping the 20) but collectively beneficial.

Given the player personas, I expect a mix of stable cooperators (PARROT, some of OWL/RABBIT/MOOSE) and persistent free-riders (GORILLA, DOG). Many players are conditionally cooperative or mixed; they may start out contributing when norms or messages encourage it but then switch to withholding after observing that free-riding pays. Chat can briefly boost cooperation early, but unless a strong coordination emerges, contributions typically decay across rounds, especially as the end approaches.

I will simulate these dynamics: initial fairly high cooperation motivated by a few committed contributors and a couple of free-riders; then gradual decline as defectors earn more and some mixed/conditional players switch to withholding. Chat messages are sparse and used mainly to pledge, encourage, or complain.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game with 6 players who can also punish or reward after viewing contributions. Multiplying the pot by 3.8 and splitting it equally gives a per-player return of about 0.6333 times the group contribution sum, so a player’s private marginal return from contributing one coin is 0.6333 — less than the private cost of 1 coin. That creates a temptation to free-ride (contribute less) while group welfare is maximized when everyone contributes more. Punishments are costly to apply (3 coins per unit) but strongly hurt targets (-12 per punishment unit), so a small number of punishment units can deter free-riding; rewards are cheaper for targets (+2 per unit) but cost the giver (3 per unit) and are less punitive/incentive-effective than punishment.

Given the player personas:
- GORILLA is reliably cooperative (likely to contribute near the endowment).
- SLOTH is moderately cooperative but responsive to norms.
- PARROT is mixed and a bit norm-focused; may reward or punish occasionally.
- DUCK, DOG, CHICKEN trend toward lower contributions but can increase if sanctioned or socially pressured (CHICKEN is the least likely to punish).
- Punishment will be used sparingly and targeted at consistent low contributors early on; rewards will be used occasionally to reinforce cooperators.

Overall expected dynamics: an initial mix of cooperation and free-riding, a few early targeted punishments to deter the most obvious free-rider(s), some modest use of rewards to support cooperators, and then a gradual rise in average contributions as norms stabilize. Punishments become rarer over time; occasional rewards persist to reinforce high contributors.

game_explanation:
This public-goods game pits individual incentives (take the 20 now) against collective benefit (leave the 20 in the pot so the multiplied total is shared). Because each contributed 20 is multiplied by 2.6 and split across 7 players, an individual gets only 2.6/7 ≈ 0.371 of each coin they contribute back to themselves — well below 1 — so the dominant selfish move is to withdraw (free-ride). But total payoff is maximized if everyone contributes (because 2.6 > 1).

With no guaranteed end and limited visibility, early rounds tend to be noisy: some players (those with cooperative priors) will contribute hoping to start a cooperative norm; others will free-ride. Given the ability to punish (costly to sender, effective against target) and reward (costly to sender, beneficial to target), groups commonly use a mix of light punishment to deter persistent free-riding and occasional rewards to reinforce cooperation. Punishment is used sparingly because it costs the punisher, and rewards are used sparingly to encourage good behavior. Messages can coordinate expectations (requests, threats, thanks), so you’ll typically see a few short chats early on and when norms shift.

Expect a dynamic where cooperators (CHICKEN, MOOSE, PARROT, DOG, GORILLA to varying degrees) try to establish cooperation, SLOTH and DUCK are more likely to free-ride initially, punishments target the persistent defector(s) early on, and, over rounds, a combination of light punishment and selective rewards nudges most players toward contribution by later rounds.

This public goods game pits a small group of players against the temptation to withdraw the shared pot versus leaving it for the group. Because each player either withdraws all 20 (contribute 0) or leaves all 20 (contribute 20), decisions are crisp: cooperate (20) or defect (0). The pot is multiplied by 2.6 and split equally, so each additional contributor raises everyone's share but benefits defectors immediately (they keep 20 plus the shared return). Rewards are symmetric in cost and benefit (2 cost to giver, 2 gain to receiver), so they are a costly but direct way to signal appreciation for cooperators (and rarely to punish by omission rather than negative transfer).

Given the personas:
- SLOTH and DOG are strongly prosocial and likely to contribute consistently and reward cooperators.
- GORILLA and PARROT are much more likely to free-ride (withdraw) and to avoid rewarding.
- DUCK, CHICKEN, and MOOSE are mixed; they lean neither strongly toward defection nor steadfast cooperation but may follow social cues. CHICKEN and MOOSE are somewhat more inclined to reciprocate positive gestures than DUCK.

I expect a stable pattern: most of the group (SLOTH, DOG, CHICKEN, MOOSE, often DUCK) will cooperate in many rounds; GORILLA and PARROT will mainly free-ride. Rewards will be sparse and targeted toward cooperators (a few 1-unit rewards per round), primarily from SLOTH, DOG, and CHICKEN, reinforcing cooperative behavior. Chat will be rare and functional — short praise, reminders, or mild complaints.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK </PLAYERS>

This is a repeated public-goods game with 10 players over 6 rounds. Each round everyone privately has 20 coins and chooses either to contribute all 20 to a shared pot or keep all 20. The pot is multiplied by 6.2 and split equally, so each 20 contributed adds 6.2*20/10 = 12.4 coins to each person (including the contributor) but costs the contributor 20 immediately. Purely selfish players therefore prefer to keep their 20 unless enough others contribute; conditional cooperators may contribute if they expect others to do so. Punishment is costly: each punishment unit costs the punisher 2 coins and removes 2 coins from the punished player's payoff. Punishment can enforce cooperation but is itself costly, and because players see who punished them, reputational concerns matter in repeated rounds. Knowing the game lasts exactly 6 rounds creates an "endgame" pressure: cooperation and punishment incentives typically decline toward the last rounds.

Given the player personas, I expect a mix of steady cooperators (MOOSE, RABBIT, CHICK, DOG, OWL), conditional cooperators (DUCK, PARROT, SLOTH), and some likely free-riders (GORILLA, CHICKEN). PARROT tends to enforce norms more than most and will use punishment sparingly but decisively; MOOSE is strongly pro-social and may punish occasional blatant free-riding. Overall pattern I predict: moderate-to-strong cooperation early, a few targeted punishments against persistent defectors, some conditional players switch behavior in response to punishments, and gradual erosion of contributions by the final round as punishment effectiveness wanes. Chat will be sparse and used mainly for norm reminders or short reactions.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game with 7 players who each face a binary choice each round: either keep all 20 coins for themselves (contribute 0) or leave all 20 in the shared pot (contribute 20). The pot is multiplied by 2.6 and split equally, so contributions help everyone but are individually costly. After contributions are revealed, players can punish (cost 2 to sender, -3 to target) or reward (cost 2 to sender, +2 to target) others; punish/reward decisions are visible in the summaries and identify senders.

Given the provided personas, I expect a small core of consistently cooperative players (PARROT and MOOSE, possibly DUCK and later GORILLA) who contribute most rounds, and a subset of more selfish players (SLOTH, CHICKEN, DOG, and initially GORILLA) who tend to free-ride. PARROT is relatively willing to enforce norms (punish or reward) and will selectively punish repeat free-riders; MOOSE is likely to support cooperators with occasional rewards. Punishment will be used sparingly (a few -1 units at a time) to nudge behavior; rewards will appear occasionally to reinforce cooperation. Over rounds we should see some migration toward cooperation as a few defectors respond to punishment or to positive social signals, while at least one stubborn defector (DOG) may persist and receive occasional punishments.

- Cooperative types (DUCK, GORILLA, CHICKEN, PARROT) tend to contribute moderately–high amounts; selfish type (DOG) tends to contribute little or nothing. SLOTH is variable and initially cautious.

Given the player personas, I expect early uncertainty and some free-riding, followed by conditional cooperation sustained by a few targeted punishments and occasional rewards. GORILLA and CHICKEN are strongly cooperative and likely to punish defectors lightly; SLOTH and PARROT are moderately cooperative and may reward cooperators; DUCK and MOOSE lean toward selfish choices but can be nudged toward cooperation after being punished; DOG is a high-enforcement, low-giving type who may free-ride initially and respond sharply to norm enforcement (either by changing behavior or retaliating). Because players don't know the total rounds, cooperation tends to be maintained by ongoing enforcement rather than end-game defection.

Punishment provides a mechanism to deter free-riding: sending 1 punishment unit costs the punisher 2 coins and reduces the target's payoff by 2. Because punishers pay to reduce others' payoffs, punishment is used sparingly — often by those who value cooperation or want to signal norms — and can provoke retaliation.

Given the player personas:

reflection:
This is a standard repeated public-goods game with costly punishment. Each round, contributing (20) benefits the group but is individually costly: a contributor gives up 20 private coins and each contributed coin yields 0.62 coins back to every player (6.2*contribution divided by 10 players), so defecting (keeping 20) strictly dominates contributing in a one-shot sense. Punishment provides a mechanism to deter free-riding: sending 1 punishment unit costs the punisher 2 coins and reduces the target's payoff by 2. Because punishers pay to reduce others' payoffs, punishment is used sparingly — often by those who value cooperation or want to signal norms — and can provoke retaliation.

Given the player personas:
- MOOSE and OWL are relatively more pro-cooperative and likely to contribute early.

game_explanation:
This repeated public goods game pits 10 identifiable players against a classic cooperation vs. free-riding tension. Each round players choose to contribute their whole 20 coins or keep it; cooperative contributions multiply strongly (×6.2) and are shared equally, so group benefit is maximized when everyone contributes. But because the private benefit from withholding (keeping 20 while still receiving a share of the multiplied pot) can exceed the private return from contributing, some players will test or persist in free-riding.

Given the provided personas, a plausible unfolding:
- A core of relatively prosocial players (DOG, MOOSE, PARROT, OWL, GORILLA) will contribute early and are willing to punish clear defectors lightly to enforce norms.
- A few players with very low cooperation priors (CHICKEN, CHICK) are likely to free-ride initially and may be slow to change. Repeated small punishments may induce one to switch after a few rounds; the most stubborn may keep defecting until the final rounds.
- Mixed-profile players (SLOTH, DUCK, RABBIT) may test the waters and shift towards contributing after being punished a couple of times, but some final-round free-riding is likely.
- Punishment is expected to be sparse and targeted: contributors punish persistent defectors with small (1-unit) sanctions rather than heavy coordinated punishment, because each unit costs the punisher real coins.

Uncertainty remains about exact switching points (who gives up free-riding when) and about final-round behavior (many players tend to reduce contributions in the terminal round). The transcript below predicts one plausible course across six rounds: early targeted punishments bring most players into contribution by rounds 3–4; one stubborn defector (CHICK) resists several rounds and is punished repeatedly, then yields near the end; the final round shows a small decline in contributions consistent with endgame free-riding.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game among seven players with binary contribution choices (contribute all 20 or keep all 20). The key tensions:
- Contributing is socially efficient: each 20 contributed becomes 52.0 total value split across all players when everyone contributes; even when only some contribute, the pot is multiplied and benefits everyone. But a single defector keeps a private 20 while still receiving a share of the multiplied pot — a strong short-run incentive to free-ride.
- Rewards are costly (2 coins per reward unit) but can send direct positive signals to encourage contributors. Because rewards are costly, they will be used sparingly and typically targeted at cooperating peers or at new cooperators to encourage them.
- Players are uncertain about others' types and do not know the total number of rounds. This typically leads cooperators (high prior on cooperation) to contribute early, defectors to exploit early, and some conditional cooperators to respond to visible reward signals and peers' past behavior.
- Over rounds we expect a core of reliably cooperative players (SLOTH, GORILLA, CHICKEN, MOOSE by their priors) to contribute most rounds, PARROT to defect consistently, DUCK to be inconsistent (sometimes cooperate but often exploit), and DOG to switch occasionally. Rewards will be sparse and directed toward perceived cooperators; defectors will rarely be rewarded. When a defector briefly cooperates, they may receive a small reward to reinforce that change.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game with a simple binary contribution choice each round (either keep the 20 coins privately or leave them all in the shared pot). The multiplier (2.6) and equal split mean an individual’s private payoff from contributing depends heavily on how many others contribute. Because players do not know the number of rounds, there is ongoing uncertainty that typically sustains some cooperation early on. Players can reward others after seeing contributions; rewards are costly to the sender (2 coins per reward unit) and beneficial to the recipient (2 coins per unit), so rewards are a way to selectively reinforce cooperative behavior even though they don't change total social surplus.

Given the provided personas, many players are predisposed to contribute (SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, DUCK/CHICKEN types tend to start cooperative), while a couple (DOG, MOOSE sometimes) are more likely to withdraw. Rewards are usually sparse — small, targeted gestures that reinforce cooperators or signal approval. Over rounds we therefore expect relatively high initial cooperation, a few persistent withdrawers, occasional experiments (one or two players deviating in some rounds), and modest, targeted rewarding of cooperators. Players will use chat only occasionally, typically to thank or complain. Because players can see who rewarded them, reward patterns can help sustain cooperation among reciprocating players.

game_explanation:
This public-goods game pits the private incentive to free-ride against the group's larger return from contributions. Each coin you keep yields 1 privately; each coin you contribute returns 3.8 to the group, or about 0.633 to each of the six players — so every contributed coin raises everyone’s payoff but lowers the contributor’s private marginal return. Given the player personas, I expect SLOTH, GORILLA and CHICKEN to start fairly high (prosocial tendencies), DUCK and PARROT to be middling, and DOG to contribute little. Because rewards are costly (3 coins to the giver per reward unit) but visible and targeted (2 coins to the recipient), they will be used sparingly to reinforce cooperative behavior rather than to fully pay or punish; rewards typically show up as small, targeted 1-unit gestures that encourage reciprocation. Over many rounds, contributions should drift downward as some players reduce contributions in response to others' lower contributions; a few cooperative players will intermittently reward top contributors to try to stabilize cooperation. Chats will be rare — brief coordination, thanks, or appeals — and rewards will be sparse and strategic.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game with 7 players and a binary contribution choice each round: either keep all 20 privately (contribution = 0) or leave all 20 in the common pot (contribution = 20). The pot is multiplied by 2.6 and split equally among all players. Individually, keeping 20 always yields a higher private payoff than contributing (because 2.6/7 < 1), so the dominant incentive is to free-ride each round. However, social preferences and the optional reward stage create motives to contribute: pro-social players may contribute even when it lowers their immediate payoff, and other players can use costly rewards (2 coins cost to giver per unit, 2 coins gain to recipient per unit) to recognize contributors. Rewards are zero-sum (giver loses what the receiver gains), so rewards mainly serve signaling and reciprocity rather than increasing total group payoffs.

Given the seven personas:
- CHICKEN, DOG, and MOOSE are the strongest priors for consistent cooperation.
- DUCK and SLOTH are moderately likely to contribute sometimes.
- GORILLA is predicted to free-ride almost always.
- PARROT is mixed/leaning toward low contributions.

I predict a pattern where a small core (CHICKEN, DOG, often MOOSE and occasionally DUCK or SLOTH) continues contributing across many rounds, while GORILLA and PARROT mostly free-ride. Rewards are used sparingly to thank cooperators; most reward edges are 1 unit (2 coins), with occasional reciprocal exchanges. Because rewards are costly, they are limited to a few edges per round. Chat is sparse and mostly used by cooperators to encourage or to call out defections.

game_explanation:
This is a 6-round finitely repeated public goods game with 10 players. Each round everyone simultaneously chooses either to contribute their entire 20-coin endowment to the public pot or to keep it. The pot is multiplied by 6.2 and split equally among all 10 players. Because the multiplication is shared equally, a single contributor increases everyone's payoff but loses 20 privately; free riders keep their 20 and also receive the shared benefit. That creates a strong individual incentive to free-ride each round. Over rounds we should expect: a few reliably prosocial players to contribute repeatedly (they value giving or are inclined to cooperate despite personal cost), some conditional cooperators to contribute early but reduce contributions after being exploited, and a majority of free-riders who rarely contribute. Messaging can encourage coordination, but with private simultaneous choices and a known end (6 rounds), cooperation is fragile and typically declines; in the final round most players will tend to defect. Payoffs will show contributors earning less than non-contributors when at least one person free-rides, which discourages cooperation unless strong social preferences or reciprocity motives sustain giving.

game_explanation:
This is a 6-round binary public goods game with costly punishment. Each round everyone has 20 coins and can either contribute 20 to a shared pot or keep all 20. Contributions are multiplied by 6.2 and split equally across the 10 players. Individually, contributing is costly (you lose 20) while giving only part of the benefit back to yourself (each 20 you contribute yields 12.4 to every player), so the private incentive is to free-ride. Punishment lets players reduce others' payoffs at some personal cost (each punishment unit costs the sender 2 coins and reduces the target by 2), which can sustain cooperation but is itself costly and can provoke retaliation.

Given the player personas, I expect a mixed start with a subset of reliably prosocial players (PARROT, SLOTH, CHICK, MOOSE, CHICK) contributing early. Many low-prosocial players (GORILLA, DUCK, DOG, DUCK-like profiles) will free-ride. Players who value cooperation will punish persistent free-riders, but punishment will be sparse because it's costly; some retaliatory punishment is plausible. Over rounds cooperation will tend to decline as contributors get demoralized and punishment becomes less effective, leaving one or two consistent contributors (PARROT especially) who keep trying to enforce norms. Chat will be limited and purposeful: short promises, reproaches, or brief reactions to punishment.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game with 10 players, 6 rounds, and a high multiplier (6.2). Each player must either keep all 20 coins or put all 20 into the group pot each round. The marginal per-capita return (MPCR) is 6.2/10 = 0.62, which is less than 1: every individual can increase their private payoff by free-riding (contributing 0) regardless of what others do, but the group as a whole is better off when more people contribute. That creates a tension: socially optimal outcome = everyone contributes; material incentive (static Nash) = everyone defects.

Which way the group actually moves depends on social preferences and learning. Some players (CHICK, PARROT, SLOTH) have strong priors toward cooperation and may contribute even when exploited; others (DOG, GORILLA, some mixed types) are likely to free-ride. Conditional cooperators will reduce contributions if they see many others defect; persistent altruists may keep contributing. Because players cannot observe choices before acting but do see round summaries, I expect an initial partial level of cooperation (a subset contribute), then a gradual decline in contributions as exploitation becomes clear — but a small core of steady contributors (especially CHICK and PARROT) will likely persist through most rounds. Chat is allowed and can encourage cooperation, but messages are sparse in typical online PGGs.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods-style interaction with a small set of players who differ in baseline pro-social tendencies. Each round every player chooses to either contribute all 20 coins to the public pot (by not withdrawing any private coins) or to withdraw all 20 (contribute 0). The pot is multiplied by 2.6 and split equally; defecting (withdrawing 20) gives a higher private payoff that round if others contribute, but cooperation increases the group return and invites positive social feedback (costly rewards). Because players do not know how many rounds remain, we should expect conditional cooperation: many players with pro-social priors will contribute early and repeatedly, and those with low pro-social priors will tend to defect. Rewarding is costly and typically sparse, used mainly to reinforce contributors. Over rounds we therefore expect:
- A majority of players (DUCK, DOG, MOOSE, and some of the mixed types) will contribute most rounds.
- PARROT (low transfer tendency) will likely defect early and may persist unless social pressure or reciprocal rewards appear.
- GORILLA and CHICKEN are mixed: they may adjust behavior in response to group patterns (sometimes cooperating).
- Rewards will be small and targeted: contributors occasionally receive 1 reward-unit (cost 2 coins) from other cooperators; defectors rarely receive rewards. Chat will be sparse and used to call out defectors or thank cooperators.

game_explanation:
This public-goods game pits private incentives against group benefits: each coin contributed returns 3.8 coins total to the group (≈0.633 to each player), so giving reduces an individual's private holding but raises everyone’s payoffs. Because contributions are private at decision time, players must form beliefs about others' likely behavior. The reward stage (cost 3 to giver, benefit 2 to recipient per unit) creates a costly way to reward cooperative behavior — not an efficient punishment device but a signaling/reciprocity mechanism: players who value fairness or reciprocity (DOG, GORILLA) will tend to give higher contributions and occasionally reward others; more self-interested or inconsistent players (PARROT, CHICKEN) will contribute little and rarely reward. Over repeated rounds without a known endpoint, stable cooperation can emerge if high contributors selectively reward one another and others respond by increasing contributions. Expect: DOG and GORILLA to be persistent high contributors and occasional rewarders; SLOTH and DUCK to contribute moderately and reward selectively; CHICKEN and PARROT to contribute little, receive few rewards, and remain targets for social messages. Chat will be sparse and used to coordinate or express thanks; rewards will be given sparingly (1–2 units) to highlight cooperators without exhausting the giver’s wallet.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game with costly, asymmetric punishment and weaker rewards. Expect a few patterns:

- Initial uncertainty and variation: some players will try high contributions (CHICKEN, PARROT, DOG), some will start low (DUCK) and some in-between (SLOTH, GORILLA). Early punishments are the main tool to discipline clear free-riding because punishment reduces a target a lot (12 coins per punishment unit) while costing the punisher only 3 coins.
- Convergence to moderate cooperation: conditional cooperators (many in this group) will respond to others' choices. If punishment/reward is used sparingly and effectively, the group tends to converge to a stable, moderate contribution (we'll show 10 coins) because the marginal return on the public pot (3.8/6 ≈ 0.633) is below 1 but above zero: contributing is socially beneficial but individually costly, so conditional enforcement helps sustain intermediate cooperation.
- Sparse use of rewards: rewards cost 3 and only give 2 to recipients (relatively weak), so they are used occasionally to reinforce good behavior or to thank reforming players, but less often than punishments for clear free-riding.
- Identification of punishers/rewarders creates reputational signals; players will occasionally send short messages to explain punishments or encourage others. Chat is sparse and purposeful.
- No one knows the total number of rounds, so we don't expect a strong endgame collapse. Over many rounds the group mostly stabilizes, with occasional violations (e.g., DUCK slipping) that trigger a few punishments, then some forgiveness in the form of small rewards once cooperation is restored.

game_explanation:
This is a 6-round public goods game with 10 players. Each round players choose whether to contribute their entire endowment (20) or nothing (0). Contributions are multiplied by 6.2 and split equally across all players, so contributors help the group but free-riders get the private benefit without immediate cost. After seeing aggregated outcomes, players can punish others at a personal cost to reduce the target's payoff; punishments are visible (targets know who punished them). The known 6-round horizon creates two main forces: (1) early rounds favor contributors who expect future reciprocity or fear punishment; (2) later rounds can see declining cooperation because punishment has less future benefit and retaliatory cycles can appear. Given the provided personas, I expect an initial mix of cooperation (several high-trust players will contribute from round 1) and free-riding (a few low-trust/low-giving players will withhold). Early light punishment by cooperative players will try to enforce contributions; some free-riders will respond by switching to contributing, while a few stubborn defectors keep free-riding and attract repeated, sparse punishments. Punishment is used sparingly (small units) because it costs the punisher; retaliation appears occasionally but becomes rarer as the end approaches. By the final round cooperation declines somewhat as the deterrent effect of punishment fades.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK </PLAYERS>

This is a 6-round public goods game with binary contributions (0 or 20) and peer punishment. Key incentives:
- Contributing (20) increases the public pot and benefits everyone; defecting (0) keeps private coins but gains a larger share of the multiplied pot, so free-riding is individually tempting.
- Punishment allows cooperators to impose costs on free-riders, but punishment itself is costly (2 coins per unit to the punisher). Punishment can sustain cooperation if used selectively early on, but it is often used sparingly because it reduces the punisher's own payoff.
- Uncertainties: players don't observe choices before contributing; they only see the revealed contributions and punishments afterward. This creates a lag: punishments are retaliatory or norm-enforcing responses to past behavior.
- Typical dynamics I expect: a reasonably high initial cooperation rate from pro-social types (MOOSE, CHICK, CHICKEN, DUCK), some early free-riders (DOG, RABBIT, maybe SLOTH or OWL), selective punishment of clear free-riders in early rounds, and then convergence toward cooperation as punishments deter defecting. In the final round, some players may defect (endgame effect) because there's no future to enforce norms; punishment will be rarer in the last round because it yields little incentive benefit.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with costly punishment and costly rewards. Expect two broad tensions: (1) individually, free-riding (contributing less) maximizes private holdings in the short run; (2) collectively, higher contributions maximize group earnings and thus each player's public return. Players with prosocial priors (DUCK, DOG, PARROT) tend to contribute more and sometimes use rewards to reinforce cooperation; self-interested or low-return priors (SLOTH, GORILLA, CHICKEN) tend to contribute less or conditionally. Punishment is costly to the punisher (3 coins per unit) and very costly to the punished (12 coins per unit), so punishments are used sparingly and mainly to signal norm enforcement (usually directed at persistent zero or very low contributors). Rewards are also used sparingly (3 cost to giver, +2 to receiver) to positively reinforce high contributions.

Early rounds will likely show high contributions from DUCK and DOG, moderate contributions from PARROT, and low contributions from SLOTH, GORILLA, and CHICKEN. Occasional punishment of persistent zeros (especially CHICKEN and SLOTH early on) will cause modest increases by those punished; conditional cooperators (PARROT, GORILLA) will inch contributions toward the observed group average. Over many rounds, a stable mix typically emerges: two strong contributors (DUCK, DOG), a few moderate contributors (PARROT, GORILLA), and one or two low contributors (SLOTH, CHICKEN). Because players do not know the game length, declines due to end-game thinking are muted; instead contributions change gradually in response to others' behavior, with sparse punishment and occasional reward to maintain cooperation.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated one-shot public goods setting with a fixed small group (7 players) and binary contribution choices each round (either contribute all 20 or withdraw all 20). Contributing (cooperating) reduces your private holding in that round but raises the shared pot, which is multiplied by 2.6 and then split equally. Defecting (keeping 20) gives a higher private payoff that round unless almost everyone else contributes. Because players do not know how many rounds remain, there is an incentive to build reputations and sustain cooperation early on: cooperating can produce higher long-run returns if it encourages reciprocal rewards from others.

The reward stage lets players send costly, directed rewards: each reward unit costs the sender 2 coins and grants the recipient 2 coins. Rewards are therefore a redistribution mechanism (they don't create efficiency gains by themselves) and are commonly used to signal approval of cooperators and to withhold payoff from defectors (by not rewarding them). Because rewards are costly, we expect them to be used sparingly — typically a small number of 1-unit gifts to acknowledge cooperation or reciprocate earlier rewards. Some players (based on their personas) are likely to cooperate consistently (e.g., DOG, SLOTH), some are likely to free-ride (e.g., DUCK, GORILLA), and a few are conditional or experimental (CHICKEN, PARROT, MOOSE), so we expect mostly stable cooperation by a majority and persistent free-riding by a minority. Chat messages will be infrequent and used for brief acknowledgements, calls for cooperation, or short complaints when someone defects.

game_explanation:
This public goods game contrasts full contribution (leave your 20 in the group) vs full withdrawal (take all 20). With 2.6 multiplication and 7 players, a single contributor helps everyone's earnings substantially, so cooperation by most players is socially efficient. However, defecting (withdrawing) gives a direct private boost in that round unless many people defect. Because players do not know how many rounds remain, they have an incentive to experiment early (to see what others do) and to enforce cooperation with sparse punishment or reward. Punishment is costly to the punisher (2 coins per unit) but hurts the target by more (3 coins per unit), so it can deter defectors at a cost. Rewards are equally costly (2 coins) but only add 2 to the target, so they are less efficient than punishment as deterrence but can encourage cooperation without escalation.

Given the personas, most players (CHICKEN, SLOTH, PARROT, MOOSE, DOG, GORILLA to a moderate extent) are predisposed toward cooperation; DUCK is the main likely free-rider early. Expect initial cooperation from most, targeted punishment of the persistent defector (DUCK) in early rounds, DUCK eventually returns to cooperating, a small number of occasional defections by others (experiments) and sparse punishment/reward responses. Chat is likely brief and aimed at signalling intentions or calling out defectors.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK, SNAKE, CROCODILE, COW </PLAYERS>

This is a 16-round public goods game with 13 players. Each round players privately choose an integer contribution between 0 and 20 from their endowment of 20 coins. Contributions are pooled, multiplied by 8.1, and the resulting public pot is split equally among all 13 players. The private marginal return from contributing one coin is 8.1/13 ≈ 0.623, which is less than 1; thus, for any individual round, free-riding (contributing 0) strictly maximizes private payoff. However, because the multiplier (8.1) is greater than 1, collective welfare is maximized when everyone contributes.

Key dynamics and uncertainties:
- Incentives favor defection every round. If players are purely self-interested and perfectly rational, contributions will be 0 in all rounds.
- Many players have behavioral priors (personas) that predict heterogeneous tendencies: some (OWL, RABBIT, SNAKE, DUCK) are more likely to contribute generously early on; others (CHICKEN, DOG, COW) tend to give very little. Mixed/conditional cooperators (PARROT, SLOTH, GORILLA, MOOSE, CHICK, CROCODILE) may start moderate and reduce contributions over time.
- Limited feedback: players do not see others' contributions, only their own net payoff each round. That makes inference about others' behavior noisy—players can infer something about total contributions from their own payoff, but it's ambiguous (a high net payoff could come from many others contributing or from their own low contribution with a sufficiently high public pot). This limited feedback typically reduces coordinated conditional cooperation and slows down the speed of learning about group behavior.
- Over repeated rounds, we therefore expect an initial phase with some cooperation from prosocial players, while persistent low contributors free-ride and reap above-average payoffs. Seeing relatively high payoffs while contributing little encourages free-riding. Because the game is finitely repeated with a known endpoint (16 rounds), contributions typically decline over time and approach zero by the final rounds; the last round especially often results in widespread defection.
- Uncertainty arises from heterogeneous priors, noisy feedback, and players' possible attempts to "test" the group by changing their contribution. Some players may sustain higher contributions longer due to intrinsic preferences (warm-glow or strong prosociality), but the general trend is a gradual decline in contributions across rounds.

Transcript below simulates the likely sequence of rounds given the player personas and the limited feedback structure. Each round block includes a brief explanation for the expected pattern, the contribution vector in the exact avatar order, and then the round summary showing each player's net payoff for that round (each player sees only their own payoff in the real game; here the transcript reports them all).

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game with 10 players, private endowments of 20 each round, integer contributions 0–20, a group multiplier of 6.6 and equal split of the multiplied pot. Individually, each coin you keep yields 1 to you now; each coin you contribute yields 0.66 back to you through the group share (6.6/10 = 0.66), so the myopic best response is to free-ride (contribute 0). Social preferences, conditional cooperation, and communication can sustain positive contributions early on, but without full, reliable feedback about others' choices contributions typically drift downward over time. Because players see only their own round payoff (but can compute the group's total from it and their own contribution), information can leak through voluntary chat messages; that can slow decline if cooperative players publicly reveal payoffs or pledge contributions. Given the provided personas, expect a mix: OWL and DUCK inclined to contribute strongly and persistently; GORILLA and CHICKEN somewhat cooperative but responsive to perceived declines; MOOSE, CHICK, and SLOTH middling-to-low and likely to drop; DOG, PARROT, and RABBIT are more likely to free-ride or give very little. Chat will be sparse and strategic (pledges, occasional payoff reveals), and contributions will trend down across the 19 rounds, with a few holdouts maintaining higher contributions.

Social preferences, conditional cooperation, and communication can sustain positive contributions early on, but without full, reliable feedback about others' choices contributions typically drift downward over time. Because players see only their own round payoff (but can compute the group's total from it and their own contribution), information can leak through voluntary chat messages; that can slow decline if cooperative players publicly reveal payoffs or pledge contributions. Given the provided personas, expect a mix: OWL and DUCK inclined to contribute strongly and persistently; GORILLA and CHICKEN somewhat cooperative but responsive to perceived declines; MOOSE, CHICK, and SLOTH middling-to-low and likely to drop; DOG, PARROT, and RABBIT are more likely to free-ride or give very little. Chat will be sparse and strategic (pledges, occasional payoff reveals), and contributions will trend down across the 19 rounds, with a few holdouts maintaining higher contributions.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game with costly punishment and anonymous contribution decisions each round. Expect tensions between players who prefer high contributions (enforcers) and those who prefer free-riding. Players like GORILLA and MOOSE have strong priors toward cooperation and will likely contribute high amounts and occasionally punish low contributors to sustain group returns. OWL and CHICKEN are the most likely to contribute little; if punished they may either increase contributions slightly or retaliate with minimal punishment (retaliation is costly and usually rare). Most other players are mixed: they will tend toward moderate contributions and respond to visible punishment signals by nudging contributions upward. Because punishments are costly to the punisher (2 coins per unit) and very damaging to the target (5 coins per unit), expect punishments to be infrequent, targeted, and small (1–2 units) early on and largely used to discipline persistent free-riders. Chat messages are likely sparse and utilitarian (requests to contribute, short defenses, or brief promises to change). Over 19 rounds, the group will likely move toward moderate-to-high cooperation, with sporadic lapses by the usual low contributors; punishments will decline as cooperation rises, with occasional retaliatory blips.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with private endowments of 20 each round. Each coin a player contributes yields only 0.66 back to themselves (6.6 total multiplier split among 10 players), so the individual monetary incentive is to free-ride (contribute 0). However, social preferences, trust history, and messaging can sustain positive contributions early on: some players are dispositionally generous or reciprocating, others are opportunistic or “slothful.” Because players do not see peers' choices and only see their own round payoff, information about others’ behavior must come through chat; that reduces coordination and speeds the unraveling of cooperation unless players repeatedly communicate and trust those messages.

Given the provided personas, I predict: an initially mixed-to-moderately cooperative phase (several high contributors: OWL, DUCK, CHICKEN, some moderate contributors like GORILLA, MOOSE, PARROT, CHICK), then a gradual decline as free-riding and uncertainty set in. Conditional cooperators (e.g., GORILLA, MOOSE) will lower contributions when they infer others are not matching their level. A few players with strong pro-social signals (OWL, DUCK) will sustain positive contributions longer, but most will converge toward low or zero contributions by the middle-to-late rounds. Chat will be sparse and used mainly to make short commitments, call out low contributions, or occasionally reveal one’s own net payoff.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game with 13 players, a private endowment of 20 coins each round, and a group return of 8.1 times total contributions split equally. The private marginal return from contributing (8.1/13 ≈ 0.623) is below 1, so each player individually loses on every coin they put in; the socially efficient move is full contribution, but the dominant private incentive is to free-ride. Punishment is available and costly (3 coins per punishment unit to the punisher; 7 coins deducted per unit from the target), and players can identify who punished them. The finite horizon (16 rounds) creates end-game incentives: punishment and cooperation tend to be stronger early/mid-game and erode toward the end. Heterogeneity in player tendencies (the provided personas) implies some players are predisposed to contribute a lot (e.g., OWL, CHICK, MOOSE, CROCODILE), some to contribute moderately (DUCK, DOG, PARROT), and some to free-ride (SLOTH, COW); others are mixed.

Predicted dynamics:
- Early rounds: many prosocial players start with high or moderate contributions; a few players free-ride (especially SLOTH and COW). Because punishment is available and some players care about fairness, we expect occasional, targeted punishments of persistent zero contributors. Punishments are likely to be small and sparse (1 unit most of the time) because punishment is costly.
- Middle rounds: contributions slowly drift down as cooperation faces the temptation to free-ride; punishments continue sporadically against persistent low contributors and sometimes against repeated mid-level undercontributors (e.g., GORILLA). Punishers are typically the high contributors (OWL, MOOSE, CHICK, CROCODILE, CHICKEN).
- Late rounds: cooperation decays further (end-game effect). Punishment becomes less frequent because its future enforcement value is reduced and punishers conserve resources; a few last punishments may appear out of spite or norm enforcement, but the final rounds have lower contributions overall.

Uncertainties: exact timing and intensity of punishments are hard to predict (they depend on players’ tolerance, whether they want to “teach” others, and risk of retaliation). Some players who generally give in static tasks (e.g., CHICKEN) might act differently in repeated strategic settings. I model punishment sparsely and targeted mainly at repeat zero contributors, with occasional checks on low-but-not-zero contributors.

game_explanation:
This is a standard repeated public-goods game with 13 players, 20 coins endowment each round, a group multiplier of 8.1 and equal split of the multiplied pot. The individual return for contributing one coin is 8.1/13 ≈ 0.623 to oneself, which is less than 1: the one-shot dominant (selfish) action is to contribute 0, but group welfare is maximized when everyone contributes. Because the game repeats 16 rounds, social preferences and conditional cooperation can sustain positive contributions early on, but the information structure matters: players do not observe others' individual contributions, and at the end of each round each player only sees their own net payoff. That payoff does allow a player to infer the aggregate group contribution (given they know their own contribution), so there is partial feedback about group behavior but no way to single out defectors.

Given the provided personas, I predict initial rounds start with many moderate-to-high contributions from prosocial/reciprocal types (SLOTH, DUCK, OWL, SNAKE) while some more self-oriented or low-offer types (GORILLA, PARROT, CHICK) contribute little. Once players infer the aggregate contribution from their payoff and see that free-riding yields higher private returns, contributions will tend to decline over rounds. Because players cannot identify specific defectors, punishment is diffuse and coordination on low contributions becomes likely: average contributions will fall across the 16 rounds, with a small core of persistent contributors (notably SLOTH, DUCK, OWL) maintaining above-average contributions while many others drift toward zero. Uncertainty remains about exact slopes — some moderate rebound or stabilization is possible if aggregate inferred contributions remain respectable, but the dominant trend expected is a decay toward low contributions by the final rounds.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game with costly, targeted punishment. Typical dynamics: many players have a moderate preference for cooperation (they contribute substantial amounts) while a few are inclined to free-ride. Because punishments are costly to impose but relatively harsh on targets (2 coins cost to punisher, 5 coins lost by the target per unit), punishment is used sparingly to discipline low contributors early on. Those punished tend to raise their contributions in subsequent rounds; as cooperators observe compliance, punishment frequency falls and overall contributions converge upward. Because players do not know how many rounds remain, end-game collapse is less likely; players continue to enforce norms intermittently. Chat is sparse and mainly used to call out or thank others.

game_explanation:
This is a 16-round repeated public-goods game with 13 players who each privately choose an integer contribution (0–20) from an endowment of 20. Contributions are multiplied by 8.1 and split equally. After seeing contributions and redistribution, players can pay to punish others: each punishment unit costs the punisher 3 coins and reduces the target's earnings by 7 coins; punishers are identified to their targets. Players know the game length in advance.

Likely dynamics:
- Early rounds: many players with cooperative priors (MOOSE, CROCODILE, COW, CHICK, RABBIT, SLOTH, CHICK) will contribute strongly; a few with low-transfer tendencies (GORILLA, SNAKE) will start low. With no prior history, punishment is sparse in round 1 but used to signal norms.
- Enforcement phase (rounds 2–6): cooperators will punish repeated low contributions, which tends to raise some defectors' contributions. Punishment remains selective and light (1–2 units) because it is costly to send.
- Mid game (rounds 7–11): contributions tend to converge to a moderately high level as norm enforcement works; occasional lapses (a sudden low contribution) prompt a few targeted punishments by high contributors.
- Endgame (rounds 12–16): strategic incentive to free-ride increases as future retaliation value declines. Some players reduce contributions; punishments may reappear but are still used sparingly because of their cost. Final round punishments may still be used to express retaliation or fairness concerns, even though they cannot change future behavior.

Throughout, expect punishments to be targeted against persistent low contributors (GORILLA, SNAKE), occasional one-off defectors (e.g., a sudden low from OWL), and mainly sent by consistent cooperators (MOOSE, CROCODILE, CHICK, SLOTH, COW, RABBIT). Punishment units will mostly be 1 or 2.

game_explanation:
This public-goods game creates a classic tension: each player gets 20 coins and choosing to contribute helps the group because the shared pot is multiplied by 8.1 and split equally across 13 players. The group-optimum is for everyone to contribute all 20, but the individual incentive is to keep coins privately while free-riding on others' contributions. Because players do not observe others' choices directly and only see their own net payoff after each round, information about the group's behavior is noisy — players must infer others' generosity from changes in their own round payoffs. Personas suggest a mix of unconditional cooperators (e.g., CHICK, SLOTH, COW, CROCODILE) and persistent free-riders or low contributors (e.g., GORILLA, OWL, SNAKE, RABBIT). I therefore predict high contributions early from cooperative types, exploitation by low contributors, gradual decay in contributions as exploitation becomes apparent from personal payoffs, intermittent attempts to sustain cooperation by some, and a final-round drop as endgame incentives push many toward zero.

This online public-goods game pits ten heterogeneous players (with different revealed social tendencies) against the usual collective-action incentives. Because punishment is available (costly to the punisher, costly-to-target), cooperative players who care about fairness or future returns may punish low contributors to sustain higher group contributions. Players do not know the total number of rounds, so there is no public endgame; that typically sustains cooperation if punishment effectively targets persistent free-riders.

Given the personas:
- RABBIT, OWL, DOG, PARROT and MOOSE are relatively pro-social or enforcement-prone; they are likely to contribute moderately-high amounts and punish obvious free-riders (but punish sparingly because punishment is costly).
- SLOTH, GORILLA, and CHICKEN have low cooperation priors and are more likely to free-ride, at least early on; they may increase contributions if punished.
- DUCK and CHICK are mixed — sometimes cooperative, sometimes testing the group norm.

Expect an emergent norm where many converge on mid-to-high contributions once a few targeted punishments discipline repeat low contributors. Punishment events are relatively rare but targeted; chat messages are sparse and usually about requests, mild reproach, or brief acknowledgements. game_explanation:
This 16-round public-goods game pits private incentives against group benefit. Each coin contributed returns 8.1/13 ≈ 0.623 to each contributor (less than 1), so the dominant individual incentive is to contribute 0 every round, while the group optimum is full contribution. Because players see contributions after each round and can punish identified individuals (cost 3 per punishment unit to the sender, -7 per unit to the target), we expect a mix of conditional cooperation and targeted punishment: some players (the "cooperators") will contribute fairly high amounts and occasionally punish clear free-riders to sustain cooperation; some players have low pro-social priors and will free-ride or retaliate if punished. Punishment is costly to send, so it tends to be used sparingly and mainly against persistent or very low contributors. Identification of punishers allows for retaliation cycles early on, but because punishment is costly and the game has a known finite horizon, cooperation tends to erode toward the end (an "endgame" effect) with fewer punishments as the value of future retaliation decreases. Expect moderate cooperation in early-to-mid rounds (sustained by a few punishers), occasional sparse punishment targeted at the smallest contributors, possible short retaliation loops, and a decline in contributions and punishment in the final rounds.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with 10 players, 20 private coins each round, and a group return of 6.6× the total contributions split equally across all players. The private incentive is to keep coins (private marginal return = 1) because each contributed coin only returns 0.66 to the contributor via the public pot (6.6/10 = 0.66). That creates a classic tension: group-optimal behavior is to contribute everything, but individual incentives push toward free riding.

Given the player personas, I expect:
- A few consistently generous types (GORILLA, RABBIT, CHICK) will begin by contributing large amounts. GORILLA and RABBIT are especially likely to contribute a lot early and persistently. CHICK tends toward cooperation as well.
- Some moderate cooperators (SLOTH, DOG, OWL, DUCK, MOOSE) may start middling and then adjust downwards if they see little cooperation; they are conditional/cooperative to varying degrees.
- Strong free-riders (PARROT, CHICKEN) will contribute little or nothing throughout.
- Conversation will be sparse and mostly about requests or short reactions (praise/disappointment). Because players do not see others' contributions before deciding and only see their own payoff at summary, there will be uncertainty about who cooperated each round. That tends to accelerate a decline in contributions across rounds as conditional cooperators respond to perceived or reported defection. In the final round (unknown to players while playing, but known to us), some players who value a final-group outcome or who are less strategic may give a small endgame boost.

Expect an initial moderate-to-high average contribution, followed by a steady decline as selfish incentives dominate, with occasional one-off recoveries or strategic persistence by a few high contributors. Chat will be sparse, with occasional coordination attempts early and short complaints later.

The incentive: contributing reduces your private holding that round but increases everyone's shared pot; because the pot is multiplied by 6.6 and split equally, group earnings rise with total contributions but each individual has an incentive to free-ride. With heterogeneous priors (some players dispositionally trusting/cooperative, others selfish), I expect an initial phase of moderate cooperation driven by the more trusting players (SLOTH, CHICK, DOG, MOOSE, RABBIT), some steady free-riding by an uncooperative player (GORILLA), and then a gradual erosion of contributions as cooperators observe inconsistent returns and respond by cutting back. Chat will be sparse and used mainly for short pledges, calls to cooperate, or occasional complaints; players can only see their own net payoff each round, so coordination is noisy and decay is likely over many rounds. A few occasional attempts to revive cooperation may occur mid-game, but without enforceable promises contributions trend down.

game_explanation:
This is a 16-round one-shot-style public goods game with private endowments 20 each round. The social return (shared pot multiplied by 8.1 then split across 13 players) exceeds zero for the group, so total contributions increase everyone's payoff; however the individual marginal return on one coin contributed is 8.1/13 ≈ 0.62 < 1, so each player has a private incentive to free-ride. Because players only see their own net payoff after each round (not others' contributions), learning about others' behavior is noisy and indirect: a player can infer something about the group's average only by combining their own contribution and the payoff they receive, but they never see peers' individual choices.

Given the provided personas, I expect a heterogeneous pattern:
- At least one consistent high contributor (COW) who values cooperation or gives high offers; she will likely keep contributing 20 every round.
- A small set of medium-to-high contributors (OWL, CHICK, DUCK, DOG, RABBIT, SNAKE) who start with moderate contributions but gradually reduce contributions as the private incentive to free-ride and ambiguous feedback pushes them down.
- Several low contributors (SLOTH, MOOSE, CROCODILE, PARROT) who give very little or nothing; PARROT, in particular, tends to give near zero.
- Mixed/more opportunistic players (GORILLA, CHICKEN) who start modestly and step down over time.

Overall dynamics: contributions begin at a moderate level, then drift downward as selfish incentives dominate and feedback is ambiguous. Some conditional cooperators will adjust up or down slightly based on their net payoff, producing small fluctuations (occasional short upticks), but overall trend is decline, with a persistent minority of high contributors (COW) and a substantial group of low contributors. In the final rounds there may be modest last-round patterning (some keep cooperating; most do not change dramatically).

game_explanation:
This public-goods game pits ten players against a standard social dilemma: each coin given to the pot returns only 0.66 coins to each player (6.6× total, split among 10), so privately it's always better to keep coins, but group payoff is maximized if everyone contributes. Players can punish after contributions at a personal cost (2 per punishment unit) that imposes a bigger cost on the target (5 per unit). Because rounds are repeated but the total number of rounds is unknown to players, we expect conditional cooperation: many players will start relatively high or medium and adjust toward peers' behavior. Those with consistently low contributions (OWL, MOOSE in the priors) are likely targets of occasional, targeted punishments by the more cooperative players (GORILLA, DUCK, PARROT). Punishments will be used sparingly because they are costly; when used, they are small (1–2 units) and aimed at persistent free-riders. Chat will be sparse and used to cajole or call out free-riding after a visible pattern emerges.

Overall dynamics expected:
- Early rounds: heterogeneous contributions; some quick punishments of clear free-riders set expectations.
- Middle rounds: conditional cooperators stabilize at moderate-to-high contributions; persistent low contributors may be repeatedly but lightly punished and sometimes respond by increasing contributions.
- Occasional deviations trigger targeted punishment; retaliatory punishment is possible but limited because retaliation is costly and identifiable.
- By later rounds, most contributors converge to mid/high contributions (around 10–18), with a few occasional low contributions continuing to be punished lightly.

game_explanation: This is a standard multi-round public goods game with costly, targeted punishment. Individually, each coin contributed returns only ~0.62 coins to the contributor (8.1/13), so the private incentive is to free-ride; the group incentive is to contribute. Because the game lasts a known 16 rounds, we expect two main forces to shape behavior: (1) a mix of intrinsic cooperators who will contribute relatively high amounts early (especially players whose profiles show high initial trust), and (2) free-riders or low-return players who contribute little. Punishment is costly (3 coins per unit for the punisher, -7 to the target per unit) and identifiable, so it will be used sparingly by those who value enforcement of cooperative norms. Early rounds tend to show higher contributions from cooperators and few punishments; once low contributions are observed, some norm-enforcers will deliver small punishments to deter free-riding, which may induce modest increases. Toward the end (endgame), contributions typically decline, and punishment can drop off because future leverage is gone (though some last-round punitive actions can appear for spite). There is uncertainty about how strongly individual players respond to punishment (some may retaliate, many will instead adjust contributions), and about heterogeneity in preferences: some avatars are likely consistent cooperators (high initial contributions), some are likely persistent free-riders, and a few are conditional cooperators who adjust with experience.

game_explanation:
This is a 16-round repeated public goods game with 13 players. Each round every player privately decides how many of their 20 coins to contribute to a common pot; the pot is multiplied by 8.1 and split evenly across all active players (13). The stage structure and private summaries mean players never see others' contributions directly, only their own round payoff — but those payoffs let them infer whether total contributions were relatively high or low.

Key incentives and likely dynamics:
- The social optimum is that everyone contributes a lot, because the pot multiplier (8.1) yields per-player returns greater than each coin contributed when many contribute (but the private marginal return for a single cooperator is low: an individual coin returns only 8.1/13 ≈ 0.623 to themselves). So free-riding is individually rational if others contribute.
- In early rounds, players with pro-social priors (high trust/dictator-type evidence: GORILLA, PARROT, CHICKEN, OWL) are more likely to contribute relatively large amounts; mixed/low types will contribute less. Given that players only observe their own payoff, early high aggregate payoffs will reinforce cooperation for conditional cooperators, but free-riders benefit immediately and have incentive to reduce contributions later.
- Over time, without explicit communication and with only private feedback, cooperation commonly decays as some players reduce contributions (learning that they can keep more private coins while still enjoying the public share). Some players (pro-socials or “dictator”-like) may sustain higher contributions than average, but their presence alone typically cannot fully prevent gradual erosion.
- There is uncertainty each round: players infer the group's total only indirectly from their payoff and their own contribution; heterogeneity in priors leads to a mix of stubborn cooperators, slow decliners, and early free-riders. Expect moderate initial cooperation followed by a gradual decline across rounds, with occasional small stabilizations from persistent contributors.

game_explanation:
This is a standard public goods game with 10 players, 20 coins each round, and contributions multiplied by 6.6 and split equally. Each coin you add increases the group's pot by 6.6 but returns only 0.66 to each player individually (6.6/10), so contributing is socially efficient (group benefit > cost) but individually costly (private marginal return < 1). Common dynamics: some players start fairly cooperative (if they expect others to contribute), while others free-ride. Because players see only their own net payoff at the end of each round, they can infer the total contribution (from their payoff and their own contribution) but not who contributed what. That fosters conditional adjustment: contributors often reduce if inferred others gave little; free-riders often keep contributing zero. Without a known end-round, we expect cooperation to erode gradually as players respond to inferred group averages, with occasional attempts (messages, a few higher contributions) to revive contributions. Chat will be sparse and used either to encourage cooperation or to announce one's own strategy; overall trend: moderate-to-high initial contributions by a few (SLOTH, DOG, OWL) then a steady decline toward low contributions by most.

game_explanation:
This public goods game pits individual incentives (keep your 20 coins) against group incentives (contribute to a pot that is multiplied by 6.6 and shared). The private marginal return per coin (0.66) is less than 1, so purely selfish players should give 0. Over many rounds, typical dynamics show a mix: some players (pro-social types) contribute above selfishly optimal amounts; others free-ride. Punishment is available and observable by target identity, so it can be used to enforce cooperation but is costly to the punisher (2 coins per unit) and punitive to the target (5 coins per unit). Because players don't know how many rounds remain, we should expect no sharp "last-round" collapse initially; instead the group may experiment with contributions and occasional punishment to establish norms. Punishment will be used sparingly — usually by more pro-social or norm-enforcing players — and often directed at persistent low contributors. Chat messages will be rare but can coordinate expectations (requests to contribute, grievances after being punished). Over 19 rounds, a plausible trajectory is: initial uncertainty with a mix of generosity and free-riding, a few early punishments that raise contributions, a period of moderate cooperation, and a gradual erosion toward lower contributions as incentives and fatigue set in; punishments re-emerge intermittently to correct big defections.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game with 13 players, 20-coin endowments each round, and a group multiplier of 8.1 shared equally. Each coin contributed returns 8.1/13 ≈ 0.623 to the contributor via the public pot, which is less than 1, so each player's private incentive is to keep coins (free-ride). Collective payoff is maximized if everyone contributes, but the private marginal payoff makes defection (contributing 0) individually dominant in a one-shot sense.

Uncertainty and information constraints matter here: players do not see others' contributions; they only observe their own net payoff at the end of each round. That makes it hard to distinguish whether a low or high net payoff came from others' behavior or from one's own contribution choice. Given the 16-round horizon and no communication, typical dynamics are: some initial cooperation from prosocial or high-trust personas, followed by gradual decline as free-riding pays off and players infer others aren't contributing much. By mid-to-late game contributions usually fall toward zero; in the final rounds, without future reputation benefits, contributions are likely to be very low or zero.

Personas guide likely play: CHICKEN, CHICK, MOOSE, COW, DUCK, and SNAKE (high trust) are predisposed to contribute early; PARROT, CHICK, and some others may give moderately; GORILLA, RABBIT, and CROCODILE are more likely to give little or zero. Because players only see their own net payoff, players who free-ride early and observe higher net payoffs (relative to cooperators) will be reinforced to continue free-riding; cooperators seeing that their personal payoff isn't especially high (marginally lower than free-riders) will reduce contributions. Expect an initial cluster of moderate-to-high contributions, then a steady decline to near-zero contributions by the middle rounds, and near-zero for the rest of the game.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game with ten players each round. Each player privately chooses an integer contribution 0–20 from their 20-coin endowment. All contributions are multiplied by 6.6 and split equally among the ten players, so each coin contributed returns 0.66 to each individual (private marginal return < 1). That structure creates a social dilemma: contributing helps the group but is individually costly; the dominant single-shot incentive is to free-ride (contribute 0). Because players do not see others' choices before deciding, they infer others' behavior only from their own round payoff and any messages. Players do not know the game length, so classic endgame unraveling is dampened, but gradual learning and conditional cooperation (or frustration) are likely across rounds.

Given the provided personas:
- OWL is highly pro-social and will consistently lead by contributing high amounts (often the full 20).
- CHICKEN, RABBIT, and DUCK are modestly cooperative and tend to contribute appreciably, especially early on or when they expect others to be cooperative.
- GORILLA and SLOTH show mixed cues: they may start with moderate contributions but reduce if they perceive others are free-riding.
- DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, and CHICK skew toward lower contributions; PARROT and DOG are likely to free-ride persistently.
- Chat will be sparse: a few promises or complaints, occasional calls for cooperation, and periodic reporting of own payoffs. Over rounds expect an initial burst of cooperation led by OWL and some peers, followed by partial decline as free-riding is observed (via individual payoffs) and some conditional cooperators pull back. Occasional attempts to revive cooperation may succeed briefly.

game_explanation:
This repeated public-goods game combines private endowments (20 coins per round), a strong multiplication of contributions (×8.1) and an opportunity to punish peers at personal cost. The dominant tension: contributing benefits the group but is costly to the individual, so individual incentives push toward free-riding while group incentives push toward cooperation. Punishment (3 coins per unit, -7 to target per unit) makes sanctioning costly but can deter low contributions and sustain higher cooperation if used selectively. Key uncertainties for each player are others' current contributions and whether they will punish low contributors — punishment is observable and targeted, so it creates reputational consequences across rounds.

Given the player personas, I predict: a core of highly pro-social players (GORILLA, PARROT, OWL, MOOSE) will mostly contribute high amounts and occasionally punish clear free-riders. Several players tend to moderate contributions (SLOTH, DUCK, RABBIT, DOG, CHICK), contributing about half to three-quarters of endowment. A few strong free-rider types (SNAKE, CROCODILE) start very low; they are likely to be the main targets of early sanctions, which should push them upward somewhat. Punishment will be used sparingly and mainly aimed at obvious low contributors; when sanctions are effective, group contributions rise and punishments fade. Toward the final rounds, some end-game decline in contributions is likely, producing occasional punitive responses but overall less sanctioning than early rounds.

game_explanation:
This is a 16-round public-goods game with costly one-to-one punishment. Each round each player chooses how much of 20 coins to contribute to a group pot; each contributed coin yields 8.1 total to the pot and is split equally across the 13 players (so each coin yields about 0.623 to each individual). Individually, keeping a coin is worth 1 to the chooser, so the dominant incentive is to free-ride; group efficiency is maximized by full contribution. Punishment can deter low contributions because a punishment unit costs 3 to the punisher and removes 7 from the punished player, but punishment is itself costly and limited by how many coins a player keeps after contribution.

Likely unfolding:
- Early rounds: many players (especially those with high cooperativeness in their personas) begin with substantial contributions, while a few low-cooperation personas contribute little. Because players see contributions after each round, cooperators sometimes use small, targeted punishments to discipline persistent low contributors. Punishment is sparse (1–2 units) because it's costly and players typically punish only clear under-contributors.
- Middle rounds: contributions can hold or slightly drift downward. Repeated light punishment keeps most players contributing moderately, but some players who offer little persistently are occasionally punished again.
- Late rounds: toward the end (final few rounds) forward-looking incentives weaken; many players reduce contributions. Punishments also become rarer (costly with little future benefit), but a few last punishments may occur in response to particularly low contributions.
- Uncertainty and heterogeneity: players do not know others' choices before they decide, so they cannot condition contributions on contemporaneous behavior—only on past revealed behavior and punishment history. The variety of personas (some consistently cooperative, some mixed, some low) yields a mixed pattern of contributions and occasional, targeted punishments.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game with 10 players, each given 20 coins per round. The group return from contributions is large in aggregate (total contributions × 6.6), but each contributor gets only 0.66 back per coin contributed, so privately it's always optimal to contribute 0. Players with prosocial tendencies (especially GORILLA) will tend to contribute a lot; more selfish or "low" types (PARROT, MOOSE) tend to give little or nothing. Because the game allows costly punishment (2 coins per punishment unit for the punisher; each unit removes 5 coins from the punished), punishment is the main tool to enforce cooperation. We should expect:

- GORILLA will consistently contribute highly and use punishment to deter persistent free-riding.
- Some medium cooperators (CHICK, CHICKEN, DOG, OWL, DUCK) will contribute modest-to-high amounts and occasionally punish blatant free-riders.
- PARROT will likely free-ride repeatedly; other players will punish PARROT sporadically to signal that free-riding is costly.
- MOOSE and RABBIT may adjust behavior after being punished—sometimes raising contributions modestly; sometimes continuing low contributions.
- Punishment will be used sparingly (few units), focused on clear low contributors (mostly PARROT, occasionally MOOSE or persistent low contributors). Chat will be sparse and mostly for signaling or short remarks.

Uncertainties: some players (SLOTH, DUCK, DOG, OWL, CHICK) may fluctuate round-to-round; retaliatory punishments are possible if someone feels targeted, but large-scale mutual punishment is unlikely because it's costly for punishers and can reduce everyone’s payoffs.

game_explanation:
This 16-round public goods game puts each of 13 players with 20 private coins per round into a classic tension: any coin a player contributes to the public pot is multiplied by 8.1 and then split equally among all players. The marginal per-capita return on a contributed coin is 8.1/13 ≈ 0.623, less than 1, so the one-shot selfish incentive is to contribute 0. However, group payoff is maximized if everyone contributes more (ideally all 20). Because players do not see others' contributions and only learn their own net payoff each round, there is limited direct social feedback; behavior will be driven by priors about others, personal payoff signals, and intrinsic cooperativeness from the personas.

Given the provided personas:
- Highly prosocial types (CHICKEN, DOG, CROCODILE, and often COW) are expected to contribute large amounts repeatedly.
- Selfish/minimizing types (SNAKE, RABBIT, CHICK to an extent) will tend to contribute very little or 0.
- Mixed/conditional types (SLOTH, GORILLA, PARROT, OWL, DUCK, MOOSE) will contribute moderate amounts early and drift downward as they infer limited reciprocity or respond to their own payoff signals.
- Because players only see their own net payoff (not others' contributions), reductions in contribution are often gradual and based on the player's sense of whether contributing improved their own payoff relative to what they would expect.

Overall expected dynamics: some stable high contributors keep the group pot elevated, which keeps private payoffs tolerable even when they contribute. Others free-ride or gradually reduce contributions over rounds. The group total slowly declines over the 16 rounds as conditional cooperators lower their contributions in the absence of clear reciprocation signals; end-game effects are muted because players can't observe others but some slight end-of-game decline appears among conditional contributors.
