game_explanation:
This public-goods game pits private incentives to keep endowment against group-level benefits from contributions. With a 6.6 multiplier and equal split, high contributions benefit everyone but are individually costly; low contributors (free riders) gain privately while reducing others' returns. Punishment is available but costly to the punisher (2 coins per unit) and harsher on targets (5 coins per unit), so it will be used sparingly as an enforcement tool: after seeing low contributions, some of the more pro-social or norm-enforcing players (the high-contribution personas) are likely to punish to deter future free-riding. Players who have historically given little (SLOTH, DUCK, GORILLA) may start low; some will respond to punishment by increasing contributions, others remain stubborn. Chat will be sparse and purposeful — short calls for cooperation, occasional calling out of defectors, and brief apologies. Over repeated rounds (unknown horizon), I expect an initial mix of high and low contributions, followed by selective punishment that raises average contributions toward a stable moderately-high level (many players near full contribution), with occasional lapses that are lightly punished.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with 10 players, 20 coins each round, and a group multiplier of 6.6 split equally. The private marginal return on each coin contributed is 6.6/10 = 0.66 — so every coin you contribute loses you 1 coin privately but gives 0.66 back, a net private loss of 0.34 per coin. Socially, full contributions maximize group payoff, but individually the dominant incentive is to free-ride (contribute 0).

Because players do not see others' choices and only observe their own net payoff each round, learning is noisy: players will base adjustments on their own payoff relative to expectations, plus any cheap-talk in chat. game_explanation:
This is a repeated, anonymous public-goods game with 13 players, 16 rounds, and private feedback: each round every player privately sees only their own net payoff (not others' contributions). The social optimum is for everyone to contribute all 20 coins (each coin returned at ~0.623 to each contributor times 13 players totals 8.1/13 per coin to each — collective multiplier benefits the group but the private marginal return is less than 1), while the individual selfish incentive is to free-ride (contribute 0) because each contributed coin reduces your private payoff in expectation.

Key likely dynamics:
- Early rounds: Without direct information about peers' exact contributions, players will rely on their prior tendencies. Those with cooperative priors (medium-high giving) will contribute moderate amounts; low-trust or selfish types will give little or nothing.
- Mid-game: Because players only see their own net payoff, they receive noisy signals about others. If a player gets a comparatively low net payoff, they might infer others are under-contributing and slightly reduce their own contributions. Overall, I expect a gradual decline in average contributions across rounds rather than abrupt collapse because feedback is limited and priors dominate behavior.
- End-game: With a known finite horizon, some players may reduce contributions toward the end (classic end-game defection), especially low-cooperation personas. Conditional cooperators with pro-social priors may still contribute modestly in the final rounds.
- Uncertainty: Because payoffs are private and aggregated effects are ambiguous, strategic conditional adjustments are muted. Expect stable individual-style patterns: cooperators moderate but steady giving, defectors at or near zero, and a few mixed/ambiguous players drifting downward over time.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game with 13 players, 16 rounds, private contributions (0–20) each round, redistribution with a strong multiplier (8.1) and pairwise costly punishment (3 coins per punishment unit to the punisher, 7 coins deducted from the target per unit). Key incentives and likely dynamics:

- Individual incentive each round: keep coins (contribute 0) vs. contribute to the public pot to raise the group payoff. The per-coin private return from contributing is negative: each coin contributed yields 8.1/13 ≈ 0.62 back to each player, so contributing is socially beneficial but individually costly.
- With repeated interaction and punishment available, cooperative contributions can be sustained because contributors can punish persistent free-riders despite the private cost of punishing.
- Punishment is costly to send (3 per unit) and powerful to inflict (7 per unit), so real participants typically punish sparingly and target clear non-cooperation.
- Early rounds often show a mix of contributions as players test each other. If low contributors persist, a few cooperative players are likely to occasionally punish to enforce norms; that tends to raise contributions of those targeted in subsequent rounds.
- Toward the end of the 16-round horizon, standard game-theoretic endgame effects make defection more attractive (less future retaliation), so contributions often drift downward in final rounds and punishment becomes less frequent.
- Because punishments are identifiable, targeted players can see who punished them and may respond in later rounds (either by increasing contributions to avoid punishment or by retaliating/punishing back, though retaliation is costly).

Each round shows a short explanation for the expectation, the contributions in the given avatar order, the punishment actions (only non-zero edges listed), and the round-summary information visible to each player (their own net payoff for the round, how much they spent on punishing others this round, and how much was deducted from them by punishment; identified punishers are listed when relevant).

game_explanation:
This is a 16-round online public goods game with 13 players. Each round everyone gets 20 coins and privately chooses an integer contribution (0–20) to a group pot. The pot is multiplied by 8.1 and split equally among the 13 active players. Because 8.1/13 ≈ 0.623 < 1, each coin you contribute yields less return to you personally than keeping it; the dominant selfish incentive is to free ride (contribute 0). Collective welfare, however, is higher when many players contribute.

Players differ by prior tendencies: some (GORILLA, CROCODILE, COW) are strongly prosocial and tend to contribute a lot; others (PARROT, OWL, DUCK, SNAKE, RABBIT) tend to give very little; the remainder are moderate or mixed. Importantly, players only see their own net payoff after each round (they do not see peers' contributions or peers' payoffs). That limited feedback makes coordinated conditional cooperation difficult: many players will stick to their prior strategy or make small adjustments based on how their private payoff compares to expectations. Over repeated rounds we therefore expect a heterogeneous pattern that drifts slowly: strong cooperators largely maintain high contributions, strong free riders keep contributing near 0, and moderate players tend to taper contributions over time as selfish incentives and ambiguous feedback push toward lower giving—especially toward the end of the 16 rounds.

The pot is multiplied by 8.1 and split equally among the 13 active players. Because 8.1/13 ≈ 0.623 < 1, each coin you contribute yields less return to you personally than keeping it; the dominant selfish incentive is to free ride (contribute 0). Collective welfare, however, is higher when many players contribute.

Players differ by prior tendencies: some (GORILLA, CROCODILE, COW) are strongly prosocial and tend to contribute a lot; others (PARROT, OWL, DUCK, SNAKE, RABBIT) tend to give very little; the remainder are moderate or mixed. Importantly, players only see their own net payoff after each round (they do not see peers' contributions or peers' payoffs). That limited feedback makes coordinated conditional cooperation difficult: many players will stick to their prior strategy or make small adjustments based on how their private payoff compares to expectations. Over repeated rounds we therefore expect a heterogeneous pattern that drifts slowly: strong cooperators largely maintain high contributions, strong free riders keep contributing near 0, and moderate players tend to taper contributions over time as selfish incentives and ambiguous feedback push toward lower giving—especially toward the end of the 16 rounds.

predicted_rounds.explanation: Round 1 is a baseline: prosocial types often start high, free-riders start low, moderates pick middling contributions. Because no prior history exists, many choose according to their disposition. With only private payoff feedback, early rounds will not reveal others' choices clearly, so initial tendencies largely persist.

Players update only from their private payoff. Those who contributed little and got high private payoffs will see little reason to increase contributions; high contributors who experienced comparatively lower private payoffs may reduce slightly. Prosocial players often remain high early on.
Sustained high private payoffs for low contributors reinforce free-riding. Some high contributors trim contributions a little, while moderately cooperative players may start modest declines.

With ambiguous feedback (each player only knows their own net), uncertainty grows. Cooperative players who observe lower private payoffs may cut back; free riders receive good returns and mostly remain at low contributions.

Midway through the early phase, some moderates shorten contributions more visibly (drop from ~11–12 down to ~10). High cooperators still contribute a lot but may shave a coin or two if their private payoff feels low relative to expectations.
Players continue to rely on private payoffs. Moderates and conditional types trim contributions further as free riding remains privately lucrative; steadfast cooperators stay relatively high.

The trend toward lower contributions continues among moderates; some small variability shows (a coin up or down) as players experiment, but the broad pattern (high contributors vs. low free-riders) holds.

As average contributions drop, private returns for contributors decline; some moderate contributors reduce further. Strong cooperators (CROCODILE, COW) still contribute maximal amounts, sustaining a modest public pot that keeps private payoffs reasonable but below early highs.

Lower group contributions are reflected in shrinking private payoffs for contributors; since each player only sees their own net, many interpretings these numbers as confirmation that contributing yields less relative benefit—so the downward drift continues.

Approaching the later stages, players anticipate endgame effects but, with only private payoff feedback, most do not sharply change strategies; the cooperative core remains, but overall giving is noticeably lower than at the start.
Small continued declines among moderates. High cooperators still contribute strongly, though their private payoffs are now distinctly lower than freeriders’, reinforcing the incentive to defect.

By now many moderate contributors have reduced further; persistent free riders keep their low contributions. The public pot is smaller, lowering everyone’s private payoffs; cooperators may persist out of disposition rather than payoff incentives.
The downward trend largely stabilizes at a lower level: most moderates have settled into smaller contributions; the most cooperative players still give sizable amounts out of disposition.

A few players reduce further heading into the final third of the game; some endgame thinking begins to appear but is muted because players never see others’ choices and thus cannot precisely punish or reward.

With the very end approaching, some players shave one or two more coins (anticipating fewer future repercussions). Core cooperators still give nontrivially, but the overall pot is smaller than early rounds.

Final round behavior often sees a last move toward self-interest. With no future rounds to reward or punish and no visible peer behavior, most participants either hold to their settled strategy or trim further; committed cooperators sometimes keep contributing (out of disposition), but average contributions are at their lowest.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated 16-round public-goods game with 13 identifiable players. Each round players decide privately how many of their 20 coins to contribute to a pot that will be multiplied by 8.1 and split evenly. After contributions are revealed, players can spend punishment points (cost 3 coins per unit) to reduce specific others’ earnings (−7 per unit to the target). Key incentives and likely dynamics:

- Contribution incentive: If everyone contributed everything, each player would gain from the multiplier. But individually, the dominant short-run incentive is to keep coins private while benefiting from others’ contributions (free-riding).
- Punishment incentive: Punishment can sustain cooperation by making free-riding costly, but punishment itself is costly to the punisher. In practice we expect occasional targeted punishments of obvious low contributors rather than mass punishment.
- Heterogeneous priors: Some players (GORILLA, PARROT, CROCODILE, SLOTH, DUCK) have strong cooperative tendencies and will tend to contribute a lot; others (CHICKEN, RABBIT, SNAKE, COW, DOG) have low cooperative tendencies and start low or remain low. MOOSE is dispositionally willing to punish (high rejection tendency) and may sanction defectors more than average.
- Adaptation and uncertainty: Players update their behavior from observed contributions and punishments. Early rounds often feature mixed contributions with a few sanctions targeted at clear defectors; if sanctioning is effective, contributions rise and stabilize. Toward the final rounds (endgame), contributions often fall because the future deterrent value of punishment decreases.
- Retaliation risk: Because players can identify who punished them, a few punished low contributors might retaliate in later rounds, but retaliation is expensive and usually limited.
- Expected overall path: modest-to-high cooperation among most cooperators early, a few free-riders targeted by sparse punishment, rising contributions through mid-game due to sanctions, then a gradual decline in later rounds as endgame effects kick in. Punishment remains relatively sparse throughout.

Stable cooperation: cooperators sustain near-maximum contributions; punishment remains unnecessary this round because most players are contributing well.
Mid-game stabilization continues. A few players still below the group average increase gradually. No one faces a glaring reason for sanctioning this round.
Most cooperative players remain at high levels. Punishment is infrequent when defectors are few and contributions are fairly uniform.
Mid-phase plateau: high contributors preserve cooperation; occasional mid-level contributors stay moderate. Players conserve coins and punishments remain rare.
As the horizon shortens, some players begin to reduce contributions (anticipating the approaching end). A small targeted punishment reminds a late defector that sanctions may still be used.

Endgame effects intensify; cooperators trim contributions somewhat. PARROT (a consistent cooperator) still occasionally punishes very low contributors to signal norms, but punishment frequency drops as players weigh costs.
Contributions decline further as the end approaches; fewer sanctions are given because punishers anticipate little future benefit from influencing behavior.
Most players reduce contributions further. A couple of mild sanctions persist from those who value norms highly, but punishment is much less common than in mid-game.
Near the end, several players free-ride more aggressively. A couple of cooperators still issue small punishments to express disapproval, but costs to punish reduce its use.

Final-round collapse is common because there is no future to influence. Most cooperators still contribute something out of disposition, but many players contribute zero; punishment is rare because it yields no future deterrence (some may still punish to express spite at personal cost).

<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK </PLAYERS>

This is a repeated public-goods game with costly punishment. Because players do not know how many rounds remain, we expect conditional cooperation to emerge: some players who are dispositionally cooperative (DUCK, CHICK, CHICKEN, MOOSE to a degree) will contribute moderately-to-high amounts early. Several players have strong low-transfer priors (GORILLA, PARROT, SLOTH, RABBIT) and will start low or free-ride. OWL has a high tendency to enforce norms (high rejection/punishment tendency) and will likely punish visible free-riders early; MOOSE may support enforcement occasionally. Punishment is costly, so punishers will be sparing and target repeated low contributors; as a result, many initially low contributors may raise their contributions a bit after being punished. Chat is sparse and typically used to threaten or justify punishment, not for long discussion. Over many rounds we expect contributions to rise from the initial mixed profile toward a moderately cooperative steady state when enforcement is present, with occasional defections and targeted punishment spikes; near the later rounds (players still unsure of horizon) there may be some drift downward if punishers cut back due to cost. Below is a round-by-round transcript (round explanations precede actions), showing contributions, any chat, punishments, and the per-player summary each round (net payoff, amount spent punishing, amount deducted by punishment, and who punished them).

# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK </PLAYERS>

This is a 10-player repeated public goods game with private endowments of 20 coins each round. Each coin put into the public pot returns 0.66 coins to each player (6.6 total divided by 10). Individually, contributing loses money (-0.34 net per coin), but group payoff increases with contributions. Because players do not see others' contributions but do see their own round payoff, they can infer aggregate cooperation only indirectly (by algebra or by comparing payoffs across rounds if they track them). Messages can be sent, so players who care about norms may try to coordinate early; free-riders may exploit that. Given the provided personas, expect a mix: a few consistently high contributors (SLOTH, CHICK, CHICK), several moderately cooperative players who may gradually lower contributions if they suspect exploitation (PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, DOG), and some low contributors or free-riders (DUCK always near 0, GORILLA trending down). Because players don't know how many rounds remain and feedback is limited, contributions will likely decline slowly over time as cooperative players respond to the incentive to keep coins or to inferred group-level drops. Chat will be sparse — occasional coordination calls early, occasional short complaints or adjustments later.

game_explanation:
This online public goods game pits ten players, each round receiving 20 private coins and simultaneously choosing an integer contribution (0–20) to a group pot. The pot is multiplied by 6.6 and split equally across all players, so each coin contributed yields 6.6/10 = 0.66 to each player. Because 0.66 < 1, the individual financial incentive is to free-ride (contribute 0) in one-shot play. However, social preferences (pro-sociality, conditional cooperation), messaging, and uncertainty about the game's horizon can sustain positive contributions early on.

Given the provided personas, I expect heterogeneous behavior:
- SLOTH and DUCK are the strongest pro-social contributors and will try to lead with high contributions and encouraging messages.
- CHICKEN, RABBIT, PARROT, and MOOSE are conditional/moderate cooperators: they contribute moderately early but reduce contributions if they infer low group contributions from their own net payoff.
- GORILLA, DOG, and OWL are inclined to contribute little or nothing; they are likely to free-ride or make occasional small contributions.
- Communication will be sparse but purposeful: a few early promises or appeals to cooperate from pro-socials, short confirmations or complaints after seeing payoffs. Because players cannot see others' contributions directly, they deduce group behavior from their own round net payoff (which reveals the group total only implicitly), and contributions tend to decay over time as free-riding becomes apparent. With an unknown horizon, cooperation can persist a little longer than in a known finite game, but I still expect a gradual decline toward low contributions by the later rounds.

game_explanation:
This repeated public-goods game pits individual incentives (each coin kept yields 1 to the contributor) against the group return from contributions (each contributed coin is multiplied by 8.1 and split among 13 players, so each contributor gets about 0.623 back per coin contributed). Because the private marginal return (1) exceeds the private share of the public benefit (~0.623), the dominant one-shot incentive is to contribute 0. Over 16 rounds, conditional cooperators (those who give more when others give more) often start by contributing a fair amount, then adjust downward if they see others free-ride. Punishment introduces a second layer: it costs 3 coins per punishment unit to the sender and reduces the target by 7 coins per unit. Punishment can deter free-riding and sustain cooperation, but it is costly to the punisher and is typically used sparingly.

Uncertainties here include how strongly each player conditions on past behavior, who decides to punish (and how much), and how persistent “hard-core” free-riders are. Based on the provided personas, we expect several reliably cooperative players (e.g., COW, SLOTH, RABBIT) to begin with relatively high contributions and to punish obvious defectors (notably CROCODILE). Some players are more mixed/moderate and will drift downward as others defect. CROCODILE’s profile strongly suggests persistent zero contributions; that player becomes the primary, frequent target of punishment. Punishment frequency will be low to moderate, concentrated on repeat low contributors; retaliation is possible but limited because punishment is costly. Over the 16 rounds we expect an initial moderate cooperation level that declines gradually as free-riding spreads, with punishment keeping contributions from collapsing entirely but not maintaining full-cooperation.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK, SNAKE, CROCODILE, COW </PLAYERS>

This is a 16-round public goods game with 13 players. Each round everyone privately chooses an integer contribution 0–20. Contributions are pooled, multiplied by 8.1, and split equally among all 13 players. The private return to the contributor from each 1-coin contribution is 8.1/13 ≈ 0.623 coins — less than 1 — so the private incentive is always to contribute 0 (free-riding), while the social optimum is to contribute the full endowment. Players receive only private feedback each round: their own net payoff (endowment minus their contribution plus their share of the multiplied pot). They do not see others' contributions individually, which leaves uncertainty about who cooperated or defected; however, observing one’s own net payoff lets a player infer something about aggregate contributions.

Given the provided personas, expect an initial bias toward positive contributions from cooperative-trustful avatars (SLOTH, GORILLA, PARROT, CHICK, COW, CHICKEN, DOG, OWL, SNAKE), moderate contributions from mixed types (DUCK, RABBIT, MOOSE), and near-zero contributions from the strongly selfish CROCODILE. Because players only see their own payoff, many who are cooperative may start high but will tend to reduce contributions over rounds once they notice free-riders enjoying higher payoffs. Parrot and some strongly prosocial types will reduce more slowly; purely selfish players will stay at or near 0. Over 16 rounds we therefore predict a decline from initially moderate-to-high average contributions toward low contributions, with a small persistent core of positive contributors (mainly parrot-like and a few other high-trust avatars).

game_explanation:
This is a finitely repeated-seeming public goods game with private endowments of 20 coins each round, contribution choices 0–20, and a public multiplier of 6.6 shared equally among 10 players. Each contributed coin yields 0.66 coins back to the contributor (6.6/10), so individual payoff-maximizing behavior is to contribute 0, but group-efficiency requires high contributions. Players do not know how many rounds there will be, and they only see their own net payoff at round summary (they cannot observe others' contributions or payoffs directly). Messages are allowed but sparse and non-binding.

Given the player personas, I expect an early-round burst of contributions driven by strongly prosocial players (DOG and CHICK, maybe GORILLA) and some conditional cooperators (OWL, CHICKEN). A minority (MOOSE, some mixed types) will free-ride from the start. Because contributors see only their own payoff and not others' contributions, inference about others' behavior is noisy; conditional cooperators will gradually reduce contributions if they feel exploited or get lower personal payoff than hoped. Over 19 rounds, the typical pattern is a moderate-to-high start followed by gradual erosion of contributions as free-riding becomes more visible through players' private payoffs and a few chat exchanges that try to coordinate but cannot fully commit. A few players (notably DOG and CHICK early on) will try to sustain cooperation longer, but by later rounds contributions will decline substantially.

game_explanation:
This is a 16-round repeated public goods game with 13 players. Each round, players decide privately how many of their 20 coins to contribute to a group pot. The pot is magnified (×8.1) and split equally among all 13 players, so contributions create a social return but individual incentives favor free-riding (each coin contributed gives 8.1/13 ≈ 0.623 to each player, less than the 1 coin cost to the contributor). Given the player personas, I expect an initial phase of moderate-to-high contributions from those with high trust/offer tendencies (GORILLA, DOG, PARROT, CROCODILE, and some others), while consistently selfish types (DUCK, RABBIT, COW) give 0 every round. Because contributions are private until after decisions and players only see their own round payoff, participants will infer others' behavior only from their own payoff trends. Typical dynamics: initial cooperation (hopes about reciprocity), followed by a gradual unwinding as cooperators detect free-riding in their private payoffs and reduce contributions. By the middle-to-late game contributions decline substantially, and in the final rounds many players defect toward 0 (endgame effect). I simulate these tendencies with gradual declines for conditional cooperators and persistent zeros for consistent free-riders.

game_explanation:
This public-goods game pits 10 players against the classic tension: each coin you keep yields 1 to you, but contributing to the public pot returns 0.66 per coin to you (6.6 total multiplier split among 10). The group optimum is full contribution, but individually the dominant strategy is to free-ride. Because players can punish after seeing contributions (at a cost to the punisher, with larger harm to the punished), punishment can support higher cooperation by deterring low contributions. Players do not know how many rounds remain, so early punishments are credible signals meant to shape behavior across future anonymous rounds.

Given the provided personas, I expect a mix: MOOSE and RABBIT are reliably high contributors and will act as enforcers; DOG and some medium types (GORILLA, PARROT) will be moderately cooperative; SLOTH, OWL, DUCK are mixed/low and may hover in the middle; CHICK and CHICKEN are persistent low contributors and will be targeted occasionally. Punishment will be used sparingly and mostly directed at the stark defectors; a few retaliatory or reactive punishments may appear early, then enforcement stabilizes cooperation among the responsive majority. Chat messages will be rare, used for mild norm-setting ("please contribute", "that's unfair") or for short complaints after punishment.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK, SNAKE, CROCODILE, COW </PLAYERS>

This 16-round public goods game puts 13 players with 20-coin endowments into a standard social dilemma: each coin you contribute is multiplied by 8.1 and divided equally among the 13 players, so each contributed coin returns ~0.623 to its contributor in expectation. Because 0.623 < 1, the strictly dominant (self-interested) action is to contribute 0 every round. However, some players have prior tendencies toward trust/giving (e.g., MOOSE, OWL, CHICK) while others lean toward lower offers or strategic exploitation (e.g., SNAKE). Players do not see peers' contributions directly; they only see their own net payoff each round, which lets them infer (noisily) how large the group pot was and update beliefs.

Likely dynamics:
- Early rounds: prosocial and high-trust players start with relatively large contributions, while more self-interested or cautious players give little or moderate amounts.
- Feedback (own net payoff only) will reveal whether the group pot was large enough to reward cooperation. Players who see high net payoffs while contributing little will be reinforced to free-ride; conditional cooperators will reduce contributions if their own net payoff implies low group cooperation or if they feel exploited.
- Over repeated rounds with complete horizon (known 16 rounds), contributions will tend to drift downward as free-riding pays off and reciprocity/experimenting information is limited. Some players (stable cooperators) will remain above zero throughout, but average contributions will decline toward 0 by the final rounds.
- Uncertainty remains throughout: since players only see their own payoff, they cannot perfectly separate others' choices from random variation or from their own contribution. This slows the collapse compared to full-information settings, but the end-game incentive still favors defection.

Transcript below simulates these tendencies: early moderate-to-high contributions by high-trust players, gradual decline as inferred exploitation increases, and low end-game contributions.

predicted_rounds.explanation: No prior round information; many players follow their baseline tendencies. High-trust players (MOOSE, OWL, CHICK) typically open with large contributions. Medium cooperators (DOG, PARROT, CROCODILE, RABBIT) give moderate amounts. Mixed/more self-interested players (SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, COW) choose modest contributions; SNAKE starts very low. With only one-shot expectations initially, cooperation is plausible but heterogeneous.

Players see their own net payoff and infer the group pot was sizeable. Free-riding is tempting: those who contributed little and received a large payoff will be reinforced to cut contributions. Conditional cooperators respond slightly downward; high-trust types remain generous but may trim a bit.

Inferred returns remain high but some players who reduced contributions in round 2 see good personal payoffs and push contributions further downward. Trustful players still give, but slightly less as they test whether others reciprocate.

By round 4, a pattern of declining contributions emerges. People who tried low contributions and kept high net payoffs stick with low offers. Conditional cooperators cut to avoid being exploited. A few remain moderately cooperative out of preference.

The downward trend accelerates. Players infer smaller group pots from lower net payoffs (or from relatively low gain per contribution) and lower their contributions to protect their private holdings. High-trust players still contribute but begin to reduce by single digits.

Many realize that free-riding is profitable in this environment. Some who were tentative cooperators drop further; a few steadfast cooperators (MOOSE, DOG) continue to give moderately. Low contributors remain at or near zero.

Mid-game inertia: the group pot is noticeably smaller now. Players who value reciprocity but see little evidence of it scale back. A small cluster persists with modest contributions; many move to 0 or near-0.

As rounds accumulate, strategic calculation strengthens. The combination of observed personal payoffs and the diminishing benefit of cooperation causes more players to contribute very little. A few players still give symbolic amounts to maintain norms or from intrinsic preference.

With continued low inferred group contributions, free-riding dominates. Some players who formerly contributed positive amounts go to 0 to avoid loss; persistent cooperators reduce but do not fully stop.

Approaching the final third, contributions are mostly low. Those who continued to give have mostly trimmed their amounts. Several players are now at 0; the group pot shrinks further, reinforcing low contributions.

Most strategic players treat cooperation as unrewarding and stay at 0. A couple of players provide small contributions either out of habit or warm-glow preferences; others keep minimal symbolic amounts or experiment with tiny contributions.
The downward drift continues. With clear evidence that cooperation is not yielding proportional personal returns, remaining cooperators reduce to token amounts and experimenters try zeros again.
Near the end-game, players increasingly treat contribution as costly and unrewarding. Some minimal contributions persist from players with stronger prosocial priors, but most contributions are zero.
Contributions are now largely symbolic where present. Several players who earlier gave modest amounts have cut to zero. The group pot is small and personal payoffs reflect little shared return.
With only one round left after this, forward-looking reciprocity is nearly irrelevant. Most players defect (0); a few keep tiny contributions out of habit or preference for giving.

Final round: the standard prediction is a continuation of defection (zero contributions) because there is no future to sustain reciprocity. A handful of intrinsically motivated players might still contribute small amounts, but average contribution is near zero.

game_explanation:
This 16-round public goods game pits individual incentives (keep coins) against a group return (each contributed coin returns ~0.623 to each player). Without enforcement, many players will free-ride because the private marginal return is below 1. However, some players here have cooperative priors (DUCK, PARROT, MOOSE, COW) and some have tendencies to punish unfairness (CROCODILE has high minimum acceptable levels; others like DUCK and PARROT are pro-social and may punish persistent free-riders). There is uncertainty about how quickly free-riding (especially from SNAKE, RABBIT, DOG) will be met with punishment and whether punishment is frequent enough to sustain high cooperation. Punishment is costly (3 per unit) but powerful (−7 to the target), so players punish sparingly—enough to signal and discipline persistent defectors but not so much as to drain their own earnings. Over rounds I expect: moderately high contributions early from pro-social types, persistent zero or near-zero from SNAKE and low amounts from a few others, occasional targeted punishments against obvious low contributors, short-lived increases in contributions by those who get punished, and a gradual decline toward the end as the shadow of future rounds weakens (last rounds see lower contributions and minimal punishment).

game_explanation:
This repeated public-goods game (16 rounds) creates tension between private incentives (keep 20 coins) and group gains (the pot is multiplied and shared). Players vary in social preferences: some (GORILLA, PARROT, DOG, MOOSE, RABBIT) are relatively cooperative and likely to contribute high amounts; others (CHICK, SNAKE, CROCODILE, DUCK, OWL) are more prone to low contributions. Because contributions are private until revealed, early rounds typically show a mix of unconditional cooperators and some free riders. Punishment is costly (3 coins per punishment unit for the punisher; −7 per unit for the target), so players punish sparingly, mainly to deter persistent free-riding or to enforce emerging norms. Identification of punishers enables targeted retaliation or restraint. Over 16 rounds I expect: (1) initial moderate-to-high contributions by cooperators with a few low contributors; (2) occasional, small punishments aimed at obvious free riders that push some conditional cooperators up; (3) a mid-game period of relatively elevated contributions as norms solidify; (4) slow erosion of cooperation toward the end as the incentive to free-ride grows, though some altruists maintain high contributions; and (5) punishment remains infrequent and small throughout (mostly single-unit actions, occasional double units).

Players can punish after seeing contributions; punishment is costly to the sender (2 per unit) but relatively harsh for the recipient (−5 per unit). Punishment is thus an enforcement tool: cooperators often pay a small cost to deter persistent free riding. Because players do not know when the game will end, conditional cooperation and reactive punishment can sustain moderate-to-high contributions for many rounds, but occasional temptation or “slacking” tends to appear and trigger selective punishment. Personas shape likely behavior: PARROT is reliably high-contributing and willing to enforce norms; CHICKEN, CHICK and DUCK are inclined to cooperate and sometimes punish; GORILLA, DOG and RABBIT are mixed; SLOTH, MOOSE and OWL are the most likely low contributors and occasional targets of punishment. Chat will be sparse and mostly functional (requests to contribute, callouts after low contributions, brief acknowledgments).

game_explanation:
This is a standard repeated public-goods game with 10 players, private endowments of 20 each round, per-coin social return 6.6 total split equally (so each contributed coin gives 0.66 to each player). Because the private marginal benefit (0.66) is less than the private cost (1), the dominant individual incentive is to contribute 0. Over repeated play, however, social motives, reciprocity, signalling, and cheap-talk can support positive contributions. Expectations and uncertainty matter: players do not see others' choices before deciding, and they only see their own net payoff after each round, so inference about group behaviour is noisy. Messages can coordinate contributions (promises, appeals, mild threats), but they are cheap and unverifiable, so their effectiveness varies by persona and observed payoffs.

Given the provided personas:
- Some players (GORILLA, OWL, DOG) have tendencies toward reciprocity and enforcement and will lean to steady positive contributions.
- Others (SLOTH, CHICK, CHICKEN, RABBIT, MOOSE, DUCK) are mixed—some start cooperative but reduce if payoffs don't justify it.
- PARROT shows low initial willingness to contribute and is likely to free-ride or contribute little.
- Over 19 rounds I predict an initial moderate cooperation level, followed by gradual erosion as free-riding and uncertainty set in, with occasional recoveries when pro-social players appeal to the group. Chat will be sparse: a few coordination attempts, a few payoff reports, and occasional expressions of frustration.

Over time, cooperation tends to rise if punishments are credible; occasional lapses (sloth, parrot-like defection) will be met by a handful of punishers. Players do not know the number of rounds, which favors stable, persistent enforcement rather than a final-round collapse.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with binary choices (contribute 20 or keep 20), a multiplier (3.7) and costly punishment (punisher pays 2 per unit, target loses 5 per unit). Incentives: individual monetary logic favors keeping the 20 (free-riding) because each contributed 20 returns only (20 * 3.7 / 6) = 12.33 to each player — less than 20 retained privately — so unilateral defection is tempting. Collective efficiency, however, is maximized when many contribute. Punishment creates a second-order incentive: cooperators can reduce the payoff advantage of free-riders by paying to impose larger losses on them. Uncertainty includes not knowing the total number of rounds, imperfect enforcement (punishment is costly), and noisy expectations about others' likely behavior.

Given the player personas:
- CHICKEN and PARROT are the most reliably pro-social; they will contribute most rounds and punish free-riders sparsely but meaningfully.
- GORILLA and DUCK are more likely to free-ride often.
- DOG is mixed and responds to targeted punishment.
- SLOTH is middling and reactive — sometimes cooperates, sometimes free-rides; less inclined to punish aggressively.

I therefore predict an initial mix (some contributors, some free-riders), some targeted punishments that mostly fall on repeat free-riders (GORILLA and DUCK) and occasional retaliation or testing. Over rounds we should see punishment used sparingly to sustain cooperation among the contributors; occasional defections lead to short punishment bursts. Chats will be rare and focused (requests, warnings, apologies, praise).

game_explanation:
This is a two-round public goods game with costly bilateral rewards. Each round, players decide how many of their 20 coins to contribute to a common pot; the pot is multiplied by 2.2 and split equally. After payoffs from the pot are calculated, players can spend 4 coins per reward unit to give 3 coins per unit to another player. Players see the revealed contributions and round summaries before the next round, so behaviour in round 1 can influence round 2 via reputation and reciprocity. Uncertainties: (1) some players (SLOTH, PARROT) are predisposed to low contributions and may free-ride; (2) cooperators (DOG, to a lesser extent GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN) may sustain high contributions, but giving rewards is costly and may reduce their contributions in the next round; (3) because rewards are costly, players tend to reward sparingly and selectively—usually to strongly cooperative players—so we should expect a few small positive reward transfers aimed at the highest contributors. Given the personas, I expect DOG to contribute fully and be a focal target for rewards; SLOTH and PARROT to free-ride; the others to contribute moderately and reward cooperators occasionally. Round 2 will show modest adjustment: cooperators largely steady, slight cutbacks from anyone who spent on rewards.

game_explanation:
This is a short (2-round) public goods game with costly, targeted rewards. The private incentive is to keep coins: each coin you contribute returns only 2.2/6 ≈ 0.367 coins to you, so pure selfish play is to contribute 0. However many players have pro-social tendencies and may contribute to increase the group's pot. Because the game lasts only two rounds, reputational effects are limited: players can reward others after Round 1 to encourage cooperation in Round 2, but there is little long-run enforcement.

Uncertainties that matter: contributions are simultaneous each round (so "cheap talk" messages can be ignored), and reward receipts are shown in players' summaries but are not attributed to specific senders (so people know they were rewarded but not by whom). Rewarding is costly (4 coins) and gives less benefit to the recipient (3 coins), so rewards are used sparingly and typically to signal approval or to reciprocate cooperation.

Using the provided personas as priors: SLOTH and PARROT have strong cooperative tendencies and are likely to contribute a lot; GORILLA tends to free-ride; DUCK is mixed/moderate; DOG is somewhat cooperative; CHICKEN tends to be cooperative but may adjust if not reciprocated. Expect contributions in Round 1 to be skewed toward higher values from SLOTH, PARROT, CHICKEN, and moderate from DOG/DUCK; rewards will be few and directed toward visible high contributors while the clear free-rider (GORILLA) is likely left unrewarded. In Round 2, contributors who feel rewarded may maintain contributions; those who perceive being ignored may lower them. Rewards remain sparse and targeted.

game_explanation:
This public-goods game sets up a tension between private temptation (withdrawing coins) and group benefit (leaving coins to be multiplied and shared). Some players (PARROT, SLOTH, DOG) have priors toward cooperation and are likely to start with higher contributions; others (GORILLA, DUCK) tend to be more self-interested and will contribute little. Early rounds often feature conditional cooperation: cooperators reward other cooperators to sustain mutually high contributions, while free-riders receive little or no reward. Because rewards are costly (2 coins per reward unit), they will be used sparingly—typically small positive incentives to good contributors rather than broad redistribution. Over time, if free-riding persists, cooperators will reduce contributions, and by the known end (round 19) many players will reduce contributions further (an endgame decline). Expect sparse chat focused on coordination, brief justifications, and occasional calls for reciprocity; rewards will mainly target visible high contributors and are used as signals early on, then decline with overall cooperation.

game_explanation:
This is a standard repeated binary public goods game with 6 players. Each round each player chooses either to contribute their entire 20-coin endowment to a group pot or to keep it. The pot is multiplied by 3.7 and shared equally among all 6 players. Contributing benefits the group (it increases the per-player payout for everyone) but is individually costly: any defector who keeps their 20 will always earn 20 coins more than a cooperator in the same round, because cooperators give up 20 and both types share the multiplied pot equally.

Key incentives and likely dynamics:
- Free-riding is individually tempting: if some others contribute, defecting yields a higher personal payoff that round.
- Conditional cooperation is common: some players will contribute if they expect others to do so; if they detect exploitation, they may punish by withholding contributions in later rounds.
- Uncertainty: players do not see others' raw contribution choices before deciding; they only see net payoffs after the round. From those payoffs players can infer how many others contributed (but not exactly who contributed).
- The group-level prediction: we often see an initial burst of cooperation (some pro-social players contribute), then a decline as defectors benefit and cooperators get frustrated. Occasional attempts to reestablish cooperation may succeed briefly if enough conditional cooperators coordinate via chat; stubborn cooperators may continue contributing despite being exploited.

Player priors (used to predict behavior):
- CHICKEN: consistently cooperative — likely to contribute most rounds.
- DOG: relatively cooperative/trusting; will often contribute but may withdraw if repeatedly exploited.
- PARROT: mixed; may imitate others and sometimes contribute, sometimes not.
- SLOTH: mixed/moderate reciprocity; may contribute initially but is inconsistent.
- DUCK: tends toward free-riding / low returns — often defects.
- GORILLA: strongly selfish — very likely to defect most or all rounds.

Given these, I predict rounds will show modest early cooperation (CHICKEN, DOG and some others), exploitation by DUCK and GORILLA, some attempts at coordination by CHICKEN (and maybe DOG/PARROT), and a gradual erosion of cooperation with occasional rebounds.

game_explanation:
This public goods game sets up a classic tension: individually, withdrawing coins is privately profitable because each contributed coin returns only 0.30 (1.8/6) to its contributor, but if everyone contributes, the group gets a much higher total payoff. Players cannot see others' current choices before deciding, so beliefs and occasional messages shape behavior. Punishment and reward are available but costly: each punishment unit costs the sender 2 and removes 3 from the target; each reward unit costs the sender 2 and gives the target +2. Because punishments and rewards are observable only in aggregate and players can't identify who targeted whom, sanctions are credible but noisy.

Given the provided personas, I expect a baseline of substantial cooperation from altruistic SLOTH and fairly steady cooperation from DOG; GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, and PARROT are more variable and will adjust up or down in response to perceived free-riding and messages. Punishment will be used sparingly and targeted at obvious low contributors to sustain cooperation; rewards will be occasional signals of appreciation. Over 19 rounds we should see early cooperation, intermittent defections (short-term drops) that are met with mild punishment and some recovery, then a modest erosion mid-game as players test others, and some partial rebounds toward the end as reputational concerns and last-round effects play out. Chat will be sparse and purposeful (reminders, apologies, short threats or thanks).

game_explanation:
This repeated public-goods game pits six anonymous players against the temptation to free-ride. Each round everyone gets 20 coins and must either contribute all (20) or nothing (0). Contributions are multiplied by 3.7 and split evenly across the six players. Because each contributed 20 yields a personal return of 20*(3.7/6) = 12.33 to the contributor (less than the 20 they gave up), unilateral contribution is costly to the individual even though mutual contribution benefits the group. With no known horizon, standard dynamics are: a few players may try to establish cooperation early (especially those with higher trust/other-regarding priors), others will exploit that by defecting, contributors receive lower individual payoffs than defectors and so tend to cut back, and cooperative behavior typically declines over time. Sparse, sincere chats can sometimes sustain contributions briefly, but repeated unilateral contributions are punished by rational self-interest. Given the provided personas, I expect SLOTH (high willingness to trust) and DOG (moderate) to try cooperating early; PARROT (very low prosocial tendencies) and DUCK (low) to mostly defect; GORILLA and CHICKEN are mixed and may experiment early then defect. Over 12 rounds cooperation will appear early but largely collapse; occasional solitary experiments to rebuild cooperation will occur but be short-lived.

game_explanation:
This is a standard two-round public goods game with post-decision costly punishment and rewards. Because each coin contributed returns only 2.2/6 ≈ 0.367 coins to each individual, the one-shot (myopic) incentive is to contribute zero. But with punishment and reward available, cooperative contributions can be sustained: players who care about group outcomes or fear being punished may contribute in round 1; others who expect no future consequences may free-ride.

Key dynamics to expect:
- Round 1: Some players with pro-social tendencies (GORILLA, DOG, SLOTH, PARROT) are likely to contribute a moderate-to-high amount; the clear free-riders (DUCK, CHICKEN) are likely to contribute little or nothing. Observing free-riding in round 1 typically provokes sparse but targeted punishment (because punishments are costly to the punisher but inflict a large loss on the target). Rewards are used sparingly to reinforce cooperators.
- Information: players only see their own round payoff and the amounts they personally spent/received on punishment/reward — they do know if they were punished, but not who punished them. That uncertainty can push punished players to change behavior in round 2 (to avoid further punishment), especially because the game ends after round 2.
- Round 2: Knowing it's the final round, some players may revert to selfish behavior, but players who were punished in round 1 often increase contributions to avoid another sanction. Punishment tends to remain sparse and targeted (typically aimed at persistent low contributors).

Below is a plausible transcript from round 1 through round 2 with sparse, purposeful chat and limited punishment/reward events.

game_explanation:
This repeated public-goods game pits individual incentives to withdraw coins (private payoff) against the group benefit of keeping coins in the pot (public payoff). The marginal return to each player of one coin contributed is 1.8/6 = 0.30 to themselves, so every player individually gains more by withdrawing a coin (keeping 1) than by contributing it (earning 0.30 back personally). That creates a standard free-rider incentive: unless players value reciprocity, fairness, or future payoff, contributions tend to fall.

However, this is a 19-round game with costly, targeted rewards available (each reward unit costs 2 coins to the sender and adds 2 coins to the recipient). Rewards are costly and neutral in aggregate (sender loses what the recipient gains) but they can sustain cooperation by rewarding high contributors and creating reputational incentives. Expect early rounds to feature some testing and modest cooperation by more pro-social players (GORILLA, CHICKEN, SLOTH), low contributions from less pro-social players (PARROT, sometimes DUCK), and sparse, targeted rewards primarily from the more cooperative players. Over time contributions will drift downward as players respond to others' behavior, with occasional pockets of higher cooperation when rewards are used strategically. Chat will be infrequent and used mainly to signal intentions or to complain/praise after outcomes.

reflection:
This repeated public-goods game pits individual incentives to withdraw coins (private payoff) against the group benefit of keeping coins in the pot (public payoff). The marginal return to each player of one coin contributed is 1.8/6 = 0.30 to themselves, so every player individually gains more by withdrawing a coin (keeping 1) than by contributing it (earning 0.30 back personally). That creates a standard free-rider incentive: unless players value reciprocity, fairness, or future payoff, contributions tend to fall.

However, this is a 19-round game with costly, targeted rewards available (each reward unit costs 2 coins to the sender and adds 2 coins to the recipient). Rewards are costly and neutral in aggregate (sender loses what the recipient gains) but they can sustain cooperation by rewarding high contributors and creating reputational incentives. Expect early rounds to feature some testing and modest cooperation by more pro-social players (GORILLA, CHICKEN, SLOTH), low contributions from less pro-social players (PARROT, sometimes DUCK), and sparse, targeted rewards primarily from the more cooperative players. Over time contributions will drift downward as players respond to others' behavior, with occasional pockets of higher cooperation when rewards are used strategically. Chat will be infrequent and used mainly to signal intentions or to complain/praise after outcomes.

game_explanation:
This public goods game pits private incentives (each coin kept privately yields 1 coin to you) against a social return (each contributed coin yields 1.8 total, or 0.3 to each of 6 players). The one-shot best response is to keep everything (contribute 0), but groups can achieve higher group payoffs if many contribute. Because the game lasts 19 rounds and participants can punish (costly to sender, harmful to target) and reward (costly to sender, beneficial to target), we expect a few typical dynamics:

- Early uncertainty: players test each other's willingness to contribute. Some will try moderate cooperation; others (especially self-oriented types) will free-ride.
- Conditional enforcement: cooperators may punish repeat free-riders or reward consistent contributors. Punishment is costly and typically used sparingly; rewards are also used sparingly to reinforce good behavior.
- Convergence: if cooperators successfully punish/reward, average contributions tend to rise to an intermediate level. If punishments are weak or inconsistent, free-riding persists.
- End-game decline: as the final rounds approach (and especially in the last round), incentives to free-ride increase because future retaliation is impossible, so contributions often fall.

Given the six personas, I predict DUCK and PARROT are relatively cooperative and likely to reward/praise cooperation; SLOTH is the most selfish and slow to cooperate; GORILLA, CHICKEN, and DOG are mixed and responsive. Punishment and reward will be sparse but targeted, primarily directed at SLOTH early on to deter free-riding and then at low contributors later; rewards will be used to reinforce high contributors. Chat will be infrequent and functional (requests, scolding, thanks).

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game with costly punishment. Each round players choose either to contribute their whole endowment (20) or keep it (0). Contributions are multiplied by 3.7 and split equally, so free-riding (giving 0) often yields a higher immediate round payout than contributing. However, because players can punish specific others (paying 2 coins to remove 5 coins from a target), contributors can deter free-riding by imposing personal costs on defectors. Players do not know how many rounds remain, which tends to sustain cooperation (no known final round to "rush" defection). Based on the provided personas:

- GORILLA and DOG are reliably cooperative, so they will almost always contribute and occasionally punish clear free-riders.
- SLOTH is somewhat cooperative but less punitive; SLOTH will usually contribute and may punish if free-riding persists.
- DUCK is conditionally cooperative — likely to respond to punishment by increasing contributions.
- CHICKEN and PARROT are the most likely to defect. They may be slow to change but should be deterred by occasional, targeted punishment.

I therefore predict an early round with a cluster of defectors and light, targeted punishment; this will shift DUCK (and then CHICKEN and eventually PARROT) toward contribution. Punishment will be sparse and targeted rather than constant; once near-universal cooperation emerges, punishment will largely stop, with occasional pulses of punishment if someone tests free-riding.

- In a short (2-round) game, players consider end-game incentives: with only one future round, some low-contributors might defect in round 1 trying to exploit others, but if punished they may raise contributions next round to avoid further losses.
- Uncertainties: players don't know who will punish or reward them, so punishment is effective mainly as a group-level enforcement signal rather than targeted retaliation.

- GORILLA and PARROT are mixed/moderate contributors and act somewhat responsively.

This is a 6-player binary public goods game: each round each player either keeps their 20 coins (contribute 0) or puts the full 20 into the public pot. The pot is multiplied by 3.7 and split equally among all six players. Socially, everyone is best off if all contribute; individually, a unilateral defection (keep 20) is typically rewarded because defectors keep private coins while still receiving a share of others' contributions.

Key likely dynamics:
- Early rounds: some players with pro-social priors (GORILLA, maybe DUCK) will try contributing to signal cooperation. Free-riders benefit in the short run because they keep 20 plus the equal share of the multiplied pot.
- Middle rounds: contributors experience lower round payoffs compared with defectors when many others defect; conditional cooperators (DUCK, CHICKEN) will reduce contributions if others don't reciprocate. Persistent cooperators (GORILLA) may continue contributing despite being exploited.
- Late rounds: because players do not know how many rounds remain, classic endgame unraveling is less certain. Still, absent coordination and with frequent defection, many will settle into defecting every round; a small number of players may continue to contribute occasionally.
- Messaging: messages can be used to propose cooperation or announce intentions, but they are cheap talk and only occasionally change behavior. Expect sparse, purposeful chat—pledges from cooperators, brief reactions from defectors, and occasional attempts to recruit others.

Predicted equilibrium path: GORILLA consistently contributes (high pro-social tendency). DUCK and CHICKEN are conditional cooperators who will contribute occasionally to test or reciprocate but mostly defect if others don't reliably join. SLOTH is lazy/mixed — mainly free-rides but may contribute once as a token. DOG and PARROT are likely consistent defectors.

game_explanation:
This is a finite, 19-round public goods game with 6 players. The marginal per-capita return (MPCR) is 1.8/6 = 0.30, so from a narrow private-payoff perspective each coin kept yields more to the individual than each coin contributed. That makes full free-riding a dominant one-shot strategy, but repeated interaction and small costly rewards can sustain cooperation early on. Expect an initial phase where relatively prosocial players (GORILLA, CHICKEN, and sometimes SLOTH/DUCK) contribute at high or moderate levels. Free-riders (DOG, PARROT) will likely contribute little. Because reward is costly (2 coins per unit), rewards will be used sparingly and typically only by cooperators to signal approval and try to sustain cooperation. Over rounds, learning and backward induction make contributions drift downward toward free-riding; sparse rewards may slow but not fully stop the decline. Chat will be sparse and mainly used early to encourage cooperation or mid-game to complain about free-riders; messages late in the game will reflect resignation or short-term tactics.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT </PLAYERS>

This public goods game pits short-term private incentives (withdrawing coins for yourself) against longer-term group returns (contributions multiplied by 1.8 then split equally). With six players, each coin contributed returns 0.3 coins to every player, so a one-coin personal contribution yields only 0.3 back to its giver but benefits the group by 1.8 total. That creates a strong incentive to withdraw (free-ride), especially early on.

Given the personas, I expect SLOTH to be reliably cooperative (high contributions, frequent small rewards), GORILLA to free-ride persistently (very low contributions), DUCK and CHICKEN to be moderate cooperators who respond to social signals, DOG to be somewhat cautious/moderate, and PARROT to be willing to punish low contributions (medium “rejection” tendencies) though punishment is costly. Because punishment and reward are anonymous and costly, players will use them sparingly: occasional punishments targeted at obvious free-riders (mostly GORILLA), and occasional 1-unit rewards to reinforce cooperators. Chat will be sparse and used to call for cooperation or to signal willingness to punish free-riders.

Over 19 rounds I expect overall contributions to stay relatively high among most players (SLOTH consistently high), GORILLA mostly low with occasional small increases after being punished, PARROT to punish GORILLA repeatedly but not excessively, and small reciprocal reward gestures between cooperators (SLOTH, DUCK, PARROT) to sustain cooperation. Punishments won’t eliminate GORILLA’s advantage completely, but will keep him from dominating too often. Rewards will be rarer than contributions, and players’ per-round net payoffs will mainly reflect private withdrawals plus the equalized share from the multiplied pot, adjusted by rare punishment/reward transfers.

game_explanation:
This repeated public-goods game with costly punishment typically settles into a pattern where some players consistently contribute (pro-social types) and others try to free-ride (more selfish types). Contributors earn less from the pot when free-riders appear, so they have an incentive to punish defectors to enforce cooperation — but punishment is costly, so it’s used sparingly and strategically. Because players do not know how many rounds there will be, we often see conditional cooperation: some players start cooperative and keep cooperating unless they are repeatedly exploited; free-riders sometimes experiment with defection but may switch to contributing after being punished. Given the personas:
- CHICKEN and PARROT are likely steady contributors.
- DUCK and GORILLA are mixed but will often contribute and may punish defectors.
- DOG is more likely to defect early but react to punishment.
- SLOTH is the most likely persistent free-rider but may occasionally contribute if punished repeatedly.
Expect a few targeted, low-intensity punishments when someone defects, a modest amount of chat (short reproaches or warnings), and overall moderate cooperation with occasional lapses across the 12 rounds.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game with binary contributions (0 or 20) each round, unknown horizon, and private decisions. The marginal per-player return from one extra contributor is 12.333... (3.7*20/6), which means each contributor loses 20 relative to a defector in the same round. That creates a strong short-run incentive to free-ride. However, some players have stronger prosocial priors (DOG, PARROT) and may contribute repeatedly to try to sustain cooperation; others (SLOTH, GORILLA) are more likely to defect. Conditional cooperators (DUCK, CHICKEN) may start by cooperating but will tend to reduce contributions if they see that contributors are being exploited (lower net payoffs). Because players only see round payoffs (not explicit contributions), they infer others' behavior from payoff differences and adjust slowly. Expect an initial burst of contributions from the prosocials and some reciprocators, followed by a gradual decline as defectors exploit cooperators; occasional attempts to restart cooperation may appear through short recruitment messages.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT </PLAYERS>

This is a repeated binary public goods game with hidden rounds and only per-round payoff feedback (players see everyone's round payoff). Contributing (20) helps the group — the pot is multiplied by 3.7 and split equally — but is costly to the contributor relative to free-riding in the same round (a contributor always ends up 20 coins worse off than a non‑contributor, because contributions are split evenly). That creates a tension: cooperating raises the group's total but free‑riding is individually attractive.

Given the six personas:
- GORILLA has a strong tendency to give and will often initiate or sustain contributions.
- PARROT and CHICKEN have mixed/medium tendencies: they may try cooperation if they see others do so, but they also respond to exploitation.
- SLOTH, DUCK, and DOG lean toward low contributions; they are more likely to free-ride early and may only join if a stable cooperating majority forms.

Expectations about dynamics:
- Early rounds: at least GORILLA (and possibly PARROT) will test cooperation. Free-riding advantages will often discourage broader uptake.
- Middle rounds: intermittent cooperation clusters form (GORILLA and PARROT, sometimes CHICKEN), occasionally attracting others to try cooperation.
- Late rounds: with enough positive history, broad cooperation can emerge, but temptation to exploit (especially in the last unknown round) causes some reversion to free‑riding. Chat is sparse and used to promise or tacitly coordinate; promises are sometimes honored, sometimes not.

Below is a simulated transcript from round 1 through round 12 following those patterns.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game where each round players choose between full contribution (20) or zero. Contributions are multiplied by 3.7 and split evenly, so individual incentives push toward free-riding (keeping 20 while still sharing returns), while group returns favor mutual contribution. Because players can punish others (at private cost) and can identify who punished them, punishment acts as a tool to enforce cooperation but is itself costly.

Given the personas:
- GORILLA is strongly prosocial and will contribute almost every round and is the most willing to punish persistent defectors.
- SLOTH is moderately prosocial and will usually contribute, occasionally punishing obvious free-riders.
- DUCK, CHICKEN, and DOG have lower propensities to cooperate; they may free-ride early or sporadically, but DUCK and DOG can switch if punished.
- PARROT is mixed and may occasionally test by defecting, then return to contribution if punished.

I expect cooperation to start mixed (some defectors), then enforcement (targeted, sparse punishments by GORILLA and sometimes SLOTH/PARROT) will lead most players to contribute in middle rounds. Punishment will be used sparingly and targeted at repeated defectors; chats will be brief — a few calls for fairness or short reactions to punishment. Because players do not know how many rounds remain, we should not see an obvious "everyone defects in the final round" pattern; rather, cooperation will be relatively stable after norm enforcement.

game_explanation:
This public goods game runs 19 rounds with six players. Each round every player withdraws some of the 20 coins for private use and the remainder is contributed to a shared pot. The total pot is multiplied by 1.8 and split evenly, so individual payoffs combine private withdrawal + equal share of the multiplied pot, minus any costs from punishing/rewarding others and minus any punishments received (which deduct 3 per punishment unit) plus any rewards received (which add 2 per reward unit).

Likely dynamics:
- Cooperative players (DOG, CHICKEN, PARROT to some degree) will contribute relatively high amounts early, trying to set a norm. Less cooperative players (SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK) start lower.
- Sparse reward/punishment will be used to enforce contributions. Rewards typically cost 2 per unit to the giver and add 2 to the receiver; punishments cost 2 per unit and deduct 3 per unit from the target. Because punishment is costly and identities of punishers are not revealed in summaries, punishment is used sparingly and typically by players most invested in maintaining cooperation (DOG and CHICKEN in these personas).
- When blatant free-riding occurs in a round, a few players will sometimes punish the free-rider to signal that defection is costly; this tends to raise the free-rider's contributions in following rounds.
- Near the end of the 19 rounds, an "endgame" effect commonly reduces contributions as future punishment/reward incentives vanish; players often lower contributions in the final few rounds and punish/reward less.

Below is a simulated transcript from Round 1 through Round 19 that follows these tendencies. For each round I give a short explanation of expected behavior, then the recorded chat, contributions (in avatar order), punishment/reward actions (tuples of (source, target, unit): positive = reward unit, negative = punishment unit), and the round summary information each player would see for themselves.

<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT </PLAYERS>

The public pot is multiplied by 3.7 and shared equally among the six players. Individually, contributing is costly (you lose 20 privately but the group's multiplier only returns ~12.33 per contributor to each player), so the dominant one-shot incentive is to free-ride. Cheap talk may revive contributions occasionally (especially if DOG and SLOTH attempt coordination), but overall trend should be decay of contribution rates toward free-riding by later rounds.
- Players do not know the total number of rounds, so late attempts to re-start cooperation can happen; expect sparse, purposeful chat used to try coordinating or to express frustration.

game_explanation:
This public-goods game pits private incentives to keep one's 20 coins against the group benefit of contributing them to a shared pot that is multiplied by 3.7 and split equally across six players. Because contributions are binary (0 or 20), the main tension is between (a) cooperating to raise everyone's return and (b) free-riding to keep the private 20 while reaping the redistributed pot. Punishment is costly to the punisher (2 coins per unit) but strongly hurts the target (-5 coins per unit), so punishment can sustain cooperation but is itself costly and may provoke retaliation.

Given the player personas:
- SLOTH, CHICKEN and (sometimes) PARROT tend toward cooperation or conditional cooperation.
- DUCK is most likely to defect (free-ride) and to retaliate if punished.
- GORILLA and DOG have mixed tendencies and may switch between cooperating and defecting depending on observed costs of punishment and others' behavior.

I predict early rounds will show a majority of contributions with DUCK repeatedly defecting. Small, targeted punishments (1 unit) by CHICKEN and PARROT will recur to deter DUCK, while DUCK will occasionally retaliate with a 1-unit punishment. Over time punishers face costs and sometimes scale back punishment, allowing some defection to spread (GORILLA and DOG may defect intermittently). Punishment remains relatively sparse (mostly 1–2 units), targeted at visible defectors; occasional escalation appears when many players coordinate to punish the same free-rider. Chat is sparse and mostly accusatory or procedural (calling out defectors, brief defenses).

game_explanation:
This is a two-round public goods game where each player has 20 coins and decides how many to contribute to a common pot. Contributions are multiplied by 2.2 and split equally. After payoffs are distributed, players may spend coins to punish or reward others (punishment: source pays 4 per unit, target loses 10 per unit; reward: source pays 4 per unit, target gains 3 per unit). Because the game is only two rounds, players weigh short-term payoff motives against reputational/reciprocal motives: early cooperation can be sustained if players expect reciprocity or if free-riders are punished; free-riding is tempting because it keeps the private holding while still getting a share of the multiplied pot. Punishment and reward are costly to the sender and somewhat blunt instruments (punishment inflicts large harm but is costly to send; reward adds only +3). Because information available at each summary is limited (players see their own punish/reward actions and what they received/deducted but not who else did what to whom), social signaling in chat and explicit admissions can shape round-2 behavior. Given the personas, I expect one clear free-rider (GORILLA) to contribute little, triggering targeted, sparse punishment by some cooperators in both rounds; consistent high cooperators (DOG, SLOTH, PARROT) will contribute relatively high amounts and may modestly reward each other, but most players will avoid heavy punishment since it's costly and the game ends soon.

game_explanation:
This repeated public-goods game (19 rounds, 6 players) creates tension between private short-run payoff from withdrawing coins and collective long-run benefit from contributing to the pot. With a 1.8 multiplier and equal split, the social return per contributed coin is 0.3 to each player (0.3*6 = 1.8), so a single coin yields 1.8 total but only 0.3 to the contributor — an individual has an incentive to free-ride. Because the horizon is finite and known (19 rounds), some end-game decline in contributions is likely. However, the presence of costly punishments (senders pay 2, targets lose 3 per unit) and rewards (senders pay 2, targets gain 2 per unit) gives players ways to sanction or encourage behavior. Punishment tends to be used sparingly because it is costly, but it can raise contributions if targeted at persistent low contributors. Rewards are also used sparingly to reinforce high contributors.

Given the provided personas:
- CHICKEN and PARROT are inclined to contribute highly and to reward or call out others.
- SLOTH, DUCK, and DUCK-like players are moderately cooperative; SLOTH tends to be steady.
- GORILLA is mixed and sometimes drifts toward lower contributions, making them a likely target for mild sanctions.
- DOG is more selfish initially and likely to be punished early; punishment may push DOG to increase contributions.

I predict an early phase of moderate-to-high contributions with a few targeted punishments of low contributors (DOG and occasionally GORILLA), some small rewards for high contributors (CHICKEN, PARROT), and intermittent brief chat messages. Over rounds, punishments produce partial compliance — contributions rise or stabilize — until a modest end-game decline as the 19th round approaches. Punishments and rewards remain sparse throughout.

# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT </PLAYERS>

This is a 2-round public goods game with 6 players. Each round everyone privately has 20 coins and chooses how many to contribute (0–20). All contributions go into a shared pot, which is multiplied by 2.2 and split equally among the six players. The individual return per coin contributed is 2.2/6 ≈ 0.367, so each coin a player contributes returns less than the coin's private cost; selfish incentives push toward contributing 0, while group welfare is maximized if everyone contributes more. After contributions are revealed, players can spend costly reward units (4 coins each) to give 3 coins to others — rewards are costly to the giver and slightly beneficial to the receiver but reduce total group wealth by 1 per unit; their main function is social signaling and reinforcement.

Given the player personas, I expect GORILLA (very pro-social) to lead with a high contribution in both rounds. PARROT tends pro-socially (high) but not as extreme, so also likely to contribute substantially. DUCK and CHICKEN are moderate-to-mixed cooperators; they may contribute some positive amount but less than the leaders. SLOTH and DOG are relatively non-cooperative (low), so expect low or zero contributions. Rewards will be sparse — players typically reward only a little to signal appreciation. In round 1, leaders may reward each other or be reciprocated by moderate cooperators. In round 2 — the final round — classic backward induction suggests lower contributions overall, but pro-social types can still contribute; rewards may continue but remain sparse.
