Each round everyone chooses how much to keep privately (withdraw) and the remainder is contributed; the group pot is multiplied by 1.8 and split equally. After seeing contributions, players can spend costly reward units (cost 2 per unit) to give 2 coins per unit to others. Because contributions are revealed before rewarding, rewards will be used to signal appreciation for high contributors and to encourage cooperation; but rewards are costly, so players will use them sparingly. Personas suggest PARROT and DUCK will be reliably high contributors and often reward cooperators; DOG and CHICKEN will be moderately cooperative and reciprocate prosocial behavior; SLOTH will contribute inconsistently at low-to-moderate levels and rarely reward; GORILLA will default to low contributions and rarely give rewards unless social pressure accumulates. Over rounds we should see cooperation rise among most players (via conditional reciprocity and rewards) while GORILLA drifts upward slowly or is socially excluded (receives few rewards). Chat is sparse—cheap talk early, thanks or short calls for cooperation later. Rewards are few, focused on consistently high contributors.

reflection:

Each round everyone chooses how much to keep privately (withdraw) and the remainder is contributed; the group pot is multiplied by 1.8 and split equally. After seeing contributions, players can spend costly reward units (cost 2 per unit) to give 2 coins per unit to others. Because contributions are revealed before rewarding, rewards will be used to signal appreciation for high contributors and to encourage cooperation; but rewards are costly, so players will use them sparingly. Personas suggest PARROT and DUCK will be reliably high contributors and often reward cooperators; DOG and CHICKEN will be moderately cooperative and reciprocate prosocial behavior; SLOTH will contribute inconsistently at low-to-moderate levels and rarely reward; GORILLA will default to low contributions and rarely give rewards unless social pressure accumulates. Over rounds we should see cooperation rise among most players (via conditional reciprocity and rewards) while GORILLA drifts upward slowly or is socially excluded (receives few rewards). Chat is sparse—cheap talk early, thanks or short calls for cooperation later. Rewards are few, focused on consistently high contributors.

game_explanation:
This public-goods game pits individual temptation to withdraw private coins against group-level gains from contributions. With a 1.8 multiplier spread equally across six players, each extra coin contributed returns 1.8/6 = 0.3 coins to each player (including the contributor). So an individual loses 1 coin privately but only gains 0.3 back per coin contributed — private incentive is to withdraw (free-ride), while group benefit is higher if many contribute. The reward stage (2 coins cost to the sender per unit, 2 coins given to the target per unit) allows players to reward cooperators at personal cost; rewards are visible during the round, but the summary does not identify who sent rewards to a particular recipient. Because contributions and rewards are observable during the round, we expect conditional cooperators to try to sustain cooperation by rewarding high contributors. Some players (CHICKEN, PARROT, DOG) have priors suggesting higher baseline contributions; others (SLOTH, DUCK, GORILLA) are more mixed. Rewards will be sparse and targeted to reinforce cooperative behavior. Over the 19 rounds we expect:
- Early rounds: relatively high contributions from CHICKEN, PARROT, and DOG; mixed for GORILLA and DUCK; low for SLOTH. Small, targeted rewards will reinforce the high contributors.
- Middle rounds: modest stability among cooperators; occasional attempts to reward borderline contributors. A few rounds with no rewards are likely.
- Endgame (last few rounds): some decline in contributions as backward induction/free-riding pressures grow; rewards taper off. CHICKEN and PARROT may stay relatively high longer, but overall cooperation drifts down.

game_explanation:
This is a two-round public goods game with costly rewards. In each round players privately choose how much of 20 coins to contribute to a common pot; the pot is multiplied by 2.2 and split evenly. After seeing contributions and redistribution, players can spend coins to reward others (each reward unit costs the sender 4 coins and gives the recipient 3 coins). Players can also send chat messages at any time.

Likely overall dynamics:
- Many players in these personas are predisposed to contribute (GORILLA, DOG, PARROT, DUCK tend toward generous initial offers), so the first round often shows above-average contributions from several players, mixed with a few low or cautious contributions (SLOTH and CHICKEN are more likely to be modest or low).
- Because rewards are costly, only a few targeted rewards are expected: players who perceive others as clearly cooperative are likely to give 1 unit to reward them; widespread rewarding is rare.
- With only two rounds, there is an end-game incentive to reduce contributions in round 2 (some decline in aggregate contribution). However, players who received rewards or are strongly cooperative may keep contributing fairly high; reciprocation with a small reward in round 2 is plausible between a few reciprocal pairs (e.g., DOG and GORILLA).
- Chat is usually sparse and used either to signal intent or to thank/encourage.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT </PLAYERS>

This 2-round public goods game balances private incentives to free-ride (keep the 20 coins) against group incentives to contribute (everyone benefits from the multiplier 2.2). Because the multiplier per-person (2.2 * total / n) yields a per-person return of 2.2*(your marginal coin)/6 ≈ 0.367 per coin contributed, any individual loses on each coin they contribute in isolation (private cost 1 vs private return ≈0.37). That creates a strong raw incentive to contribute little or nothing.

Against that, players can use rewards and punishments. Punishment is costly to the punisher (4 coins per unit) but imposes a larger hit on the target (-10 per unit), so it can deter free-riding. Reward is also costly (4 per unit) and gives a smaller boost to the target (+3 per unit), so it’s used sparingly to encourage cooperation or reciprocate. Because punishment and reward actions are anonymous in the summary, signaling and reputation-building are imperfect: people learn only their own round outcome and how much they spent/received, not who did what.

With only two rounds, backward induction encourages low contributions in round 2 (no future to condition on). But round 1 can be used to signal intentions, to punish free-riders, or to reward cooperators (which may change behavior in round 2). Persona priors influence likely choices: GORILLA and PARROT are relatively pro-social and likely to contribute high; SLOTH is strongly free-riding-prone and likely to contribute little; DUCK and DOG are conditional/cooperative types and may punish free-riders; CHICKEN tends to contribute little. Expect moderate use of reward/punishment — enough to influence behavior but sparse overall.

game_explanation:
This repeated public-goods game pits a mix of likely cooperators and freeriders against one another with a modest multiplier and a costly punishment system. Expect a small core of contributors (DUCK, PARROT and often GORILLA) to contribute regularly, while SLOTH tends to free-ride persistently. DOG and CHICKEN are more variable: they may free-ride early but can be nudged to cooperate after punishment. Punishment will be used sparingly and primarily by cooperators to discipline repeat free-riders; punishers pay a cost (2 coins per unit) so punishments are usually small (1–2 units). Chats are rare and purposeful — short calls to cooperate or short complaints after exploitation. Over time the group will converge toward more cooperation, but SLOTH will occasionally revert to defection, prompting targeted, limited punishment rather than wholesale retaliation.

- CHICKEN and PARROT (high prosocial priors) will contribute a lot most rounds and reward each other modestly — they act as consistent cooperators and mild enforcers of cooperation.

- GORILLA and DOG are more self-interested / low contributors; they contribute little or zero and rarely (if ever) reward others.

game_explanation:
This is a 2-round public goods game with 6 players (SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT). Each round everyone privately chooses how many of their 20 coins to contribute to a common pot. The pot is multiplied by 2.2 and split equally, so each coin contributed yields 2.2/6 ≈ 0.3667 coins back to each contributor personally. That means contributions help the group (social return > 1 per coin) but are individually costly (private return < 1), so there is a strong incentive to free-ride. The reward stage allows players to spend 4 coins to give 3 coins to someone else; rewards are costly for the group (net social loss of 1 coin per reward unit) but can be used to sustain cooperation if players reward cooperators. Because rewards are anonymous to recipients (they don't know who rewarded them in the summary stage), targeted reciprocity is weakened. Also, players do not see others' current-round choices before contributing, but contributions are revealed before rewarding.

Given the provided personas:
- DUCK is strongly pro-social and likely to contribute a lot both rounds.
- DOG and CHICKEN are moderately pro-social and may contribute moderate amounts.
- SLOTH, GORILLA, PARROT have low prosocial signals and are likely to free-ride or contribute little.
- Rewarding will be sparse: cooperative players (DUCK, DOG, CHICKEN) may reward other cooperators a little; free-riders rarely reward.

Expectations across two rounds:
- Round 1: DUCK contributes near-maximum; DOG and CHICKEN moderate; SLOTH/GORILLA/PARROT low. After seeing revealed contributions and redistribution, a few cooperators will give 1-unit rewards to acknowledge others (not many edges).
- Round 2: Because players cannot see others' past contributions in their summary, but observe their own payoff and reward amounts, free-riders who benefited will likely continue low contributions. DUCK will likely keep contributing high; moderate cooperators may hold or slightly reduce contributions. Rewarding remains sparse and largely reciprocal among the pro-social players.

This is a repeated public-goods game with 19 known rounds. With 6 players, contributions are multiplied by 1.8 and split equally, so cooperation benefits everyone but free-riding (contributing little and keeping the private withdrawal) can pay off immediately. Players with cooperative tendencies (GORILLA, DUCK, DOG, CHICKEN) are likely to contribute fairly high amounts; PARROT is likely to free-ride (contribute 0) throughout. SLOTH has a mixed profile: initially selfish but may respond to social pressure or rewards by increasing contribution slowly.

Because punishment and reward are costly to send, expect sparse use: occasional punishments targeted at persistent free-riders (mainly PARROT) and occasional small rewards to reinforce high contributors. Punishments will be small (1–2 units) because each punishment unit costs the sender 2 coins and removes 3 coins from the target. Rewards are likewise small (1 unit) and cheaper in net effect for the target (+2 to target, -2 to sender). Chats will be infrequent but purposeful — short appeals for cooperation or short reproaches toward obvious free-riders.

game_explanation:
This is a 2-round public goods game with 6 players. Each round everyone has 20 coins and chooses an integer contribution (0–20). Contributions are pooled, multiplied by 2.2, and split equally. After payoffs are revealed, players can spend coins to punish (cost 4 to source, -10 to target per unit) or reward (cost 4 to source, +3 to target per unit). Players also may send chat messages. The final round is round 2, so backward induction tends to reduce cooperation in round 2; however round 1 often shows more cooperation because players expect some reciprocity or retaliation via punishment/reward. Given the provided personas, we expect:
- CHICKEN: high contributor in round 1 (and fairly cooperative in round 2).
- GORILLA: relatively high contribution in round 1, moderate in round 2.
- DOG, PARROT: moderate contributions.
- DUCK and SLOTH: low contributors in round 1; SLOTH may respond to being punished by increasing slightly in round 2.
Punishments and rewards are used sparingly: likely small punishments aimed at clear free-riders in round 1 and a few targeted rewards to encourage cooperators. In round 2, with no future, punishment is less likely; a couple of final rewards to friendly cooperators are plausible.

game_explanation:
This public goods game gives each player 20 coins each round and forces a binary choice: contribute all 20 to the group pot or keep all 20 privately. The pot is multiplied by 3.7 and split equally among the six players. Because each coin contributed returns only 3.7/6 ≈ 0.6167 coins to the contributor herself, contributing is individually costly (expected private return < 1 coin). The dominant short-run incentive is to free-ride (contribute 0) while others contribute. However, if enough players contribute, everyone benefits collectively; cooperation can arise from reciprocity, signalling (cheap talk), or uncertainty about how long the game lasts.

Given the player personas, most individuals show low-to-mixed tendencies to give or reciprocate in comparable tasks. That profile predicts a modest chance of some early-round contributions (testing or hopeful cooperation) followed by a rapid decline toward free-riding once players observe that defectors earned more. Because players do not know the number of rounds, some participants might try intermittent cooperation to test for reciprocity, but the overall steady-state is likely low contributions.

Below is a simulated transcript of rounds 1–12. Chats are sparse and purposeful (announcements, tests, reactions). Contribution lists always follow the player order shown at game start: SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT.

game_explanation:
This 6-player public goods game sets up a tension between individual incentive to free-ride (keep 20) and group benefit from contributing (send 20 so pot grows). Players CHICKEN and DOG have very high tendencies to cooperate; DUCK and GORILLA are moderately cooperative/conditional; SLOTH and PARROT are strong free-rider priors. Because players do not know the horizon, conditional punishments can sustain cooperation: contributors may spend small amounts to punish repeated defectors, lowering defectors' net gains over time. Punishment is costly to the punisher (2 coins per unit) and much costlier to the target (5 coins per unit), so punishments will be used sparingly and typically only by repeat cooperators to signal enforcement. Chats will be sparse and used to request or complain about cooperation/punishment. Over 12 rounds I expect CHICKEN and DOG to contribute consistently, DUCK to contribute most rounds but defect occasionally, GORILLA to test defect a couple times, and SLOTH and PARROT to largely free-ride; cooperators will mainly punish SLOTH, PARROT and occasional defecting GORILLA/DUCK, keeping punishment small (1–3 units total) and targeted.

game_explanation:
This is a two-round public goods game with 6 players. Each round players privately decide how many of 20 coins to contribute to a common pot; the pot is multiplied by 2.2 and split evenly, so contributions create a group benefit but each coin contributed yields less back to the contributor than it costs (individually costly but socially beneficial). Because the game lasts two rounds, players will balance short-run self-interest against reputation and conditional responses: contributors who expect to be rewarded or feared (to avoid punishment) may give more in round 1, while free-riders risk being punished. Punishment is very effective on targets (−10 coins per punishment unit) but costly to the sender (4 coins per unit), so punishments are usually used sparingly and targeted at clear free-riders. Rewards are cheaper to targets (+3 per unit) but still cost senders 4 coins, so they too are used selectively to reinforce cooperation.

Given the player personas, we expect a mixed-but-moderate cooperation level in round 1 (some moderate contributors, one near-zero free-rider). Those who value fairness and retaliation (e.g., CHICKEN, DOG) are likeliest to punish a zero contributor; more prosocial players (PARROT, DOG) are likeliest to reward high contributors. After round 1, low contributors who were punished may increase contributions in round 2 to avoid further penalties; cooperators may maintain or slightly increase contributions. Overall: modest initial cooperation, targeted punishment of the clearest free-rider, a few small rewards to high contributors, and adjustment in round 2.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT </PLAYERS>

This is a 19-round public goods game where each player starts each round with 20 coins in the shared pot, decides how many coins to withdraw for private use (so contribution = 20 - withdrawal), and then the sum of contributions is multiplied by 1.8 and split equally among the six players. After seeing the redistributed payoffs, players can spend coins to reward others: each reward unit costs 2 coins to the sender and gives 2 coins to the recipient. Players can chat at any time, but messages are public and sparse; reward sources are anonymous in summary info, so enforcement is noisy.

Likely dynamics:
- Early rounds: players with cooperative priors (GORILLA, DOG, PARROT, DUCK) will contribute relatively high amounts, while a more opportunistic or low-return profile (CHICKEN) may contribute less. SLOTH tends toward moderate-to-low active engagement and may drift to free-riding if rewards are costly.
- Middle rounds: cooperators use sparse rewards to reinforce contributors (targeted 1-unit rewards), trying to sustain moderate cooperation; defectors enjoy high private payoffs and may not be punished sufficiently to change behavior. Chat may contain short appeals to contribute.
- Late rounds: anticipation of the finite horizon (last rounds) tends to reduce contributions — players shift toward withdrawal to secure private gains. Rewards decline as players conserve coins and backward induction reduces cooperation.
- Uncertainties: reward anonymity makes it hard to coordinate enforcement; small reward sizes blunt incentives; players vary in responsiveness to social cues, so some gradual adjustment is expected rather than sharp collapse until the final rounds.

The transcript below simulates these tendencies across 19 rounds: contributions in the avatar order SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT; sparse chat; sparse 1-unit reward gestures concentrated early-to-mid game in support of contributors. All contributions are integers 0–20 and reward units are positive integers.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT </PLAYERS>

This is a standard repeated public-goods game with binary contributions (0 or 20). The social optimum is for everyone to contribute (each would get 74 when all contribute), but each individual's private incentive is to free-ride because a 20-coin contribution only returns about 12.33 coins to its contributor when shared among six players (20 * 3.7 / 6 ≈ 12.33). That makes contribution individually costly each round. Because players do not know how many rounds remain, we expect an initial period where prosocial players (GORILLA, CHICKEN) try to contribute or encourage others, while more selfish or opportunistic players (PARROT, DUCK, SLOTH, DOG are mixed) tend to free-ride. Over time — especially if contributors see they receive lower net payoffs than free-riders — contributions will trend down toward zero. Cheap-talk messages may briefly coordinate contributions, producing occasional isolated contributions or short-lived resumptions, but the prevailing dynamic will be decay in cooperation. Summaries showing only round payoffs (not intentions) will reinforce the payoff comparison that favors free-riding, so we predict several early contributions followed by falling contributions and occasional one-off attempts to restart cooperation.

game_explanation:
This public goods game pits six players (SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT) against the incentives to free-ride versus the social benefits of contributing. Each round everyone privately chooses 0 or 20; the pot is multiplied by 3.7 and split equally, so cooperation increases everyone's payoffs but free-riders get a short-term advantage. Punishment allows contributors to sanction free-riders (each punishment unit costs the sender 2 coins and subtracts 5 coins from the target), which can enforce cooperation but is itself costly and may provoke retaliation. Given the players' personas, I expect a mix of low baseline cooperation (several players tend to free-ride) but a small group (CHICKEN and DUCK) will try to sustain contributions and occasionally punish non-contributors. Punishment will be used sparingly at first to deter persistent defectors (notably SLOTH and GORILLA), sometimes provoking retaliatory punishments. Over the 12 rounds we should see bouts of full cooperation after enforcement, occasional defections and targeted punishments to restore norms, and a bit more retaliation and variability near the end as players react to past punishments.

game_explanation:
This 2-round public goods game presents a classic cooperation vs. free-riding dilemma with an added reward stage. Each round players decide how much of their private 20 coins to contribute; the group pot is multiplied by 2.2 and split equally. Because the multiplier is less than the group size (2.2 < 6), individual incentives favor contributing 0, but group payoff increases with total contributions. The reward stage is costly for senders (4 coins per reward unit) and benefits recipients (3 coins per unit) — rewards redistribute but reduce total surplus (net -1 per unit). Still, because rewards let players signal or reciprocate—especially across the two rounds—cooperators (high-contribution personas: DOG, CHICKEN, PARROT) are likely to contribute more and to use small rewards to recognize others. Low-contribution personas (SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK) tend to contribute little; seeing rewards directed at cooperators may nudge them slightly in round 2, but only modestly because rewards are visible as transfers but costly and cannot be tied to identities in the summary. game_explanation:
This PGG puts players in a repeated setting with uncertainty: each round everyone privately decides how many of their 20 coins to keep (withdraw) and how many to contribute to the shared pot; contributions are not visible to others. The shared pot is multiplied by 1.8 and split equally, so there is a public-good benefit to contributing but a private incentive to withdraw coins. After payoffs are computed, players can spend their round earnings to send reward units (each unit costs 2 coins to the sender and gives 2 coins to the recipient). Messages can be posted, but they are cheap signals and also do not reveal contributions.

Because players cannot directly observe others' contributions, conditional cooperation based on observed contributions is weak; instead, behavior will be driven by prior social preferences, occasional signaling via chat, and sparse use of rewards to promote cooperation or to thank perceived cooperators. Personas shape priors: DUCK and DOG are the most likely to contribute a lot; SLOTH and PARROT are middling cooperators; GORILLA and CHICKEN lean toward lower contributions. DOG and DUCK (pro-social types) are the likeliest to use rewards to reinforce cooperation; GORILLA and CHICKEN rarely reward. Overall I expect relatively stable contribution levels across rounds (since players cannot verify others' choices), with occasional small reward flows concentrated among DUCK, DOG, SLOTH, and PARROT.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game with 19 rounds. Each round players trade off private withdrawal (keeps coins) versus contributing to a shared pot that is multiplied by 1.8 and split evenly. The social dilemma: any one player can increase their private payoff by withdrawing more (contributing less), because the private return from contributing one coin is only 0.3 to themselves (1.8/6), so the individual incentive is to free-ride. However, group payoff is maximized when everyone contributes more. The punishment and reward stage lets players impose costs on low contributors (punishment: costs 2 per unit for sender, deducts 3 per unit from target) or incentivize high contributors (reward: costs 2 per unit for sender, adds 2 per unit to target). Because punishment is effective but costly, expect it to be used sparingly, targeted at persistent low contributors. Rewards are cheap relative to their signaling value and will be used occasionally to reinforce consistently high contributors (notably PARROT, who by persona is likely to contribute fully). Over repeated rounds players may adjust: some (PARROT) will cooperate consistently; some (SLOTH, GORILLA) may lean toward lower contributions and sometimes get punished; DUCK (punitive persona) is likely to punish free riders occasionally; CHICKEN and DOG will be moderate cooperators who drift down over time. Overall we expect moderate contributions early, occasional punishment targeted at clear free riders, a rough stabilization of contributions in the middle rounds, and a gradual decline toward the end as the threat of future punishment weakens — with sparse chat used to call out behavior or to signal intent.

# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT </PLAYERS>

This is a two-round public goods game with costly punishment and rewards. Players start each round with 20 coins and secretly choose how much to contribute (0–20). Contributions are multiplied by 2.2 and split equally. After contributions are revealed, players can punish (source pays 4, target loses 10) or reward (source pays 4, target gains 3). Players see only their own round summary (their net payoff and their own punishment/reward activity), so they face uncertainty about who acted against them. Because the game lasts only two rounds, incentives are mixed: some players who are dispositionally cooperative (GORILLA, PARROT) are likely to contribute a lot from the start and to use the sanctioning tools sparingly to enforce cooperation. Players with low prosocial priors (SLOTH, DUCK) may start with low contributions and risk being punished; after seeing personal losses they may increase contribution in round 2 to avoid further sanctions, but some may still free-ride because the horizon is short. Overall, expect: a few high contributors (GORILLA, PARROT), some medium contributors (DOG, CHICKEN), and a couple low starters (SLOTH, DUCK). Punishment/reward use will be sparse and targeted—mainly cooperators rewarding other cooperators and punishing clear free-riders.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game with 6 players and 19 rounds. Each player starts each round with 20 coins: they withdraw some privately and contribute the rest to the public pot. The pot is multiplied by 1.8 and split equally. After contributions are revealed, players can pay to punish (cost 2 per unit; removes 3 per unit from the target) or reward (cost 2 per unit; gives 2 per unit to the target). Because punishments and rewards are costly to send and anonymous in reception, players will balance enforcing cooperation with the direct cost of sanctioning. Expect these broad dynamics:

- Early rounds: conditional cooperators (SLOTH, DUCK, PARROT, GORILLA to a degree) will contribute moderately; clear low contributors (DOG, sometimes CHICKEN) invite selective punishment. Rewards may be used sparingly to encourage contributors.
- Middle rounds: contributions drift down as free-riding pays off and the cost of continual sanctioning accumulates; sanctions become sparser because they are costly.
- Endgame (final rounds): standard "endgame" decline — people stop investing in costly enforcement; contributions fall sharply toward selfish withdrawal.
- Chat will be sparse and functional (calls for cooperation, brief acknowledgements). Punishments and rewards will also be sparse and mostly targeted at repeated low contributors early on.

Positive unit = reward; negative unit = punishment. Only non-zero action edges are listed.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT </PLAYERS>

This is a repeated public goods game where each player has a one-shot incentive to free ride (keep their 20) while everyone else contributes 20. If everyone contributes, the group payoff is maximized and contributors earn substantially more than the endowment; if some defect, contributors earn less than defectors that round. Because players do not know how many rounds remain, there is no fixed "last round" for coordinated end-game defection; that uncertainty typically sustains some cooperation early on but cooperation commonly declines over time as players respond to observed payoffs and the temptation to free ride. Chat can help sustain cooperation if players credibly coordinate, but messages are cheap (non-binding) so some players will still defect even when promising to contribute.

Using the provided personas: DUCK, CHICKEN, and DOG are the most likely to try cooperating early and to send encouraging messages. SLOTH is moderately cooperative but responsive to peers' behavior. GORILLA and PARROT are more mixed/more likely to free ride. I therefore predict initial partial cooperation (several contribute, a couple free ride), then gradual unraveling as cooperators see that defectors get higher round payoffs. Chat will be sparse: a few appeals early, a couple of complaints as cooperation collapses, then quiet as everyone settles into free riding.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT </PLAYERS>

This is a two-round public goods game with a subsequent voluntary positive-reward stage. The key tensions:
- Contributing to the public pot sacrifices private endowment (20 coins) but creates a multiplied group benefit (sum multiplied by 2.2 and split equally), which incentivizes conditional cooperation: contributors can earn more if others also contribute.
- Free-riding (contributing 0) is individually attractive if others contribute; because the multiplier is shared across all players, defectors can get a good return without paying the cost.
- Rewards are costly to give (4 coins per reward unit) and give only 3 coins to the recipient, so rewarding is an altruistic/strategic gesture rather than a wealth-increasing move. Players typically reward cooperators and sometimes reciprocate rewards; rewards are likely sparse.
- Messaging can be used to signal intent (cheap talk), but messages are non-binding. With only two rounds, there is some end-game incentive to exploit cooperators in round 2, but observed personas make some players more likely to keep cooperating or to free-ride.
- Uncertainty: players cannot identify who rewarded them in their private summary, which reduces direct attribution of praise or punishment, but the public reward edges exist in the transcript (here we simulate them).

Given the player priors:
- SLOTH and DUCK are likely to contribute little or nothing (free-riders).
- GORILLA, CHICKEN, and PARROT lean toward moderate contributions and are more likely to reward cooperators.
- DOG is mixed/moderate but somewhat less consistently pro-social.

I therefore predict a first round with a mix of modest cooperation from GORILLA/CHICKEN/PARROT and free-riding by SLOTH/DUCK; a few small mutual rewards will follow (1-unit rewards are common). In round 2, cooperators who were rewarded will mostly continue contributing at similar levels; free-riders will likely continue to give little. Rewards will reinforce cooperation but remain sparse.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game with costly punishment and binary contribution choices (0 or 20). The group multiplier (3.7) means that group payoff is maximized when everyone contributes, but any individual can gain in the short run by defecting (keeping 20 while still receiving a share of the pot). Because players can punish (at private cost, producing larger damage to the target), punishment serves as a way to enforce cooperation: after early free-riding is detected and punished, most players tend to move toward higher cooperation to avoid sanctions. Messages are possible but typically sparse; punishment is costly so players usually punish sparingly and mainly target clear one-shot or repeated defectors. Given the personas:

- SLOTH and CHICKEN are reliably cooperative and will often punish to enforce cooperation.
- PARROT is a medium-high reciprocator and somewhat willing to punish to sustain cooperation.
- GORILLA and DOG are mixed and may test cooperation with occasional defections.
- DUCK has low trust and may defect initially or occasionally, but repeated punishment can push DUCK toward contributing.

Over 12 rounds (unknown to players while playing), I expect early moderate cooperation with a couple of targeted defections that get punished; after that cooperation largely stabilizes with occasional isolated defections and sparse, targeted punishment.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game with 6 players (SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT). Each round everyone decides how much of 20 coins to put into the shared pot (contributions 0–20). The pot is multiplied by 1.8 and shared equally, so there is a group-level incentive to contribute (everyone benefits from others' contributions) but a private incentive to withdraw (free-ride) because any one coin contributed yields only 1.8/6 = 0.3 back to the contributor in the redistribution. The reward stage lets players transfer valuable signals to others at a cost (2 coins per reward unit) and gives 2 coins per unit to the target — rewards are a way to reinforce cooperation but are costly to the giver.

Given player personas:
- GORILLA, PARROT, CHICKEN are the most consistently cooperative/prosocial — likely to contribute relatively high amounts early and sometimes reward cooperators.
- DUCK tends to trust others and may start cooperative but is less likely to reward strongly.
- SLOTH and DOG are more selfish/low-giving — likely to contribute little and rarely reward.

Typical dynamics expected here:
- Early rounds: cooperation is relatively high as cooperators lead; a few small, targeted rewards help signal appreciation and encourage continued cooperation.
- Mid-game: some decline as free-riders (SLOTH, DOG) persist and cooperative return to smaller contributions; rewards remain sparse because rewarding costs are significant.
- Late rounds: standard end-game effect — contributions fall further as the finite horizon becomes salient. A few players (GORILLA, PARROT) may keep contributing a bit, and occasional rewards appear, but overall contribution rates drop.

I will simulate the game from round 1 through round 19, showing brief round explanations, sparse chat, contributions (in the exact avatar order), reward tuples only when non-zero, and the per-round summary header.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT </PLAYERS>

This is a short (2-round) public goods game among six players. Each round, players decide privately how many of their 20 coins to contribute to a shared pot; contributions are multiplied by 2.2 and split equally. The private return on each contributed coin (2.2/6 ≈ 0.367) is well below 1, so the dominant selfish action is to contribute 0. However some players have pro-social priors and tend to contribute more (GORILLA and CHICKEN), while others tend to give little (DUCK, SLOTH). Because the game is only two rounds, some players may reduce contributions in the final round (end‑game effect), but those with intrinsic cooperative preferences (GORILLA, CHICKEN) often continue contributing.

The reward stage allows costly transfers: each reward unit costs 4 coins to the sender and gives the recipient 3 coins. This is a relatively expensive way to signal approval or punish defection; players typically send very few reward units, often 0 or 1, and they reward cooperators rather than defectors. Rewarding is anonymous (players see only their own spend/receive totals), which makes direct reciprocal chains less precise but still informative: receiving a reward can signal that at least one other person recognized your cooperation. Messages tend to be sparse and used to signal intentions or thank anonymous rewarders.

Given the personas:
- GORILLA is likely to contribute near the endowment (strong cooperator) and to reward cooperators (but sparingly).
- CHICKEN is cooperative and likely to contribute high.
- PARROT, DOG, and others are moderate; PARROT might increase cooperation if signaling/rewards are favorable.
- DUCK and SLOTH lean toward low contributions.

I therefore predict moderate overall cooperation driven by GORILLA and CHICKEN, sparse rewarding targeted at high contributors, and modest adjustments in round 2 (cooperators staying high; low contributors staying low).

game_explanation:
This public-goods game gives each player a 20-coin endowment each round, but players choose how many coins to withdraw privately; the remainder is contributed to the group pot. The pot is multiplied by 1.8 and split equally across the six players, so an individual's private-withdrawal tradeoff depends on how much others contribute: contributing more lowers your private take that round but raises the group pot and therefore the equalized share for everyone. The presence of a costly bilateral reward mechanism (each reward unit costs the sender 2 coins and gives the recipient 2 coins) allows players to reward cooperators and/or punish free-riders, but rewards are observable only in amounts and not traceable to specific senders in the private summaries. Communication is allowed but usually sparse.

Likely dynamics:
- Early rounds: uncertainty about others leads to mixed contributions. A few players with pro-social priors (PARROT, DOG, sometimes SLOTH) contribute relatively high amounts; others (DUCK, GORILLA, CHICKEN) tend to contribute less. Small, targeted rewards will be used sparingly to sustain cooperation — mostly by PARROT and occasional reciprocation from DOG and SLOTH.
- Middle rounds: players infer patterns. If free-riding persists (DUCK contributing 0; GORILLA low), cooperators may reduce how much they reward or cut contributions. Rewards will mostly be used to sustain a small cooperative core; punishment via withholding rewards is more common than costly negative actions.
- Late rounds: finite horizon (19 rounds) causes some unraveling; contributions tend to decline. Rewards decline as forward incentives vanish, though a few last-ditch rewards may appear from committed cooperators. Overall payoffs shift so low contributors often end up better short-term, but social players try to steer contributions early on using sparse rewarding.

game_explanation:
This public goods game pits a simple binary contribution decision (0 or 20 coins) against a multiplier (3.7) and a costly punishment stage (2 coins per punishment unit for the punisher, 5 coins deducted per unit from the punished). Because shares are equal, a lone free rider who contributes 0 benefits more in that round than contributors — so free riding is immediately individually attractive. However, repeated free riding invites punishment by contributors who want to enforce cooperation. Punishment is credible because targets can see who punished them, enabling targeted reciprocity and reputational responses. The usual dynamic: early rounds test others (some cooperate, some free ride), contributors may begin to punish conspicuous free riders sparingly, which can cause either retaliation (escalated punishment cycles) or behavioral change (free riders begin contributing to avoid punishment). Players who are conditionally cooperative (Duck, Parrot, Chicken) will tend to contribute and punish free riders occasionally; strongly selfish players (Gorilla, Dog) are likely to free ride, with Gorilla particularly unlikely to change unless punishment is sustained. Sloth is ambivalent and may switch between free riding and contributing. Chat messages are sparse and used to signal intentions or protest punishments. Overall I predict an initial mix (some contribute, some free ride), a few rounds of targeted punishment aimed at clear free riders (especially Gorilla and Dog), some retaliation, then gradual convergence toward more contributions as punishments signal norms — with occasional lapses and light punishment thereafter.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT </PLAYERS>

This 2-round public goods game sets up a tension between private incentives to keep coins and group incentives to contribute. Each player's contribution increases the shared pot, which is multiplied by 2.2 and split equally, so conditional cooperators tend to contribute more because they benefit indirectly from others' contributions. Free-riders (who contribute little) can earn more in the short run, which often triggers targeted punishment from cooperators because punishment is an instrument to deter low contributions even though it is costly to the punisher. Rewards are less punishing to the source for the group (they cost the sender 4 and give the receiver 3) and are often used sparingly to reinforce cooperation.

Uncertainties: players don't know who punished or rewarded them, only the net effects, so identification-based retaliation is limited (retaliation is diffuse). Because the game lasts only two rounds, some players may defect in round 1 planning to free-ride, while conditional cooperators may punish or reward in round 1 to shape round 2 behavior. In round 2 (the final round) punishments and rewards are more about fairness and immediate payoff, so we expect cooperators to maintain high contributions (or increase slightly), persistent free-riders to continue low contributions, and a small number of punishment and reward actions focused on the most salient free-riders and cooperators.

game_explanation:
This is a 2-round public goods game. Each round, players privately choose how many of their 20 coins to contribute to a common pot. The pot is multiplied by 2.2 and split evenly, so each coin contributed returns 2.2/6 ≈ 0.3667 to each player — less than the private cost of 1 coin. That makes free-riding (contribute 0) individually attractive even though group payoffs are higher when everyone contributes. After contributions, players can spend costly reward units (cost 4 to sender, +3 to a target) to reward others; rewards are therefore net-costly to the sender and used sparingly to reinforce cooperation or signal approval. Players see only their own round payoff and reward inflows/outflows at the summary stage, not who sent rewards.

Given the provided personas, we expect a mix: SLOTH, DOG, and PARROT are relatively more pro-social and likely to contribute more; GORILLA and CHICKEN are moderate contributors; DUCK is likely to free-ride (contribute 0). Rewards will be few and targeted at visibly high contributors (in transcript inference), with cooperators sparingly rewarding other cooperators. Because the game lasts only 2 rounds, reputational incentives are limited; rewards are mainly expressive or last-round reciprocation.

Because contributions are all-or-nothing (0 or 20), choices are stark. Because players don’t know the length of the game, earlier punishments can encourage cooperation in later rounds; toward the end some players may test the norm with defections, eliciting more punitive responses. I’ll simulate a plausible unfolding: initial mixed cooperation with targeted punishment of obvious free-riders (especially DOG and GORILLA early), increasing cooperation midgame as punishments enforce norm, occasional lapses punished, and a few retaliatory/final-round punitive acts.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with binary contributions (0 or 20) and optional costly punishment. Individual incentives favor free-riding (keep 20 and still receive an equal share of the multiplied pot), while groups benefit when more people contribute. Because the pot is multiplied by 3.7 and split equally, each contributed 20 increases the group's total return but still gives the contributor less immediate private payoff than a free-rider—so cooperation must be sustained by social mechanisms (expectations, reciprocity, or punishment). Punishment is costly to the punisher (2 coins per punishment unit) but reduces the punished player substantially (5 coins per unit), so occasional targeted punishment can deter persistent free-riding.

Uncertainties: players do not know how many rounds remain, so future retaliation or reward is possible and affects incentives. Personas give priors: CHICKEN is highly likely to free-ride constantly; PARROT and DOG are more likely to free-ride or oscillate; DUCK tends to be cooperative; SLOTH and GORILLA are mixed. Expect modest initial cooperation from DUCK and maybe SLOTH/GORILLA, targeted punishment of obvious free-riders (sparse, low units), occasional retaliation from punished defectors, and gradual movement toward higher contribution when punishment is credible. Chat messages are typically infrequent and used to ask for cooperation or to protest punishment.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT </PLAYERS>

This is a repeated public goods game with binary contributions (0 or 20). Each round players choose privately; the group pot is multiplied by 3.7 and split equally among all six players. Because non-contributors keep their 20 and still receive an equal share of the multiplied pot, free-riding typically pays off in the short run. With cheap talk allowed but no knowledge of total rounds, expect initial attempts at cooperation (especially from players with high "trust" priors like PARROT, and medium cooperators like DUCK and DOG). Once players observe round payoffs and see free-riders earn more, cooperation will tend to decline. Occasional messages can restore cooperation temporarily (promises, pleas, or one-off "experiments"), and a few players may alternate between cooperating and defecting. GORILLA’s prior suggests low initial contributions but possible signaling behavior; SLOTH may start cooperative due to trusting cues but then exploit when it pays; CHICKEN is likely to defect often; PARROT will try to contribute frequently; DUCK and DOG will contribute fairly often but react to being exploited.

I will simulate these dynamics: early partial cooperation, then erosion as free-riders earn higher net payoffs, punctuated by sparse chat messages and a few rounds of renewed cooperation. At each round I list predicted short explanations, one or two chat entries, the contribution vector in the exact avatar order, and the round summary (net payoffs shown to players). Numbers are rounded to two decimals.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT </PLAYERS>

This is a standard public-goods game with a strong social dilemma: each player privately has 20 coins and can either contribute all (20) or nothing (0). The group pot is multiplied by 3.7 and split evenly among six players, so each coin contributed returns 3.7/6 ≈ 0.6167 coins to each contributor — less than 1. That means contributing is socially beneficial if others also contribute (everyone is better off if many contribute), but individually costly (the dominant individual incentive is to free-ride).

Given that players do not know how many rounds remain, we expect initial rounds to show some cooperation driven by optimism and conditional cooperators. Over time, because defecting (contributing 0) yields higher immediate payoffs when others contribute, repeated free-riding will likely erode cooperation. Player personas bias expectations: CHICKEN is very likely to contribute consistently; DUCK is likely to free-ride; GORILLA and PARROT are moderately likely to contribute early but will retaliate or reduce contributions if exploited; DOG is conditional and may try to match group trends; SLOTH is mixed/tends to free-ride. Chat is sparse and will be used for short pledges, disappointments, and a few revival attempts. Payoffs shown each round are the net payoff for that round (private remainder + share of multiplied pot) and are visible for all players at the round summary.

game_explanation:
This is a 2-round public-goods game where each player gets 20 coins per round and decides privately how much to contribute (0–20). Contributions are multiplied by 2.2 then split equally among the six players; a single coin you contribute reduces your private holding by 1 but increases the group pot by 2.2, so your private return from contributing is 2.2/6 ≈ 0.37 — less than 1. That means contributing is socially beneficial but individually costly, so free-riding is tempting. Because the game lasts two rounds, some players who care about reciprocity or reputation will be willing to contribute in round 1 to encourage others to cooperate in round 2.

The reward stage allows costly transfers: each reward unit costs a sender 4 coins and gives the recipient 3 coins. Rewards are therefore a blunt tool — they can reward good contributors or (by omission) signal disapproval of free riders. Because rewards are costly, players use them sparingly. Uncertainty includes not knowing others' round-1 choices before deciding, and the fact that summary information only shows your own net payoff and your own reward sent/received amounts (players cannot identify who rewarded them in their summary), so interpersonal signalling is imperfect.

Given the provided personas:
- DUCK (very prosocial) is likely to contribute near 20 in both rounds and reward others who cooperate.
- PARROT (very cooperative) also likely contributes high and reciprocates rewards.
- SLOTH, CHICKEN are moderate cooperators (middle contributions); SLOTH may be a bit cautious, CHICKEN mixed.
- GORILLA and DOG are likely low contributors (free riders). They rarely send rewards.

I therefore predict a pattern where DUCK and PARROT contribute high amounts and reward each other and other cooperators sparsely; SLOTH and CHICKEN contribute moderately; GORILLA and DOG contribute little. Rewards are sparse and directed toward high contributors.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game with 6 players, 19 known rounds, and active punishment/reward. Individually, keeping a coin privately yields 1 to the chooser while contributing it returns only 0.3 to the chooser (1.8/6), so the dominant selfish incentive is to withdraw coins (free-ride). However, conditional cooperators can sustain higher contributions if they expect others to reciprocate or if punishment deters free-riding. Because players cannot identify who punished them and punishments are costly to send, expect: (1) some early cooperation from the moderately pro-social players (DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT) and mixed behavior from GORILLA; (2) persistent low contribution from SLOTH; (3) occasional, sparse punishments directed at clear free-riders (mostly SLOTH) by a few cooperators to enforce norms; (4) a small number of occasional rewards to reinforce consistent contributors; (5) a gradual decay of cooperation as the known horizon approaches (players reduce contributions toward the end). Chats will be rare and focused (requests, shaming). Punishment and reward actions will be used sparingly because they cost senders real payoffs.

game_explanation:
This is a short (2-round) public goods game with six players. The private marginal return for one coin contributed is 2.2/6 ≈ 0.37, so purely selfish incentives push players toward contributing 0 each round. However, social preferences, reciprocity, and the possibility of costly punishment or costly rewards often lead many players to contribute at least some amount in early rounds. Because the game is finite and players know it lasts exactly two rounds, we expect some decline in contributions by Round 2 (backward-induction pressure), but also strategic signaling in Round 1: pro-social types (GORILLA, DOG, DOG-like DUCK) will contribute relatively high amounts to encourage reciprocal behavior and to earn rewards; low-trust or low-effort types (SLOTH, PARROT) will tend to give little. Punishment and reward are used sparingly in most real experiments: a few players will punish obvious free-riders or reward high contributors. Punishments are costly (4 to sender, -10 to target) and therefore tend to be small and targeted; rewards are also costly to the sender (4) and provide a small benefit to the target (+3), so they are used selectively to signal approval.

Expectations for dynamics here:
- Round 1: GORILLA and DOG are likely to contribute strongly; DUCK moderately; SLOTH and PARROT low; CHICKEN low-to-moderate. A few targeted punishments of the worst free-riders (SLOTH, PARROT) and a couple of small rewards to high contributors.
- Round 2: Some decline in contributions overall, but punished players may increase slightly to avoid further sanction. Final-round incentives reduce the power of future retaliation/reciprocity, so punishment will remain sparse and selective.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game with six players who know there are 19 rounds. The main incentives: contributing increases the group pot (multiplied by 1.8 and shared equally), while withholding gives a private benefit that is not multiplied. Because contributions are shared equally, conditional cooperation and social enforcement are typical: people who see others contribute more often reciprocate by contributing more themselves; conversely, free-riding invites punishment or withholding of rewards. Punishment is costly to the punisher (2 coins per unit) and disproportionately costly to the punished (−3 per unit), so players tend to use punishment sparingly but enough to discipline persistent free-riders. Rewards are cheaper on targets (each reward unit +2 to recipient for 2 cost to sender) and are often used to encourage cooperators.

Given the player personas:
- GORILLA is highly cooperative and willing to both contribute a lot and invest in social enforcement (rewards/punishments) to sustain cooperation.
- PARROT and DOG are moderately cooperative and will often reciprocate rewards; PARROT may use some punishment or reward selectively.
- DUCK, DOG, and PARROT tend to respond to incentives: they increase contribution when rewarded and may reduce when they see others free-ride.
- SLOTH and CHICKEN have lower initial cooperation; they may be slow to contribute unless disciplined or rewarded.

I expect early rounds to show heterogeneous contributions (some high contributors like GORILLA and PARROT, some low ones like SLOTH and CHICKEN). Punishments and rewards are sparse and targeted: punishments mostly aimed at obvious low contributors and rewards targeted to sustain high contributors. Over mid-game we see a modest increase in contributions from conditional cooperators; toward the end, classic end-game effects reduce contributions, and punishment becomes rare in the final round.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT </PLAYERS>

This is a repeated, anonymous public goods game with binary contributions (0 or 20) each round. Each coin contributed is multiplied by 3.7 and shared equally across all six players. Individually, keeping your 20 is always a tempting best response (you get the same share from the pot whether you contributed or not, plus you keep your 20 if you defect). Group payoff is maximized when multiple people contribute, but any contributor is exploited if others free-ride.

Given the six personas:
- GORILLA is moderately cooperative and likely to try contributing occasionally to signal good faith.
- CHICKEN shows some reciprocation and may contribute a few times to support cooperation.
- SLOTH, DOG, DUCK, and PARROT have stronger tendencies to keep their endowments (free-ride), though a few may "experiment" once or twice.
- Because players do not know the game length, early rounds often see some experimentation and a few contributions; without sustained reciprocation, cooperation typically decays.

I therefore predict a few initial contributions (from GORILLA and CHICKEN), leading to short-lived partial cooperation. Other players will mostly free-ride. Over rounds cooperation will mostly fall to zero, with occasional isolated contributions as individuals test whether cooperation can be re-established. Chat will be sparse and utilitarian: brief promises, brief disappointments, and occasional attempts to restart cooperation.

game_explanation:
This public goods game pits six players (SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT) against a repeated social dilemma over 19 rounds. Each round everyone effectively decides a contribution (0–20): coins withdrawn privately are 20 − contribution, and the common fund (sum of contributions) is multiplied by 1.8 and split equally (so each player's public payoff equals 0.3 × total contributions). After contributions are revealed, players can spend coins to reward others: each reward unit costs the sender 2 coins and gives the recipient 2 coins (so rewards reallocate payoff but do not change total group payout). Players see their own round payoff and their own reward-sent and reward-received amounts (others' summaries are not shown to them). Rewards are anonymous in player summaries, but we report them explicitly here.

Qualitatively, given the player priors:
- DUCK, CHICKEN, and PARROT lean more toward cooperation and mutual encouragement; they are most likely to contribute relatively high amounts and to use small rewards to sustain cooperation.
- DOG and GORILLA lean toward low contributions and exploit the public benefit; they rarely reward.
- SLOTH behaves moderately selfish but can fluctuate; often contributes at medium levels and chats/rewards sparingly.

Expectations across the 19 rounds:
- Early rounds begin with mixed contributions: cooperators contribute relatively high amounts and defectors low. Small, reciprocal positive rewards among cooperators will appear sporadically to signal approval; these are sparse because reward units cost the sender.
- Mid-game, cooperators try to sustain cooperation via small mutual rewards; defectors continue to free-ride since punishment via costly rewards is rare.
- Toward the end (final rounds), contributions gradually decline (end-game effect), and rewards taper off, though players may still send a few final thank-you rewards or reciprocal tokens.

game_explanation:
This public-goods game pits short-run private incentives (keep coins) against a group benefit (contributions multiplied by 1.8 and split equally). With six players and 19 rounds, players face a strategic trade-off: contributing helps everyone but costs you privately; withholding gives a private advantage unless others contribute enough. The reward stage lets players transfer positive reinforcement (costly to sender, beneficial to recipient), which can be used to reward cooperators or—by omission—discourage free-riding. Because identities of rewarders are not revealed, social signaling comes mainly through contributions, occasional chat, and visible net payoffs to oneself only.

Given the personas:
- CHICKEN is highly pro-social and likely to contribute strongly and consistently, and to reward fellow cooperators.
- PARROT and GORILLA are moderately cooperative, likely to contribute moderately and use rewards selectively.
- DOG is pragmatic and may reward visible cooperators occasionally.
- DUCK and SLOTH skew toward low contributions; SLOTH in particular tends to free-ride and rarely reward.

Typical unfolding predicted: early rounds show a mix of contributions with CHICKEN leading cooperation; small, targeted rewards reinforce cooperators. Over repeated rounds, conditional contributors (GORILLA, PARROT, DOG) adjust toward CHICKEN's level if rewards reliably follow cooperation. Free-riders (SLOTH, sometimes DUCK) receive few rewards; this social omission weakens their payoffs relative to cooperators, but it may take many rounds to change behavior. Near the end of the 19-round horizon, overall contributions commonly drift down (end-game effect), though CHICKEN may remain relatively cooperative longer. Chat will be sparse—short encouragements or mild calls for fairness—and reward actions will be used sparingly to signal approval rather than to punish.

game_explanation:
This 2-round public goods game creates a tension between private incentives to free-ride and group incentives to contribute. Each player's contribution raises the total pot (multiplied by 2.2) and benefits everyone equally, so individuals who expect others to contribute have an incentive to contribute little. At the same time, the post-contribution reward stage lets players pay (4 coins) to give another player a positive transfer (recipient gets 3 coins). Rewards are costly and therefore typically sparse; they are most often used to reinforce good contributors (rewarding cooperation) or, less commonly, to punish very low contributors by omission rather than costly negative transfers (there is no direct punishment here, only rewarding).

Given the supplied personas: DUCK is strongly cooperative and likely to contribute a large amount (possibly the maximum). GORILLA tends to free-ride and will likely contribute very little or zero. DOG and PARROT are more conditional/cooperative — they may contribute moderate amounts and selectively reward high contributors. SLOTH and CHICKEN are mixed/relatively low contributors. Because players know there are only two rounds, some conditional cooperators may reward in round 1 to encourage cooperation in round 2, and some may raise their round-2 contributions if they saw others contributed in round 1. Overall I expect DUCK to contribute very high in both rounds; a few others will contribute moderately, some will free-ride, and rewards will be sparse and targeted toward DUCK and occasionally toward DOG as a secondary high contributor.

game_explanation:
This 14-round public goods game sets up a tension between private incentives (keep your 20 coins) and collective benefit (contribute 20 to the pot, which is multiplied by 2.6 and split equally). Players have heterogenous priors: some (CROCODILE, DOG, RABBIT) are strongly prosocial and likely to contribute repeatedly; others (SLOTH, PARROT, CHICKEN, OWL, MOOSE) are more selfish or indifferent and are likely to defect (contribute 0). Several players (GORILLA, DUCK, CHICK, SNAKE, COW) are mixed and may condition on how many others contribute, plus on whether cooperators punish defectors or reward cooperators.

Expectations about dynamics:
- Early rounds often reveal baseline tendencies: prosocial players contribute; selfish players free-ride. Mixed types will test the field.
- Punishment is costly to the punisher but can sustain cooperation if used selectively; rewards can reinforce cooperators but are also used sparingly.
- Because punishments/rewards are anonymous at the individual-receiver level, players only see aggregate effects; but senders still can choose to punish/reward based on observed group behavior.
- Over repeated interactions, cooperation may be maintained by a few consistent punishers/rewarders, but defections typically rise toward the end (end-game effect) as the future incentive to sustain reputation disappears.
- In this simulation I predict a moderate level of cooperation for the first half sustained by core cooperators and occasional sanctions, followed by erosion in the later rounds and a sharp decline toward the end. Punishment and reward actions are sparse and targeted.

game_explanation:
This online public-goods game gives each player the simple binary choice each round: keep the 20 coins privately (contribute 0) or leave them in the shared pot (contribute 20). The shared pot is multiplied by 2.9 and split equally, so full-group contributions benefit everyone but create a temptation to free-ride. Because contributions are revealed each round and anonymous rewarding is allowed after redistribution, we should expect the following dynamics:

- Early rounds: a subset of reliably prosocial players (based on their personas) will contribute to signal cooperation. Some defectors will try to exploit the pot. Small, targeted positive rewards will be used sparingly to endorse contributors.
- Middle rounds: rewards may stabilize cooperation for a while — contributors tend to reward other contributors to sustain the pattern. Occasional defectors may be coaxed back into contributing if they are rewarded or see steady public cooperation.
- Late rounds: standard end-game incentives make contributions decline. With diminishing future benefits from sustaining cooperation, more players will defect; rewards become rarer and cooperation typically collapses toward the end.
- Uncertainty: Because rewards are costly and anonymous at the summary level, altruistic motivation, reciprocity expectations, and some experimentation drive variability. Players who are consistently selfish (Parrot, Sloth, Chicken) will defect often; strongly prosocial players (Crocodile, Owl, Duck, Snake) will contribute much more of the time and use rewards sparingly to encourage contributors.

We simulate a plausible 26-round path consistent with these tendencies: cooperation present early, somewhat sustained by occasional rewards, and a gradual decline in the final rounds.

game_explanation:
This 26-round public goods game pits 12 players who each decide every round whether to contribute their full 20 coins to a shared pot (cooperate) or withdraw it for private use (defect). The pot is multiplied by 2.9 and split equally, so unilateral defection yields a private benefit but reduces everyone’s share; mutual cooperation increases group payoffs. Because choices are binary and simultaneous, uncertainty about others' intentions is high each round. Payoff-relevant dynamics to expect:

- Early rounds: some players will test cooperation while a few may immediately free-ride. Cooperators like MOOSE, SNAKE, and SLOTH are predisposed to contribute; DUCK is predisposed to defect. Mixed types (GORILLA, PARROT, OWL, CHICK, CHICKEN, RABBIT, CROCODILE) may lean toward cooperation early but can switch.
- Intermediate rounds: social enforcement may appear. Punishment (costly to sender, harsher on target) and occasional rewards will be used sparingly to deter chronic defection or to encourage contributors. Players who dislike being exploited (DOG, PARROT, RABBIT) may punish occasional defectors. Rewards will be rarer than punishment but can be used to signal approval.
- Late rounds: finite-horizon unraveling tends to reduce cooperation as the end approaches. Expect a gradual decline in contributions and fewer costly enforcement actions, especially in the final few rounds.
- Communication: chat is possible but limited; expect a few short appeals, calls for cooperation, or short reproaches. Messages are noisy and non-binding.
- Information: because players cannot see who punished or rewarded them, enforcement creates uncertainty and collective norms may be sustained only if punishers are persistent and visible through messages.

I will simulate a plausible 26-round transcript consistent with these tendencies: overall moderate cooperation early, some enforcement, short-lived stabilization, and a decline toward the final rounds. Chat will be sparse and punishment/reward actions limited and targeted.

game_explanation:
This repeated public-goods game pits short-term incentives to free-ride (keep 20 coins and still get a share of the multiplied pot) against longer-run incentives to contribute so the group earns more total and to avoid being punished or to earn rewards. Because contributions are binary (0 or 20) and the public pot is multiplied by 2.6 then split equally, a lone contributor lowers their private payoff relative to a lone defector in the same round, but if enough people contribute the group payoff rises. Players see past contributions and the gross amounts punished/rewarded (but not who took those actions), so reputational signals and anonymous enforcement can sustain cooperation early and mid-game. Toward the end (final rounds) the incentive to defect strengthens (endgame), so contributions typically decay.

Given the supplied personas, I expect a stable “core” of cooperative types (PARROT, OWL, RABBIT, SNAKE) who contribute most rounds; several conditional cooperators (DUCK, MOOSE, CHICK, CHICKEN, sometimes GORILLA) who contribute when cooperation is reasonably high or when punished; and a handful of persistent or opportunistic defectors (SLOTH, DOG, CROCODILE, COW) who often free-ride. Punishment and reward will be used sparingly: a few 1-unit punishments to deter repeat defectors and occasional 1-unit rewards to encourage contributors. Over 14 rounds we should see initial moderate cooperation, a peak/plateau mid-game as punishments recruit conditional cooperators, then a gradual decline into the endgame with few enforcement actions toward the last rounds.

game_explanation:
This public-goods game pits 12 players against a repeated choice between keeping 20 coins privately (defect) or leaving them in the shared pot (contribute 20). The group pot is multiplied by 2.9 and split evenly, so individual incentives favor withdrawing (defect) because a lone contributor helps everyone but is personally worse off than a defector in the same round. However, contributors can be rewarded afterward: sending 1 reward unit costs the sender 1 and gives the recipient 1. Rewards are visible as totals but not traceable to specific senders in players' summaries, so they can help sustain cooperation by creating private incentives to cooperate (if cooperative players reward each other), or fail to do so if rewards are rare.

Given the player personas, I expect an early phase with a cluster of prosocials (GORILLA, CROCODILE, DUCK, sometimes MOOSE and RABBIT) contributing. Free riders (DOG, CHICKEN, CHICK, PARROT, OWL) will typically defect. Rewards will be sparse and targeted: CROCODILE and GORILLA (and occasionally MOOSE or DUCK) will give small positive rewards to reinforce cooperation. Over many rounds, unless rewards are reliably generous and widespread, conditional cooperators will start to withdraw contributions as they see exploitation. That leads to declining cooperation through the middle rounds. A few attempts to revive cooperation (short spurts of messages and a few reward offers) may occur midgame, mostly by CROCODILE and GORILLA. In the final rounds, the standard backward-induction pressure and fatigue will drive near-universal defection, with rewards largely drying up.

game_explanation:
This repeated public goods game pits a mix of reliably pro-social, mixed, and self-oriented players against each other for 14 rounds. Because contributions are binary (0 or 20) and everyone sees round-by-round contributions and reward flows afterward, expect a blend of conditional cooperation and selective rewarding. Very pro-social types (DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, CHICK, CHICK) are likely to contribute frequently; strongly selfish types (CHICKEN, SNAKE, GORILLA) will usually free-ride. Mixed types (SLOTH, DUCK, RABBIT, OWL, CROCODILE, COW) will respond to the observed group cooperation — contributing when many others do and pulling back when exploitation looks persistent. Rewards will be used sparingly and primarily to reinforce cooperative behavior (small positive rewards from cooperators to other cooperators). Over time, rewards may help sustain cooperation early on, but as end-game approaches (final rounds) contributions should decline (end-game effect), with only the most intrinsically pro-social players maintaining contributions.

game_explanation:
This is a 26-round public-goods game with 12 players. Each round each player either keeps the 20 coins privately (contribute 0) or leaves them in the public pot (contribute 20). The pot is multiplied by 2.9 and divided equally, so cooperating players earn more from collective contributions but are vulnerable to free-riders. Because contributions are binary and players see only their own round payoff and how much they spent/received on punishment/reward (not who did what), players form informal suspicions from payoffs and sparse chat. Typical dynamics:

- Early rounds: conditional cooperators tend to contribute (20) optimistically; a few selfish types test free-riding. Because payoffs are noisy but informative, early defections can be detected and evoke targeted punishment from some players who wish to enforce cooperation.
- Middle rounds: punishments and a few rewards may sustain cooperation if they deter free-riders. However, punishing is costly, so enforcement is typically sparse. Some players give up and start free-riding to avoid being exploited.
- Late rounds (endgame): cooperation often unravels as the horizon shortens; players expect less future retaliation and shift to private withdrawals (0). Punishment and reward activity declines.

Given the provided personas, I expect a small persistent set of cooperators to begin by contributing, some clear free-riders (SLOTH, SNAKE) to persist early, selective punishment of suspected defectors to appear in early-to-mid rounds, and an overall gradual decline in cooperation toward the final rounds.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game with 12 players, 26 rounds, binary contribution choices each round (either contribute the full 20 or withdraw it all). The pot is multiplied by 2.9 and split evenly, so individual incentives push a single player to withdraw (keep 20) while group payoff is maximized if everyone contributes. Because the game has many rounds and punishment/reward is available (albeit costly), we expect a mix of behaviors:

- Early rounds: some testing — several reliably cooperative players will contribute immediately, some players will start by free-riding to test enforcement.
- Mid game: if cooperators occasionally punish or reward, norms emerge and most players converge to contributing (higher cooperation).
- Near the end (last few rounds): the incentive to “defect” increases (endgame effect). Expect more free-riding in the final rounds; cooperators may punish sparingly even when there's no future to enforce, and rewards become rarer.
- Punishment will be used sparingly because it costs the punisher; rewards may be used occasionally to reinforce cooperation.
- Chat is sparse and used for short calls to cooperate or mild scolding when free-riding appears.

I simulate a plausible path: moderate cooperation in round 1, some enforcement leads to near-universal cooperation from round 3 through round 19, isolated defections and sparse punishments later, and rising free-riding in the final rounds with a last-round spike in withdrawals.

game_explanation:
This is a 14-round public goods game with 13 players. Each round, players decide privately whether to contribute their entire 20-coin endowment or keep it all. Contributions are multiplied by 2.6 and evenly split, so each 20 contributed increases every player's payout by 4 (because 20*2.6/13 = 4). That creates the classic social dilemma: contributing helps the group but is costly individually (you give up 20 to get only 4 back per contributed 20), so free-riding (not contributing) often yields higher individual payoff unless social enforcement exists.

With inexpensive/private decisions and anonymous post-decision punishment/reward, likely dynamics:
- Many players with cooperative priors (PARROT, RABBIT, CHICK, DUCK, SLOTH, SNAKE, GORILLA/MOOSE/COW to varying degrees) will initially contribute. A subset (DOG, OWL, CROCODILE, sometimes CHICKEN) are more likely to free-ride.
- Punishment is costly for the punisher (3 coins per unit) and very damaging to the target (-11 per unit). Because punishments are expensive for the source, expect punishments to be used sparingly and mainly by players who value cooperation or have higher punishment tendencies (e.g., CHICK, GORILLA). Punishments will usually target persistent free-riders (DOG, CROCODILE, OWL, CHICKEN early on).
- Rewards are also used sparingly (cost 3 per unit, target gains +4), often by reliably cooperative players to encourage cooperation (PARROT, RABBIT).
- Over rounds, targeted punishments can incentivize some defectors to switch to cooperating. Conversely, toward the end (final rounds) cooperation tends to decline (the "endgame" effect), and many players may defect as cooperation’s future benefits vanish.
- Uncertainty: who will be punished/rewarded is anonymous in the summary, so reputation-building is coarse. Small, intermittent punishment/reward is most realistic — enough to influence some behavior but not a flood of costly sanctions.

Below is a round-by-round simulated transcript (decisions, sparse punish/rewards, and round summaries). I use the exact avatar order given.

game_explanation:
This 14-round public goods game pits mixed motivations against simple incentives. Each round players privately choose either to keep their 20 coins or contribute all 20 to the common pot. Contributions are multiplied by 2.6 and split equally among the 13 players, so contributing benefits the group but costs the individual 20 in private holdings. After the contribution stage, players can spend costly reward units (cost 3 each) to give positive transfers (value 4 each) to specific peers — a way to reward cooperators or encourage desirable behavior. Because players cannot identify who gave them rewards in the summary, rewards serve mainly as signals to the group rather than direct reciprocal credits.

Given the player personas, I expect a stable core of consistent contributors (avatars with "very high" pro-social tendencies: CHICKEN, MOOSE, OWL, COW, and many medium/positive types like CHICK) to contribute most rounds. A cohort with weak or negative priors (GORILLA, RABBIT, CROCODILE, SLOTH, SNAKE) are likely to free-ride repeatedly. Mixed-priority players (DOG, PARROT, DUCK) will experiment: sometimes contributing, sometimes not, responding to observed group behavior and reward feedback. Rewards will be used sparingly and mostly by cooperators to encourage other cooperators; because rewards are costly, they will be limited in number and size. Expect gradual patterns rather than dramatic swings, with a small end-game decline in contributions as forward-looking incentives vanish in the final round.
