game_explanation:
This public-goods game pits individual incentives (keep 20 privately) against a weak group return from contributing: every 20-coin contribution is multiplied by 2.6 and split equally among the 13 players, so each coin contributed returns 2.6/13 ≈ 0.20 coins to its giver (≈4 coins back to a contributor who gives 20). Selfish short-run incentives therefore favor free-riding: contributing loses you about 16 coins relative to keeping your 20. At the same time, a costly reward system (cost 3 to the source, gives 4 to the target per unit) allows players to transfer resources to cooperators and, indirectly, sustain cooperation — rewards are costly to give but slightly increase aggregate welfare (+1 net per unit).

Given the player personas, I expect a stable core of high cooperators (SLOTH, MOOSE, RABBIT, CHICK) who tend to contribute frequently, with medium/mixed players (GORILLA, OWL, CROCODILE, DUCK, DOG) experimenting sometimes, and several low/cooperative-averse players (CHICKEN, PARROT, SNAKE, COW) usually free-riding. Because contributions are revealed each round, conditional cooperators will react: cooperation can erode when many defect, and occasional rewards will be used sparingly to encourage reliable contributors. Rewarding is typically targeted and low-volume (1–2 units) because it is costly; defectors with higher round payoffs are more likely to afford small rewards to signal approval or encourage cooperation. Near the final rounds, we may see modest endgame erosion but some true cooperators keep contributing.

This public-goods game pits individual incentives (keep 20 privately) against a weak group return from contributing: every 20-coin contribution is multiplied by 2.6 and split equally among the 13 players, so each coin contributed returns 2.6/13 ≈ 0.20 coins to its giver (≈4 coins back to a contributor who gives 20). Selfish short-run incentives therefore favor free-riding: contributing loses you about 16 coins relative to keeping your 20. At the same time, a costly reward system (cost 3 to the source, gives 4 to the target per unit) allows players to transfer resources to cooperators and, indirectly, sustain cooperation — rewards are costly to give but slightly increase aggregate welfare (+1 net per unit).

Given the player personas, I expect a stable core of high cooperators (SLOTH, MOOSE, RABBIT, CHICK) who tend to contribute frequently, with medium/mixed players (GORILLA, OWL, CROCODILE, DUCK, DOG) experimenting sometimes, and several low/cooperative-averse players (CHICKEN, PARROT, SNAKE, COW) usually free-riding. Because contributions are revealed each round, conditional cooperators will react: cooperation can erode when many defect, and occasional rewards will be used sparingly to encourage reliable contributors. Rewarding is typically targeted and low-volume (1–2 units) because it is costly; defectors with higher round payoffs are more likely to afford small rewards to signal approval or encourage cooperation. Near the final rounds, we may see modest endgame erosion but some true cooperators keep contributing.

game_explanation:
This is a 26-round repeated public goods game with 12 players. Each round each player either contributes the full 20 coins to the shared pot (cooperate) or withdraws them for private use (defect). The pot is multiplied by 2.9 and evenly split, so one cooperator's contribution helps everyone — but it is costly to that contributor unless enough others also contribute. Because contributions are binary (0 or 20), decisions often follow coarse social-type tendencies: some players are predisposed to cooperate (e.g., GORILLA, CHICK, MOOSE), some tilt toward defection (OWL, CHICKEN, CROCODILE), and many are mixed and conditional. Rewards are available after payoffs are revealed; they are anonymous in the summary (players see only their own sent/received reward amounts), so rewards can sustain cooperation but are expensive to the rewarder.

Likely dynamics here:
- Early rounds: a small core of cooperators tries to establish cooperation; they use sparse reward transfers to reinforce each other. Because with only a few cooperators cooperators often lose relative to defectors, reward use is targeted and limited.
- Middle rounds: if cooperation reaches a tipping point (5–6 cooperators), cooperators actually become net-beneficial, and cooperation grows. Rewards taper off because cooperation becomes self-sustaining.
- Later rounds: as the known end of the game approaches, conditional cooperators start to drop out; cooperation falls and rewards largely disappear. By the final rounds, defection dominates (endgame collapse).
- Chats are sparse and mostly used to thank or to express disappointment.

Below is a round-by-round transcript following that trajectory. Use the exact avatar order for each contributions list.

# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK, SNAKE, CROCODILE, COW </PLAYERS>

This 14-round public goods game with punishment and reward creates three linked incentives each round: (1) contributing 20 benefits the group (it increases the shared pot, which is multiplied by 2.6 and shared), but contributing is costly to the individual compared with free-riding; (2) punishment (costly to the punisher) can deter free-riding by imposing heavy losses on targets; (3) rewards (also costly) can reinforce cooperative choices. Given the players' personas, I expect a mix of stable cooperators (e.g., CHICK, SNAKE, DUCK, CHICKEN) and persistent defectors (e.g., DOG, MOOSE, OWL, CROCODILE early on), with some players responding to sanctions by shifting to cooperation. Punishments and rewards will be used sparingly — enough to sustain intermediate cooperation in the middle rounds but not so often as to eliminate free-riding entirely. Toward the very end (rounds 12–14), cooperation will decline (end-game effects), with most players choosing to free-ride, except a small set of steadfast cooperators.

Notes on numbers you will see: each round the shared pot per player equals 4 × (number of contributors). Each punishment unit costs a sender 3 coins and reduces the recipient by 11 coins. Each reward unit costs a sender 3 coins and gives the recipient +4 coins. Summaries show, for each player: net payoff for the round, how much they spent on punishment and reward that round, how much was deducted from them by punishment that round, and how much they received from rewards that round.

game_explanation: This is a repeated public-goods game with 12 players over 26 rounds. Each round everyone chooses either to leave all 20 coins in the shared pot (contribute 20) or withdraw all 20 (contribute 0). Contributing is socially efficient but personally costly each round, so unconditional incentives favor free-riding. Because the game is finite and known to last 26 rounds, we expect some "endgame" decline in cooperation late in the sequence. However, early and middle rounds can sustain cooperation through social preferences and targeted sanctions or rewards: cooperators may use small punishments to deter persistent free-riders and occasional rewards to encourage contributors. Chat offers a cheap signalling channel but cannot identify who punished or rewarded, so messages will be sparse and mainly about norms or calls to punish. Punishment and reward are costly to the sender and therefore used sparingly; expect only a few non-zero edges per round. Some players (based on personas) are predisposed to cooperate (GORILLA, MOOSE, CHICK) and others to defect (PARROT, RABBIT, CROCODILE, SLOTH). Conditional cooperators (DUCK, OWL, CHICKEN, SNAKE, DOG) will adjust across rounds: they may cooperate if enough others do and if defectors face sanctions, but drift toward defection as the game nears its end or if sanctions fade.

game_explanation:
This public goods game pits 12 distinct personalities against a simple binary contribution choice: contribute all (20) or withdraw all (0). The group payoff multiplies contributions by 2.9 and splits them equally, creating a social incentive to contribute but an individual incentive to free-ride. Punishment (costly to the punisher, doubly costly to the target) and reward (costly to the giver, beneficial to the recipient) provide social enforcement and incentives. Because the horizon (26 rounds) is known, there is scope for early rounds to establish norms with occasional sanctions and praise, followed by gradual erosion of cooperation as the end approaches (the classical endgame effect). However, with a mix of strongly cooperative types (e.g., MOOSE, DOG) and some consistent low contributors (e.g., SLOTH, CHICK), we expect a stable core of contributors in the middle rounds supported by occasional punishment of persistent defectors and occasional rewards to reinforce cooperation. Chat will be sparse, mostly calls to cooperate or short explanations. Punishment and reward will be used judiciously rather than widely.

game_explanation:
This public-goods game pits 13 anonymous players against the standard temptation to free-ride. Each round every player chooses whether to contribute their entire 20-coin endowment to the public pot (0 or 20). Contributions are multiplied by 2.6 and split equally across all 13 players, so a single contributor increases everyone’s share but personally bears the full 20-coin cost that round unless rewarded. After returns are distributed, players can send costly positive rewards: each reward unit costs the sender 3 coins and gives the recipient 4 coins. Rewards are visible in totals but not traceable to specific senders in players’ personal summaries.

Typical incentives and dynamics to expect:
- Early rounds often see relatively high cooperation driven by optimism and conditional cooperators (some players will contribute expecting reciprocation).
- As rounds proceed, free-riding tends to increase; conditional cooperators reduce contributions if they perceive low cooperation in peers.
- Rewarding can sustain cooperation by selectively rewarding contributors, but rewards are costly to send and thus used sparingly. Players most inclined to reward are those who value reciprocity or have tended to cooperate.
- Endgame: in final rounds, forward-looking incentives vanish and many players shift to defection; rewards also decline.

Given the player personas (some consistently high cooperators like SNAKE and CHICK, some low cooperators like PARROT and COW, and many mixed), I predict an initial period of moderate-to-strong cooperation, a mid-game decline, intermittent recoveries when rewards reinforce contributors, and a collapse toward defection in the final round. Rewards will be sparse and mostly directed at visible cooperators, often single-unit gifts.

# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK, SNAKE, CROCODILE, COW </PLAYERS>

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK, SNAKE, CROCODILE, COW </PLAYERS>

This repeated public-goods game creates a tension between private incentive (keep your 20 each round) and group benefit (if everyone contributes, each gets much more). With 13 players, a single 20-coin contribution is multiplied by 2.6 to 52 coins and shared: each player receives 4 coins from that one contribution. So an individual contributor loses 16 coins net from their own contribution, although mutual contributions are collectively beneficial. Because choices are simultaneous and private each round, the immediate dominant incentive is to free-ride. However, the optional reward stage (cost 3 to giver, +4 to recipient per unit) can support cooperation: rewarding a contributor is costly for the giver but helps sustain contributors and creates reputational/reciprocal incentives across rounds.

Given the 14-round horizon, we expect:
- Early rounds: some players with more pro-social tendencies (CROCODILE, MOOSE, CHICKEN, RABBIT, GORILLA, SLOTH) will contribute to signal cooperation.
- Middle rounds: conditional cooperators will adjust — if many free-ride, cooperation will decline. Rewards will be used sparingly to reinforce contributors; because rewards are costly, they will usually be small (1 unit) and targeted at seen contributors.
- Endgame: toward the final rounds, incentive to contribute weakens (endgame decline), so contributions likely fall, with few players (notably CROCODILE and MOOSE) sometimes remaining cooperative out of consistency or preference.
- Uncertainty: players do not see others' choices before deciding and cannot identify who rewarded them, which makes sustaining cooperation fragile; rewards help but are limited.

Transcript shows these dynamics unfolding with sparse, targeted rewards and fluctuating cooperation across 14 rounds.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game with 12 players and 26 rounds. Each round each player either contributes their full 20 coins to the public pot (contribute = 20) or withdraws it privately (contribute = 0). The group pot (sum of contributions) is multiplied by 2.9 and split equally among all 12 players. Because each coin contributed yields only 2.9/12 ≈ 0.2417 coins back to the contributor, contributing is individually costly even though group payoff is higher if many contribute. Rewards are available after redistribution: sending a reward unit costs the sender 1 coin in that round and gives 1 coin to the named target. Rewards are anonymous in the summary (players don’t see who rewarded them), and players may chat.

Given these incentives and the personas:
- A small core of pro-social players (DUCK, MOOSE, OWL) are likely to contribute consistently; several others (DOG, CHICK, SNAKE, some mixed players) will cooperate sometimes, especially when cooperation appears to be paying off; a few are inclined to free-ride (RABBIT, CROCODILE, PARROT) and will mostly defect.
- Rewards will be used sparingly to encourage contributors (small 1–2 unit gestures), particularly by reciprocating cooperators like MOOSE and DUCK. Because reward senders are visible in the transcript but anonymous in the in-game summaries, players learn from contribution histories and reward patterns indirectly; chat messages will be rare but purposive (commitments, thanks).
- Over early rounds we should see modest cooperation grow as conditional cooperators respond to cooperator signals and small rewards; mid-game may show a peak in cooperation because rewards and reciprocity sustain it; toward the end an “endgame” decline (backloading of defection) becomes likely — fewer contributors and fewer rewards as players anticipate final rounds.

# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK, SNAKE, CROCODILE </PLAYERS>

This is a 26-round online public-goods game with 12 players. In every round each player either keeps all 20 coins privately (contribution 0) or contributes all 20 to the public pot (contribution 20). The total contributed each round is multiplied by 2.9 and split equally among everyone. After payoffs are revealed, players may spend coins to punish (cost 1 to sender, deduct 2 from recipient per unit) or to reward (cost 1 to sender, add 1 to recipient per unit). Players see only their own round summary and cannot identify who punished/rewarded them.

Likely dynamics:
- Early rounds typically see moderate-to-high cooperation from prosocial players (GORILLA, DOG, DOG's allies) and some steady free-riders (MOOSE, SNAKE, CHICK early). Cooperation is sustained by occasional targeted punishments and occasional small rewards to reinforce cooperation.
- Punishments are sparse and mostly directed at clear non-cooperators; rewards are rarer and usually small, used to reinforce cooperation or reward punishers.
- Over the horizon (toward the final rounds) cooperation slowly decays (end-game effect). As fewer players contribute, punishment remains but becomes less effective at restoring full cooperation; players who have been consistently cooperative (GORILLA, DOG) tend to keep cooperating longer.
- Chats are sparse and mostly normative: short calls to cooperate, occasional threats or expressions of frustration.

Below is a round-by-round transcript from round 1 through round 26. Each round starts with an explanation, then any brief chat, the contributions (in the exact avatar order listed at the top), the punishment/reward actions (as tuples (source, target, unit); positive for rewards, negative for punishments), and then the summary shown to players listing their own net payoff for the round and their spending/receipt related to punishment and reward.

game_explanation:
This online public-goods game pits 13 players against a classic tension: contribute 20 coins to a shared pot (which benefits everyone when multiplied) or keep the 20 privately. The pot is multiplied by 2.6 and split evenly, so a lone contributor suffers a private loss but benefits the group; unconditional free-riding yields private gain but risks social sanctions. Because players see only aggregated outcomes and anonymous punishment/reward totals (not who did what), incentives to enforce cooperation come from altruistic preferences and deterrence: some players will use costly punishment to deter persistent free-riders, and some will use costly rewards to reinforce cooperators. Typical dynamics: an initial mixed level of cooperation driven by intrinsic prosocials (e.g., DOG, CROCODILE, DUCK, CHICKEN) and defectors (e.g., SLOTH, PARROT, CHICK). Sparse punishment and rewards appear sporadically; punishments are used cautiously because they are costly to the sender but can reduce long-run free-riding. Over rounds cooperation may drift downward as players test incentives or anticipate the end-game; targeted punishments can recover cooperation temporarily. Final-round free-riding is possible because future retaliation is irrelevant. Uncertainty remains: who will punish or reward is driven by private tendencies (some players are predisposed to reward or punish) and by observed history; some players will be stubbornly selfish and others consistently cooperative.

game_explanation:
This public-goods game sets up a standard social dilemma: each player chooses whether to keep 20 coins privately (contribute 0) or leave the 20 in the common pot (contribute 20). The pot is multiplied by 2.9 and divided equally, so contributing helps the group but is individually costly when many others free-ride. Because contributions are revealed before rewarding, players can target rewards to cooperators and try to sustain cooperation; however reward receipts are anonymous in the summary, so feedback is noisy. Typical dynamics: initially pro-social players (high trust/send tendencies) contribute, defectors free-ride, cooperators attempt to sustain cooperation with small directed rewards. If rewards compensate cooperative losses, cooperation can stabilize among a core; otherwise cooperators shrink. Toward the end of a finite 26-round game we expect an “endgame” decline in cooperation as future retaliation or reciprocal incentives vanish. Chats are usually sparse and rewards small and targeted.

Contributions are multiplied by 2.6 and split equally, so when many players contribute everyone benefits from the multiplied pot, but the individual incentive is to free-ride (keep your 20 while others contribute). Because players cannot observe others' choices before deciding, early-round behavior is driven by priors about others (player personas) and by general social tendencies. Over rounds, players can use small, costly rewards to encourage cooperators — reward units cost 3 to the giver and give 4 to the receiver. Rewards are typically sparse in real PGGs because they are personally costly; they are most often targeted at visible cooperators to reinforce contribution.

Given the supplied personas, I predict an initial moderate level of cooperation from prosocial avatars (PARROT, CHICK, SNAKE, COW, CHICK) and lower cooperation from the more selfish/low-giving avatars (OWL, CROCODILE, DUCK, DOG). SLOTH, GORILLA, MOOSE, and RABBIT are mixed and may move around between contributing and free-riding. Because rewards are costly, expect a few 1-unit rewards each round targeted mostly at contributors; these rewards will help sustain cooperation for a while but not fully prevent a gradual decline across 14 rounds as free-riding temptation and endgame effects accumulate. Rewards will be sparse and mostly single-unit gestures directed at the most consistently cooperative players (PARROT, CHICK, SNAKE, COW, occasionally MOOSE).

game_explanation:
This 12-player repeated public-goods game combines a binary contribution choice (contribute the full 20 or take 20 privately) with costly punishment and rewarding. Early rounds typically show a mix of cooperation and a few free-riders; cooperators sometimes use small punishments or occasional rewards to enforce norms. Punishment is costly to both sides (source pays 1 per unit, target loses 2 per unit), so players punish sparingly — often only after repeated defection or blatant free-riding. Rewards are rarer and usually small, used to reinforce good behavior.

Because players know the game lasts exactly 26 rounds, we expect a gradual decline in cooperation toward the end (the "endgame effect"): many players will continue cooperating in the middle rounds due to norm enforcement and goodwill, but some opportunistic tests or re-entries to defection occur. In late rounds, cooperation collapses as backward induction and short-run incentives dominate. Chat messages are sparse and usually used to call out defectors or encourage cooperation.

Because players know the game lasts exactly 26 rounds, we expect a gradual decline in cooperation toward the end (the "endgame effect"): many players will continue cooperating in the middle rounds due to norm enforcement and goodwill, but some opportunistic tests or re-entries to defection occur. In late rounds, cooperation collapses as backward induction and short-run incentives dominate. Chat messages are sparse and usually used to call out defectors or encourage cooperation.

game_explanation:
This is a 14-round binary public goods game with 13 players. Each round every player privately decides whether to contribute their entire 20-coin endowment (cooperate) or keep it (defect). All contributions go to a common pot, which is multiplied by 2.6 and then split evenly among the active players. After contributions are revealed, players may spend costly actions to punish (cost 3 to source, −11 to target per punishment unit) or reward (cost 3 to source, +4 to target per reward unit) others. Punishment and reward are sparse in most real PGGs because they are costly; they are used mainly to deter or reinforce behavior. Players see round summaries (their own net payoff, how much they spent on punishment/reward, and amounts received/deducted) and the peers’ aggregate summaries, but they cannot identify who punished or rewarded anyone.

Likely dynamics: many players with pro-social priors (GORILLA, PARROT, COW, MOOSE, DUCK, SLOTH) will tend to contribute early. Some players with low pro-social priors (SNAKE, CROCODILE, CHICK, CHICKEN) are more likely to defect. Sparse punishment by players who dislike free-riding (e.g., OWL) and occasional rewards by especially generous players (e.g., PARROT, GORILLA, COW) will sometimes push defectors to return to cooperation. Over repeated rounds cooperation typically rises at first but drifts down as players learn that others sometimes defect and as the “endgame” approaches; punishment can slow decay but is costly and thus used sparingly, and it often declines in the final rounds.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game with 12 players, binary contribution choices each round (contribute all 20 or withdraw all 20), a high multiplier on the common pot (2.9×) and an equal split, and a thin layer of costly positive reciprocity (reward units that cost 1 to the sender and give 1 to the recipient). The fundamental tension: free-riders (who keep 20 and still get a share of the multiplied pot) typically earn more immediately than contributors; that creates pressure for cooperation to unravel. Positive rewards can partially offset that incentive by transferring coins from some players to others and by signaling/encouraging contributors, but rewards are themselves costly and cannot directly punish defectors — they only reward valued behavior.

Given the provided personas, I expect a small core of reliably prosocial players (e.g., SLOTH and a few early cooperators) to keep contributing most rounds; a mixed group (OWL, RABBIT, CHICK, CHICKEN, DUCK at times) to shift back and forth; and a set of steady defectors (GORILLA, SNAKE, PARROT, MOOSE) who rarely contribute. Rewards will be sparse and mostly directed at contributors to sustain a cooperative nucleus; cooperation will decline from early rounds but not vanish completely — especially SLOTH (very cooperative) will keep contributing and occasionally reward others. Chats will be infrequent and used to note perceived fairness or call out patterns.

game_explanation:
This 26-round public goods game has clear tensions. Contributing (leaving 20 coins in the pot) is socially efficient because the pot is multiplied by 2.9 and then shared, so groups of contributors raise everyone’s payoffs. Withdrawing (taking 20 coins privately) is individually safe but reduces the public return for others. Players have heterogeneous priors: some (PARROT, MOOSE, GORILLA, DUCK) are relatively pro-social and likely to contribute frequently; others (DOG, RABBIT, OWL) are consistently self-interested and likely to withhold contributions; several players are mixed and may adapt to what they see (SLOTH, CHICKEN, CHICK, SNAKE, CROCODILE).

Because contributions are revealed each round before rewards, contributors can be rewarded and free-riders can be ignored (or indirectly “punished” by withholding rewards). Rewards are costly (1 coin per unit) so players will use them sparingly to signal approval or reinforce cooperation. Expect early rounds to show moderate-to-high cooperation from the prosocials and sparse rewarding targeted to contributors. Over the middle rounds, exploitation by defectors may erode cooperation among the mixed types, producing fluctuation. Toward the end (final few rounds), standard backward-induction/endgame effects tend to reduce contributions as players anticipate there's little future to sustain reciprocity; rewards drop as players conserve resources.

The transcript below follows those dynamics. I show each round’s short explanation, any chat, the contribution vector (in the required avatar order), any reward actions (source, target, units), and occasional summary chat lines. Rewards are kept sparse and mainly directed to contributors.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game with 12 players and a fixed 26-round horizon. Each round every player faces a simple binary choice: keep the whole 20 privately (contribute 0) or leave it in the shared pot (contribute 20). The pot is multiplied by 2.9 and split equally, so free-riding (keeping 20) gives an immediate private advantage unless others contribute. Players can reward others after redistribution (cost 1 to giver, +1 to receiver); rewards are the only costly interpersonal sanction/reward available. Players see only their own round payoff and reward use/receipt, not who acted.

Likely high-level dynamics:
- Early rounds: some pro-social players (DUCK, DOG, SNAKE and a few others) will contribute, producing a visible group return advantage for contributors but a private advantage for free-riders. A few players will use small positive rewards to encourage cooperators.
- Middle rounds: as players observe personal payoffs (cooperators earning less than defectors in mixed groups) and because contributions are all-or-nothing, cooperation typically declines. Rewards are used sporadically to prop up cooperators; those efforts have mixed success.
- Endgame (last several rounds): with a known finite horizon, many players stop cooperating — cooperation typically collapses. A few stubborn prosocials may continue contributing a bit longer, sometimes receiving small rewards from allies, but overall contributions trend toward zero by the final rounds.

Uncertainties:
- Some players are “very cooperative” in their priors (DUCK, DOG, SNAKE) and likely to sustain cooperation longer; others (GORILLA, CHICKEN, CHICK) are likely to defect almost always. Medium-type players (MOOSE, RABBIT, CROCODILE, SLOTH, PARROT, OWL) may switch depending on observed outcomes and rewards. Rewarding is costly, so it will be sparing and targeted.

game_explanation:
This 12-player public goods game has a strong individual incentive to withdraw the full 20 coins each round (defect) because the per-contributor return (2.9*20/12 ≈ 4.83) is far below 20. However, repeated interaction (26 rounds) plus the punishment/reward stage creates scope for social enforcement: cooperators can punish persistent defectors (at cost to themselves) or reward cooperators to sustain contributions. Expect an initial testing phase where some players contribute and others free-ride. Cooperators — especially players with cooperative priors (GORILLA, CHICKEN, RABBIT, OWL, SNAKE) — will mostly contribute early and may occasionally punish or reward. Low-cooperation priors (CHICK, PARROT, DUCK, sometimes SLOTH) will tend to defect, at least early and in the final rounds as the endgame approaches.

Punishment is costly (1 to source, -2 to target) so it will be used sparingly and mostly against repeated defectors; rewards are also used sparingly to reinforce cooperation. Over rounds, expect some convergence to a norm: many players shift toward contributing to avoid being punished, while a few persist as free-riders. In the final 3–4 rounds the incentive to enforce cooperation weakens (limited future retaliation), so cooperation typically unravels as some players revert to defecting. Chat will be sparse and used for short calls-to-cooperate, warnings, or thanks.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game with 13 players, 14 rounds, and two key instruments: a binary contribution (0 or 20) and a costly positive reward (cost 3 to the source, gives +4 to the recipient). Mechanically, each round each player keeps 20 minus their contribution and then receives an equal share of the multiplied pot (sum of contributions × 2.6, split among all 13 players). Because the multiplier (2.6) is less than group size (13), the private marginal return from contributing (2.6/13 per coin) is much smaller than the private cost: a player who contributes 20 loses 20 but directly recoups only 20*(2.6/13)=4 to themselves — a net private loss of 16 compared with free-riding. So the one-shot incentive is to free-ride, but group welfare is maximized when people contribute. Rewards provide a possible incentive: each reward unit costs 3 but gives the recipient +4, so rewarding can be used to support cooperators (socially beneficial overall) but is itself costly for the sender.

Empirically, repeated PGGs like this often start with substantial cooperation from more pro-social players, then drift downward as free-riding appears and retaliation/withholding of contributions follows. Because players cannot see choices before acting and only learn round summaries (their own payoff, their own reward given, and amounts of reward they received — and peers' corresponding numbers but not identity of the rewarders), beliefs about others matter and information is noisy. Rewarding tends to be sparse in real data — occasional small positive transfers aimed at sustaining cooperation or reciprocating past kindness. Near the end of a known-horizon game, many players defect more strongly (end-game effect), although a few may keep contributing out of principle.

Using the player priors provided, I expect:
- Early rounds: many high-trust or high-contribution-leaning players (DOG, MOOSE, SNAKE, RABBIT, DUCK, CHICKEN, CHICK) will contribute 20; more self-oriented types (SLOTH, PARROT, OWL, some mixed types) will start at 0. A cluster of contributors will get small, targeted rewards from neighbors who value cooperation.

- Late rounds: overall contributions will decline substantially; rewards become rarer as players conserve coins and anticipate the end.

reflection:
This is a repeated public-goods game with 13 players, 14 rounds, and two key instruments: a binary contribution (0 or 20) and a costly positive reward (cost 3 to the source, gives +4 to the recipient). Mechanically, each round each player keeps 20 minus their contribution and then receives an equal share of the multiplied pot (sum of contributions × 2.6, split among all 13 players). Because the multiplier (2.6) is less than group size (13), the private marginal return from contributing (2.6/13 per coin) is much smaller than the private cost: a player who contributes 20 loses 20 but directly recoups only 20*(2.6/13)=4 to themselves — a net private loss of 16 compared with free-riding. So the one-shot incentive is to free-ride, but group welfare is maximized when people contribute. Rewards provide a possible incentive: each reward unit costs 3 but gives the recipient +4, so rewarding can be used to support cooperators (socially beneficial overall) but is itself costly for the sender.

Empirically, repeated PGGs like this often start with substantial cooperation from more pro-social players, then drift downward as free-riding appears and retaliation/withholding of contributions follows. Because players cannot see choices before acting and only learn round summaries (their own payoff, their own reward given, and amounts of reward they received — and peers' corresponding numbers but not identity of the rewarders), beliefs about others matter and information is noisy. Rewarding tends to be sparse in real data — occasional small positive transfers aimed at sustaining cooperation or reciprocating past kindness. Near the end of a known-horizon game, many players defect more strongly (end-game effect), although a few may keep contributing out of principle.

Using the player priors provided, I expect:
- Early rounds: many high-trust or high-contribution-leaning players (DOG, MOOSE, SNAKE, RABBIT, DUCK, CHICKEN, CHICK) will contribute 20; more self-oriented types (SLOTH, PARROT, OWL, some mixed types) will start at 0. A cluster of contributors will get small, targeted rewards from neighbors who value cooperation.
- Middle rounds: contributions will fluctuate and trend downward as a few players experiment with free-riding and others respond; occasional rewards will be used strategically but sparsely to prop up cooperators.
- Late rounds: overall contributions will decline substantially; rewards become rarer as players conserve coins and anticipate the end.

Below is a round-by-round simulated transcript consistent with these incentives and priors. Reward actions are sparse and small (1 unit = cost 3, benefit 4). Only non-zero reward edges are listed.

game_explanation:
This is a standard finite public goods game with 13 players, repeated for 14 rounds. Each round players decide privately whether to contribute their entire endowment (20) or keep it (0). Contributions are multiplied by 2.6 and split equally, so any individual contribution produces a personal return of 0.2 * 20 = 4 to the contributor but gives other players an equal share as well. Absent social preferences and rewards, the dominant temptation is to free-ride (keep 20) because contributing lowers an individual's payoff by 16 given the per-person share. The reward mechanism (cost 3 to giver, +4 to recipient per reward unit) allows players to selectively reward cooperators and thus partly offset the private cost of contributing — but rewards themselves are costly so they are typically used sparingly.

Given the provided personas, we expect a small stable core of intrinsically cooperative players (MOOSE, PARROT, DUCK, CHICK/CHICKEN at times) to begin by contributing. Many low-trust or low-return players (SLOTH, RABBIT, CROCODILE, DOG, SNAKE) are likely to free-ride from the start. Because rewards are allowed but costly and are anonymous in the summary, we predict modest, targeted reciprocity: cooperators (especially PARROT and MOOSE) will reward one another repeatedly, while others send few or no rewards. Observing that free-riders systematically earn more in early rounds, some conditional cooperators will reduce contributions over time; nonetheless, repeated mutual rewarding among a core subset can sustain some cooperation through the middle and late rounds. Uncertainty remains about how often conditional cooperators (GORILLA, OWL, CHICKEN, CHICK, DUCK) will switch — so we model a gradual decline in overall contributions with a persistent two-person cooperative kernel (PARROT and MOOSE), occasional support from DUCK and CHICK, and sparse reward transfers targeted to reward cooperate behavior.

game_explanation:
This 14-round public-goods game sets up a tension between short-term self-interest (keep your 20 coins) and group benefit (contribute 20 to a pot that is multiplied and shared). With anonymous, simultaneous contributions each round, free-riding is initially attractive: non-contributors keep the private 20 and still receive their share of the pot. However, the small, costly reward mechanism (cost 3 to the sender, gives 4 to a chosen peer) allows cooperators to incentivize each other and selectively bolster contributors. Because rewards are visible in the round summaries (how much each player gave and received) but players cannot be uniquely identified as the source of reward receipts, incentives to reward are tempered by noise — rewarding is useful to encourage cooperators, but it’s hard to single-handedly signal to a specific cooperator that you are supporting them. Typical dynamics expected here:
- A core of naturally cooperative personas (DUCK, DOG, RABBIT, SNAKE, OWL, COW, and often MOOSE) will contribute early and repeatedly.
- A set of likely defectors (SLOTH, GORILLA, PARROT, CHICK) will typically free-ride, occasionally experimenting with contribution.
- Conditional cooperators (CHICKEN, CROCODILE, MOOSE) may contribute in some rounds and not others.
- Cooperators will reward each other sparingly (1 unit is common) to reinforce cooperation; rewards will be concentrated on reliable contributors and occasionally used to encourage one-off contributors.
- Over time, rewards will taper and some conditional cooperators may lapse, and in the final round many players will reduce contributions because future reciprocity is impossible.

Below is a round-by-round transcript consistent with these priors: who contributes (0 or 20), who sends small reward units to whom, and short explanations for each round's expected moves.

# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK, SNAKE, CROCODILE </PLAYERS>

This is a repeated public-goods game with 12 players, a 2.9 multiplier on the pot and a small, costly reward stage after redistribution. Each player must either contribute all 20 coins (cooperate) or withdraw all 20 (defect). Because the marginal per-coin return to any single contributor is 2.9/12 ≈ 0.242 < 1, contributing is individually costly even though it raises the group's total. Without explicit identification of who contributed, contributors are vulnerable to exploitation: defectors keep private money and still share the multiplied pot. The reward stage can partially offset that exploitation by transferring coins to cooperators, but rewards are costly to send and anonymous in the summary (players don't know who sent which reward), which limits precise reciprocity.

Given the player personas, some players (GORILLA, CHICK, DOG, CHICKEN, CHICK, OWL, RABBIT) are relatively pro-social and likely to start by contributing, while others (SLOTH, DUCK, DUCK-like DUCK, MOOSE, DUCK, DUCK) are likely to free-ride. Conditional/coaxing behavior (PARROT, SNAKE, CROCODILE) may experiment early and then adjust based on observed payoffs. Expect initial cooperation by a block of players, followed by gradual decline as contributors see lower net payoffs than withdrawers, with a small core of steady cooperators persisting. Rewards will be sparse and mainly flow from committed cooperators to other cooperators (small positive reinforcement), not mass redistribution. Chat will be infrequent and used for encouragement, mild admonishments, or short declarations of intent.

This 26-round public goods game forces a binary choice each round: contribute all 20 coins (cooperate) or withdraw them all (defect). The shared pot is multiplied by 2.9 and split equally, so defectors get a short-term private advantage every round if many others contribute. Because contributions are revealed each round and rewards are available (costly to give, beneficial to recipient), we expect a few dynamics:

- Early uncertainty: some prosocial types (MOOSE, RABBIT, SLOTH, PARROT, CROCODILE) will tend to contribute early, while low-giving types (CHICKEN, OWL, CHICK, SNAKE, DOG) will try defecting. Mixed types (GORILLA, DUCK) will be conditional.
- Selective rewarding: a small set of players who value cooperation will use sparse, directed rewards to reinforce contributors; rewards are costly, so they will be used sparingly and strategically (1–2 units typically) rather than widely.
- Conditional cooperation and reputation: cooperators who see others cooperate (or are rewarded) are more likely to keep cooperating; defectors who are singled out with withheld rewards may be nudged toward cooperation, but because rewards are positive only, enforcement is mainly through preferential rewarding, not punishment.
- End-game decline: toward the final rounds (especially the last 3–4 rounds), cooperation will drift down as the finite horizon becomes salient; some persistent cooperators may keep contributing out of preference or consistency, but many will defect in the final round.
- Chat will be sparse and purposeful: occasional calls to cooperate, thanks, or short threats/promises about rewarding cooperators.

I will simulate likely contributions, sparse chat, and targeted rewards for rounds 1–26. The contribution list uses the exact avatar order. Reward lists include only non-zero edges as tuples (source_avatar, target_avatar, unit).

game_explanation:
This is a 14-round online public-goods game with 13 players. Each round every player privately decides whether to contribute their whole 20-coin endowment (cooperate) or nothing (defect). Contributions are multiplied by 2.6 and shared equally among all players; punishment and reward occur after redistribution. Punishment is costly to the sender (3 coins per punishment unit) and very costly to the target (-11 coins per punishment unit). Rewards are also costly to the sender (3 coins) but beneficial to the target (+4 coins).

Likely dynamics and incentives:
- Individual incentive: given the multiplier and group size, any single contribution returns only (2.6*20)/13 = 4 coins to the contributor, so contributing is individually costly (net -16) relative to keeping the 20. That creates a temptation to free-ride.
- Social dynamics: cooperators may use sparse punishment to discourage repeated free-riding and use sparse rewards to reinforce cooperation. Punishment is effective but costly to send, so expect punishments to be used sparingly and targeted at repeat defectors. Rewards will appear occasionally between reciprocating cooperators.
- Uncertainties: whether formerly punished players switch to cooperating depends on how strongly they care about avoiding punishment; some low-altruism types may continue to defect until punished repeatedly. Toward the final rounds, cooperation typically collapses (end-game effect), so expect more defection and less punishment in rounds 13–14.
- Over the 14 rounds we predict: an initial moderately high cooperation rate (many contribute in early rounds), targeted punishments of obvious defectors early on, a period of sustained high cooperation when punishments are effective, and a gradual erosion of cooperation in the final rounds as future retaliation possibilities vanish.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with 13 players, 14 rounds, and binary contributions (0 or 20). Each round players decide simultaneously whether to contribute their entire 20 or keep it. Contributions are pooled, multiplied by 2.6, and split equally; because 2.6/13 = 0.2, each coin contributed by any player increases every player's share by 0.2. That creates a tension: contributing costs you 20 privately but increases everyone's payout; free-riding (contributing 0) yields higher immediate payoff if others contribute.

After contributions are revealed, players can spend coins to punish or reward specific peers. Punishment units are costly to the sender (3 per unit) but remove a lot from the target (11 per unit). Rewards cost 3 to send and give +4 to the recipient. These incentives make punishment an expensive enforcement device and rewards modest but less costly to targets.

Given the player personas, I predict:
- A cluster of generally cooperative players (RABBIT, CHICK, CROCODILE, COW, CHICK, DOG, DUCK, PARROT) will contribute frequently early on.
- A small set of more selfish or low-return players (SLOTH, OWL, CHICKEN, SNAKE) will often defect early.
- DOG and a few others with fairness/retaliation tendencies will punish persistent defectors, but punishment will be used sparingly because it's costly to the sender.
- RABBIT/COW/PARROT will occasionally reward cooperators to encourage cooperation.
- Cooperation will start fairly high, dip as some test free-riding, and show local rebounds when punishments or rewards signal norms — then decline again toward the final rounds (players know the game ends), with many players defecting late. Punishment frequency will be low-to-moderate; rewards will be used occasionally to reinforce cooperators.

Avatar order is always: SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK, SNAKE, CROCODILE, COW.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game with 12 players, 26 rounds, binary contributions each round (either leave the full 20 in the public pot or withdraw it all). Multiplication by 2.9 and equal split creates a tension: contributing is costly privately but benefits the whole group, and defecting yields a private gain plus the shared benefit. Because players observe only aggregate outcomes and anonymous punish/reward signals, reputations are coarse and enforcement must be costly (punishers pay 1 per unit). Expect heterogeneous behavior reflecting the player priors: some players (DOG, CHICKEN, CHICK, GORILLA, PARROT) are inclined to contribute; others (SLOTH, RABBIT, OWL, SNAKE) tend to free-ride. Mixed/conditional players (DUCK, MOOSE, CROCODILE) will track whether cooperators punish or reward: when defectors are punished and cooperators are rewarded, cooperation can increase; toward the end (final rounds) forward-looking incentives disappear and cooperation typically collapses.

Likely unfolding:
- Early rounds: moderate cooperation (several contributors), sparse rewards and light punishments as players test norms.
- Middle rounds: if punishers act, defectors get targeted and cooperation may rise/stabilize; cooperators sometimes reward each other.
- Late rounds: forward-looking motives weaken; many players start to defect, punishments become less effective and more sporadic; by round 26 expect near-universal defection.

game_explanation:
This public goods game is a repeated, anonymous interaction with 13 players each round. Each player chooses between keeping all 20 coins privately or contributing all 20 to a shared pot that is multiplied by 2.6 and split equally. Because the multiplier (2.6) divided by group size (13) yields 0.2 per contributed coin to each player, contributing is privately costly: given the same number of contributors, a cooperator always ends up with 20 coins less than a free-rider. That creates a classic social dilemma: group payoff is maximized by more contributors, but individual incentives push toward free-riding.

The reward stage (cost 3 to giver, +4 to target per reward unit) lets players reward others after observing contributions. Rewards are costly and used sparingly in most experiments; they can sustain cooperation by compensating cooperators or signaling approval, but anonymity (receivers do not see who rewarded them) weakens direct reciprocity. Players see amounts each peer spent/received on rewards in the summary, which allows noisy inference about who is supporting cooperation and may shape later rounds.

Given the player personas, I predict a mix of stable cooperators (DUCK, DOG, MOOSE, GORILLA, sometimes COW and CHICK), persistent free-riders (RABBIT, CROCODILE, CHICKEN, OWL), and several conditional/mixed players (SLOTH, PARROT, SNAKE, CHICK). Early rounds show a moderate level of cooperation (several prosocials contribute). Small, targeted positive rewards flow from cooperators to other cooperators. Midgame cooperation can rise as conditional cooperators join after receiving rewards; late-game (anticipating the end) cooperation drifts down as players reduce contributions and rewards. Rewards remain sparse throughout: a few 1-unit rewards directed at contributors each round rather than broad redistribution.

game_explanation:
This is a 26-round public goods game with 12 players. Each round every player chooses either to contribute all 20 coins (leave 0 for private use) or withdraw all 20 (contribute 0). The group pot is multiplied by 2.9 and split equally; so contributing benefits everyone but is individually costly. Punishment (cost 1 to sender, −2 to target per unit) and reward (cost 1 to sender, +1 to target per unit) allow players to sanction or reinforce behavior. Choices are simultaneous and anonymous with regard to contribution and to who punished or rewarded you. Knowing the game length (26 rounds) encourages endgame decline in cooperation; early rounds are more conducive to cooperation. Given the player personas, expect a stable cooperative core (CROCODILE, RABBIT, SNAKE, some others) who contribute early; some persistent defectors (MOOSE, DOG) who rarely contribute; a handful of conditional cooperators (SLOTH, GORILLA, OWL, CHICK) who can be nudged by rewards or light punishment; rewards used sparingly to encourage cooperation and occasional punishment used sparingly against persistent free-riders. Over time cooperation will be gradually eroded, especially in the final few rounds, as the incentive to sustain cooperation falls.

game_explanation:
This public-goods game pits private incentives (keep 20) against a group benefit from contributing (20 to the pot becomes 52 and is shared). Individually, keeping your 20 and free-riding always yields a higher private payoff given any fixed number of contributors, so defection is the dominant selfish choice. However, many players have some tendency toward conditional cooperation (rewarding or matching others) and some are more prosocially inclined; that makes early, small pockets of cooperation possible. Rewards provide a costly way to support cooperators (each reward unit costs the sender 3 coins and gives the recipient 4 coins), but rewards are anonymous as to who gave them in the summary, so rewards mainly serve as private incentives to sustain contribution rather than public reputational signals.

Typical unfolding: a small set of cooperative-minded players contribute early; they are rarely enough to overcome the free-riding temptation of others, so overall contribution rates tend to fall over rounds. Rewards are used sparingly to reinforce cooperation in the early-to-mid rounds by those who care about group outcomes or fairness. As the horizon approaches, contributions usually decline (end-game effect) and rewards dwindle. The predictions below use the player personas as priors: CHICKEN and MOOSE are the most likely steady cooperators; CROCODILE, CHICK and RABBIT may cooperate early but are more variable; PARROT, DOG, SLOTH, DUCK and OWL lean toward defection. Rewards are modest and targeted to visible cooperators.

game_explanation:
This public-goods game pits a small group of mixed pro-social and self-interested players against the usual incentives: contributing (leaving the 20 coins in the shared pot) increases the group's total payout but is individually costly; withholding (withdrawing 20) yields a private gain but lowers the group payoff. Because contributions are all-or-nothing (0 or 20), choices are stark and easy to interpret by others' payoff patterns (even though identities of punishers/rewarders are not shown). The presence of low-cost rewards and costly punishments creates mechanisms for enforcement and social signaling: a few players can use sanctions to discipline free-riders or use rewards to reinforce cooperators. However, both tools are used sparingly in typical experiments.

Early rounds tend to reveal core types: a cluster of reliable cooperators (e.g., OWL, SLOTH, some medium prosocials) will contribute while a handful of consistent free-riders (PARROT, DUCK, CHICK) will withhold. If cooperative players begin to punish defectors, we often see partial behavioral convergence toward cooperation in the middle of the horizon. Near the end of the game (final rounds), strategic considerations (end-game effects) and the costliness of punishment commonly lead to unraveling: some players defect again because future retaliation is limited. Messages are infrequent and usually used to praise, threaten, or coordinate; punishment is likewise sparse and targeted.

Against this background and given the player priors, I expect the first few rounds to show a majority of contributions with a small subset of defectors. Targeted punishment from a few norm-enforcers will follow, increasing cooperation mid-game. Later rounds will show more stability, with a small resurgence of defection as the end of the game approaches, and a final-round dip in contributions.

game_explanation:
This repeated public-goods game pits individual incentives (keep your 20 each round) against group payoffs (contribute 20 and raise the shared pot). The marginal per-coin return to any individual from the pot is 1.9/10 = 0.19 coins, well below 1, so the one-shot dominant strategy is to contribute 0. Yet because the game is repeated for 15 rounds and players have heterogeneous social preferences (some reliably cooperative, some reliably selfish, others conditional), we expect dynamic patterns:
- Early rounds: conditional cooperators and very prosocial types (DOG, CHICKEN, DUCK, OWL, PARROT, possibly GORILLA) will give contributions, trying to build cooperation. Selfish types (SLOTH, MOOSE, RABBIT) will free-ride.
- Middle rounds: as defectors earn higher net payoffs by free-riding, some conditional cooperators will withdraw; cooperation will often erode, producing oscillations (short-lived recoveries when a few try to rebuild).
- Late rounds / endgame: forward-looking players (especially less prosocial ones) will defect more; cooperation will typically collapse toward more zeros. A highly prosocial outlier (DOG) is likely to keep contributing much of the time.
- Communication will be sparse and strategic: a few short pre-commitments, encouragements, or complaints. Because contributions are binary (0/20) and players only see round summaries (their own and others’ payoffs), decisions will revolve around observed payoff patterns rather than mood-laden chit-chat.

Now the play-by-play transcript, rounds 1–15.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK, SNAKE, CROCODILE, COW, PINGUIN, MONKEY </PLAYERS>

This is a repeated, anonymous public-goods game with voluntary costly rewards. Each round players choose a contribution (0–20) from a private endowment of 20. The pot is multiplied by 3.7 and split equally. After seeing everyone’s contributions and summary reward totals, players may spend coins to reward others (cost 4 per reward unit, gives 5 to the target). Players don’t know the total number of rounds, which typically encourages continued cooperation early on and gradual adjustments based on peers’ actions.

Likely dynamics:
- Some players are predisposed to free-ride (SLOTH, DOG, MONKEY, maybe CROCODILE and SNAKE) and will contribute very little or zero across rounds. They rarely reward others.
- A cluster of conditional cooperators (RABBIT, CHICK, PINGUIN, MOOSE, PARROT, CHICKEN) will contribute moderate-to-high amounts, especially early, and reward those who contribute more.
- Mixed strategists (GORILLA, OWL, COW, DUCK) will tend toward moderate contributions and adjust gradually to observed returns.
- Rewarding is costly so it will be used sparingly and mostly directed at consistently high contributors (MOOSE, PINGUIN, RABBIT, PARROT) rather than to punish defectors.
- Over rounds we expect a stable core of contributors to persist; defectors receive few or no rewards and remain low. Small fluctuations occur as players react to the group average and to being rewarded.

game_explanation:
This repeated public goods game pits 15 players each round with 20 private coins. Each simultaneously chooses an integer contribution (0–20) to the group pot; the pot is multiplied by 3.7 and split equally. After payoffs are redistributed, players can spend costly rewards (each unit costs 4 to the sender and gives 5 to a chosen target). Players do not know how many rounds will be played and cannot see others' decisions before contributing; they do see peers' contributions and reward totals (but not who gave them) in the round summary.

Likely unfolding:
- Pro-social avatars (PARROT, SNAKE, MOOSE, DOG, possibly SLOTH) tend to contribute high amounts and use sparse rewards to encourage each other.
- Some avatars (COW, PINGUIN, DUCK, MONKEY, sometimes CHICKEN) have low giving tendencies and will often free-ride.
- Rewards will be used sparingly and targeted: small positive incentives to consistent cooperators rather than broad redistribution. Because reward sources are anonymous in the summary, players who care about reciprocity will still use rewards as signals to sustain cooperation; others will simply withhold rewards and free-ride.
- Over rounds we expect a stable core of high contributors who mutually reward (often 1 unit), while several low contributors persist. Contributions will drift modestly (small declines due to temptation to free-ride and uncertainty about horizon) but remain higher for consistently prosocial players.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK, SNAKE, CROCODILE, COW, PINGUIN, MONKEY </PLAYERS>

This online public goods game typically shows tension between private incentives (keep your 20 coins) and group gains (contribute to the pot to get multiplied benefit). Because the pot is multiplied by 3.7 and split across all 15 players, the social optimum is high contributions, but the individual incentive is to free-ride and enjoy others' contributions. Players are uncertain about others' behavior and do not know the total number of rounds; that encourages cautious strategies and gradual learning.

Given the provided personas, I expect a mix of stable cooperators (MOOSE, SNAKE, CROCODILE, COW, MONKEY, OWL) who start or stay relatively generous, and a few consistent low contributors (GORILLA, PARROT, some low-scoring Duck/Dog/Chick variants). Conditional cooperators (many of the mixed-score players) will adjust their contributions slowly in response to the net payoff they see. Punishment and reward are costly: punishment costs the sender 4 coins and subtracts 7 from the target; reward costs 4 and adds 5 to the target. Because those costs are nontrivial, punishments and rewards usually happen sparingly and targetedly — typically directed at clear free-riders (e.g., GORILLA, PARROT) or used to reinforce high contributors (a MOOSE/SNAKE/CROCODILE cluster). Expect a handful of punishments aimed at persistent zero contributors and occasional small rewards from prosocial players to sustain cooperation. Over rounds, small adjustments occur: some defectors stay low (getting punished occasionally), some marginal players inch up, and cooperators try to sustain contributions by rewarding each other and by selective punishment.

By now patterns are clearer. Some lower contributors (PARROT) continue slowly increasing from 0 to small amounts; the prosocial cluster continues targeted rewards and a few persistent punishers keep penalizing the zero contributor. Contributions are fairly stable.

In the final observed round, small positive changes have accumulated for marginal contributors (PARROT), but persistent zero contributions by GORILLA continue to attract targeted punishments. Prosocial players keep modestly rewarding one another to sustain cooperative clusters.

game_explanation:
This 15-round public-goods game gives each player 20 coins every round and a simple binary choice: contribute 20 to the group pot or keep 20. Contributions are multiplied by 1.9 and split equally among the 10 players, so contributing benefits the group but costs the contributor immediately. After contributions are revealed, players can punish others at a cost of 4 coins per punishment unit (each unit removes 8 coins from the target). Because punishment is costly, players will use it sparingly — mainly to deter persistent free-riders who reduce group payoffs. Expect some early free-riding by players with low cooperative tendencies (e.g., GORILLA), targeted punishments by more reciprocity- or fairness-minded players (e.g., DUCK, MOOSE), and reactive learning: punished defectors often switch to contributing. Once cooperation becomes common, punishments fall away. Near the known end (last rounds) some players will try to exploit the final-period logic and defect, and punishers may reduce enforcement because punishment yields no future cooperation benefit. Chats are typically short warnings, explanations, or reactions; punishment events are also relatively rare and targeted.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK </PLAYERS>

This is a 15-round public goods game where each player can either contribute all 20 coins or nothing. The group pot is multiplied by 1.9 and split equally across the 10 players, so each player's per-round share equals 0.19 times the total contributions. Contributing is socially efficient (it raises the group return) but individually costly unless enough others also contribute. Because players only see round payoffs (not direct contributions) they will infer others' behavior from observed payoffs; free-riding is tempting because non-contributors keep their 20 plus the group share, often earning more than contributors. Given the personas:

- CHICK and CHICKEN are very likely to contribute consistently (strong unconditional cooperators).
- PARROT, DUCK, MOOSE, and SLOTH are somewhat inclined to cooperate early but may reduce contributions if they see exploitation.
- GORILLA, DOG, RABBIT, and OWL lean toward lower contributions (free-riding or opportunistic).
- Expect an initial burst of cooperation, then a decline as free-riders benefit. Occasional attempts to re-start cooperation may occur, and CHICKEN in particular is likely to keep contributing throughout. Chat will be sparse: a few appeals to cooperate early, occasional complaints or statements of strategy, but not constant discussion.

game_explanation:
This is a 15-round public goods game with 10 players. Each round, each player chooses either to contribute all 20 coins to the group pot or keep all 20 privately. The pot is multiplied by 1.9 and split evenly across all players: with 6 contributors (120 total), each player gets 22.8 from the pot; with different numbers of contributors the per-player share changes accordingly. Because defectors keep 20 privately and still receive a share of the pot, there's a strong short-run incentive to free-ride. However, participants can punish others after seeing contributions: each punishment unit costs 4 to the sender and subtracts 8 from the target. Punishment is costly to the punisher, so it tends to be used sparingly, typically by conditional cooperators to discipline repeat free-riders. Players know the game length (15 rounds), so punishment and contributions may be strategically deployed (e.g., greater punishment earlier to establish norms; last-round incentives may reduce punishment).

Given the personas, we expect several consistent defectors (MOOSE, GORILLA, SLOTH, DOG) who rarely contribute, and several conditional or consistent cooperators (CHICK, DUCK, PARROT, RABBIT, CHICKEN, OWL) who usually contribute and sometimes punish persistent defectors. Punishment is likely to be low-frequency and targeted at the persistent defectors, aimed to deter rather than to exact revenge. Chat will be sparse — short calls for cooperation or occasional reprimands.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK, SNAKE, CROCODILE, COW, PINGUIN, MONKEY </PLAYERS>

This is a repeated public-goods game with 15 players each round. Each player privately receives 20 coins and chooses an integer contribution (0–20). Contributions are multiplied by 3.7 then split equally among all active players, so each coin contributed returns about 0.247 coins to each player. Because 0.247 < 1, the private incentive is to contribute 0 (free-ride), but the group optimum is for everyone to contribute more. After contributions are revealed, players can punish (cost 4 per unit to sender, -7 per unit to target) or reward (cost 4 per unit to sender, +5 per unit to target). Punishment and reward are typically used sparingly in real PGGs; punishments target clear low contributors while rewards reinforce consistent cooperators. Players make decisions without knowing the total number of rounds and cannot see who punished or rewarded them, so reputation effects are noisy.

Given the player personas: GORILLA and CHICKEN are strong pro-social anchors and likely contribute high early; CROCODILE, SLOTH, MOOSE and some others have low giving tendencies and are more likely to free-ride; many players (DOG, COW, RABBIT, SNAKE, PINGUIN, PARROT, OWL, MONKEY) are moderate contributors and conditional cooperators who will adjust based on observed group behavior. Expect initial moderate-to-high contributions from several players, a few free-riders, a few targeted punishments and occasional rewards. Over rounds contributions often slowly decline unless enforcement (punishments/rewards) stabilizes cooperation; punishments will be infrequent but targeted, and rewards will be sparse but given to steady contributors.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated anonymous public-goods game with a low marginal per-capita return (MPCR = 3.7/15 ≈ 0.247). The selfish, payoff-maximizing move each round is to contribute 0, because each coin you keep yields more to you personally than the share it returns from the public pot. Despite that, many players carry social preferences or conditional-cooperation priors: some will contribute initially out of pro-sociality or trust, while others will free-ride.

The reward stage (cost 4 to giver, benefit 5 to recipient) makes rewarding a costly signal that slightly increases total group wealth (+1 net to group per reward). Because rewards are costly, they tend to be used sparingly and targeted: cooperators typically reward other cooperators to sustain cooperation; free riders rarely receive rewards. Over rounds, we should expect:
- An initial cluster of relatively high contributors (the prosocial personas) and a cluster of low contributors (the selfish or low-trust personas).
- Sparse, targeted rewards mostly flowing among high contributors, reinforcing a small cooperative core.
- Gradual erosion of broad cooperation as free-riding persists and reward budgets are limited; some stable reciprocity between a handful of cooperators might remain across rounds.
- Uncertainty about the total number of rounds increases experimentation and cautious adjustment; players update contributions based on whether cooperating was rewarded.

The transcript below follows these expectations: early rounds show moderate contributions from cooperators and near-zero contributions from clear free-riders; rewards are few and mostly directed at the top contributors; cooperation slowly declines but a small core (MOOSE, RABBIT, GORILLA, CHICK, SNAKE, COW) continues modest mutual reinforcement.

# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK </PLAYERS>

This repeated public-goods game (15 rounds, binary contribution 0 or 20, pot ×1.9 split equally among 10 players) creates a clear tension: contributing helps the group but costs the contributor 20 in private holdings that round. Because the pot is split equally, free riders (those who keep their 20 and let others contribute) earn more in the short run when some others cooperate. That incentive typically causes cooperation to fall over rounds unless players coordinate or punish defectors.

Given the player personas, I expect an initial cluster of contributors (players with higher giving/ trust tendencies: CHICKEN, MOOSE, CHICK, RABBIT, SLOTH, DOG, OWL to varying degrees), and a small core of persistent free riders (GORILLA, DUCK, PARROT). After early rounds reveal the free-rider advantage in payoffs, conditional cooperators will gradually reduce contributions. A few players (notably MOOSE and sometimes CHICKEN) will try to sustain cooperation across many rounds, producing a slow decline rather than an immediate collapse. Chat will be sparse and strategic: early commitments, occasional complaints, a few reassurances, and short final remarks.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK, SNAKE, CROCODILE, COW, PINGUIN, MONKEY </PLAYERS>

This is a repeated-style public goods game with private endowments of 20 each round, a group multiplier (3.7) and a later opportunity to give costly positive rewards (each unit costs 4 to the giver, gives 5 to the receiver). The private marginal return on a single coin contributed (3.7/15 ≈ 0.247) is well below 1, so pure self-interest predicts zero contribution. However, social preferences, reciprocity, and observed prior behavior (the provided personas) push some players toward substantial contributions. Because players do not know the total number of rounds, they are less likely to defect outright for an "endgame" — conditional cooperators can sustain contributions through repeated reciprocity and selective rewarding.

Uncertainties that shape behavior here:
- Players don't see others' individual past choices beyond what's revealed each round, so beliefs about others' types are noisy.
- Rewards are anonymous as to who gave them, so players can reward to promote cooperation but cannot directly sanction or target someone visibly for punishment; rewards are mainly used sparingly to reinforce high contributors.
- Giving a reward is costly, so only players who value long-run cooperation or who themselves contributed highly are likely to use rewards.

Prediction summary: a minority of strong cooperators (GORILLA, MONKEY, DOG, RABBIT, COW) will contribute a lot each round and occasionally exchange small mutual rewards. Many players with mixed priors will contribute moderately (5–13). A few (notably SNAKE) will free-ride near 0. Rewards will be infrequent and aimed at the most consistent contributors to sustain cooperation rather than widespread reward distribution.

This 15-round public goods game gives each player 20 coins each round and a binary choice: keep 20 (contribute 0) or put 20 into the common pot. The pot is multiplied by 1.9 and split equally among the 10 players, so each coin contributed increases every player's return by 0.19 coins. That makes free-riding (keeping your 20) privately better each round, since a contributor receives less personal payoff than a defector when others' actions are fixed. However, some players have social preferences or conditional-cooperation tendencies (based on the personas): CHICK, OWL, SLOTH, RABBIT, MOOSE are relatively more likely to contribute early; GORILLA, DOG, PARROT tend to defect; DUCK and CHICKEN are mixed. Expect initial partial cooperation (some players contribute out of social preference), quick recognition that defectors earn more, and then a general decline in contributions with occasional attempts via chat to revive cooperation. Because players see round payoffs of everyone, feedback is strong and will accelerate the shift toward defection; yet a few principled contributors may persist through many rounds (CHICK in particular). Chat will be sparse and typically used to pledge cooperation or complain when contributions fall.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated one-shot public goods environment where players get 20 coins each round, decide privately how much to contribute (0–20), the group pot is multiplied by 3.7 and split equally, and then players may spend coins to punish (cost 4 to sender, -7 to target) or reward (cost 4 to sender, +5 to target). Because punishing is costly to the punisher and inefficient (it destroys 3 coins per punishment unit), players typically punish sparingly and only to enforce cooperation. Rewards are also used sparingly but can support cooperation by directly increasing targets' payoffs.

Given the player priors:
- A handful (PINGUIN, MONKEY, SNAKE, MOOSE, CHICK, CHICKEN) have very high baseline willingness to contribute and will tend to contribute near the endowment.
- Several players are medium cooperators (SLOTH, DUCK, DOG, RABBIT, CROCODILE), likely to contribute moderate amounts (around 10–15).
- Some players (GORILLA, PARROT, OWL, PARROT, COW) are lower cooperators or opportunistic; they may attempt low contributions early but respond to sanctions.

game_explanation:

Because the multiplier (1.9) yields a per-player return of 3.8 for each 20 contributed, an individual who defects gains more in the short run unless others punish defections. With costly punishment allowed (4 coins per punishment unit to the punisher, 8 coins deducted per unit from the target) and identification of punishers, players who value cooperation or care about fairness will sometimes pay to deter free-riding. Over repeated rounds we should see: an initial level of cooperation from generally prosocial players (GORILLA, RABBIT, some mixed cooperators), early punishment targeted at clear free-riders (DUCK, PARROT, sometimes CHICK), and then dynamic adjustments—defectors sometimes convert after being punished, but end‑game incentives (last rounds) produce more defections and weaker punishment. Punishment is costly and thus used sparingly; chat messages are infrequent and usually warnings, explanations, or short protests.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game with 15 anonymous players who repeatedly choose how much of their 20-coin endowment to contribute to a common pot. Contributions are multiplied by 3.7 and split equally, so contributing helps the group but can leave a contributor worse off relative to free riders. After contributions are revealed, players can spend costly punishment or reward units: each punishment unit costs the sender 4 coins and removes 7 coins from the target; each reward unit costs 4 coins and gives 5 coins to the target. Players see round summaries (their own net payoffs and the amounts they spent/received on punishments/rewards, plus the corresponding aggregated info for peers), but they cannot identify who acted toward whom.

Likely dynamics: some players have pro-social priors (e.g., CHICKEN, PARROT, DOG, CROCODILE) and tend to contribute high amounts consistently; others (GORILLA, CHICK, COW) tend to free-ride. Because punishment and reward are costly, we expect sparing use: a few players with stronger fairness preferences (e.g., OWL and occasionally others) will punish consistent free-riders (mainly COW, sometimes GORILLA or CHICK), and cooperators will occasionally reward high contributors. Over rounds, cooperators may slightly reduce contributions if many free-ride, while targeted punishment can deter some low contributors. Overall cooperation drifts modestly downward across the nine rounds, with occasional small rewards to sustain high contributors and sparse punishments targeted at repeat free-riders.

game_explanation:
This 10-player public-goods game combines a simple binary contribution decision (0 or 20) with costly peer punishment (4 coins per punishment unit sent, each unit deducts 8 coins from the target). With a multiplication factor of 1.9 and equal split, each contributor adds 38 coins to the pot; each contribution effectively increases the group pot by 38 and each player's share is 3.8 times the number of contributors. Incentives: in any single round free-riding (contribute 0) yields a higher private payoff than contributing, unless punishment is used enough to offset that advantage. Uncertainties stem from whether players will punish free-riders (and how often), and from beliefs about how others will change behavior when punished or when the endgame approaches (later rounds often see more free-riding because there's less future to enforce cooperation).

Given the player personas, I expect a mix of steady cooperators (SLOTH, DOG, RABBIT, MOOSE, OWL at times), steady low contributors (DUCK, PARROT, GORILLA more reluctant), and a small set of actors willing to punish to enforce cooperation (CHICK notably punitive, some enforcement from DOG/OWL intermittently). Early rounds should show partial cooperation with a few punishments that encourage broader cooperation. Midgame will see high cooperation, with occasional defections punished once. Approaching the final rounds, defection will rise (endgame effect) and punishment will be used more sparingly because it's costly and there's little future to enforce. Chat will be sparse — a few warnings, thanks, or short reproaches.
