game_explanation:
This is a repeated anonymous public-goods game with an added reward stage. Key incentives:
- Contributing to the public pot reduces your private holdings by your contribution but increases everyone’s payoff through the multiplication (3.7) and equal split. The marginal per-player return on a 1-coin contribution is 3.7/15 ≈ 0.247 — less than 1 — so individually rational myopic play is to contribute 0. Collective payoffs are maximized by high contributions, so conditional cooperators may contribute if they expect others to do so.
- Rewards are costly to the sender (4 coins per reward unit) but deliver 5 coins per unit to the recipient. Rewards can sustain cooperation by selectively rewarding high contributors, but they are relatively expensive, so players tend to reward sparingly.
- Because players do not know the total number of rounds, there is no guaranteed endgame unraveling; instead, behavior often drifts slowly: cooperators try to sustain others via rewards and contributions, while persistent free-riders tend to remain low contributors. Observed summary information (per-player contributions, how much each spent on rewards, how much each received) provides a basis for conditional adjustments.
Uncertainties include heterogeneity in preferences (some players are strong unconditional cooperators; others free-ride) and risk/expectations about whether targeted reward will be reciprocated later. In practice we expect: several players contribute near the endowment (sustained cooperators); a cluster of low contributors remains; rewards are used sparingly and targeted toward the reliably high contributors, which stabilizes their contributions but does not fully eliminate decay among conditional cooperators.

Rewarding is a pro-social instrument: it costs 4 to the rater and gives 5 to the target, so it can bolster cooperators but is relatively costly, so players use it sparingly.

Typical dynamics: an initially mixed profile of contributions, with conditional cooperators gradually lowering contributions in response to persistent free-riding. Rewards appear intermittently and sparsely, mostly directed toward consistently high contributors as positive reinforcement rather than toward punishing low contributors.

game_explanation:
This is a standard repeated public-goods game with 10 players and 15 rounds. Each round players choose whether to contribute all 20 coins or keep them. Contributions are multiplied by 1.9 and split equally, so full cooperation is collectively best but individually risky. Because players see only outcomes (not choices before deciding) and can punish after seeing the round's results, expect the following dynamics:

- Early rounds: some intrinsically cooperative players (those with pro-social priors) will contribute while several low-giving types will free-ride. That creates tension: contributors get lower immediate payoffs than free-riders.
- Punishment will appear sporadically and targeted: cooperators (or norm-enforcers) punish persistent free-riders to discourage exploitation. Punishment is costly to the punisher, so it will be used sparingly and focused on repeat offenders.
- Midgame: if punishment is credible and targeted, more players will switch to contributing to avoid costly sanctions, raising group cooperation.
- Endgame: as the horizon approaches (final rounds), incentives to punish decline and some players will revert to free-riding. Cooperation often unravels somewhat in the last 1–2 rounds.

Throughout, chats are rare and strategic: short appeals to contribute or calls-out after punishments. Punishments are labelled in units (each unit costs 4 to the source and removes 8 from the target). I’ll simulate a plausible path with a small core of steady contributors (SLOTH, DOG, PARROT, CHICKEN) and growing norm enforcement that boosts cooperation midgame, followed by some decline in the final round.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK </PLAYERS>

This is a 15-round public goods game with a 1.9 multiplication and equal split among 10 players. Each round players decide whether to contribute their whole 20 or nothing. The social dilemma: if many contribute, everyone is better off (each contributor gets back more than 20 in the group outcome), but any individual does better in the short run by free-riding when others contribute.

Given the personas:
- SLOTH and RABBIT are strong free-riders (likely 0 almost always).
- PARROT is an unconditional cooperator (likely contributes most rounds).
- DOG, DUCK, CHICK, and CHICKEN are moderately cooperative but conditional; they may contribute early and then reduce contributions after being exploited.
- GORILLA, OWL, and MOOSE are mixed/conditional; they respond to observed payoffs and short messages, so may defect after being repeatedly exploited but could join coordinated efforts (especially near the end).

I predict early rounds will see a modest cluster of contributors (many cooperative types try contributing initially). Because returns are split equally, free-riders will earn more in early rounds, causing conditional cooperators to pull back. Over mid-game contributions will fall, leaving PARROT (and for a while DOG) contributing alone and getting low returns. Toward the final rounds some players may coordinate via chat to restore contributions for the last rounds (a common pattern: end-game moral or coordination). Chats will be sparse and mainly used to announce intentions or respond to exploitation.

game_explanation:
This is a standard repeated public goods game with costly punishment and reward. Each coin you keep yields more to you individually (you keep the 1 coin) than you get back from the group return of a single contributed coin (3.7 total, split among 15 players → ≈0.247 to each player). That creates a private incentive to free-ride, while the group benefits when people contribute. Because players cannot see others' choices before deciding and cannot identify who punished/rewarded them, enforcement proceeds through anonymous signals: players can use punishment to reduce the earnings of low contributors (it costs 4 per punishment unit to the sender and subtracts 7 from the target) or reward to boost cooperative actors (cost 4 per unit to the sender, gives +5 to the target). Punishment and reward are typically used sparingly because both are costly.

Given the provided personas, I expect a mix of behaviors:
- Some players (OWL, CHICK, CHICKEN, MONKEY, SNAKE) tend toward higher contributions; others (CROCODILE, GORILLA, DUCK) tend to free-ride. PARROT and MOOSE are more enforcement-oriented and may use punishment occasionally; CHICK, OWL and MONKEY are more likely to exchange small reciprocal rewards.
- Early rounds show heterogeneity; a few targeted punishments toward persistent zeros provoke small increases in free riders over subsequent rounds, while cooperative clusters sustain moderate-high contributions. Overall contributions tend to rise modestly or stabilize near a moderate level as occasional punishments and sparse rewards enforce norms without excessive cost.

The pot is multiplied by 1.9 and divided equally among the 10 players, so each contributing player's immediate material incentive is to free-ride (contributing costs 20 to the individual while adding only 3.8 to each player’s share). Group payoff is highest when everyone contributes (each would get 38.0 coins in that round if all 10 contribute), but individual temptation favors withholding.

Uncertainties and dynamics to expect:
- Early rounds often show some cooperation from intrinsically pro-social players (here: RABBIT, GORILLA, OWL, CHICKEN and some others) while clear free-riders (PARROT) may defect immediately.
- Players can infer others’ choices from the round summary (contributors receive 20 less than non-contributors because contributors give up their private 20), so behavior and short messages can produce conditional reciprocity: conditional cooperators are likely to match the group's prior contribution level.
- Messages are sparse but can coordinate cooperation or call out defectors. Expect an initial rise or moderate cooperation, then gradual unraveling as selfish incentives and anticipated end-game effects accumulate. Some very altruistic players (RABBIT, often GORILLA) may continue contributing late; PARROT is predicted to never contribute.
- Expect modest chat use — brief commitments, thanks, a few calls-out, and occasional admission of strategy.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game with anonymous punishment and reward. Key incentives: contributing to the public pot reduces your private holding but raises the group's payoff (the pot is multiplied by 3.7 and split equally). Punishing and rewarding peers is costly to the actor (4 coins per unit) and has asymmetric effects on targets (punishment deducts 7 coins per unit; reward adds 5 coins per unit). Players do not see others' current-round choices before deciding, only summaries after rounds. They also don't know how many rounds remain.

Likely dynamics:
- Initial uncertainty typically produces a mix of contributions: some players with pro-social priors will contribute relatively high amounts (to signal cooperation), while selfish or low-trust players will free-ride.
- Punishment is costly and anonymous, so only a subset of players (those motivated to enforce norms or who expect future interaction) will use it; punishment tends to be targeted at extreme low contributors and is sparse.
- Rewards are also used sparingly but can sustain cooperation by reinforcing high contributors.
- Over rounds, conditional cooperators tend to increase contributions when they see others contributing or when punished/rewarded; persistent free-riders may be targeted repeatedly and often increase contributions modestly to avoid sanctions.
- Because players cannot identify who punished or rewarded them, retaliation is limited; punishment mainly acts through the aggregate feedback in summaries.
- Expect a gradual stabilization toward moderate-to-high contributions as sanctions and rewards reinforce cooperative norms, though a few low contributors usually remain or slowly increase.

Uncertainties:
- Some players with mixed priors may fluctuate, and the exact number of punishers/rewarders is hard to predict. Behavioral noise and risk preferences can cause occasional drops or recoveries in contribution levels.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with 15 anonymous players who each get 20 coins per round. Contributions (0–20) are pooled, multiplied by 3.7, and shared equally; after seeing contributions and redistribution, players can reward others (each reward unit costs the giver 4 coins and gives the recipient 5 coins). Players do not know how many rounds there will be, so they face a tension between short-term incentives to free-ride and longer-run incentives to sustain cooperation. Rewarding is the only second-stage sanctioning/incentive device here; because it is costly, players tend to reward sparingly and usually target reliably cooperative peers. Expect a small core of consistently high contributors (the highly prosocial personas) who are rewarded occasionally to sustain their cooperation; a small set of low contributors (the “free-riders”) who get little or no reward; and the rest clustered in the middle, adjusting slowly in response to observed contributions and who gets rewarded. Because players cannot identify who rewarded them individually, reward patterns will be visible at the aggregate level (who gave how much total), which still informs subsequent contribution choices. Over 9 rounds I predict stable high contributions from GORILLA, DOG, PARROT and some others (PINGUIN/OWL), persistent low contributions from CROCODILE and DUCK, and modest gradual decline among the middling players, with sparse reward edges mostly among the cooperators.

game_explanation:
This public goods game pits 15 anonymous players against the standard tension: each round everyone receives 20 coins and can either keep coins privately or contribute them to a shared pot that is multiplied and split. Contributing benefits the whole group (the multiplier 3.7 makes contributions socially valuable) but is individually costly. After the redistribution, players can spend private coins to punish (costly to both parties) or reward (costly to the source, beneficial to the target). Because participants do not see who assigned punishment/rewards, sanctions serve as a public coordination device rather than direct personal retaliation.

Given the player priors, I expect a mixed-but-rising cooperation pattern. Highly prosocial players (MOOSE, RABBIT, CROCODILE, MONKEY to some extent) will contribute near the top of the range and consistently be targets of small rewards from others who can afford them. Several players (GORILLA, PARROT, PINGUIN, SLOTH) tend toward low contributions initially; they will receive occasional (sparse) punishments intended by others to discourage free-riding. Most punishments and rewards will be small and infrequent because they are costly to the sender; rewards will generally be used to reinforce high contributors, punishments used sparingly against repeated low contributors. Over rounds the group tends to converge toward moderate-to-high contributions, with a small subset of persistent low contributors who attract intermittent punishment.

This public goods game pits private incentives (keep your 20 coins) against a group benefit (every coin contributed is multiplied by 3.7 and shared equally). With 15 players, the marginal group return per contributed coin is only 3.7/15 ≈ 0.247, so the individually optimal choice is to contribute 0. However, many players have prosocial priors (Rabbit, Chick, Dog, Cow, etc.) and some are conditional cooperators (Gorilla, Parrot, Moose). Because players cannot see others' contributions before deciding, we expect a mix of initial experimentation: a subset of high-contribution players will contribute large amounts (20 or near it), several medium cooperators will contribute moderate amounts (8–15), and a set of persistent free-riders (e.g., Owl, Duck) will contribute 0.

Free-riders tend to persist, which slowly depresses average contributions across rounds, but intermittent rewarding keeps a core of contributors active.

game_explanation:
This is a 15-round binary public-goods game with 10 players. Each round each player either contributes their entire 20-coin endowment to the public pot or keeps it. The pot is multiplied by 1.9 and split equally among the 10 players, so each coin contributed yields 0.19 coins to every player. Because contributors forfeit the private 20 while non-contributors keep it, a free rider earns 20 coins more than a cooperator in the same round, plus they both receive the same share of the multiplied public pot. That creates a constant, strong short-term incentive to defect (contribute 0), while cooperation benefits the group and can be sustained only by expectations of reciprocity, promises, or social preferences.

Given the provided personas, I predict one consistent, strongly pro-social player (GORILLA) will keep contributing frequently. A couple of players with moderate pro-social tendencies (RABBIT, MOOSE, DUCK, DOG) will contribute sometimes early on but will quickly reduce contributions if they observe that cooperators earn less than free riders. PARROT and SLOTH are expected to free-ride almost always; CHICKEN, OWL, and CHICK are likely to defect more often than not despite occasional promises. Players see round-by-round net payoffs for everyone, so contributions are observable via the 20-coin gap between contributors and non-contributors; this typically produces an early decline in cooperation as defectors get higher payoffs and cooperators get discouraged. The pot is multiplied by 1.9 and split equally among the 10 players, so each coin contributed yields 0.19 coins to every player. Because contributors forfeit the private 20 while non-contributors keep it, a free rider earns 20 coins more than a cooperator in the same round, plus they both receive the same share of the multiplied public pot. That creates a constant, strong short-term incentive to defect (contribute 0), while cooperation benefits the group and can be sustained only by expectations of reciprocity, promises, or social preferences.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with optional peer rewards. Each round players decide how much of 20 coins to contribute to a common pot (multiplied by 3.7 and shared), then can spend 4 coins per reward unit to give 5 coins to another player. Typical dynamics here are: many players who are dispositionally cooperative (high dictator/offer/return tendencies in the personas) will start with high contributions; a few strongly selfish players will contribute little or nothing. Conditional cooperators (medium profiles) will adjust over rounds based on observed group behavior: they tend to increase contributions if others are contributing and reduce if they see many free riders. Rewards tend to be used sparingly to reinforce cooperative behavior (rewarding high contributors) or occasionally to punish/skip free riders; because rewards are costly, expect mostly 1-unit awards and only a handful per round, targeted at clear cooperators. Over nine rounds we expect a cluster of high contributors (GORILLA, DUCK, DOG, MOOSE, RABBIT, CROCODILE, OWL) to remain high; mixed types (SLOTH, PARROT, MONKEY, SNAKE, COW) will vary around the mid-high range depending on how much positive reinforcement they receive; consistently low types (CHICK, PINGUIN, CHICKEN) will mostly remain low and receive few or no rewards. The visible reward flows will reinforce cooperation for those who get them; absent identification of who rewarded them, players only see aggregated reward given/received and so adjust based on patterns rather than direct reciprocity.

This is a 15-round public goods game with 10 players, 20 coins privately each round, binary contributions (0 or 20). A contributed 20 enters a pot that is multiplied by 1.9 and split equally among all 10 players. Because the individual return from a single contribution is 3.8 to each player (less than the private cost 20), the strictly selfish equilibrium is everyone contributing 0. However, many players have pro-social priors (DOG, PARROT, RABBIT, CHICK are relatively cooperative), while MOOSE is consistently non-cooperative; others (SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, OWL) are mixed. Importantly, after each round players see each other's net payoffs, which reveal exactly who contributed (contributors earn 20 less than non-contributors). That makes reputational reciprocity possible: cooperators can punish free-riders by ceasing to contribute in later rounds or try to coordinate with messages.

Predicted dynamics: early rounds may show some cooperation from the more pro-social players and a few initial experimenters. Once players see who free-ride, cooperators will re-evaluate. Without formal enforcement, contributions tend to fall toward a small core of steady cooperators (DOG, PARROT, RABBIT, CHICK) who keep contributing out of intrinsic preference; others (MOOSE, SLOTH, GORILLA, OWL, DUCK, CHICKEN) mostly free-ride, occasionally testing cooperation. Chat is typically sparse and used to signal intentions or call out observed free-riding.

game_explanation:
This public goods game pits private incentives against collective benefit. Each coin you contribute reduces your private holding by 1 but increases the shared pot; the pot is multiplied by 3.7 and split equally among 15 players, so one extra contributed coin returns only 3.7/15 ≈ 0.247 to each player (including the contributor). The selfish, one-shot incentive is therefore to contribute 0; the group-optimum is for everyone to contribute more. The punishment/reward stage creates a mechanism to enforce cooperation: punishments (−7 to a target per unit, cost 4 to sender) deter low contributions, and rewards (+5 to a target per unit, cost 4 to sender) incentivize high contributions. Because players do not see who punished/rewarded them, retaliation is less targeted; instead, players use punishment/reward to signal group norms based on observed contributions.

Given the provided personas, I expect a mix: a few reliably high cooperators (CHICK, MONKEY, SLOTH) will contribute near the top; several low-trust players (GORILLA, PARROT, DOG, OWL) will start low and be targets of sparse punishment; many others (DUCK, SNAKE, COW, CHICKEN, PINGUIN, MOOSE) will contribute moderately and conditionally adjust upward if low contributors are penalized. Punishment and reward will be used sparingly — a few 1-unit punishments/rewards each round — and overall contributions should drift upward modestly across rounds as defectors are nudged to contribute more.

game_explanation:
This is a 15-round public goods game with 10 players. Each round everyone gets 20 coins and chooses either to contribute all (20) to the public pot or keep their 20. The pot is multiplied by 1.9 and split equally among all 10 players, so each contributor lowers their private holding by 20 but increases everyone’s share; defectors keep their 20 and also receive the equal split. That structure gives strong private incentive to free-ride (defect) each round, while groups do best if many contribute. Typical dynamics: early rounds often see some cooperation from prosocial or trusting players (here: SLOTH, DOG, OWL, etc.), while some low-giving types (PARROT, DUCK, GORILLA, etc.) free-ride. Conditional cooperators may reduce contributions after being exploited; some prosocials persist for a while but cooperation usually drifts down over time, accelerating near the known end (round 15). Cheap talk (chat) can try to coordinate contributions but often remains sparse and not fully reliable. Uncertainty here includes not knowing others' choices before committing and whether promises will be kept; we should therefore expect an initial moderate level of cooperation that slowly erodes, with occasional testing messages and a likely collapse toward the final rounds.

game_explanation:
This public goods game pits a private incentive to keep your 20 coins against a group benefit when others contribute. Because any individual who withholds the full 20 always keeps that 20 while still receiving the pot share, free-riding is the dominant short-run incentive. Punishment (cost 4 to punish, -8 to the punished per unit) and communication are the mechanisms that can sustain cooperation: costly punishments can deter free-riding if contributors coordinate to penalize defectors, but punishment itself is costly and can provoke retaliation. Uncertainties include how strongly stubborn defectors (low pro-social priors) will resist punishment, whether generous types (high pro-social priors) will invest in enforcement, and how contribution rates will change as the horizon shortens (endgame decline). I expect an initial cluster of cooperators (social types like OWL, MOOSE, CHICK, RABBIT, PARROT) to contribute and to begin modest, targeted punishment of persistent defectors (SLOTH, GORILLA, DOG). That enforcement will raise cooperation for several rounds. As the game approaches the final rounds, the incentive to defect increases and some players will exploit the group, causing cooperation to slip and punishment to reappear sporadically. Chat will be sparse and used mainly to pledge, shame, or thank.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game with 15 anonymous players, a multiplication factor (3.7) that makes group contributions socially valuable but leaves an individual incentive to free-ride, and a costly, targeted positive-reward stage (each reward unit costs the giver 4 coins and gives the recipient 5 coins). Because contributions are revealed before rewards, rewards will mainly be used to recognize/praise high contributors (not to punish directly). Players do not know how many rounds remain, which tends to support more persistent cooperation early on (no last-round collapse immediately), and the asymmetric cost/benefit of rewarding (net -1 to social surplus per reward unit) means rewards will be used sparingly — enough to signal appreciation or encourage cooperators, not to fully substitute for contribution decisions.

Given the player personas, I expect a stable core of high contributors (DOG, PARROT, CROCODILE, MONKEY, CHICK and often CHICKEN) who contribute near the endowment, a cluster of medium contributors (OWL, DUCK, SNAKE, MOOSE, PINGUIN) who contribute moderate amounts, and a few low contributors (COW, RABBIT). Rewards will be relatively rare and directed toward the high contributors; where dyadic reciprocity forms between two consistently generous players (e.g., PARROT and DOG), we'll see repeated 1-unit rewards exchanged. Observed dynamics across nine rounds should therefore show persistent cooperation among the prosocial core, modest variability among mixed players, and consistent low plays by COW and RABBIT, with small, targeted reward flows that slightly reinforce the cooperators.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game with costly punishment and rewarding. Each round each player decides how much of their 20 private coins to contribute to a shared pot; every coin contributed yields 3.7 coins total that are split across all active players. Because 3.7/15 < 1 (each contributed coin returns less than 1 coin to the contributor), the individually selfish incentive is to contribute 0; collective payoff is maximized when many contribute. Players are heterogeneous: some are predisposed to give a lot (strongly pro-social), some to give little or none. The punishment and reward stage provides a costly enforcement mechanism: sending a punishment unit harms a target (-7) while costing the sender 4; a reward unit benefits a target (+5) while costing the sender 4. Because senders cannot be identified by recipients, signaling and calibrated enforcement are noisier than in identified settings.

Likely dynamics: early rounds show variation as players probe each other — strong cooperators signal by high contributions, free-riders reveal themselves with low contributions. Conditional cooperators (who increase contributions when others do) will adjust across rounds. Punishment and reward will be used sparingly: players rarely spend many coins on incentives, but will sometimes reward clear cooperators and occasionally punish persistent free-riders to enforce norms. Because the game length is unknown, there is no final-round collapse with certainty, but toward later rounds some decline in cooperation can emerge as players try to conserve private payoffs if they suspect the end is near. Overall expect a mix: a core of consistently high contributors (DOG, CROCODILE, PINGUIN, COW, OWL, some conditional cooperators) and a set of low contributors (SLOTH, GORILLA early, CHICK, PARROT), with occasional 1-unit rewards or 1-unit punishments targeted to salient cooperators or consistent defectors.

This is a repeated, anonymous public-goods game with costly positive rewards. Each coin contributed returns only 3.7/15 ≈ 0.247 coins to the contributor (private return < 1), so the dominant private incentive is to keep endowment (contribute 0). Socially, full contribution would raise group payoffs, so people with cooperative preferences may contribute despite private incentives.

Given the player personas, I expect a mixed population: a minority of reliably cooperative types (notably CROCODILE, PARROT, DOG, RABBIT, and some medium contributors) who contribute substantially, and several consistently low contributors (GORILLA, DUCK, OWL, and some others) who free-ride. Conditional cooperators (e.g., PARROT, DOG, RABBIT, MOOSE) will adjust contributions up or down based on observed group behavior and rewards. Because players do not know the total number of rounds, early rounds will show more experimentation and some higher contributions; over time contributions typically drift downward as free-riding persists.

Rewards are costly (4 coins each) but do give a net benefit to the recipient (5 coins). Players tend to reward sparingly — usually 0–2 units per recipient and only for clearly above-average contributions. Because rewards are anonymous (players don't learn who rewarded them), rewards serve mainly as costly signals to the group; they can sustain cooperation somewhat but only when a few players are willing to bear the cost repeatedly. Expect CROCODILE and PARROT to be focal high contributors and mutual reward partners in several early rounds; conditional cooperators (DOG, RABBIT, MOOSE) will reward occasionally. Overall pattern likely: initial moderate-to-high cooperation from a subset, gradual decline in average contributions across rounds, and sparse positive rewards concentrated on high contributors.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game with voluntary contribution (0–20 each round), a multiplication of the group pot by 3.7 and an equal split among 15 players, followed by optional costly punishment and reward. Key incentives:
- Contributing benefits the whole group because each coin contributed yields 3.7/15 ≈ 0.247 of return to each individual — so the private return per contributed coin is less than 1, creating a tension: individual incentive is to keep money, group incentive is to contribute.
- Punishment (cost 4 to the source, −7 to the target) and reward (cost 4 to the source, +5 to the target) let players directly sanction or reinforce others, but both are costly to the actor. Because punishments are more costly to the target than to the source (−7 vs. −4), punishment can be an effective deterrent; rewards are cheaper to help the target (they net +1 to group efficiency per reward unit: source pays 4, target gains 5).
- Players do not know the number of rounds, which tends to sustain cooperation early (no guaranteed last round), but conditional cooperators often reduce contributions if they perceive many low contributions.
- Empirically, punishment and reward are used sparingly. High cooperators (e.g., PARROT, CHICK, PINGUIN, DUCK) tend to contribute a lot and occasionally spend to punish persistent low contributors; low or selfish players (e.g., CHICKEN, DOG, OWL, MONKEY sometimes) contribute little. Conditional cooperators (e.g., SLOTH, COW, RABBIT, MOOSE) adjust contributions based on others.
- Given these tendencies, I expect initial rounds to show many high contributions from pro-social personas, some low contributors, a little targeted punishment early against persistent zeros, occasional small rewards to reinforce high contributions, and gradual modest decline or fluctuation in contributions over rounds.

game_explanation:
This is a 15-round binary public goods game with 10 players. Each round everyone has 20 coins and chooses either to contribute their whole 20 to the common pot or keep it. The pot is multiplied by 1.9 and split equally, so individual incentives favor free-riding: if others contribute, a defector keeps their 20 and still benefits from the multiplied pot. Conditional cooperators (who respond to others' behavior) and unconditional cooperators will tend to contribute early, but seeing that defectors earn more usually causes a decline in contributions over time. Because the horizon is finite and known, there is additional pressure toward endgame defection. Uncertainty remains about who will keep cooperating despite being exploited — some players (e.g., DOG, OWL in these personas) are much more likely to contribute repeatedly, while others (e.g., GORILLA, SLOTH) are more likely to free-ride. Chat is allowed and can briefly coordinate or call out behavior, but messages are typically sparse and used for short promises, appeals, or complaints. Expect an early burst of cooperation, rapid exploitation by free-riders, and a gradual collapse to a small core of persistent cooperators (likely DOG and sometimes OWL) by the later rounds.

game_explanation:
This public-goods game pits 16 players against the classic incentive tension: contributing to the group increases the shared pot (contributions ×4.5 split equally), but each player also forgoes private coins when they contribute. Because the multiplier per coin is 4.5/16 = 0.28125 per player, every coin you contribute returns less to you than it would if kept (private return = 1), so the individual incentive is to free-ride (contribute 0). Social preferences, beliefs about others, and strategic reward choices can sustain higher contributions.

Uncertainties and likely dynamics:
- Early rounds generally show optimism/cooperation: many players will contribute above their private-interest level because they expect others to do the same or because of pro-social tendencies in some personas (e.g., CHICK, OWL, GORILLA).
- A subset of players (CROCODILE, COW, PINGUIN, MONKEY) are likely to contribute very little or zero every round. Their behavior reduces the marginal benefit to contributors and creates downward pressure on others' contributions over time.
- Rewarding is costly (4 coins per reward unit) and returns only 2 coins to the target; it's used sparingly. Players who are more pro-social or who want to signal appreciation will reward high contributors, but because rewards are net-costly, only a few units are likely to be sent and mainly to particularly consistent cooperators (e.g., CHICK, OWL).
- Over rounds we expect modest decline in average contributions as free-riding becomes visible in summaries; however, persistent high contributors (notably CHICK) will continue, and a small set of players will occasionally reward them, sustaining a minority of high contributors.
- Chat will be sparse and purposeful: greetings, occasional calls-out or thanks after rewards, short strategic comments ("I won't reward free-riders"). Reward actions will be asymmetric and infrequent, primarily directed at top contributors.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game played for 17 rounds. Each round players privately decide how much (0–20) to contribute from their endowment; contributions are multiplied by 4.5 and shared equally. After seeing contributions, players can punish (costly to the punisher, harsh to the target) or reward (costly to the giver, modest benefit to the recipient). Players also may send brief messages.

Likely dynamics:
- Early rounds typically see moderate-to-high cooperation from intrinsically prosocial players (SNAKE, COW, FROG, some PARROT/MOOSE), while a minority (CHICK, MONKEY, DOG, CHICK) often contribute little or nothing.
- When a few players free-ride repeatedly, others sometimes use costly punishment to enforce cooperation. Punishment is used sparingly (a few units, not mass retaliation) because it's expensive.
- Rewards are used even more sparingly, to reinforce high contributors.
- Over time, if punishment/reward sustain cooperation, average contributions stay moderate. Toward the final rounds, some players reduce contributions (endgame free-riding), so cooperation often drifts down in rounds 16–17.
- Chat is rare and purposeful (requests to cooperate, short explanations, thanks).

Transcript follows round-by-round. Each round begins with an explanation of anticipated incentives and behaviors for that round, then the observed chat, contributions, punishment/reward actions, and the summary stage (players see their own round payoff and the punishment/reward details that concern themselves).

reflection:
This is a repeated public goods game played for 17 rounds. Each round players privately decide how much (0–20) to contribute from their endowment; contributions are multiplied by 4.5 and shared equally. After seeing contributions, players can punish (costly to the punisher, harsh to the target) or reward (costly to the giver, modest benefit to the recipient). Players also may send brief messages.

Likely dynamics:
- Early rounds typically see moderate-to-high cooperation from intrinsically prosocial players (SNAKE, COW, FROG, some PARROT/MOOSE), while a minority (CHICK, MONKEY, DOG, CHICK) often contribute little or nothing.
- When a few players free-ride repeatedly, others sometimes use costly punishment to enforce cooperation. Punishment is used sparingly (a few units, not mass retaliation) because it's expensive.
- Rewards are used even more sparingly, to reinforce high contributors.
- Over time, if punishment/reward sustain cooperation, average contributions stay moderate. Toward the final rounds, some players reduce contributions (endgame free-riding), so cooperation often drifts down in rounds 16–17.
- Chat is rare and purposeful (requests to cooperate, short explanations, thanks).

Transcript follows round-by-round. Each round begins with an explanation of anticipated incentives and behaviors for that round, then the observed chat, contributions, punishment/reward actions, and the summary stage (players see their own round payoff and the punishment/reward details that concern themselves).

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public goods game with 16 players, each given 20 coins per round for 17 rounds. The private incentive each round is to keep all 20 (contribute 0) because each contributed coin returns only 4.5/16 = 0.28125 coins to the contributor via the public pot — a private loss per contributed coin. The social incentive (for the group) is the opposite: total welfare is highest if everyone contributes their full endowment. Because contributions are private at decision time and revealed only after, players form beliefs about others' likely contributions. The reward mechanism is costly to the giver (4 coins per reward unit) and gives a modest benefit to recipients (2 coins per unit), so rewards are an inefficient but targeted tool to encourage (or thank) cooperators; they can sustain cooperation only if players expect reciprocal rewards or derive reputation/value from being seen as rewarding/cooperating.

Given the player personas, I expect one clearly pro-social player (GORILLA) to contribute consistently at a high level. A small cluster of moderately cooperative players (CROCODILE, SNAKE, MONKEY, OWL, DUCK, PARROT, DOG, RABBIT) will contribute at medium levels and respond to occasional rewards. Several players have stable low-cooperation tendencies (SLOTH, CHICKEN, CHICK, FROG, COW, PINGUIN) and will mostly free-ride. Rewards will be sparse and directed toward visible cooperators (GORILLA often receives thanks/reciprocity), and cooperation will drift up and down depending on whether cooperators are rewarded. Toward the final rounds, there is an endgame tendency for cooperation to collapse (players stop paying costs for long-term reciprocity), so contributions will fall, and fewer rewards will be sent.

game_explanation:
This 17-round public goods game balances private incentives (keep your 20 coins) against collective benefit (every coin multiplied by 4.5 and shared equally). Early rounds typically test norms: many players will contribute moderately to see who reciprocates, while a few will free-ride. Because punishment is costly but powerful (source pays 4 per unit; target loses 15 per unit), sanctions will be used sparingly — mainly to deter persistent free-riders — and rewards (costly to giver, small benefit to receiver) will be used occasionally to reinforce high contributors. Because the horizon is known (17 rounds), expect contributions to drift down toward the end unless the group successfully sustains cooperation with timely sanctions/rewards. Chat will be minimal but used to coordinate or call out deviators.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game with 17 rounds, private contributions each round, and costly positive rewards after contributions are revealed. Players know the horizon, so there is an incentive to condition behavior on others' past contributions early and to reduce cooperation toward the end (backward induction). Because rewards are costly (4 coins per reward unit) but allow signaling and reciprocity (they produce observable transfers and identify senders), we expect:
- A small core of reliably pro-social players to contribute consistently (especially SNAKE and SLOTH), and to use sparse rewards to acknowledge other cooperators.
- Many players who are reluctant to give (DUCK, MOOSE, CHICK, OWL, CROCODILE and others with low dictator/return tendencies) will contribute little or zero most rounds, dragging down the group average.
- Conditional cooperators (GORILLA, PARROT, RABBIT, PINGUIN, MONKEY) will adjust contributions upward if they see cooperation and/or receive rewards, and downward when free-riding persists.
- Rewards will be used sparingly to reinforce cooperation — typically 1 unit (cost 4) directed to high contributors — rather than broad redistribution. Over rounds, rewards may sustain a modest stable subset of contributors, but overall cooperation will trend down as the endgame approaches. Chat will be occasional and focused (promises, thanks, short complaints).

game_explanation:
This 17-round public goods game pits 16 players with heterogeneous social preferences against strong individual incentives to free-ride. Each coin contributed is multiplied by 4.5 and split across all players; the private return per contributed coin (4.5/16 ≈ 0.281) is well below the private cost (1), so purely selfish players prefer 0 contribution. However, many players have pro-social priors (high past giving/trust) and some have reputational or enforcement tendencies; this creates two forces:

- Early rounds: cooperators (high-contribution personas) try to sustain moderate-to-high contributions. Free-riders (low-contribution personas) exploit the pot early.
- Mid rounds: conditional cooperators react to observed group behavior. Sparse but targeted punishment (costly to sender, severe to recipient) is an efficient deterrent here — even one punishment unit is highly damaging to a target, so punishments tend to be rare but effective. Rewards are possible but less powerful per coin sent (costly to sender, modest benefit to recipient) and thus used sparingly to signal appreciation.
- Late rounds / end-game: strategic enforcement weakens because future reciprocity is limited; many players reduce contributions in the final rounds. Punishment becomes less frequent toward the end for two reasons: (1) senders avoid the cost if no future benefit, and (2) there is less expectation of enforcing cooperation before the game ends.

Overall I expect initial moderate cooperation from pro-social players, repeated low contributions from a small set of persistent free-riders, a few targeted punishments early-to-mid game that temporarily raise some contributions, sparse rewarding of steady cooperators, and an overall gradual decline in contributions culminating in low final-round contributions.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK, SNAKE, CROCODILE, COW, PINGUIN, MONKEY, FROG </PLAYERS>

This is a repeated public-goods game with costly punishment and costly rewards. Each round players decide how much of their private 20 coins to contribute to a common pot; the pot is multiplied by 4.5 and split equally. Because each coin a player contributes returns less than 1 coin back to them individually (4.5/16 ≈ 0.28), the dominant short-run incentive is to keep money (free-ride). Contributions therefore depend on social preferences (intrinsic cooperativeness), expectations about others, and the credibility of post-round sanctions (punishments and rewards). Punishment is powerful (each unit you spend costs you 4 but deducts 15 from the target), while rewards cost 4 and give only +2 to the target — both are costly, so players use them sparingly.

Given the provided personas, I expect a heterogeneous pattern: some reliably prosocial players (CHICK, COW, FROG, PINGUIN, CHICK) will contribute strongly early and often; many others are mixed or selfish (DOG, CHICKEN, MONKEY, CROCODILE) and contribute little. Early rounds will show moderate contributions from cooperators and low contributions from others. Punishment will be used sparingly by a few enforcement-minded types (OWL, FROG, sometimes CHICK/COW) to deter the lowest contributors (notably DOG and CHICKEN). Rewards will be occasional, directed at high contributors. Over rounds, absent heavy consistent sanctions, average contributions slowly decline (classic endgame effect). Punishment keeps some contributors from collapsing to zero but does not eliminate free-riding entirely. In the final round, with no future consequences, expect a sharp drop in contributions from conditional cooperators, though a few intrinsically cooperative players may still give.

game_explanation:
This is a 17-round public goods game with costly peer rewards. Each round players privately choose how much of their 20 coins to contribute to a common pot; every coin contributed is multiplied by 4.5 and then split equally among the 16 players, so individually each contributed coin returns only 0.28125 to the contributor. The dominant private incentive is therefore to free-ride (contribute 0), while group welfare is maximized if everyone contributes more. The availability of costly rewards (each reward unit costs 4 coins to the giver and gives 2 coins to the recipient) creates a channel for conditional cooperation: players who value reciprocity or reputation may reward high contributors to encourage future contributions. Because rewards are inefficient (net loss overall) they tend to be used sparingly and strategically — to reward consistent cooperators or to try to sustain a cooperation norm.

Given the player personas, I expect a mix of types: some reliably more pro-social (e.g., OWL, RABBIT, SLOTH, RABBIT) who contribute relatively high amounts early; several mixed or medium types who contribute moderate amounts but respond to incentives (GORILLA, CHICK, PINGUIN, CHICK); and several low contributors (PARROT, MOOSE, MONKEY, DOG, CHICKEN, DOG) who often contribute little or nothing. Early rounds will show moderate contributions from conditional cooperators, sparse reward use (experimenters often wait to see behavior before rewarding), and a small number of targeted rewards to high contributors. As rounds progress, conditional cooperators will increase contributions if rewarded or if they see others contributing; free-riders will often remain low. Mid-game (rounds ~5–12) cooperation peaks when reward signaling establishes expectations; in the final few rounds contributions will drift down due to end-game effects (players anticipating no future returns to reciprocity), and reward activity drops accordingly. Chats will be sparse and primarily used to announce intentions or call out persistent free-riders.

game_explanation:
This public-goods game combines repeated contribution decisions with optional costly positive rewards. Expect these broad dynamics:

- Early rounds: some players with pro-social priors (e.g., SLOTH, GORILLA, CROCODILE, SNAKE, COW) will contribute relatively high amounts to signal cooperation. Others (OWL, CHICK, FROG, some low-trust profiles) are likely to contribute little or nothing. Because contributions are revealed before rewards, early rounds will feature a few targeted rewards to reinforce visibly high contributors.
- Middle rounds: contributions typically drift downward as cooperators see free-riding by low contributors and adjust downward to avoid exploitation. Rewards will remain sparse and targeted: cooperators reward other cooperators to sustain mutual cooperation, but reward costs (4 coins per unit) limit frequency and size.
- Late rounds and endgame: players know the horizon. Some will increase contributions slightly before the final round to reciprocate recent rewards or to cash in on reputational reciprocity, while others will drop contributions in the final round (classic endgame decline). Rewards in later rounds serve as “thank you” gestures but are still modest.
- Uncertainties: individual variation in responses to being rewarded (some reciprocate, some free-ride), and intermittent chat messages might coordinate modestly. Overall cooperation is maintained above zero by a small cluster of reciprocating players, but full contribution by all is unlikely.

- Late rounds and endgame: players know the horizon. Some will increase contributions slightly before the final round to reciprocate recent rewards or to cash in on reputational reciprocity, while others will drop contributions in the final round (classic endgame decline). Rewards in later rounds serve as “thank you” gestures but are still modest.
- Uncertainties: individual variation in responses to being rewarded (some reciprocate, some free-ride), and intermittent chat messages might coordinate modestly. Overall cooperation is maintained above zero by a small cluster of reciprocating players, but full contribution by all is unlikely.

game_explanation:
This 17-round public goods game balances a private incentive to free-ride (each coin kept yields 1 privately) against a weak social return per coin contributed (4.5 is split among 16 players, so each coin returns ~0.281 to each player). Without enforcement, rational self-interest predicts low contributions; with social preferences (altruism, reciprocity) and communication/punishment/reward, contributions can be higher. Punishment here is unusually potent (each punishment unit costs 4 to the punisher and removes 15 from the target), so players will use it sparingly but it can strongly deter repeat free-riding. Rewards cost 4 and give only +2 to the target, so they’re also costly signals rather than efficient transfers. Expect initial heterogeneity: some strongly pro-social players (SLOTH, CHICK, MONKEY, PARROT, FROG) will contribute high early, some consistent low contributors (MOOSE, CROCODILE, OWL, DOG, SNAKE) will free-ride. Early targeted punishments of persistent zeros will be used occasionally to enforce norms; modest rewards will be sent to recognize high contributors. Over rounds contributions should move toward a moderate cooperation equilibrium (many players contributing around 8–14) as punishments and praise shape behavior, while a few stubborn low contributors remain relatively low but are intermittently sanctioned.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK, SNAKE, CROCODILE, COW, PINGUIN, MONKEY, FROG </PLAYERS>

This 17-round public goods game pits 16 players against a social dilemma: each round everyone decides privately how many of 20 coins to contribute to a shared pot. Contributions are multiplied by 4.5 and split evenly, so high total contributions benefit everyone but each individual has a private incentive to keep coins. Because the game is repeated and players can reward peers (costly to the giver, beneficial to the receiver), we expect a split between steady cooperators (who continue to contribute a lot and are often rewarded) and persistent free-riders (who contribute little and rarely get rewarded). Early rounds will see some exploration: conditional cooperators will test others' cooperativeness and reward apparent contributors. Midgame, reward signals tend to stabilize patterns: cooperators get occasional small rewards that sustain their contributions; free-riders stay unrewarded and continue low contributions. Toward the end, classic endgame effects make many players reduce contributions (since future reciprocation is limited), so average contributions decline further and rewards become rarer. Chat messages will be sparse and purposeful — short praise, complaints about free-riding, and occasional promises to reward cooperators. Rewards themselves will be sparse and small (one or two reward units) because each unit is costly (4 coins) and yields only 2 coins to the target.

game_explanation:
This repeated public goods game combines a moderate endowment (20 coins) with a high multiplication factor for the group pot (×4.5) and bilateral costly punishment/reward. Expect three main forces to shape behavior across 17 rounds:

- Conditional cooperation: Many players who observe others contributing tend to match or increase their contributions. That sustains fairly high contributions early and through the middle of the game.
- Free-riding and endgame decline: A subset of players with low pro-social priors will contribute little or nothing. Toward the final rounds (when future retaliation is less effective), contributions tend to fall as some players exploit cooperators.
- Targeted sanctions and small rewards: Punishment is expensive to deliver (source pays 4 per unit) but very damaging to targets (−15 per unit), so punishments are used sparingly and mainly to deter persistent low contributors. Small rewards are used occasionally to reinforce high contributors. Because punishment is identifiable, it is used mostly early-to-mid game to discipline persistent free riders; toward the end, punishments decline as they’re less instrumentally useful.

Overall predicted dynamics: relatively high average contributions from rounds 1–10 sustained by a few punitive threats and occasional rewarding. A core of reliably cooperative players (e.g., GORILLA, CHICKEN, COW, PINGUIN) contribute near the maximum throughout. A small group of persistent low contributors (OWL, MOOSE, MONKEY, SNAKE) start low; some increase after receiving punishment, others remain low. Toward rounds 13–17 there is a gradual drift downward with a pronounced fall in the last round(s) as strategic incentives switch to one-shot thinking and fewer punishments are used.

game_explanation:
This public goods game pits 16 identifiable players (with varied cooperative tendencies) against the classic tension: keep coins privately (safe) or contribute to a pot that benefits everyone after a multiplier. Because the pot is multiplied by 4.5 and split equally, a dollar invested typically yields a private-return less than the cost (since each coin invested gives each player 4.5/16 = 0.28125 back), so purely selfish incentives push toward zero contribution. However, many players here are conditional cooperators (some strongly cooperative personas), and the presence of costly punishment and reward gives a mechanism to sustain cooperation: cooperators can reward other cooperators or punish persistent free-riders, shifting incentives. Punishment is costly to both source and target (4 cost to sender, −15 to target per unit), so it is used sparingly and usually only when players want to deter repeated defection. game_explanation:
This repeated public-goods game pits private incentives (you lose 1 coin per coin contributed but only get ~0.281 back from the group) against group benefit (the pot is multiplied by 4.5). With 16 players, contributing is individually costly, so free-riding is expected. However, the one-sided reward mechanism (each reward unit costs 4 for the giver and gives 2 to the recipient) allows players to signal appreciation for cooperators even though each reward is itself costly for the group. Early rounds usually see higher contributions as many players try cooperation; as rounds progress, free riding typically causes average contributions to decline. Rewards will be used sparingly (because they are costly) and mainly to reinforce consistently high contributors or to reciprocate a direct benefit. Chat will be rare and used to coordinate praise or to complain when cooperation falls.

Patterns you can expect:
- A mix of stable high contributors (some very prosocial avatars) and stable low contributors (some selfish avatars).
- Moderate players will drift downward over time as they react to observed group trends.
- Rewards will be sparse and targeted: a few 1-unit rewards to recognize high contributors, occasional reciprocation, and less reward activity as the game nears the end.
- Chat messages will appear occasionally to thank or to signal shifting strategies.

game_explanation:
This is a 17-round public goods game with optional punishment and reward after contributions. Players face the usual tension: contributing to the public pot benefits the group (pot multiplied by 4.5 and split equally) but is individually costly relative to free-riding. There is uncertainty about others' contributions early on, so many players will start with a mix of conditional cooperation and free-riding. Some players (e.g., MONKEY, CHICK, PINGUIN, DOG) have priors toward higher cooperation and may try to sustain contributions. Others (e.g., SLOTH, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, RABBIT) are more likely to contribute little or nothing.

Punishment is a costly but strong deterrent (each punishment unit costs 4 to the sender and subtracts 15 from the target), while reward is also costly to the sender but provides smaller gains to the recipient (cost 4, recipient +2). Because punishment is powerful, we expect it to be used sparingly and mainly early on to discipline repeated zero-contributors. Rewards are likely to be used even more sparingly to acknowledge consistent high contributors. Over rounds we expect conditional cooperators to adjust: contributors will reduce contributions if free-riding persists; a small number of punishers can sustain higher contributions by targeting persistent defectors. Toward the final round, anticipation of no future consequences often leads to some end-game drop in contributions.

game_explanation:
This public goods game runs 17 rounds with 16 players. Each round, everyone privately chooses an integer contribution (0–20) from a 20-coin endowment; the group pot is multiplied by 4.5 and split equally. After seeing contributions and payoffs, players may spend costly reward units (cost 4 to giver, +2 to target) to reward others. Players can chat at any time; they see who rewarded them but not others' payoffs. Knowing the horizon (17 rounds) and given the player personas, I expect:
- A modest level of cooperation early on driven by highly prosocial players (MOOSE, CHICK) and some conditional cooperators (SLOTH, SNAKE).
- Some players (DOG, OWL, certain low-giving personas) will contribute little or nothing repeatedly, which will gradually depress cooperation.
- Rewards will be used sparingly, mainly early on to reinforce generous contributors (mutual reinforcement between MOOSE and CHICK; occasional spot rewards to high contributors like SNAKE or SLOTH). Because rewards are costly, they remain small (1–2 units).
- Over rounds cooperation drifts downward as free-riding persists and reward budgets are limited; toward the end, contributions are low (endgame effect), with only a few persistent cooperators continuing to give small amounts.
- Chat appears infrequently and strategically (calls to cooperate, short complaints, brief thanks).

game_explanation:
This repeated public-goods game (17 rounds) sets up a tension between private incentives (keep your 20 coins) and group returns (every coin gives 4.5 to the pot, split over 16 players). The individually rational action is to contribute 0, but social preferences, reciprocity, and the ability to punish/reward can sustain positive contributions. Expect early-round variation: a few prosocial players (CHICKEN, PARROT, some medium contributors) will contribute heavily and try to encourage cooperation. Some players predisposed to low transfers (GORILLA, CROCODILE, SNAKE, MOOSE to an extent) will contribute little or free-ride. Punishment will be used sparingly but strategically to sanction repeat low contributors; rewards will be rare and aimed at signaling appreciation of high contributors. Over rounds, peers react to observed behavior: punishments can raise contributions modestly, but toward the final rounds contributions tend to decline (end-game effect). Chat messages will be infrequent and goal-directed (encourage, threaten punishment, thank), and punishments will be limited in number and intensity each round.

game_explanation:
This is a 17-round public goods game with 16 players. Each round every player gets 20 coins and chooses an integer contribution (0–20) to a shared pot. The pot is multiplied by 4.5 and split equally, so each coin contributed returns 4.5/16 ≈ 0.28125 to each player. Individually, keeping a coin is strictly better for that player; collectively, contributing raises everyone’s payoff. After the contribution stage players can spend coins to punish (cost 4 to source, -15 to target per unit) or reward (cost 4 to source, +2 to target per unit) other players. Players can also send chat messages at any time; punishers/rewarders are identified in summary info.

Likely dynamics and incentives
- Incentive tension: rational self-interest pushes toward 0 contribution, but social preferences and conditional cooperation push toward positive contributions.
- Early rounds: many pro-social players will contribute fairly high; some low-trust or low-cooperation types will contribute little. Small, sparse rewards will be used to reinforce high cooperators; punishment will be used sparingly but strategically against persistent low contributors because punishment is costly to the punisher but highly damaging to the target.
- Feedback & learning: punished low contributors often raise their contributions in subsequent rounds. Rewarded cooperators tend to maintain contributions. A few players (with low return tendencies) may defect intermittently, provoking targeted punishment.
- Endgame: toward rounds ~15–17, many players anticipate termination and may reduce contributions (free-riding), so contributions often fall near the final rounds. Punishments may decline late because payoffs are short-lived; some last-round “spite” punishments or reward gestures can still occur but are rarer.

Overall, expect moderate-to-high contributions by strong cooperators (GORILLA, RABBIT, PARROT, CHICK), mixed/mid contributions by many, and occasional low contributions from a few (OWL, MOOSE, DUCK, FROG) with sparse punishment used to enforce norms.

game_explanation:
This public goods game pits private temptation (keep your 20 coins) against the group benefit of contributing to a shared pot (each coin yields 4.5 total, split among 16 players → ~0.281 each back to every player). The one-shot dominant strategy is to contribute 0, but many players have prosocial priors and may contribute some amount initially. Because contributions are revealed before rewarding, players can condition rewards on others' revealed behavior; rewards are costly to the giver (4 per reward unit) and transfer a smaller benefit (2 per unit) to the receiver, so rewards are mainly a signaling/enforcement device rather than welfare-enhancing. Expect:

- A core of reliably high contributors (Duck, Dog, Parrot, Monkey, Crocodile) who give large amounts early and keep doing so to the extent they are reinforced.
- Several conditional cooperators (Gorilla, Moose, Frog, Snake, Chicken, Chick, Cow, Rabbit) who contribute moderate amounts and slowly adjust down if they see declining group returns or weak reciprocity.
- Persistent low contributors/free-riders (Sloth, Owl, Pinguin) who contribute near 0.

- Chat will be infrequent: brief praise, calls-out of obvious free-riders, or thanks when rewarded.

Game dynamics: early rounds exhibit moderate-to-high cooperation sustained by mutual small rewards among the cooperative cluster. As the game progresses, many conditional cooperators slowly reduce contributions (observing lower marginal returns and fewer cross-group rewards), while the cooperative cluster continues to reward each other, keeping their mutual contributions relatively higher through round 17. Final round may see a small bump in mutual rewarding (endgame reciprocity).

game_explanation:
This public goods game creates a clear tension. Each round starts with 20 coins per player in the shared pot; each player can either withdraw their 20 (keep it private, contribute 0) or leave it in the pot (contribute 20). The pot is then multiplied by 11.5 and split equally among all active players. Mathematically, no matter what others do, each individual earns 8.5 fewer coins by contributing (11.5 gained to the group minus the 20 they gave up), so withdrawing (free‑riding) strictly dominates contributing in one-shot logic.

However, players do not know how many rounds remain, and many personas have mixed or prosocial priors. That uncertainty can sustain cooperation early on: some high‑prosocial individuals (RABBIT, PARROT, OWL, CHICK, etc.) are predisposed to contribute, at least initially, while low‑reciprocity types (SLOTH, DUCK, CROCODILE, SNAKE) are likely to withdraw from the start. Over repeated rounds with no communication, we expect initial contributions from a core of prosocial players, then a steady decline as players observe that withdrawing yields higher immediate payoffs and others stop contributing. By the middle rounds cooperation should fall substantially, and by the later rounds most players will be withdrawing; occasional moralistic or consistent cooperators might still contribute sporadically.

predicted_rounds.explanation: Players enter without information about how many rounds there will be. Prosocial personas (PARROT, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK, COW, PINGUIN and some mixed types) are likely to test cooperation by leaving their 20 in the pot. Selfish or low‑trust personas (SLOTH, DUCK, CROCODILE, SNAKE) are likely to withdraw immediately. Some ambivalent players (DOG, MONKEY, FROG, WHALE, MOOSE) may try contributing once to see what others do. Expect a substantial but not full set of contributors.

Players see round‑1 payoffs: withdrawers earned more (20 extra) than contributors. Many ambivalent or payoff‑sensitive players will switch to withdrawing. A core of reliably prosocial players (PARROT, RABBIT, OWL, CHICKEN, COW, PINGUIN, CHICK) may continue contributing for a few rounds, but the size of the contributing group will shrink.

The advantage of withdrawing is now clear in payoffs; many who tried contributing in round 1 switch out. A smaller coalition of reliable cooperators (PARROT, RABBIT, OWL, PINGUIN) remains to test whether cooperation can be sustained. Expect further decline in contributors.

With repeated evidence that free‑riding yields a higher round payoff, most conditional cooperators defect. A very small set of consistent cooperators (PARROT and RABBIT) may persist out of principle or a strong cooperative disposition. Expect only a couple of contributors.

Most players are now consistently withdrawing. A very small number of deeply prosocial players may still leave their money in (PARROT is a strong candidate to continue). Given the decreasing returns to contributing, even some prosocials may stop after seeing repeated lower net payoffs.

After several rounds where withdrawers consistently earned more, most players transition to always withdrawing. At this point the strategic incentive dominates social preference for the majority. Expect everyone to withdraw.
Once a consensus of withdrawing forms, it's self‑reinforcing: no one gains by unilaterally contributing. Expect repeated full withdrawal.
The group is in a stable withdrawing equilibrium; with no communication and known payoff advantages to withdraw, contributors are unlikely to reappear. Expect all withdraw.
With repeated evidence and no mechanism to punish or reward future rounds (players don't know horizon but have converged), cooperation remains absent. Continue to expect universal withdrawal.

If players still don't know the total number of rounds, some may keep playing as if future interactions matter; others will treat this as essentially the end and continue withdrawing. A handful of strongly prosocial players sometimes make a final symbolic contribution — RABBIT is the likeliest to do so. Expect at most one lone contribution.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK, SNAKE, CROCODILE, COW, PINGUIN, MONKEY, FROG, ELEPHANT, WHALE, HORSE </PLAYERS>

This is a repeated public goods game with a binary choice each round: either withdraw the 20 coins for private use (contribute 0) or leave the 20 in the shared pot (contribute 20). The pot is multiplied by 11.5 and split equally across all active players (19 players here), so each contributing player's act increases the group pot substantially — but that increase is shared by everyone. For any fixed number of other contributors, taking the 20 privately yields a strictly higher individual payoff than contributing (you keep the 20 plus the shared return), so defection is individually dominant. However, people differ in social preferences and beliefs: some personas (e.g., DOG, COW, CROCODILE, DUCK) are strongly pro-social or reliably cooperative in other tasks, while others (SLOTH, PARROT, MONKEY, FROG, ELEPHANT) are consistently self-regarding. Because players do not see others' simultaneous choices before acting but they do see round summaries afterwards, early rounds often show a mix of cooperation and free-riding. Observing that defectors earn more each round incentivizes cooperators to switch to defection in subsequent rounds. Since players do not know how many rounds remain, there is no common “last round” to encourage immediate collapse, but behavioural drift toward defection over repeated rounds is likely. I therefore predict an initial cluster of contributions led by pro-social personas, followed by steady decline as more players shift to taking the private 20 after seeing higher net payoffs for defectors. A small core of persistent cooperators may remain for several rounds.

game_explanation:
This repeated public-goods game pits individual incentives (withdraw 20 privately) against group returns (keep 20 in the shared pot so the group benefits). Because the pot each player can contribute is all-or-nothing (contribution = 20 or 0), payoffs reveal other players' choices: each player receives the same share of the multiplied public pot, and any player who withdrew 20 will always earn exactly 20 more than any contributor in the same round. That makes free-riding strictly individually profitable in each round, but some players have prosocial priors and may contribute early (hoping to sustain cooperation). Since players see every peer's round payoff at the summary stage, they can infer who contributed and adjust. Given these incentives and the personas, I predict some initial cooperation from the most prosocial/trusting players (e.g., FROG, ELEPHANT, some high-trust or medium cooperators), but cooperation will tend to decline over rounds as the advantage of withdrawing is repeatedly observed and no communication/punishment is possible. The likely pattern: moderate contributions early, then a steady unraveling toward everybody withdrawing by the final rounds.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game with 19 players who each either keep all 20 coins (contribute 0) or leave them all in the shared pot (contribute 20). The pot is strongly multiplied (x11.5) and split equally, so contributions create large shared returns — defecting (keeping 20) yields a short-term private advantage but may prompt punishment from cooperators. Punishment is available and costly: each punishment unit costs the source 2 coins and removes 3 coins from the target. Because punishment is visible (who paid how much is visible), punishers can target persistent free-riders, but punishing is costly so players punish sparingly. Players do not know the game length, which tends to stabilize cooperation when punishment deters free-riding early.

Likely unfolding:
- Early rounds: a mix of contributors and withdrawers. Cooperators will punish some free-riders but only a few targets and small units.
- Middle rounds: seeing punishments, many previously defecting players will switch to contributing to avoid future penalties; overall cooperation tends to rise.
- Later rounds: cooperation stabilizes at a high level, with occasional opportunistic defections and light, targeted punishment to enforce norms.
- Punishment remains sparse because it costs the punisher; punishments typically occur against repeat or opportunistic defectors rather than being indiscriminate.

# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK, SNAKE, CROCODILE, COW, PINGUIN, MONKEY, FROG, ELEPHANT, WHALE, HORSE </PLAYERS>

This is a repeated public-goods game with 19 players per round. Each player either contributes 20 coins to the shared pot (cooperate) or withdraws all 20 (defect). The pot is multiplied by 11.5 and split equally, so cooperation benefits the group but individual incentives push toward free-riding. Punishment lets players impose costs on specific targets (2 coins cost to punish, 3 coins deducted per unit from the target), making norm enforcement possible but itself costly.

Likely unfolding:
- Early rounds: conditional cooperators (those who typically give in allocation tasks) will contribute; a few stubborn selfish types (profiles with very low transfer/giving scores) will free-ride. Observers who value reciprocity or fairness will punish some visible free-riders to deter future defection.
- Middle rounds: some initially punished defectors may switch to cooperation; a small set of persistent low-cooperators (PINGUIN, WHALE in these priors) may keep free-riding. Punishment will be sparse and targeted — enough to enforce norms for most players but not so common as to be ubiquitous (because punishment is costly).
- Later rounds: because the number of rounds is unknown, full "end-game" collapse is less likely; cooperation remains fairly high. Punishment frequency may taper as group norms stabilize and players conserve resources, but a few targeted punishments recur against persistent free-riders.

Uncertainties:
- Some players with mixed priors (e.g., DUCK who sent a lot in trust but returned little) are hard to predict — they might alternate between cooperation and exploitation.
- The exact identity of punishers and magnitude of punishment is variable; real subjects punish sparingly. I therefore predict occasional 1-unit punishments, concentrated on persistent defectors.

Likely unfolding:
- Early rounds: conditional cooperators (those who typically give in allocation tasks) will contribute; a few stubborn selfish types (profiles with very low transfer/giving scores) will free-ride. Observers who value reciprocity or fairness will punish some visible free-riders to deter future defection.
- Middle rounds: some initially punished defectors may switch to cooperation; a small set of persistent low-cooperators (PINGUIN, WHALE in these priors) may keep free-riding. Punishment will be sparse and targeted — enough to enforce norms for most players but not so common as to be ubiquitous (because punishment is costly).
- Later rounds: because the number of rounds is unknown, full "end-game" collapse is less likely; cooperation remains fairly high. Punishment frequency may taper as group norms stabilize and players conserve resources, but a few targeted punishments recur against persistent free-riders.

Uncertainties:
- Some players with mixed priors (e.g., DUCK who sent a lot in trust but returned little) are hard to predict — they might alternate between cooperation and exploitation.
- The exact identity of punishers and magnitude of punishment is variable; real subjects punish sparingly. I therefore predict occasional 1-unit punishments, concentrated on persistent defectors.

Group norms are stable; many players stop punishing except occasionally. Persistent free-riders remain few. Expect little to no punishment this round.

Players still do not know whether the game ends; given the unknown horizon, many continue contributing. A few last reminders (small punishments) may be applied to persistent defectors, but overall cooperation persists.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game with 19 players. Each round each player either contributes the full 20 coins to the shared pot or withdraws the full 20 (so each contribution is binary: 0 or 20). The pot is multiplied by 11.5 and split equally across all players. That implies each coin placed in the pot yields 11.5/19 ≈ 0.6053 coin back to each individual; a single contributor loses 1 private coin but gets only about 0.6053 back per coin, so giving 20 coins is privately costly in the short term. The private incentive is to withdraw (free-ride). However, some players have stronger pro-social priors (e.g., ELEPHANT, HORSE) and are more likely to keep contributing even when it lowers their immediate round payoff; some players (e.g., MONKEY, WHALE, GORILLA, DUCK) have priors toward low contribution. Many players are mixed or conditional and will react to observed payoffs of others.

Key dynamics and uncertainties:
- Free-riding dominates narrowly in one-shot payoff terms, so without other forces we expect contribution rates to decline over repeated rounds.
- Because players only see round payoffs (not raw contributions) and don’t know how many rounds remain, they infer others’ behavior from relative payoffs each round. Contributors who see that defectors earned more will tend to reduce contributions; a small core of strongly pro-social players may continue contributing despite lower payoffs.
- Expect an initial round with moderate cooperation (some give it a try), then a decline as many realize withdrawing yields higher net payoffs. A small stable “cooperative core” (e.g., ELEPHANT, HORSE, and sometimes one or two medium cooperators) may persist through later rounds, with occasional short-lived returns to cooperation from conditional cooperators (PARROT, DOG, CROCODILE, etc.) depending on the payoff signals they observe.
- Because players cannot communicate and don’t know the total number of rounds, strategic end-game cooperation is unlikely; that uncertainty usually favors early cooperation followed by decay to low-but-nonzero contribution rates.
