predicted_rounds.explanation: This is the first encounter. Some players with pro-social priors try contributing; many with low pro-social priors test free-riding. Given the mix of personas, a moderate number of players contribute in round 1. Contributors earn less than withdrawers this round (because the per-player return per contributed coin < 1), so contributors will see lower net payoffs than free-riders — which will tend to push conditional cooperators away from contributing in subsequent rounds.

Players see round 1 payoffs: contributors earned about 133.16, withdrawers about 153.16. Many mixed-type players interpret this as a signal that free-riding is payoff-superior, so several of the round-1 contributors switch to withdrawing. A small set of medium-to-high pro-social players and some reciprocators keep contributing (PARROT, DOG, CROCODILE, COW, PINGUIN, ELEPHANT, HORSE). Overall contributions drop.

Round-2 payoffs again favor withdrawers (about 104.74) over contributors (about 84.74). Conditional cooperators who switched in round 2 may now revert to withdrawing. Only the most pro-social or strongly conditional players keep contributing: ELEPHANT and HORSE remain contributors; PARROT, who is fairly prosocial, tries to keep cooperating this round but many others withdraw.

The gap between withdrawers and contributors persists: withdrawers ≈ 56.32, contributors ≈ 36.32. PARROT re-evaluates and (given the clear payoff signal) steps back this round; a tiny core (ELEPHANT and HORSE) continues to contribute due to strong pro-social tendency. Most others withdraw.

Seeing repeated evidence that withdrawers do better, most mixed players continue to withdraw. The cooperative core (ELEPHANT and HORSE) remains stable — these avatars have strong priors favoring contribution and are relatively insensitive to the one-round payoff loss. No new players rejoin yet.

A small conditional player (PARROT) tries contributing again, perhaps hoping a cooperative streak will emerge or expecting others to reciprocate. This temporarily raises total contributions; conditional cooperators sometimes "test" re-entry when a tiny cooperative core is visible. ELEPHANT and HORSE persist.

PARROT’s attempt did not attract a broader return to cooperation (other players' payoffs remained higher when withdrawing), so PARROT drops back to withdrawing. The two strongly pro-social players (ELEPHANT, HORSE) continue as the persistent cooperative core.

A medium-type player (DOG) gives cooperation another try — perhaps DOG is experimenting or hoping to coordinate with the small core. This produces a small uptick to three contributors. It’s still not enough to overturn the private incentive; withdrawers earn more, but the tiny group of cooperators persists.

DOG’s experiment did not trigger broader cooperation; conditional cooperators revert to withdrawing. The persistent core of ELEPHANT and HORSE remain the only contributors. By now most players have observed several rounds where withdrawing gives them higher immediate payoff, so the group remains largely non-cooperative with a small stable kernel.

Near-round 10, a couple of conditional cooperators (PARROT, DOG) try another coordinated attempt alongside the persistent core. This yields a modest increase in contributions for the final shown round. Because players don't know the horizon, some conditional experimentation can occur even late — especially among avatars with moderate pro-social priors.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated, anonymous public-goods game with 19 players who each either keep the whole 20 coins privately (contribute 0) or leave the whole 20 in the public pot (contribute 20). The pot is multiplied by 11.5 and split equally, so cooperating is socially efficient but individually costly unless enough others cooperate. Because players do not know the number of rounds, forward-looking endgame behavior is unlikely; instead, we expect conditional cooperation and sparse, targeted punishment to enforce norms. Some avatars have strong pro-social priors (e.g., MOOSE, OWL, CROCODILE, MONKEY, SNAKE) and will tend to contribute; others (e.g., PARROT, SLOTH, GORILLA) have priors toward free-riding. Punishment is costly for the punisher, so players will punish sparingly and typically target repeat defectors to try to deter them. Because punishers are anonymous and punishment is costly, enforcement tends to be intermittent: a few players do most of the punishing, gradually reducing the number of free-riders.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game with 19 players each round deciding whether to leave the full 20-coin pot in the shared pool (contribute 20) or remove it for private use (contribute 0). The public pot is multiplied by 11.5 and split equally, so each player's round payoff is the equal share of the multiplied public pot plus whatever they withdrew privately (0 if they contributed, 20 if they withdrew). Players cannot see decisions before choosing, but after each round they see every player's round payoff (so they can infer who contributed vs. withdrew because the two payoff types differ by exactly 20).

Given the player personas, I expect:
- A core of strongly prosocial players (CHICKEN, PARROT, MONKEY, FROG, WHALE, HORSE, and to an extent MOOSE) will be inclined to contribute early and resist switching, at least for several rounds.
- Several players have low observed prosocial tendency (GORILLA, PINGUIN, some others) and will likely withdraw from the public pot consistently.
- Many players are "mixed" or conditional (SLOTH, DOG, RABBIT, OWL, CROCODILE, COW, etc.) and will respond to feedback: if withdrawers get higher payoffs they tend to drift toward withdrawal; if cooperating yields reasonable returns they may stay contributing.
- Because the total number of rounds is unknown, there is no terminal-round unraveling. Still, seeing withdrawers consistently get an immediate +20 over contributors creates strong incentives to defect; I therefore predict cooperation will start moderate-to-high and then decline as more players free-ride and conditional cooperators switch.

I will simulate these dynamics: some steady cooperators remain for many rounds, several early defectors stay out, and many mixed players defect across rounds as they see withdrawers receiving higher payoffs.

The pattern from Round 7 holds: the handful of cooperative players stays committed for now; others continue to withhold. With the public pot small, the payoff gap remains in favor of withdrawal.

Fatigue and repeated evidence that withdrawal yields larger immediate gains pushes one or two of the remaining cooperators to stop contributing (e.g., PARROT or CHICKEN might relent). Here, the contributor set shrinks further to three players (CHICKEN, MONKEY, WHALE).

Near the end of this simulated sequence, only a tiny minority (here MONKEY and WHALE) remain willing to contribute; most players withdraw each round because the guaranteed +20 advantage is persistent and public returns are low. This is the typical erosion of cooperation when withdrawal is privately attractive and defection incentives are visible.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK, SNAKE, CROCODILE, COW, PINGUIN, MONKEY, FROG, ELEPHANT, WHALE, HORSE </PLAYERS>

This is a binary public-goods game: each round each player either withdraws the whole 20 coins for private use (contribute 0 to the public pot) or leaves the 20 in the pot (contribute 20). The sum of contributed coins is multiplied by 11.5 and split equally among all 19 players. With k contributors the shared payoff per player is (20*k*11.5)/19 = 230*k/19. A player who withdraws keeps 20 privately and also receives the shared payoff; a full contributor forgoes the private 20 but still receives the shared payoff. So every round unilateral withdrawal strictly yields +20 over contributing, but if many withdraw the shared payoff falls. Because players do not know the total number of rounds, repeated-play incentives (conditional cooperation, reciprocity, punishment via withdrawal) can emerge but are tempered by free-riding temptations and uncertainty about when to “cash out.”

Given the provided personas, I predict an early-round optimism where many prosocial avatars (DUCK, CHICKEN, FROG, HORSE, COW, ELEPHANT, etc.) tend to leave their 20 in the pot. Some avatars with low trust/return cues (OWL, SNAKE, CROCODILE, WHALE, some opportunistic ones like SLOTH/GORILLA) are likely to withdraw immediately. Once players see round summaries (they can infer who withheld because payoffs reveal private + shared vs. shared-only), conditional cooperators respond to exploitation by reducing contributions over rounds. That leads to a gradual decline in contributions across rounds and a likely endgame collapse (more withdrawals) as the game continues without known horizon.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK, SNAKE, CROCODILE, COW, PINGUIN, MONKEY, FROG, ELEPHANT, WHALE, HORSE </PLAYERS>

This is a repeated public-goods setting with a strong social multiplier (11.5) but private withdrawal that gives each player a direct one-for-one benefit. Individually, taking the 20 coins privately strictly dominates leaving them in the pot (the private gain of 20 is larger than the personal share of returning contribution), so the Nash equilibrium is for everyone to withdraw (zero contribution). However, many players have prosocial priors (high trust / generous history), so we expect an initial burst of contributions from those players. Conditional cooperators will respond to observed peer behavior: if many others appear to have withdrawn, they will reduce contributions in later rounds; if they see others persist in contributing, they may continue.

Because players only see round payoffs (their own and peers') and not explicit chat, they infer contributions from payoff differences (contributors receive the shared, multiplied pot; withdrawers receive that same share plus 20). That makes free-riding visible and tends to accelerate unraveling among conditional players. Also, players do not know when the game ends, so classic end-game defection won’t be concentrated in the last rounds — instead we expect a decline from early hopeful cooperation toward a stable core of unconditional cooperators (those with strong prosocial priors), with the rest withdrawing in most later rounds.

Below I simulate a plausible 10-round path consistent with those incentives and the provided player priors: an initial mixed-cooperation round, a sharp drop as conditional cooperators respond to visible free-riding, and then a stable minority of steadfast contributors (the prosocial “core”) for the remaining rounds.

Because punishments are costly, expect only a few 1-unit punishments each round, targeted at repeat or blatant defectors. Over 10 rounds I predict gradual movement toward high cooperation after early punishment, with occasional tests (single defection) that are quickly punished.

game_explanation:
This is a repeated public-goods game with 19 players who each decide whether to contribute the full 20 coins to the shared pot or keep all 20 for private use. The public pot is multiplied by 11.5 and split equally, so each player’s share equals (11.5 * total_contribution) / 20. Contributing is socially efficient (it increases total resources shared) but individually costly: a contributor forgoes 20 private coins and only recoups a fraction of that via the shared split. Because players do not see others’ choices before deciding and they do not know how many rounds the game will last, early-round behavior mixes cooperation and cautious/strategic free-riding.

Punishment is available after payoffs are revealed: each punishment unit costs 2 coins for the punisher and subtracts 3 coins from the target. Punishment is costly but can deter free-riding; in many real PGGs cooperators use occasional, targeted punishments against clear defectors, which tends to raise cooperation over time. However, punishment is typically used sparingly — most players will not punish every defection, and a few stubborn or strongly selfish players may never switch to cooperation even under sanction.

Based on the provided personas, I predict:
- A majority of players are dispositionally cooperative or reciprocity-prone (CROCODILE, MONKEY, WHALE, DUCK, DOG, PARROT, etc.) and will often contribute, especially after the first couple rounds.
- A minority are strongly selfish or can be slow to respond to social incentives (SLOTH, FROG, SLOTH especially persistent), and will tend to free-ride repeatedly.
- Punishment will be targeted and modest: cooperators will occasionally expend small amounts to sanction obvious, repeated defectors early on; as cooperation becomes the norm, punishment frequency drops and becomes focused on the persistent non-cooperators.
- Over rounds we should see rising cooperation as players learn about others’ behavior and persistent defectors are selectively punished; a few stubborn defectors may remain throughout.

# GAME STARTS

This is a binary public-goods decision each round: each player either withdraws the full 20 coins for private use (contribution = 0) or leaves the 20 in the group pot (contribution = 20). The group pot is then multiplied by 11.5 and divided equally among all 19 players. With xi contributors, every player receives (230*xi)/19 from the pot; contributors only receive that share, while withdrawers receive the same share plus their private 20. That creates a clear individual incentive to withdraw (private +20) because withdrawers always earn 20 more than contributors for the same number of contributors. However, if many people contribute, the public return can be large and some players with pro-social tendencies (per personas) will continue contributing even though they earn less than withdrawers in that round. Players cannot see contemporaneous choices; they only see round summaries (every player's net payoff), so they can infer who contributed (payoff differences of about 20) and adjust. They also do not know how many rounds remain, which can sustain cooperation early. Expect initial cooperation from strongly pro-social personas, then gradual drift toward more withdrawal (free-riding) as the advantage of withdrawing is visible in summaries. Some consistently pro-social players will keep contributing throughout; some conditional cooperators will fall off after seeing withdrawers reap noticeably higher payoffs.

This online public-goods game supplies each player with a shared pot of 20 coins; each person either leaves the 20 in the pot (a full contribution) or withdraws it (contributes 0). The pot is multiplied by 11.5 and split equally across all active players, so individual incentives favor withdrawing (keep private 20) while group payoffs are maximized when everyone contributes. Punishment allows players to spend 2 coins per unit to reduce a target's earnings by 3 per unit; punishment is costly to senders and anonymous to targets. Because punishers aren't identified, punishments act as a social signal about norms rather than targeted retaliation. Players do not know how many rounds remain, so early rounds often show testing and norm-establishment behavior: many of the more prosocial personas (high trust/return tendencies) are likely to contribute early, while low-trust or low-return personas are more likely to withdraw. Punishment is usually used sparingly to deter repeat free-riding; when used, it's typically 1 unit from one or a few contributors aimed at persistent defectors. Over rounds we expect: (1) an initial majority of contributors with a few defectors; (2) selective, low-cost punishment aimed at the most obvious free-riders; (3) some defectors switching to contribute after being deterred, while a few persistent low-cooperators may continue to be punished; (4) occasional opportunistic defections later on and sparse retaliatory punishment, but no heavy, widespread punishment because it's costly.

game_explanation:
This public-goods game sets up a classic tension: each player can either leave the full 20-coin pot in the group (contribute 20) or withdraw it for private use (contribute 0). The group pot is multiplied by 11.5 and split evenly among the 19 players. If k players contribute, everyone receives (230·k)/19 from the multiplied pot; non-contributors also keep the private 20 coins, so non-contributors always earn 20 more than contributors in the same round. That makes withdrawing (defecting) the individually dominant action in a one-shot sense, while full contribution by everyone is the group optimum (everyone would earn 230 if k=19).

Given these payoffs and the player personas:
- We expect some initial contributions from the most pro-social players (e.g., GORILLA, COW, OWL, ELEPHANT, SLOTH) who have higher prior tendencies to give.
- Many players (PARROT, DOG, RABBIT, SNAKE, CHICKEN in some cases) are more likely to withdraw immediately.
- Because players see round-by-round net payoffs for all peers at the summary stage, they can infer who was contributing: contributors and withdrawers differ by exactly 20 coins in the same round, so exploited contributors will observe that withdrawers earned strictly more and will tend to switch to withdrawing in subsequent rounds.
- Over repeated rounds without communication and with an unknown horizon, conditioning and learning typically produce a decline in contributions: early partial cooperation is eroded as defectors reap the extra 20 each round, leaving a small set of persistent cooperators (stubborn prosocials) and mostly withdrawers.

# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK, SNAKE, CROCODILE, COW, PINGUIN, MONKEY, FROG, ELEPHANT, WHALE, HORSE </PLAYERS>

This is a standard binary public goods game with 19 players and a strong group multiplier (11.5). Each player has an endowment of 20 and must either contribute all 20 (cooperate) or keep all 20 (defect). The social return per token contributed is 11.5/19 ≈ 0.605 to the group (0.605 to each player), so from an individual standpoint every token contributed costs 1 privately but returns only ≈0.605 to the contributor — making defection the dominant short-term incentive. However, many players have prosocial priors (high trust/send or high dictator/ultimatum offers) that make them likely to contribute at least early on; others are low-giving types who will defect immediately. Because players do not know how many rounds remain and only observe peers' net payoffs after each round, they will infer who cooperated from payoff differences. Seeing defectors consistently earn more (they keep 20 plus the public share), many conditional cooperators will reduce contributions over subsequent rounds. I therefore predict an initial cluster of contributions from prosocial types, followed by a gradual decline toward widespread defection as players learn that defecting yields higher immediate payoffs. A few consistently prosocial players may continue contributing into middle rounds before giving up; by the end, nearly everybody defects.

This is a standard binary public goods game with 19 players and a strong group multiplier (11.5). Each player has an endowment of 20 and must either contribute all 20 (cooperate) or keep all 20 (defect). The social return per token contributed is 11.5/19 ≈ 0.605 to the group (0.605 to each player), so from an individual standpoint every token contributed costs 1 privately but returns only ≈0.605 to the contributor — making defection the dominant short-term incentive. However, many players have prosocial priors (high trust/send or high dictator/ultimatum offers) that make them likely to contribute at least early on; others are low-giving types who will defect immediately. Because players do not know how many rounds remain and only observe peers' net payoffs after each round, they will infer who cooperated from payoff differences. Seeing defectors consistently earn more (they keep 20 plus the public share), many conditional cooperators will reduce contributions over subsequent rounds. I therefore predict an initial cluster of contributions from prosocial types, followed by a gradual decline toward widespread defection as players learn that defecting yields higher immediate payoffs. A few consistently prosocial players may continue contributing into middle rounds before giving up; by the end, nearly everybody defects.

predicted_rounds.explanation: Players start with limited information about others, so many of the clearly prosocial personas contribute immediately (they have high past trust/dictator signals). Mixed/more selfish personas lean to defect. Expect a moderate-size cooperating minority and many defectors. After the round, payoff differences will reveal who cooperated (they will have substantially lower net payoffs than defectors).

Players observe Round 1 payoffs: defectors earned ~129 vs cooperators ~109, so defecting clearly paid off. Many conditional cooperators reduce contributions; staunch prosocials remain contributing. Expect fewer contributors than Round 1.

The pattern from Round 2 reinforces that defectors receive higher payoffs. Some earlier cooperators drop out; a persistent core of prosocials remains. Expect a smaller cooperating minority than Round 2.

Repeated evidence that defecting yields higher payoffs pushes more conditional cooperators to defect. A small group of committed contributors persists (but is shrinking). Expect slightly fewer cooperators than Round 3.

As the cooperating set gets small, even those remaining may start to defect because their private return (keeping 20) plus the now-smaller group share still beats contributing. Expect only the most prosocial cluster to contribute.
The cooperating minority shrinks further; group share is now noticeably smaller. Only a few strongly prosocial players keep contributing; most now defect every round to maximize immediate payoff.

With only one or two persistent contributors left, the public return per person is small; defecting becomes even more attractive. Expect most cooperators to have given up; maybe one or two principled players remain.

The private incentive to defect dominates strongly now. It is plausible only a single very committed player (e.g., extreme prosocial) still contributes, or none do. Here I assume one last committed contributor persists for another round.

Seeing that single contribution produces a tiny return (contributors now earn less than defectors), even the last committed contributor is likely to reconsider. It is plausible they still contribute one more round out of principle; here they do.

By now nearly everyone has seen cooperating yield lower payoffs than defecting every round. The final rounds are very likely to feature universal defection. I therefore predict no contributions in Round 10.

game_explanation:
This is a classic one-shot-style public goods game repeated without a known end point. Each round every player chooses whether to leave the entire 20-coin endowment in the shared pot (contribute 20) or withdraw it all for private use (contribute 0). The shared pot is multiplied strongly (×11.5) and split equally among all 19 players. Individually, taking the 20 privately always increases your round payoff by 20 compared with contributing, because the equal split of the multiplied pot means a contributor's private sacrifice is not fully returned to them. That makes withholding (defection) the individually dominant action, while universal contribution is group-optimal.

Key likely dynamics and uncertainties:
- Early rounds often start with some cooperation from prosocial or optimistic players (those with high giving/trust cues). Because players only see round payoffs (their own and peers'), not the raw contribution choices directly, payoffs reveal patterns but can be noisy — a high payoff could come from being one of few defectors or from many others cooperating.
- Seeing defectors earn a clear +20 advantage relative to cooperators (same share plus private 20) tends to push mixed or self-interested players toward defection in subsequent rounds. Without communication and without a known end, cooperation typically decays.
- Heterogeneity in personas matters: a subset with very strong prosocial priors (e.g., DOG, FROG, PINGUIN, OWL) are more likely to keep contributing for longer; others (low-trust/low-give profiles) are likely to defect early. Because players only see payoffs, some players may try “conditional” responses (switching after seeing defects) but overall the trend is toward more free-riding and eventual full defection.
- Small shocks or occasional returns from cooperation can temporarily sustain some contributors, but absent enforcement or communication the equilibrium with no contributions is the most stable long-run outcome.

predicted_rounds.explanation: Players enter without knowledge of how others will behave. Many prosocial avatars (DOG, FROG, PINGUIN, OWL, SLOTH, CHICKEN, MOOSE, RABBIT, DUCK, PARROT) are biased toward contributing at the start; others (SNAKE, CROCODILE, COW, MONKEY, ELEPHANT, HORSE, GORILLA, CHICK) are more likely to take privately. I predict a majority-but-not-universal initial cooperation as optimistic types test the waters.

Players observe that defectors earned ≈20 more than cooperators this round (153.16 vs 133.16). That clear advantage persuades many mixed/more self-interested players to switch to taking privately. A core of prosocials (DOG, FROG, PINGUIN, OWL, SLOTH, CHICKEN, MOOSE, RABBIT) likely remain contributing, but the number of contributors drops.

The payoff gap persists: defecting continues to give ≈20 more than contributing. Some previously contributing but less strongly prosocial players (e.g., PARROT, MOOSE or RABBIT) may start defecting; a smaller core of steadfast contributors (DOG, FROG, PINGUIN, OWL, CHICKEN, SLOTH) likely remains.

With cooperation rarer and payoffs showing defectors keep receiving a consistent advantage, many remaining conditional cooperators switch to taking privately. Only the most prosocial, persistent types (often DOG, FROG, PINGUIN, OWL) continue contributing.

As the benefit from the shared pot (per person) shrinks when few contribute, subgroup incentives swing further toward private taking. Only a tiny handful (the most prosocial, or those who value contributing intrinsically) continue. Expect 1–3 stubborn contributors.

Most players have seen consistent evidence that withholding yields a higher personal payoff than contributing. Only an ultra-prosocial player (e.g., DOG) might still contribute as a matter of preference or principle; otherwise, defection dominates.

Given the repeated signal that private taking yields strictly higher payoffs, and with no communication or enforcement, the stable equilibrium becomes universal defection (everyone withdraws the 20). Expect all players to take privately from here onward.
After observing round 7, there is no incentive to unilaterally return to contributing: anyone who tried to contribute would earn less than defectors. Full defection persists.
Stable universal defection continues; no new information to reverse behaviour.
Given the prior rounds and persistent individual advantage to defecting, all players continue to withdraw privately. End-state is universal defection.

game_explanation:
# GAME STARTS
<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK, SNAKE, CROCODILE, COW, PINGUIN, MONKEY, FROG, ELEPHANT, WHALE, HORSE </PLAYERS>

This is a repeated public-goods game without identified punishers. Each player decides whether to leave their 20 coins in the shared pot (contribute = 20) or withdraw them for private use (contribute = 0). The public pot is amplified by a large multiplier (11.5) and then split equally across all 19 players, so coordinated contribution benefits everyone. However, because private withdrawal gives an immediate +20 on top of the shared return, free-riding can look attractive unless cooperators punish defectors. Punishment is costly (2 coins per punishment unit) and only partially effective (deducts 3 coins per unit from the target), and players cannot see who punished them — only the total deducted and the amount each peer spent on punishment.

Likely dynamics:
- Early rounds: many players are inclined to contribute (norm-following and high expected group benefit), but a few low-prosociality players (from persona cues) may defect. Cooperators sometimes punish defectors to enforce norms, but punishments are typically sparse because punishment is costly.
- Middle rounds: punishment can deter repeat defection; some previously punished players switch to contributing. But because defecting yields a private gain when many contribute, some opportunistic defecting reappears occasionally.
- Later rounds: if punishment successfully discourages defection, cooperation rises; if it is too sparse or too costly, a mild level of defections can persist, with cooperators selectively punishing.

I will simulate a plausible 10-round sequence where punishment is used sparingly, targeted mostly at observed defectors, and where some players adjust behavior after being punished.

predicted_rounds.explanation: First round: many players start by contributing, but a few with low prosocial priors test the waters and free-ride. Cooperators respond with a small number of single-unit punishments directed at visible defectors to signal enforcement.

After seeing their deductions, the previously punished players are likely to shift toward contribution. A lone opportunist (short-run machete-seeker) defects again; cooperators punish that defector with a couple of single-unit sanctions.

Most players cooperate; a few opportunistic players who avoided punishment previously test defection again. Cooperators deliver a small number of single-unit punishments targeted at the defectors who reappeared.

Most players contribute again. A couple of players defect intermittently; cooperative players (especially high prosociality ones) issue single-unit punishments aimed at those defectors to maintain norms.
Some players who previously faced little cost try to defect again and several cooperators respond with targeted single-unit punishments. Punishment frequency remains modest.
Most players contribute again; a recurring lone defector is singled out with a couple of one-unit punishments from committed cooperators.
Full cooperation: after repeated small sanctions, most players converge on contributing. With no defectors present, there is no punishment this round.
A couple of players opportunistically defect again. Cooperators respond with focused single-unit punishments to deter recurrence.
Some familiar defectors reappear; a few cooperators again send single-unit punishments. Punishment remains targeted and limited.

Final round (unknown to players during play): a small number of defections persist; a last targeted punishment is applied to a repeat defector. Players typically keep punishment short and focused to avoid excess cost.

game_explanation:
This public goods game sets a strong binary choice each round: each player either contributes all 20 coins to the shared pot or withdraws them all (contribute 0). The shared pot is multiplied by 11.5 and equally split, so cooperative contributions can produce a sizable per-person benefit, but any single contributor is vulnerable to exploitation when others withdraw. Because punishments are costly to send (2 coins per punishment unit) and impose larger costs on the target (3 coins lost per unit), punishment can sustain cooperation but is used sparingly in practice. Anonymity of punishment senders (players do not learn who punished them) lowers targeted retaliation but also makes punishment a somewhat blunt instrument for enforcing norms.

Given the player priors, I expect a majority of players to cooperate in most rounds (PINGUIN, WHALE, SNAKE, DUCK, RABBIT, COW, HORSE and several mixed/mid-level cooperators like SLOTH, GORILLA, PARROT, OWL, CHICK) while a smaller subset will persistently defect (CROCODILE, MOOSE, FROG, ELEPHANT and some low cooperators like CHICKEN and initially MONKEY or DOG). Early rounds will show cooperators contributing and a handful of defectors free-riding; cooperators who care about fairness (or have high cooperative priors) will punish defectors occasionally. Because punishment is costly, punishers send small numbers of punishment units and focus on repeated defectors. Over rounds, some marginal defectors (e.g., DOG, possibly MONKEY or CHICKEN) may shift toward cooperation after being punished a couple of times, while deeply non-cooperative types (CROCODILE, MOOSE, ELEPHANT, FROG) are more likely to keep defecting. Punishment frequency should decline as cooperation consolidates (or as punishers tire), and actions will be sparse — only a few punishment edges per round.

<PLAYERS> SLOTH, GORILLA, DUCK, CHICKEN, DOG, PARROT, MOOSE, RABBIT, OWL, CHICK, SNAKE, CROCODILE, COW, PINGUIN, MONKEY, FROG, ELEPHANT, WHALE, HORSE </PLAYERS>

predicted_rounds.explanation: First round: many players start cooperatively by default (high trust / prosocial priors), while several low-cooperation personas test the group by withdrawing all 20. Cooperators who see clear free-riding may punish a few of the obvious defectors once contributions are revealed. Punishment will be light and targeted.

Players observe who contributed and the aggregate punishment totals (but not who punished whom). Some punishments and visibility may not fully deter stubborn defectors; cooperators may continue to target repeat defectors while limiting how often they punish because of costs.
Some marginal defectors (facing repeated small punishments) may switch to cooperating; others stay stubborn. Punishers remain selective and modest in how many punishment units they send.

Mid-game: a few players who were initially testing the group (e.g., MONKEY) may now start contributing as social norms strengthen; persistent defectors remain targets. Punishment continues but remains restrained to avoid excessive cost to punishers.

As the group stabilizes, more borderline defectors may join contributors (e.g., CHICKEN switches), leaving a smaller core of persistent free-riders. Punishment pays off for norm enforcement but is used less frequently as the set of defectors shrinks.

With most players cooperating, punishments become rare; a few cooperators still send small punishments toward the remaining defectors to discourage relapse. Punishment costs make many cooperators stop punishing repeatedly.

Some punishers make a last stronger effort against the most persistent defector; otherwise the group remains cooperative. A slightly larger punishment unit may be used occasionally to signal seriousness.

After repeated sanctioning, the most targeted defector (CROCODILE) may finally switch to contributing; a small set of stubborn non-cooperators (MOOSE, FROG, ELEPHANT) may remain. Cooperators now largely relax their punitive activity, but a few still issue small punishments against repeat offenders.

With nearly everyone contributing except a very small set of persistent non-cooperators, targeted punishments continue occasionally toward the remaining defectors; most players refrain to avoid unnecessary cost.
Final shown round: players still do not know when the game ends, so behavior stays similar. A few last small punishments target the remaining defectors; cooperation is high overall.
